topics.title: Definition and Examples of CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition and Examples of CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered integration of **computation, networking, and physical processes**. In a CPS, embedded computers and networks continuously **monitor, coordinate, and control** the physical processes, typically through tight **feedback loops** in which physical phenomena affect computations and computational decisions, in turn, actuate changes in the physical world.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECPT413 - Module 1):**
> *A Cyber-Physical System is a system featuring a tight combination of, and coordination between, the system's computational and physical elements. Embedded computers and networks monitor and control the physical processes, usually with feedback loops where physical processes affect computations and vice versa.* — Adapted from the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** Framework.

**Authoritative definitions aligned with KTU references:**

- **NSF (National Science Foundation, USA):** "Physical and engineered systems whose operations are monitored, coordinated, controlled and integrated by a computing and communication core."
- **NIST CPS Public Working Group:** "Smart systems that include engineered interacting networks of physical and computational components."
- **Lee & Seshia (MIT, 2017) — "Introduction to Embedded Systems: A Cyber-Physical Systems Approach":** "A CPS is a system in which a mechanical, chemical, or other physical process is controlled by a computational algorithm running on a distributed platform."

> [!NOTE]
> The acronym **CPS** was popularized in the 2006 U.S. National Science Foundation (NSF) workshop that formally recognized the convergence of **cyber** (computation/communication) and **physical** (sensing/actuation) processes.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine the **human body**:

| Body Part | CPS Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Brain | Computation Unit (CPU/MCU) | Decision making |
| Nervous System | Communication Network | Data transfer |
| Eyes / Skin / Ears | Sensors | Perception of environment |
| Muscles / Glands | Actuators | Action on the environment |
| The Physical World | Physical Process | Real-world state |

> Just as the human brain continuously senses the environment, processes information, and commands muscles to act, a **CPS continuously senses, computes, and actuates** within engineered systems.

**A simpler real-world analogy:** Think of an **automatic washing machine**. The **sensors** detect water level, cloth type, and temperature. The **microcontroller** decides the wash cycle duration. The **actuators** (motor, water valve, heater) physically execute the wash. This continuous sense–compute–actuate loop makes it a CPS.

---

## 1.3 Why CPS Matters — Engineering Significance

CPS forms the **backbone of Industry 4.0, smart cities, autonomous systems, and IoT-driven infrastructure**. The KTU 2024 syllabus places this topic at the very beginning because every subsequent module (security, attack modelling, defense) builds upon an accurate understanding of what a CPS is, and what it is not.

> [!TIP]
> **CPS ≠ IoT ≠ Embedded System.** While related, a CPS specifically emphasizes the **bidirectional, real-time, closed-loop interaction** between cyber and physical domains. An embedded system is a *component* of a CPS, and IoT is a *subset* that emphasizes connectivity.

---

## 1.4 Common Misconceptions to Avoid

- ❌ "CPS is just another name for IoT." — IoT focuses on connectivity and data; CPS focuses on **control and feedback**.
- ❌ "Any computer-controlled machine is a CPS." — A desktop PC controlling a printer via USB is *not* a CPS; a closed-loop process controller in a refinery *is*.
- ❌ "CPS is purely software." — The physical element is **mandatory and non-negotiable**.



<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of CPS

Every CPS, regardless of its application, is composed of three tightly coupled pillars. The KTU examiner expects students to articulate these pillars clearly.

1. **Cyber Layer (Computation + Communication)**
   - Microcontrollers, microprocessors, FPGAs, edge/cloud servers.
   - Communication protocols: Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee, LoRa, 5G, CAN, Modbus, OPC-UA.
   - Software: RTOS, control algorithms, AI/ML inference engines.

2. **Physical Layer (Sensing + Actuation + Process)**
   - **Sensors** (transducers) convert physical variables (temperature, pressure, position) into electrical signals.
   - **Actuators** (motors, valves, relays) convert electrical signals into physical actions.
   - The controlled **process** is the real-world system (engine, power grid, human body).

3. **Bridge / Interface Layer**
   - **ADC/DAC converters**, signal conditioning circuits, and **transducers**.
   - Real-time operating constraints ensure deterministic behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **security boundary** in a CPS exists at the interface between the cyber and physical layers. This is precisely why the course code **PECPT413** is dedicated to securing this interface.

---

## 2.2 Operational Feedback Loop (The Heart of a CPS)

A CPS operates as a **closed-loop control system**. The classical loop has four stages:

- **Sense:** Sensors acquire physical state variables.
- **Compute:** Processing unit evaluates the state against a desired setpoint.
- **Communicate:** Data is exchanged with peers, edge, or cloud.
- **Actuate:** Actuators apply physical changes to drive the system toward the desired state.

This loop typically runs at **millisecond to microsecond timescales** in safety-critical systems, and at **second to minute timescales** in supervisory control.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> The following table summarizes the **mathematical abstractions and key notations** for CPS as required in KTU 2024 examinations. All notations are **LaTeX-rendered** and use `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table structure.

| Symbol / Notation | Description | Domain / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $x(t)$ | Continuous-time **state vector** of the physical process | $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| $u(t)$ | Control input vector from cyber layer | $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ |
| $y(t)$ | Measured output vector from sensors | $\mathbb{R}^{p}$ |
| $A$ | State transition matrix | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ |
| $B$ | Input matrix | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ |
| $C$ | Output matrix | $\mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$ |
| $D$ | Feed-through matrix | $\mathbb{R}^{p \times m}$ |
| $\dot{x}(t)$ | Time derivative of state, $\dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| $K$ | Controller gain matrix | $\mathbb{R}^{m \times p}$ |
| $\tau_{d}$ | **Network-induced delay** | seconds (s) |
| $T_{s}$ | Sampling period | seconds (s) |
| $\omega_{n}$ | Natural frequency | rad/s |
| $\zeta$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless |
| $y(t) = f(x(t), u(t))$ | **Generic CPS output function** | — |
| $u(t) = K \cdot (r(t) - y(t))$ | **Negative feedback control law** | — |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Engine Control Units (ECUs) regulate fuel injection in **real-time** at up to 100 Hz.
- **Healthcare:** Pacemakers sense cardiac rhythm and deliver electrical pulses; delays > **100 ms** can be fatal.
- **Energy:** Smart grids balance load across millions of nodes using **SCADA** (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) systems.
- **Manufacturing:** Industrial robots achieve positional accuracy of **±0.02 mm** using CPS-grade feedback control.
- **Aerospace:** Fly-by-wire systems in modern aircraft replace mechanical linkages with **redundant CPS networks**.



<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Mathematical State-Space Model of a CPS

A CPS is fundamentally a **continuous-time dynamical system** with embedded digital control. The **state-space representation** is the standard analytical tool adopted in KTU-endorsed references for PECPT413.

### 3.1.1 Continuous-Time State-Space Equations

A general **linear time-invariant (LTI)** CPS in continuous time is described by two coupled equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}(t) &= A \cdot x(t) + B \cdot u(t) \quad &\text{(State / Process equation)} \\
y(t) &= C \cdot x(t) + D \cdot u(t) \quad &\text{(Output / Sensor equation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — State Equation Derivation (Physical Layer):**
We begin with the physical process whose behaviour is governed by a first-order linear differential equation. Let $x(t)$ represent the state (e.g., temperature, position, voltage) of the physical process.

- The natural evolution of $x(t)$ is governed by the system dynamics matrix $A$.
- The control input $u(t)$ influences $x(t)$ through the input matrix $B$.
- Therefore: $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$.

**Step 2 — Output Equation Derivation (Cyber Layer / Sensors):**
The sensors measure a linear combination of states, weighted by matrix $C$, and may also directly sense the input through $D$.

- Therefore: $y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$.

---

### 3.1.2 Incorporating the Controller (Closing the Loop)

A CPS **closes the loop** by feeding back the measured output $y(t)$ to a digital/analog controller that computes a new control input $u(t)$.

A classical **proportional (P) controller** with gain $K$ uses a reference signal $r(t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e(t) &= r(t) - y(t) \quad &\text{(Tracking error)} \\
u(t) &= K \cdot e(t) = K \cdot [r(t) - y(t)] \quad &\text{(Negative feedback law)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Closed-Loop Substitution:**
Substituting the controller into the state equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}(t) &= A x(t) + B \cdot K [r(t) - y(t)] \\
&= A x(t) + B K r(t) - B K [C x(t) + D u(t)]
\end{aligned}
$$

For a strictly proper system where $D = 0$ (no direct feed-through, the common engineering case for physical sensors):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}(t) &= (A - B K C) x(t) + B K r(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix $\big(A_{cl} = A - B K C\big)$ is the **closed-loop state matrix**. Stability requires all eigenvalues of $A_{cl}$ to lie in the **open left-half of the complex plane**, i.e., $\text{Re}\big(\lambda_i(A_{cl})\big) < 0$ for every eigenvalue $\lambda_i$.

---

### 3.1.3 Analytical Matrix — System Performance Criteria

The KTU 2024 rubric expects students to articulate **performance metrics** in terms of these matrices. The following table consolidates the analytical results.

| Performance Metric | Definition | Formula | Engineering Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stability** | Bounded output for bounded input | All $\lambda_i(A_{cl}) < 0$ | $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ |
| **Steady-State Error** | Limiting error as $t \to \infty$ | $e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t)$ | Ideally $0$ for regulation |
| **Settling Time** | Time to reach $\pm 2\%$ of final value | $T_{s} \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | Domain-specific |
| **Overshoot** | Peak deviation beyond setpoint | $M_{p} = e^{-\pi \zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | $< 5\%$ for industrial CPS |
| **Rise Time** | Time to go from $10\%$ to $90\%$ of final value | $T_{r} \approx \dfrac{1.8}{\omega_n}$ | Domain-specific |

---

### 3.1.4 Worked-Out Example — A Thermostat CPS

Consider a room heating system modelled as a first-order LTI CPS.

**Step 1:** Let $x(t)$ be the room temperature (°C). The cooling follows Newton's law:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = -a \, x(t) + b \, u(t)
$$

where $a = 0.05 \, \text{s}^{-1}$ (cooling rate) and $b = 0.1 \, \text{°C/s}$ (heater gain).

**Step 2:** A single temperature sensor measures the room temperature directly:

$$
y(t) = x(t)
$$

So the matrices are $A = [-0.05]$, $B = [0.1]$, $C = [1]$, $D = [0]$.

**Step 3:** With a proportional controller $K = 0.8$ and setpoint $r(t) = 25 \, \text{°C}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{cl} &= A - B K C = -0.05 - (0.1)(0.8)(1) = -0.13
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** The closed-loop eigenvalue is $\lambda = -0.13$. Since $\text{Re}(\lambda) = -0.13 < 0$, the CPS is **asymptotically stable**.

**Step 5:** Settling time:

$$
T_{s} \approx \frac{4}{0.13} \approx 30.77 \text{ seconds}
$$

> ✅ **Result:** The thermostat will regulate the room temperature to 25°C within roughly 31 seconds — a realistic engineering outcome.

---

### 3.2 CPS Architecture Layer Analysis

A CPS can be decomposed into **four architectural layers** as per the KTU 2024 module structure.

| Layer | Components | Key Property | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Physical Process** | The actual plant / system | Continuous dynamics | Chemical reactor, vehicle |
| **L2 — Sensing & Actuation** | Sensors, ADC, DAC, motors | Real-time I/O | Thermocouple, servo motor |
| **L3 — Computation & Communication** | MCU, RTOS, network stack | Determinism, latency | ESP32, CAN bus, 5G |
| **L4 — Application & Services** | Cloud, analytics, HMI | Scalability, intelligence | AWS IoT, Grafana dashboard |



<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The CPS Closed-Loop Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the canonical **sense → compute → communicate → actuate** feedback loop that defines every CPS. All node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Physical Process] -->|Measured variables| node2[Sensor Subsystem]
    node2 -->|Analog signals| node3[ADC and Signal Conditioning]
    node3 -->|Digital samples| node4[Computation Unit MCU CPU]
    node4 -->|Control decision| node5[Communication Network]
    node5 -->|Networked command| node6[Actuation Subsystem]
    node6 -->|Physical effect| node1
    node4 -->|Telemetry data| node7[Edge or Cloud Services]
    node7 -->|Analytics and AI insight| node4
    node8[Human Operator HMI] -->|Setpoint reference| node4
    node4 -->|Status display| node8
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Trace the loop clockwise from the **Physical Process** → Sensor → ADC → Computation → Network → Actuator → back to the Physical Process. This is the defining characteristic of a CPS.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture Diagram — Layered CPS Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L4[L4 APPLICATION AND SERVICES]
        nodeA1[Cloud Analytics]
        nodeA2[HMI and Dashboards]
        nodeA3[AI and ML Inference]
    end
    subgraph L3[L3 COMPUTATION AND COMMUNICATION]
        nodeB1[RTOS and Control Software]
        nodeB2[Network Stack WiFi 5G CAN]
        nodeB3[Security and Authentication]
    end
    subgraph L2[L2 SENSING AND ACTUATION]
        nodeC1[Sensors Temperature Pressure Position]
        nodeC2[Actuators Motors Valves Relays]
        nodeC3[ADC and DAC Converters]
    end
    subgraph L1[L1 PHYSICAL PROCESS]
        nodeD1[Plant under control]
        nodeD2[Environmental disturbances]
    end
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
```

> [!TIP]
> The **bidirectional arrows** between every layer are intentional — they emphasize that CPS is **not a unidirectional pipeline** but a **deeply interactive, closed-loop stack**.

---

## 4.3 Plain-Text Schematic (for reproduction in answer sheets)

For KTU answer scripts where drawing tools are unavailable, the following **ASCII schematic** is examiner-friendly and accepted in valuations.

```
            +-----------------------------------+
            |         APPLICATION LAYER          |
            |  (HMI, Cloud, AI, Dashboards)      |
            +-----------------+-----------------+
                              ^  v  (data & control)
            +-----------------+-----------------+
            |     COMPUTATION & COMMUNICATION    |
            |  (RTOS, MCU, Network Stack)        |
            +-----------------+-----------------+
                              ^  v  (sensor data & actuator cmd)
            +-----------------+-----------------+
            |   SENSING & ACTUATION INTERFACE    |
            |  (Sensors, ADC, Actuators, DAC)    |
            +-----------------+-----------------+
                              ^  v  (physical signals)
            +-----------------+-----------------+
            |        PHYSICAL PROCESS           |
            |   (Plant, Environment, Human)     |
            +-----------------------------------+
```

> **Note for students:** Always draw the **bidirectional arrows** between layers in your exam answer. Examiners specifically look for the **closed-loop nature** of the CPS architecture as a marker of conceptual understanding.

---

## 4.4 Reference Examples Mapped to Layers

| Real-World CPS | L1 Physical Process | L2 Sensor / Actuator | L3 Compute / Network | L4 Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicle** | Road, obstacles | LiDAR, camera, motor | ECU, CAN bus, 5G | Path planning AI |
| **Smart Grid** | Power distribution | Smart meter, breaker | SCADA, IEC 61850 | Load forecasting |
| **Insulin Pump** | Blood glucose | Glucose sensor | Embedded controller | Patient app |
| **Smart Building HVAC** | Indoor air | Temp, CO₂ sensor | BMS controller, BACnet | Energy dashboard |
| **Industrial Robot** | Workpiece | Encoder, force sensor | Real-time controller | Production scheduler |



<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam Pattern - Model Question, Dec 2024]

**Q: Define a Cyber-Physical System (CPS). Give two real-world examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered system in which a **computational/communication core** is tightly integrated with a **physical process** through sensors and actuators, operating in a **closed feedback loop** where physical phenomena affect computations and vice versa.
>
> **Examples (1 mark — any two):**
> 1. **Autonomous Vehicle** — Sensors (LiDAR, camera) perceive the road; on-board computers process data; actuators steer, accelerate, and brake the vehicle in real-time.
> 2. **Smart Grid** — Smart meters sense consumption; SCADA systems balance load; automated switches reroute power dynamically.
>
> *(Acceptable alternatives: Medical pacemaker, industrial robotic arm, smart HVAC, drone flight controller, water treatment plant.)*

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam Pattern - Model Question, Dec 2024]

**Q: List and briefly explain any four characteristics of Cyber-Physical Systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Characteristic | Brief Explanation (½ mark each) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Real-time operation** | Decisions and actuations must occur within strict time deadlines (often milliseconds). |
| 2 | **Closed-loop feedback** | Continuous sensing → computation → actuation forms a tight control loop. |
| 3 | **Heterogeneity** | Integrates hardware (sensors, actuators), software, and networks of different types. |
| 4 | **Safety-criticality** | Failures can cause physical harm, environmental damage, or loss of life. |

> *(Other acceptable characteristics: Cyber-Physical Coupling, Distributed Architecture, Determinism, Security sensitivity, Scalability.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. You must answer **one full question** out of two. Each sub-part is typically **7 marks**.

---

### Question B — Option A. [KTU University Exam Pattern - Model Question, Dec 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **architecture of a Cyber-Physical System** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **sensing, computation, communication, and actuation layers**.
**Q: (b)** Discuss **five real-world application domains** of CPS, giving **at least one detailed example** for each domain.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Architecture of CPS (3 marks):**

A CPS is organized as a **four-layered closed-loop architecture**:

1. **L1 — Physical Process Layer:** The real-world plant or environment (e.g., a chemical reactor, the human body, a power grid).
2. **L2 — Sensing & Actuation Layer:** Converts physical signals to electrical (and vice versa) via sensors, ADC/DAC, and actuators.
3. **L3 — Computation & Communication Layer:** Embedded processors running real-time control algorithms; communication networks (CAN, Wi-Fi, 5G).
4. **L4 — Application & Services Layer:** HMI, cloud analytics, AI/ML inference, and supervisory control software.

**Role of each layer (3 marks):**

- **Sensing layer** acquires physical variables (temperature, pressure, position) and converts them to digital data.
- **Computation layer** runs control laws (e.g., $u = K(r - y)$) and decision algorithms.
- **Communication layer** transmits data between distributed nodes and to higher layers, often under strict latency constraints.
- **Actuation layer** physically modifies the controlled process (heaters, motors, valves).

**Neat block diagram (1 mark):** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes. Examiner expects a labelled closed-loop diagram with bidirectional arrows.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

| # | Domain | Detailed Example | Why it is a CPS |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transportation** | **Autonomous Vehicle** — Uses LiDAR, cameras, GPS as sensors; runs SLAM and path planning; controls steering and braking in real-time. | Continuous sense-compute-act loop ensures passenger safety. |
| 2 | **Healthcare** | **Smart Insulin Pump** — Continuous glucose monitor (CGM) senses blood sugar; embedded controller computes required dose; micropump delivers insulin. | Closed-loop control replaces manual monitoring. |
| 3 | **Energy** | **Smart Grid** — Smart meters sense consumption; SCADA balances generation and load; automated switches reroute power. | Real-time feedback prevents blackouts. |
| 4 | **Industrial Automation** | **Collaborative Robot (Cobot)** — Force sensors detect human proximity; controller adjusts speed/trajectory; servo motors execute motion. | Enhances safety in human-robot workspaces. |
| 5 | **Smart Cities** | **Adaptive Traffic Control** — Inductive loops and cameras sense traffic density; central controller computes signal timings; signal lights are actuated. | Reduces congestion through real-time data. |

> **Examiners' Tip:** A common mistake is to merely *list* domains without explaining the **CPS feedback loop** in each example. Always mention the **sensor → compute → actuator** chain explicitly.

---

### Question B — Option B. [KTU University Exam Pattern - Model Question, Dec 2024]

**Q: (a)** Compare **traditional embedded systems** with **Cyber-Physical Systems** in terms of architecture, timing, control, and security.
**Q: (b)** Explain the **state-space mathematical model** of a CPS with a simple example. Why is the **closed-loop state matrix** $A_{cl} = A - BKC$ important?

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| Dimension | Traditional Embedded System | Cyber-Physical System |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Standalone microcontroller with fixed firmware | Distributed, networked, multi-layer architecture |
| **Timing Constraints** | Soft real-time (e.g., mobile apps) | Hard real-time (millisecond to microsecond deadlines) |
| **Control Focus** | Often open-loop or no control | Strict closed-loop feedback control |
| **Interaction with Physical World** | Limited or none | Deep, bidirectional, continuous |
| **Security Sensitivity** | Moderate (e.g., consumer devices) | High (safety-critical — lives at stake) |
| **Scale** | Single device | Often system-of-systems (e.g., entire smart grid) |
| **Communication** | Optional | Mandatory and often redundant |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** A CPS is an *evolution* of the embedded system concept, extending it with **networking, physical control, and safety-critical real-time constraints**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**State-space model of a CPS (4 marks):**

A continuous-time LTI CPS is described by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}(t) &= A x(t) + B u(t) \\
y(t) &= C x(t) + D u(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the state vector of the physical process.
- $u(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the control input from the cyber layer.
- $y(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ is the measured sensor output.
- $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ are constant matrices of appropriate dimensions.

**Example (2 marks):** A first-order thermal CPS with $A = -0.05$, $B = 0.1$, $C = 1$, $D = 0$ and controller $K = 0.8$ gives $A_{cl} = -0.13$, which is stable since $\text{Re}(\lambda) = -0.13 < 0$.

**Importance of $A_{cl} = A - BKC$ (1 mark):** The closed-loop state matrix governs the **eigenvalues** that determine **stability, transient response, and robustness** of the CPS. KTU examiners often award full marks only if the student explicitly mentions that **all eigenvalues of $A_{cl}$ must have negative real parts for stability**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in CPS Definition Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating CPS with IoT** — Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if the answer does not emphasize the **closed-loop, real-time control** aspect that distinguishes CPS from IoT.
> 2. **Omitting the physical layer** — A definition that mentions only "computing and networking" *without* the physical process is incomplete and will lose at least 1 mark.
> 3. **No diagram in 7-mark sub-parts** — Even a hand-drawn ASCII/block diagram is mandatory in any "explain the architecture" question. **Zero diagram = -1 to -2 marks.**
> 4. **Examples without feedback explanation** — Stating "smartwatch is a CPS" without explaining the sense-compute-actuate loop is considered a *weak* answer.
> 5. **Confusing state-space variables** — Examiners specifically watch for the misuse of $x$, $u$, $y$. Use $x$ for state, $u$ for input, $y$ for measured output.
> 6. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — Always mention units (°C, seconds, m/s) for full credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Definition and Examples of CPS**

- ✅ CPS = **Computation + Communication + Physical Process** integrated via a **closed feedback loop**.
- ✅ Core authorities: **NIST** and **NSF** definitions are the gold standard for KTU 2024.
- ✅ The three pillars are: **Cyber (computation/network)**, **Physical (sensors/actuators)**, **Bridge (ADC/DAC/transducers)**.
- ✅ CPS is **not** the same as **IoT** (CPS emphasizes control; IoT emphasizes connectivity).
- ✅ CPS is **not** the same as a generic **Embedded System** (CPS is a superset with networking and physical control).
- ✅ The four-layer architecture: **L1 Physical → L2 Sensing/Actuation → L3 Computation/Communication → L4 Application/Services**.
- ✅ The state-space model: $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ and $y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$.
- ✅ Closed-loop matrix: $A_{cl} = A - BKC$; stability requires $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$.
- ✅ Key examples to memorize: **Autonomous Vehicle, Smart Grid, Medical Pacemaker, Industrial Robot, Smart HVAC, Adaptive Traffic Control, Smart Insulin Pump**.
- ✅ Real-time, safety-critical, heterogeneous, distributed, and security-sensitive are the **hallmark characteristics**.
- ✅ In exam answers, always include a **neat block diagram** with **bidirectional arrows** to show the closed loop.
- ✅ The course code **PECPT413** focuses on **securing** the cyber-physical interface — this module lays the foundation for every later module.

> 🎯 **One-line takeaway:** *A Cyber-Physical System is any engineered system in which a digital computation core continuously senses, decides, and actuates a physical process through a deterministic, real-time, closed feedback loop — making it simultaneously a control system, an embedded system, and a networked system.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration of Computing, Networking, and Physical Elements in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Integration of Computing, Networking, and Physical Elements in CPS ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cyber-Physical System (CPS):** A Cyber-Physical System is an engineered, networked system whose operations are monitored, coordinated, controlled, and integrated by a **computing and communication core** that is deeply intertwined with the **physical processes** it senses and actuates. Formally, a CPS can be defined as the tight conjoining of computation, communication, and physical dynamics, where feedback loops run in both the **cyber** and **physical** domains. In the context of **Integration**, the term refers to the **holistic, seamless unification** of three originally disparate technological layers into a single, coherent, time-sensitive control system: | Layer | Primary Function | Key Technologies | |---|---|---| | **Computing Layer** | Decision making, data processing, control logic | Microcontrollers, SoCs, Edge/Fog/Cloud compute, RTOS | | **Networking Layer** | Data transport, synchronization, coordination | CAN, EtherCAT, WirelessHART, 5G, MQTT, OPC-UA | | **Physical Layer** | Sensing, actuation, physical state evolution | Sensors, Actuators, Mechanical/Chemical/Thermal processes | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECPT413 Module 1):** The integration of these three layers is *not* a loose interconnection — it is a **co-design** problem where temporal, functional, and security boundaries between the cyber and physical worlds must be jointly engineered. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Analogy — The "Smart Human Body"** > Imagine the human body as a CPS: > - **Physical Elements** = the muscles, organs, and limbs (analogous to sensors, motors, valves, and physical processes). > - **Computing Elements** = the brain and spinal cord (the microcontrollers, processors, and control algorithms that make decisions). > - **Networking Elements** = the nervous system (the wired/wireless buses, protocols, and signals that carry information between brain and body). > > Just as a reflex arc **integrates** a touch (sensory input), a decision (compute), and a withdrawal (physical actuation) in milliseconds — a CPS integrates sensing, computation, and actuation in a tightly coupled feedback loop. The "smartness" lies in how *tightly* and *correctly* these three layers communicate under real-time constraints. ### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics in CPS Integration > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Quantities You Must Know:** > - **Latency (L):** End-to-end delay from physical event to actuation response. Unit: **milliseconds (ms)** or **microseconds (µs)**. Typical hard real-time bound: **≤ 1 ms** for safety-critical CPS. > - **Jitter (J):** Variation in latency over time. Unit: **ms**. Critical metric for control stability. > - **Sampling Rate (f_s):** Frequency at which the physical process is digitized. Unit: **Hertz (Hz)**. Nyquist bound: f_s ≥ 2·f_signal. > - **Time-to-Actuate (T_act):** Total loop time = Sensing + Computation + Communication + Actuation. > - **Clock Skew (Δt):** Maximum deviation between distributed node clocks. Unit: **µs** (PTP/TSN) to **ms** (NTP). ### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Integration — Quick View > [!NOTE] > **1. Computing-Physical Integration** — The processor must observe and steer continuous physical dynamics (differential equations), not just discrete events. > > **2. Network-Physical Integration** — The network must carry *deterministic*, time-stamped sensor/control traffic; conventional best-effort IT networks are insufficient. > > **3. Computing-Networking Integration** — Compute nodes must synchronize their clocks, share state, and execute distributed control algorithms (consensus, flocking) over the network. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a CPS Integration Node A CPS is rarely a single monolithic device — it is a **distributed ensemble** of nodes, each performing **Sense → Compute → Communicate → Actuate**. The integration architecture is decomposed into the following logical sub-blocks: #### A. The Sensing Subsystem (Physical → Cyber) - **Transduction:** Converts physical quantities (temperature T, pressure P, velocity v, voltage V) into electrical signals. - **Signal Conditioning:** Amplification, filtering, linearization. - **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):** Digitizes at sampling rate f_s, quantization resolution b bits. - **Time-Stamping:** Each sample t_k is tagged with a synchronized clock (PTP/TSN) for deterministic state estimation. #### B. The Computing Subsystem (Cyber Core) - **Microcontroller / SoC:** Executes control law u(t) = K·e(t), where e(t) = r(t) − y(t) is the error signal. - **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS):** Schedules tasks under hard deadlines (Rate Monotonic, EDF). - **State Estimator:** Kalman Filter, Luenberger Observer — fuses noisy sensor data into an estimate x̂(t) of the physical state. - **Security Module:** Hardware Root of Trust (RoT), secure boot, cryptographic accelerators (AES, SHA, ECC). #### C. The Networking Subsystem (Cyber Backplane) - **Fieldbus / Real-Time Ethernet:** EtherCAT, PROFINET, TSN — for industrial CPS. - **Wireless Sensor-Actuator Networks (WSAN):** WirelessHART, 6LoWPAN, 5G URLLC — for spatially distributed CPS. - **Middleware:** DDS, OPC-UA, MQTT-SN — for semantic interoperability and publish/subscribe data flow. #### D. The Actuation Subsystem (Cyber → Physical) - **Digital-to-Analog Conversion (DAC):** Converts control command to analog. - **Power Electronics:** Motor drives, PWM inverters, valve actuators. - **Plant (Physical Process):** The continuous-time physical system being controlled. ### 2.2 The Canonical CPS Feedback Loop (The "CPS Heartbeat") > [!IMPORTANT] > The CPS loop is fundamentally a **discrete-time controller wrapped around a continuous-time plant** — and the network sits *inside* the loop, not outside it. $$ y(t) \xrightarrow{\text{ADC}} y[k] \xrightarrow{\text{Estimator}} \hat{x}[k] \xrightarrow{\text{Controller}} u[k] \xrightarrow{\text{DAC + Actuator}} u(t) \xrightarrow{\text{Plant}} y(t) $$ **Why this matters:** In classical control, the network is *outside* the loop (treated as instantaneous). In CPS, the network is *inside* the loop — meaning **network delay τ_net, packet loss p, and jitter** directly affect **stability margins** of the physical system. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Exam — PECPT413 Module 1** | # | Formula / Concept | Description | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T_{\text{loop}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{net}} + T_{\text{act}}$ | Total CPS loop time | Sets hard real-time deadline | | 2 | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\text{signal,max}}$ (Nyquist) | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing | Sensor/ADC design | | 3 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{quant}} = 6.02b + 1.76 \text{ dB}$ | Quantization signal-to-noise ratio (b bits) | ADC resolution choice | | 4 | $u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i \sum e[k] + K_d \Delta e[k]$ | Discrete PID control law | Embedded controller design | | 5 | $u(t) = -K\hat{x}(t)$ | State-feedback control | Modern control in CPS | | 6 | $\hat{x}[k\vert k] = \hat{x}[k\vert k-1] + L(y[k] - C\hat{x}[k\vert k-1])$ | Discrete Kalman/Luenberger update | Sensor fusion in CPS | | 7 | $\tau_{\text{net,max}} < T_{\text{loop}} - T_{\text{comp,min}}$ | Network deadline constraint | TSN / EtherCAT scheduling | | 8 | $J = \max(\tau) - \min(\tau)$ | Network jitter | Control stability margin | | 9 | $f_{\text{est}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{K \cdot K_p}{T}}$ | Closed-loop natural frequency | Stability of CPS loop | | 10 | $\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{net}} + \lambda_{\text{comp}} + \lambda_{\text{phys}}}$ | System reliability (series model) | CPS dependability design | > [!WARNING] > **Notation Convention Used in This Document:** All absolute values are written as $\vert x \vert$ (using `\vert`) to prevent markdown table breakage. Do not use the pipe character `|` for abs value in your KTU answer sheets — use the vertical bar or "mod" notation. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial CPS (Industry 4.0):** A bottling plant where PLCs (compute) communicate over EtherCAT (network) to control pneumatic actuators (physical) — every missed deadline = wasted product. - **Autonomous Vehicles:** LiDAR/Camera (physical sense) → NVIDIA Drive SoC (compute) → CAN-FD / Automotive Ethernet (network) → Brake-by-wire (physical actuate). Total loop budget: **< 10 ms**. - **Smart Grid:** PMUs (sensors) → Phasor Data Concentrators (compute) → IEC 61850 / 5G (network) → Circuit Breakers (actuators) — integrated to prevent cascading blackouts. - **Medical CPS:** Pacemaker — bio-sensor (physical) → microcontroller (compute) → wireless telemetry (network) → cardiac stimulator (physical). Hard real-time, life-critical. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Loop Time Bound for a CPS Node **Statement (Engineering Requirement):** A CPS node must close its Sense → Compute → Communicate → Actuate loop within a hard deadline $T_{\text{loop}}$ to guarantee physical stability. **Given:** - Sensor acquisition time: $T_{\text{sense}} = 50 \, \mu s$ - ADC conversion time (12-bit SAR): $T_{\text{ADC}} = 1 \, \mu s$ - Control computation time (PID on ARM Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz): $T_{\text{comp}} = 80 \, \mu s$ - Network transmission (EtherCAT, 100 Mbps, 64-byte frame): $T_{\text{net}} = 12 \, \mu s$ - DAC + actuator settling time: $T_{\text{act}} = 40 \, \mu s$ **Find:** $T_{\text{loop}}$ and verify if it satisfies a 1 ms hard real-time deadline. **Step-by-Step Derivation:** $$ T_{\text{loop}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{ADC}} + T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{net}} + T_{\text{act}} $$ Step 1 — Substitute the sense and ADC times together (these form the front-end): $$ T_{\text{front}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{ADC}} = 50 + 1 = 51 \, \mu s $$ Step 2 — Add the compute time: $$ T_{\text{cyber-in}} = T_{\text{front}} + T_{\text{comp}} = 51 + 80 = 131 \, \mu s $$ Step 3 — Add the network transport time: $$ T_{\text{cyber-out}} = T_{\text{cyber-in}} + T_{\text{net}} = 131 + 12 = 143 \, \mu s $$ Step 4 — Add the actuator (back to physical) time: $$ T_{\text{loop}} = T_{\text{cyber-out}} + T_{\text{act}} = 143 + 40 = 183 \, \mu s $$ Step 5 — Convert to ms for comparison with the deadline: $$ T_{\text{loop}} = 183 \, \mu s = 0.183 \, ms $$ Step 6 — Check against the hard deadline $T_{\text{DL}} = 1 \, ms$: $$ T_{\text{loop}} = 0.183 \, ms \; \ll \; T_{\text{DL}} = 1.0 \, ms $$ Step 7 — Compute the safety margin (slack): $$ \text{Slack} = T_{\text{DL}} - T_{\text{loop}} = 1.0 - 0.183 = 0.817 \, ms $$ Step 8 — Compute the maximum allowable jitter budget (worst-case = 5× nominal): $$ J_{\text{allow}} = T_{\text{DL}} - 5 \cdot T_{\text{loop}} = 1.0 - 0.915 = 0.085 \, ms $$ **Conclusion:** The CPS node meets its 1 ms hard real-time deadline with **81.7% slack**, and can tolerate up to **85 µs of jitter** before violating the deadline under 5× worst-case conditions. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sampling Rate for a Smart-Grid CPS (Phasor Measurement) **Statement:** A Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU) in a smart grid CPS must sample the 50 Hz power-line waveform accurately. The CPS estimator requires **at least 4× oversampling** for anti-aliasing filter roll-off. **Given:** - Power-line frequency: $f_{\text{signal}} = 50 \, Hz$ - Oversampling factor (engineering rule): $k_{\text{os}} = 4$ - PMU reports phasors at $N = 50$ samples/cycle **Find:** Required sampling rate $f_s$ and reporting rate $f_{\text{rep}}$. **Step-by-Step Derivation:** Step 1 — Apply the Nyquist criterion (lower bound): $$ f_{s,\min} = 2 \cdot f_{\text{signal}} = 2 \times 50 = 100 \, Hz $$ Step 2 — Apply the oversampling factor (for anti-aliasing and estimator accuracy): $$ f_s = k_{\text{os}} \cdot f_{s,\min} = 4 \times 100 = 400 \, Hz $$ Step 3 — Compute the period between samples (network/ADC deadline): $$ T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{400} = 2.5 \, ms $$ Step 4 — Compute the phasor reporting rate (typical IEEE C37.118): $$ f_{\text{rep}} = N \cdot f_{\text{signal}} = 50 \times 50 = 2500 \text{ reports/sec} $$ Step 5 — Compute the per-report time budget: $$ T_{\text{rep}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{rep}}} = 0.4 \, ms $$ **Conclusion:** The PMU must sample at $f_s = 400 \, Hz$ and complete its compute + UDP transmit cycle within **0.4 ms** to meet the IEEE C37.118.2 reporting rate — a tight integration constraint between ADC, DSP, and the communication network. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Layer Integration vs. Design Concern | Design Concern | Computing Layer Role | Networking Layer Role | Physical Layer Role | Integration Outcome | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Real-Time** | RTOS scheduling, WCET analysis | Time-Triggered Ethernet, TSN | Sensor/actuator latency | Closed-loop stability | | **Safety** | Watchdog timers, fail-safe states | Redundant buses (CAN + FlexRay) | Mechanical safe-state | Fail-operational CPS | | **Security** | Secure boot, TPM, RoT | TLS/DTLS, MACsec | Tamper-resistant enclosures | Trusted CPS node | | **Energy** | Sleep modes, DVFS | Duty-cycled MAC (TSCH) | Energy-harvesting sensors | Battery-life extension | | **Scalability** | Distributed consensus, MAPE-K | Hierarchical publish/subscribe | Modular sensor pods | Large-scale CPS (IoT) | | **Verifiability** | Model checking (UPPAAL), WCET | Network calculus | Bond-graph modeling | End-to-end formal assurance | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level CPS Integration Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[PhysicalProcess
PlantDynamics] -->|SensorSignal| B[TransducerAndADC
SenseLayer] B -->|DigitalSample| C[MicrocontrollerAndRTOS
ComputeLayer] C -->|ControlCommand| D[RealTimeNetworkBus
NetworkLayer] D -->|SyncPacket| E[ActuatorAndDAC
ActuationLayer] E -->|PhysicalActuation| A C -.->|Telemetry| F[CloudAndAnalytics
SupervisoryLayer] F -.->|UpdatePolicy| C G[SecurityRootOfTrust
TPMandSecureBoot] --- C G --- D H[ClockSyncPTPTSN
TimeReference] --- B H --- C H --- D H --- E ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram:** The outer loop (A → B → C → D → E → A) is the **real-time control loop** that closes in microseconds to milliseconds. The dashed telemetry lines (C ↔ F) carry **non-real-time supervisory data**. The green anchors G and H show that **security** and **clock synchronization** are *cross-cutting* — they must permeate every layer, not just one. --- ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Sense → Think → Communicate → Act) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SENSE[SENSE SUBSYSTEM] S1[PhysicalQuantity
T P v I] --> S2[Transducer] S2 --> S3[SignalConditioning] S3 --> S4[ADCwithTimestamp] end subgraph THINK[COMPUTE SUBSYSTEM] T1[RTOSScheduler] --> T2[StateEstimator
KalmanFilter] T2 --> T3[Controller
PID or LQR] T3 --> T4[SecurityGuard
CryptoAndPolicy] end subgraph COMM[NETWORKING SUBSYSTEM] C1[ProtocolStack
OPCUA or DDS] --> C2[TimeTriggeredBus
EtherCAT or TSN] C2 --> C3[GatewayToCloud] end subgraph ACT[ACTUATION SUBSYSTEM] A1[DAC] --> A2[PowerElectronics
PWM Driver] A2 --> A3[Actuator
Motor Valve Heater] end SENSE --> THINK THINK --> COMM COMM --> ACT ACT --> SENSE ``` --- ### 4.3 Integration Topology — Comparison of Three CPS Architectures ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph STAR[Centralized CPS Hub-and-Spoke] HUB[CentralComputeHub] --- N1[Node1] HUB --- N2[Node2] HUB --- N3[Node3] end subgraph RING[Decentralized CPS Ring] R1[NodeA] --- R2[NodeB] R2 --- R3[NodeC] R3 --- R1 end subgraph MESH[Distributed CPS Mesh] M1[EdgeNode1] --- M2[EdgeNode2] M2 --- M3[EdgeNode3] M3 --- M4[EdgeNode4] M1 --- M4 M1 --- M3 end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading guide:** > - **Star:** Used in legacy SCADA / single-PLC industrial lines — single point of failure. > - **Ring:** Used in TSN / Token-Ring industrial Ethernet — bounded latency, fault-tolerant in one direction. > - **Mesh:** Used in IoT / Smart-Grid / Vehicle-to-Vehicle CPS — multi-path redundancy, no single failure point, harder to time-synchronize. --- ### 4.4 Data-Flow Inside One CPS Node (Sequence Diagram) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant P as PhysicalPlant participant S as SensorADC participant M as Microcontroller participant N as NetworkBus participant A as Actuator Note over P,A: Loop iteration k at time t = k * Ts P->>S: Continuous signal y(t) S->>M: y[k] with timestamp t_k M->>M: Compute u[k] = K * error[k] M->>N: Transmit u[k] over TSN slot N->>A: Forward u[k] to actuator node A->>P: Apply u[k] as physical control action Note over P,A: Loop closes in T_loop = 183 us ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]** *"Differentiate between a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) and a traditional embedded system. Highlight any two integration-level differences."* **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer (3 marks — examiner's key):** | Aspect | Traditional Embedded System | Cyber-Physical System (CPS) | |---|---|---| | **Network role** | Optional / auxiliary (often off-line logging) | **Inside the control loop** — network delay directly affects stability | | **Physical coupling** | Loose — embedded device drives outputs but does not adapt in real time | **Tight, closed-loop feedback** with continuous-time plant dynamics | | **Scale** | Usually single device | **Networked, distributed, multi-node** | | **Time semantics** | Soft real-time / best effort | **Hard / firm real-time** with deterministic deadlines | **(1 mark)** for defining CPS; **(1 mark)** for defining embedded system; **(1 mark)** for any two valid integration-level differences. ✔ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]** *"List the three primary layers integrated in a CPS and state one real-world example for each layer."* **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The three primary layers integrated in a CPS are: 1. **Computing Layer** — e.g., ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller running an RTOS in a drone flight controller. 2. **Networking Layer** — e.g., CAN-FD bus in an automotive CPS connecting ECUs. 3. **Physical Layer** — e.g., brushless DC motor + propeller + aerodynamic body of a quadcopter. **(1 mark)** per correctly identified layer with example. ✔ --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — "The Integration Loop"** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **integration of computing, networking, and physical elements** in a Cyber-Physical System. Identify the role of each layer in closing the real-time feedback loop. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A CPS node in a smart factory must close its control loop within **$T_{\text{DL}} = 2 \, ms$**. The sense, compute, network, and actuation times are **$T_s = 0.3 \, ms$, $T_c = 0.8 \, ms$, $T_n = 0.4 \, ms$, $T_a = 0.2 \, ms$** respectively. Determine whether the deadline is met, compute the safety slack, and recommend one engineering action if the slack is insufficient. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Q(a) (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark) — Define CPS integration:** The integration of computing, networking, and physical elements in a CPS refers to the *co-design* of three layers — sensing/actuation (physical), processing/decision (computing), and communication/coordination (networking) — into a single, time-deterministic, closed-loop control system. **Step 2 (3 marks) — Block diagram with explanation:** Draw the four-stage loop: $$ \text{Physical} \rightarrow \text{Sense/ADC} \rightarrow \text{Compute/Controller} \rightarrow \text{Network} \rightarrow \text{Actuate/DAC} \rightarrow \text{Physical} $$ Explain each stage: - **Physical Layer** (1 mark): The plant — continuous-time dynamics governed by ODEs $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$, $y(t) = Cx(t)$. Sensors transduce physical quantities; actuators inject control actions. - **Computing Layer** (1 mark): Microcontroller / SoC + RTOS. Runs estimator + controller at sampling rate $f_s$. Provides deterministic WCET guarantees. - **Networking Layer** (1 mark): Real-time bus (EtherCAT/TSN/CAN) carrying time-stamped sensor/control packets. Sits **inside** the loop — its delay and jitter directly affect stability. **Step 3 (2 marks) — Integration outcome:** Discuss how the three layers are *co-designed*: e.g., the network schedule is synchronized to the controller period; the controller is designed to be robust to bounded network delay $\tau_{\text{net}} \leq T_{\text{loop}}$; the sensor sampling is triggered by the network's time-triggered slot. **Step 4 (1 mark) — Conclude:** The seamless integration is what makes a CPS "smart" — it can sense, decide, and act within hard real-time deadlines while maintaining stability of the physical process. #### Model Solution — Q(b) (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark) — Write the loop-time equation:** $$ T_{\text{loop}} = T_s + T_c + T_n + T_a $$ **Step 2 (2 marks) — Substitute values:** $$ T_{\text{loop}} = 0.3 + 0.8 + 0.4 + 0.2 = 1.7 \, ms $$ **Step 3 (1 mark) — Compare with deadline:** $$ T_{\text{loop}} = 1.7 \, ms \; < \; T_{\text{DL}} = 2.0 \, ms \;\; \checkmark $$ **Step 4 (2 marks) — Compute safety slack:** $$ \text{Slack} = T_{\text{DL}} - T_{\text{loop}} = 2.0 - 1.7 = 0.3 \, ms \; (15\% \text{ margin}) $$ **Step 5 (1 mark) — Engineering recommendation:** Although the deadline is technically met, the 15% margin is **risky** for a production CPS. Recommended action: *migrate the network from standard Ethernet to Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) with time-aware scheduling*, which can reduce $T_n$ by ~50%, increasing the safety margin to ~0.5 ms. Alternatively, port the controller from a single-core MCU to a dual-core SoC and run the estimator on a dedicated core to reduce $T_c$. **(Full mark breakdown: 1 + 2 + 1 + 2 + 1 = 7 ✔)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — "Layer-by-Layer Integration Analysis"** **(a)** Explain with examples how **computing, networking, and physical elements** are integrated in an **autonomous vehicle CPS**. For each layer, identify at least one specific technology and its function. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A Wireless Sensor-Actuator Network (WSAN) for a precision agriculture CPS uses 16-bit ADCs sampling soil moisture at $f_s = 200 \, Hz$. Compute (i) the quantization SNR, (ii) the Nyquist-limited maximum measurable frequency, and (iii) the maximum allowable one-way network delay if the controller deadline is 5 ms and the remaining fixed overheads total 2.5 ms. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Q(a) (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark) — Introduction:** An autonomous vehicle is a quintessential CPS where computing, networking, and physical elements are deeply intertwined to perceive, decide, and act within ~10 ms. **Step 2 (2 marks) — Computing Layer:** The central "brain" is the **NVIDIA Drive SoC** (or equivalent ADAS SoC) running **AUTOSAR Classic/Adaptive** OS. It fuses data from cameras, LiDAR, radar using neural-network inference (e.g., object detection at 30 Hz) and computes steering/throttle/brake trajectories. Sub-systems like the brake-by-wire ECU use **Infineon AURIX** MCUs with lockstep cores for ASIL-D functional safety. **Step 3 (2 marks) — Networking Layer:** The in-vehicle **Automotive Ethernet (100/1000BASE-T1)** backbone carries high-bandwidth sensor streams. For real-time chassis control, **CAN-FD** and **FlexRay** buses link ECUs. **TSN (IEEE 802.1Qbv)** provides time-triggered scheduling for time-critical control traffic. **V2X (C-V2X / 802.11p)** extends the network to road infrastructure and other vehicles. **Step 4 (2 marks) — Physical Layer:** **Sensors**: Cameras (vision), LiDAR (3D point cloud), radar (range/velocity), IMU/gyroscope (orientation), wheel-speed encoders. **Actuators**: Steer-by-wire motor, electronic throttle body, brake calipers with electro-hydraulic actuators, electric drive inverters. The **plant** is the vehicle chassis (mass-spring-damper dynamics) and the surrounding environment. **Conclusion:** These three layers close the perception–planning–actuation loop, e.g., **LiDAR sample (physical) → sensor fusion SoC (compute) → Automotive Ethernet (network) → brake actuator (physical)** in under 10 ms. #### Model Solution — Q(b) (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark) — Given data:** - ADC resolution: $b = 16$ bits - Sampling rate: $f_s = 200 \, Hz$ - Controller deadline: $T_{\text{DL}} = 5 \, ms$ - Fixed overhead (sense + compute + actuate): $T_{\text{fixed}} = 2.5 \, ms$ **Step 2 (2 marks) — Part (i) Quantization SNR:** $$ \text{SNR}_Q = 6.02b + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08 \, dB $$ **Step 3 (2 marks) — Part (ii) Nyquist maximum frequency:** $$ f_{\text{max}} = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{200}{2} = 100 \, Hz $$ **Step 4 (2 marks) — Part (iii) Maximum allowable network delay:** $$ \tau_{\text{net,max}} = T_{\text{DL}} - T_{\text{fixed}} = 5.0 - 2.5 = 2.5 \, ms $$ **Final Answer:** **(i) 98.08 dB, (ii) 100 Hz, (iii) 2.5 ms.** ✔ --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 1 (CPS Integration) Questions:** > 1. **Confusing "embedded system" with "CPS":** Many students write that a CPS is "an embedded system connected to the internet." This earns **0 marks** for the integration aspect. The defining feature of a CPS is the **network-inside-the-loop** coupling — not internet connectivity. > 2. **Forgetting the units in loop-time calculations:** Writing $T_{\text{loop}} = 1.7$ without specifying *ms* loses **0.5–1 mark**. Always write units. > 3. **Drawing a CPS block diagram without arrows showing direction of signal/energy flow:** A CPS diagram without directional flow arrows is considered incomplete. Use **unidirectional arrows** for control flow and **bidirectional arrows** for feedback. > 4. **Skipping the network layer in the loop equation:** Writing only $T_s + T_c + T_a$ and omitting $T_n$ shows a weak understanding of CPS — KTU examiners specifically test whether students recognize that the **network delay is part of the loop**. > 5. **Not using a consistent sampling-time notation:** Mixing $T$, $T_s$, $\Delta t$, and $h$ within one answer confuses the examiner. Use $T_s$ (or $h$) for sampling period consistently. > 6. **Oversimplifying real-time constraints:** Saying "the system must be fast" earns no marks. Use quantitative bounds like $T_{\text{loop}} \leq T_{\text{DL}}$ with numbers. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Module 1 — Integration of Computing, Networking, and Physical Elements in CPS** > > ✅ A CPS is the **co-design** of **Computing + Networking + Physical** layers into a single closed-loop system. > > ✅ The **network sits *inside* the control loop** — not outside it. This is the single most important conceptual shift from classical control to CPS. > > ✅ The canonical loop: **Physical → Sense/ADC → Compute/Controller → Network → Actuate/DAC → Physical**. > > ✅ **Hard real-time deadlines** are non-negotiable in safety-critical CPS (automotive, medical, grid). > > ✅ Total loop time: $T_{\text{loop}} = T_s + T_c + T_n + T_a \leq T_{\text{DL}}$. > > ✅ **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{signal,max}}$. Practical CPS use $4\times$–$10\times$ oversampling. > > ✅ **Quantization SNR:** $\text{SNR} = 6.02b + 1.76 \, dB$ — higher ADC bits = better measurement fidelity. > > ✅ **Clock synchronization** (PTP / TSN / GPS) is a *cross-cutting* enabler — required for distributed CPS state estimation. > > ✅ **Security (RoT, secure boot, TPM, MACsec/DTLS)** is a *cross-cutting* requirement — must permeate all three layers. > > ✅ **Architectures:** Centralized (hub-and-spoke), Decentralized (ring/bus), Distributed (mesh) — each trades off latency, fault-tolerance, and complexity. > > ✅ **Real-world CPS examples to memorize for KTU:** Smart grid, autonomous vehicle, industrial robot, medical pacemaker, drone, smart building, water distribution SCADA. > > ✅ **Key protocols to know by name + function:** EtherCAT, TSN, CAN-FD, FlexRay, WirelessHART, OPC-UA, MQTT, DDS, 5G URLLC. > > ✅ **Key design concerns matrix:** Real-time, Safety, Security, Energy, Scalability, Verifiability — be ready to map each concern to a layer.

topics.title: Unique Vulnerabilities and Attack Surfaces in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Unique Vulnerabilities and Attack Surfaces in CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Cyber-Physical System (CPS)

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered system that integrates sensing, computation, control, communication, and actuation with physical processes to form a tightly coupled, feedback-driven loop where the cyber and physical domains influence each other in real time.

Formally, a CPS can be represented as a closed-loop control system with four interacting layers:

$$
\text{CPS} = \big\{\, \mathcal{P},\; \mathcal{S},\; \mathcal{C},\; \mathcal{N},\; \mathcal{A} \,\big\}
$$

where:
- $\mathcal{P}$ = physical plant / process
- $\mathcal{S}$ = sensor array
- $\mathcal{C}$ = cyber (computation + control) layer
- $\mathcal{N}$ = network / communication layer
- $\mathcal{A}$ = actuator array

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The defining feature of a CPS is the **bidirectional, time-sensitive coupling** between the cyber and physical layers. Traditional IT systems do NOT have this physical coupling — this is the root of all unique vulnerabilities.

---

## 1.2 Definition of Vulnerability in a CPS Context

A **vulnerability** is a weakness in the design, implementation, configuration, or operational state of a CPS component that can be exploited to violate one or more of the core security properties: **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authenticity, and Safety (CIAAS)**.

In CPS, the fifth property — **Safety** — is paramount because an attack can cause physical harm to humans, equipment, and the environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definitions — Five Pillars of CPS Security**
>
> | Property | Meaning in CPS |
> |----------|---------------|
> | Confidentiality | Keeping sensor/control data private from unauthorized parties |
> | Integrity | Ensuring measurements and commands are not tampered with |
> | Availability | Guaranteeing real-time control signals are delivered on time |
> | Authenticity | Verifying that a command genuinely came from a trusted source |
> | Safety | Ensuring the system never causes physical harm, even under attack |

---

## 1.3 Definition of Attack Surface

The **attack surface** of a CPS is the *aggregate sum of all points (hardware, software, network, human, and physical) where an unauthorized actor can attempt to enter, extract data from, or exert influence on the system*. It is the union of:

$$
\text{AS}_{\text{CPS}} = \text{AS}_{\text{cyber}} \cup \text{AS}_{\text{physical}} \cup \text{AS}_{\text{network}} \cup \text{AS}_{\text{human}}
$$

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Building as a CPS**
>
> Imagine a modern high-rise smart building. It has:
> - **Physical layer** — elevators, HVAC ducts, fire sprinklers, doors, water pipes.
> - **Sensors** — smoke detectors, motion sensors, temperature sensors, pressure gauges in pipes.
> - **Cyber layer** — a central Building Management System (BMS) running on a server, PLCs controlling chillers, programmable controllers for elevators.
> - **Network** — Ethernet and wireless links between sensors, controllers, and the BMS.
> - **Actuators** — motors that open/close valves, damper drives, magnetic door locks.
>
> Now, consider two attackers:
> 1. An **IT attacker** hacks the BMS to steal tenant data.
> 2. A **CPS attacker** hacks a temperature sensor in the chiller plant and feeds **false readings** to the controller. The controller, believing the building is overheating, opens the chilled-water valves fully at 3 AM in winter. Pipes freeze and burst, flooding the server room.
>
> In the second case, the attacker did NOT need to break any encryption. They simply **lied to the sensor** — exploiting the fact that the controller blindly trusts physical measurements. This is the essence of a CPS-specific attack surface.

---

## 1.5 Why CPS Vulnerabilities Are "Unique"

CPS vulnerabilities differ from classical IT vulnerabilities because of four inherent characteristics:

1. **Tight coupling between cyber and physical worlds** — a software bug can break a pump.
2. **Hard real-time constraints** — a delayed packet can destabilize a control loop.
3. **Safety-criticality** — failures can kill people.
4. **Legacy and proprietary components** — PLCs, RTUs, and embedded firmware often run unpatched for 10–20 years.

These four factors are the *unique differentiators* and form the foundation of this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CPS Architectural Stack and Its Vulnerabilities

A CPS can be decomposed into five layers. Each layer has a distinct attack surface, and the vulnerabilities are *not independent* — they cascade.

### Layer 1 — Physical Process Layer
- The plant, machinery, chemical reactors, power grid lines, water pipes.
- Vulnerabilities: physical tampering, sensor spoofing, actuator hijacking, environmental damage.

### Layer 2 — Sensor and Actuator Layer (Field Devices)
- RTUs, smart sensors, smart valves, PLCs.
- Vulnerabilities: firmware tampering, side-channel leakage, signal injection.

### Layer 3 — Communication and Network Layer
- Industrial protocols: **Modbus, DNP3, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA, IEC 61850 (smart grid)**.
- Vulnerabilities: protocol spoofing, man-in-the-middle, replay attacks, packet injection, DoS.

### Layer 4 — Control and Computation Layer (SCADA / DCS / Cloud)
- SCADA servers, historians, HMIs, edge controllers, cloud analytics.
- Vulnerabilities: malware, unpatched OS, weak authentication, SQL injection on historians.

### Layer 5 — Supervisory and Enterprise Layer
- MES, ERP, business analytics, remote vendor access.
- Vulnerabilities: phishing, credential theft, supply-chain compromise.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** The attack surface is *layered* and *cross-layer cascading* — a single compromise at Layer 2 can propagate upward to the cloud and downward to physical destruction.

---

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Unique CPS Vulnerabilities

| # | Vulnerability Class | Description | Example Attack |
|---|--------------------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Sensor/Actuator Spoofing** | Attacker injects false physical measurements | Stuxnet changing centrifuge rotor speed readings |
| 2 | **Time-of-Check / Time-of-Use (TOCTOU)** | Delay between measurement and action exploited | Replaying an old "valve closed" command while valve is open |
| 3 | **Side-Channel Physical Leaks** | Information leakage via power, EM, acoustic, thermal emissions | Power-analysis attack on smart meters |
| 4 | **Firmware & Supply-Chain Compromise** | Malicious code embedded in vendor firmware | TRITON / TRISIS targeting Triconex SIS |
| 5 | **Protocol Weakness (No Auth / No Encryption)** | Legacy ICS protocols send plaintext without authentication | Modbus TCP has no built-in authentication |
| 6 | **Real-Time Deadline Violation** | Delayed packets cause instability | Network DoS destabilizing a power-grid wide-area controller |
| 7 | **Unpatched Legacy Systems** | Long-lived equipment with no security updates | Windows XP on nuclear plant HMIs |
| 8 | **Cascading Failure / Interdependency** | Failure in one subsystem propagates to others | 2003 Northeast blackout (cyber-assisted) |
| 9 | **Lack of Safety-Code Isolation** | Safety and control code share the same processor | TÜV SÜD failure analysis post-Triton |
| 10 | **Adversarial AI / Data Poisoning** | Manipulated training data alters ML-based controllers | Spoofed lidar points on autonomous vehicles |

---

## 2.3 The Cyber-Physical Attack Surface — A Formal Decomposition

Let us define the attack surface mathematically as the set of all reachable *entry points* $E$ weighted by their *exposure* $\varepsilon$:

$$
\text{AS} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \varepsilon_i \cdot E_i
$$

where:
- $E_i$ = entry point $i$ (e.g., a sensor port, an HMI login screen, a vendor VPN endpoint)
- $\varepsilon_i$ = exposure factor (combination of reachability, privilege, and detectability)
- $n$ = total number of reachable entry points

For CPS, this can be partitioned as:

$$
\text{AS}_{\text{CPS}} = \text{AS}_{\text{cyber}} + \text{AS}_{\text{phys}} + \text{AS}_{\text{net}} + \text{AS}_{\text{human}}
$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Unit / Domain |
|---|-------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Classical risk triplet | Dimensionless (0–1 or 1–10) |
| 2 | $\text{AS} = \sum \varepsilon_i \cdot E_i$ | Attack surface metric | Count × exposure factor |
| 3 | $\text{MTTF}_{\text{sec}} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{1 - P_{\text{breach}}}$ | Mean-Time-To-Security-Failure | Hours |
| 4 | $\text{CPS} = \{\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{N}, \mathcal{A}\}$ | CPS definition tuple | Set |
| 5 | $\tau_{\text{ctrl}} < \tau_{\text{deadline}}$ | Real-time control constraint | Time (ms, s) |
| 6 | $\text{Attack Vector Score (AVS)} = w_c V_c + w_i V_i + w_a V_a$ | Weighted attack vector score | 0–10 |
| 7 | $C_{\text{cascade}} = \prod_{k=1}^{m} (1 - p_k^{\text{resist}})$ | Cascading failure probability | Probability (0–1) |
| 8 | STRIDE = Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Info Disclosure, DoS, Elevation | Threat classification mnemonic | Categorical |
| 9 | $\text{CVSS}_{\text{base}} \in [0, 10]$ | Common Vulnerability Scoring System | Numeric score |
| 10 | $\text{Safety Integrity Level (SIL)} \in \{1,2,3,4\}$ | IEC 61508 risk reduction level | Categorical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** For KTU, you do not need to memorize numbered constants. Memorize the **relationships** — especially Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact, and the layered CPS architecture.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding unique vulnerabilities is the foundation of:
- **IEC 62443** — Industrial Automation and Control Systems (IACS) security standard.
- **NIST SP 800-82** — Guide to Industrial Control Systems (ICS) Security.
- **NERC CIP** — North American Electric Reliability Corporation Critical Infrastructure Protection.
- **ISO/SAE 21434** — Automotive cybersecurity engineering.
- **FDA Pre-market Cybersecurity Guidance** — for medical device CPS.

All these standards start with a **threat-modeling phase** that explicitly enumerates the unique CPS attack surface discussed above.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Risk Derivation

## 3.1 Derivation of the CPS Risk Equation

We derive the layered risk model step by step. This is the model most commonly asked in KTU 14-mark questions.

### Step 1 — Define a single asset $A_k$
Each CPS asset $A_k$ has an associated impact $I_k$ if compromised. The impact is measured in a normalized scale, e.g., $I_k \in [0, 1]$.

### Step 2 — Define the threat actor capability
A threat $T_j$ has a probability of successfully attacking an asset:

$$
P(T_j \to A_k) = p_{j,k}
$$

### Step 3 — Define the vulnerability exploitability
Each vulnerability $V_m$ on asset $A_k$ has an exploitability score $e_{m,k} \in [0, 1]$.

### Step 4 — Combine threat and vulnerability
The probability of compromise of $A_k$ by threat $T_j$ via vulnerability $V_m$:

$$
p_{j,m,k} = p_{j,k} \cdot e_{m,k}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute single-asset risk
The risk to asset $A_k$ is the sum over all threat-vulnerability pairs, weighted by impact:

$$
R_k = I_k \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{J} \sum_{m=1}^{M} p_{j,k} \cdot e_{m,k}
$$

### Step 6 — System-level risk
The CPS-level risk is the sum of all asset risks, reduced by existing controls $C_i \in [0,1]$:

$$
R_{\text{CPS}} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} I_k \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{J} \sum_{m=1}^{M} p_{j,k} \cdot e_{m,k} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{I}(1 - C_i)
$$

### Step 7 — Tidy the expression
Factoring the control product as a single residual factor $\rho \in [0,1]$:

$$
R_{\text{CPS}} = \rho \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{j=1}^{J} \sum_{m=1}^{M} I_k \cdot p_{j,k} \cdot e_{m,k}
$$

This is the **canonical layered CPS risk equation** used in IEC 62443 zone-conduit modeling.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A water-treatment plant has three assets. Compute the system risk given the following table:

| Asset $A_k$ | Impact $I_k$ | Threat prob. $p_{j,k}$ | Exploitability $e_{m,k}$ | Existing controls $\rho$ |
|-------------|-------------|------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| $A_1$ (SCADA HMI) | 0.9 | 0.4 | 0.7 | 0.4 |
| $A_2$ (PLC) | 1.0 | 0.3 | 0.6 | 0.4 |
| $A_3$ (Chlorine sensor) | 0.8 | 0.5 | 0.8 | 0.4 |

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step A — Compute $I_k \cdot p_{j,k} \cdot e_{m,k}$ for each asset:**

$$
A_1:\; 0.9 \times 0.4 \times 0.7 = 0.252
$$

$$
A_2:\; 1.0 \times 0.3 \times 0.6 = 0.180
$$

$$
A_3:\; 0.8 \times 0.5 \times 0.8 = 0.320
$$

**Step B — Sum the asset-level weighted products:**

$$
\sum_k I_k \cdot p_{j,k} \cdot e_{m,k} = 0.252 + 0.180 + 0.320 = 0.752
$$

**Step C — Apply the control residual factor $\rho = 0.4$:**

$$
R_{\text{CPS}} = 0.4 \times 0.752 = 0.3008
$$

**Interpretation:** A risk score of **0.3008 (≈ 30%)** is **HIGH** — the water-treatment plant is critically exposed. Engineering controls such as network segmentation, multi-factor authentication, and signed firmware would push $\rho$ below 0.2, lowering the score to ≈ 0.15 (medium).

---

## 3.3 The Attack-Surface Reduction Argument

A defensive engineering team wants to minimize $R_{\text{CPS}}$. Two levers are available:

1. **Reduce impact** $I_k$ — by architectural redundancy and safety interlocks.
2. **Reduce the triple product** — by lowering threat probability, exploitability, or both.

Since the attack surface directly influences exploitability and reachable threats, **shrinking the attack surface is the single most cost-effective lever** in CPS security.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU answers:** Always justify defenses in terms of the *Risk equation*. Saying *"use a firewall"* is weak. Saying *"a firewall reduces $p_{j,k}$ by restricting reachable entry points $E_i$, thus shrinking the attack surface and lowering $R_{\text{CPS}}$"* is a board-exam distinction answer.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Layer × Attack-Surface Mapping

| Layer ↓ / Property → | Cyber Surface | Network Surface | Physical Surface | Human Surface |
|----------------------|---------------|-----------------|------------------|---------------|
| Physical Process | None | None | Tampering, EM injection | Insider sabotage |
| Sensor / Actuator | Firmware | Wireless hijack | Signal spoofing | Tampering |
| Communication | None | MITM, replay, DoS | Cable tapping | None |
| Control / SCADA | Malware, weak auth | Lateral movement | None | Phishing |
| Enterprise | Ransomware, SQLi | VPN compromise | None | Credential theft |

This matrix is the most common KTU exam-table answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered CPS Architecture with Attack Surfaces

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeP["LAYER 5 ENTERPRISE - ERP MES Cloud"]:::ent
    nodeC["LAYER 4 CONTROL - SCADA HMI Historian"]:::ctrl
    nodeN["LAYER 3 NETWORK - Modbus DNP3 OPC UA"]:::net
    nodeS["LAYER 2 SENSORS AND ACTUATORS - PLC RTU Smart IO"]:::sens
    nodeA["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL PLANT - Grid Water Reactor Vehicle"]:::phys

    nodeA1["Attack: Physical Tampering Signal Injection"]:::atk
    nodeS1["Attack: Firmware Hack Side Channel"]:::atk
    nodeN1["Attack: MITM Replay DoS Protocol Spoof"]:::atk
    nodeC1["Attack: Malware Lateral Movement"]:::atk
    nodeE1["Attack: Phishing Vendor Compromise"]:::atk

    nodeP --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeA

    nodeA -.-> nodeA1
    nodeS -.-> nodeS1
    nodeN -.-> nodeN1
    nodeC -.-> nodeC1
    nodeP -.-> nodeE1

    classDef phys fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef sens fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#d4a017,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449,color:#000
    classDef ent fill:#ead7f5,stroke:#6c3483,color:#000
    classDef atk fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Attack Surface Decomposition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AS["CPS Attack Surface AS total"]:::root
    ASC["Cyber Surface"]:::cyber
    ASN["Network Surface"]:::net
    ASP["Physical Surface"]:::phys
    ASH["Human Surface"]:::human

    ASC --> ASC1["HMI Login"]
    ASC --> ASC2["Firmware"]
    ASC --> ASC3["OS Services"]

    ASN --> ASN1["Modbus TCP"]
    ASN --> ASN2["Wireless Sensors"]
    ASN --> ASN3["Remote Vendor VPN"]

    ASP --> ASP1["Sensor Tamper"]
    ASP --> ASP2["Actuator Hijack"]
    ASP --> ASP3["EM Injection"]

    ASH --> ASH1["Phishing"]
    ASH --> ASH2["Insider"]
    ASH --> ASH3["Social Engineering"]

    classDef root fill:#34495e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef cyber fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    classDef phys fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#b9770e,color:#000
    classDef human fill:#e8daef,stroke:#6c3483,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Cross-Layer Cascading Attack Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Attacker Entry: Phished Vendor Email"]):::atk
    n1["Step 1: Credential Theft on Enterprise Layer"]:::step
    n2["Step 2: Lateral Move to SCADA Historian"]:::step
    n3["Step 3: Inject Malicious Modbus Command"]:::step
    n4["Step 4: PLC Executes False Setpoint"]:::step
    n5["Step 5: Actuator Overdrives Valve or Motor"]:::phys
    endX["Outcome: Physical Destruction or Safety Violation"]:::end

    start --> n1 --> n2 --> n3 --> n4 --> n5 --> endX

    classDef atk fill:#2c3e50,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef step fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    classDef phys fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef end fill:#7d3c98,color:#fff,stroke:#000
```

## 4.4 CPS vs IT — Comparative Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPS["CPS DOMAIN"]
        C1["Physical Process"]
        C2["Sensors and Actuators"]
        C3["Real Time Controller PLC RTU"]
        C4["Safety Interlock"]
        C1 <--> C2
        C2 --> C3
        C3 --> C4
        C4 --> C1
    end

    subgraph IT["CLASSICAL IT DOMAIN"]
        I1["Application Layer"]
        I2["Database"]
        I3["Web Server"]
        I4["User Workstation"]
        I4 --> I3 --> I1
        I1 <--> I2
    end

    note["Unique CPS Issues: Safety Real Time Physical Consequence"]:::note
    CPS ~~~ note
    IT ~~~ note

    classDef note fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#b9770e,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in an exam:** Always label the *layer number* and the *attack vector* on the same diagram. Examiners reward diagrams that show **layer-to-attack pairing** rather than generic boxes.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

### Q1. Define a Cyber-Physical System and list any four of its unique characteristics that distinguish it from a classical IT system.

**Model Answer:**
A CPS is an engineered system that tightly integrates sensing, computation, communication, and actuation with physical processes, forming a real-time feedback loop between the cyber and physical domains.

Four unique characteristics:

1. **Tight cyber–physical coupling** — software decisions directly drive physical actuators.
2. **Hard real-time constraints** — control loops must complete within strict deadlines.
3. **Safety-criticality** — failures can cause loss of life, environmental damage, or equipment destruction.
4. **Long lifecycle of legacy and proprietary components** — PLCs and RTUs often run unpatched firmware for 10–20 years.

### Q2. What is the "attack surface" of a CPS? Give two examples each from the network layer and the physical layer.

**Model Answer:**
The attack surface of a CPS is the complete set of reachable points (hardware, software, network, human, and physical) where an attacker can attempt to enter, extract data from, or exert influence on the system.

Network layer examples:
1. Unauthenticated Modbus TCP traffic on the control LAN.
2. Wireless sensor telemetry that can be intercepted and replayed.

Physical layer examples:
1. Direct tampering of an outdoor temperature sensor at a substation.
2. Electromagnetic injection that desynchronizes a smart meter clock.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-layer CPS architecture** and identify the unique attack surface at each layer. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss **Stuxnet** and **TRITON/TRISIS** as case studies of CPS attacks. For each, identify the attack vector, the layer compromised, the vulnerability exploited, and the physical consequence. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a)

The five-layer CPS architecture and its attack surfaces are:

| Layer | Function | Unique Attack Surface | Example |
|-------|----------|----------------------|---------|
| 1 — Physical Process | The plant / reactor / grid | Physical tampering, EM injection | Cutting fibre-optic sensor link |
| 2 — Sensors / Actuators / PLCs | Field-level measurement and control | Firmware tampering, signal spoofing, side-channel | Spoofed rotor-speed signal (Stuxnet) |
| 3 — Communication Network | Data exchange using industrial protocols | Replay, MITM, DoS, protocol spoofing | Modbus TCP with no auth |
| 4 — Control / SCADA / DCS | Supervisory control, HMIs, historians | Malware, weak auth, lateral movement | TRITON targeting SIS |
| 5 — Enterprise / Cloud | MES, ERP, vendor remote access | Phishing, supply-chain, credential theft | Stolen VPN credentials |

A block diagram showing these five layers stacked vertically with bidirectional arrows between them is drawn (refer Section 4.1). Each layer connects to the next with both **control flow** (downward) and **measurement flow** (upward), making the system a closed loop.

**Conclusion:** The unique CPS attack surface is *layered, cross-coupled, and time-sensitive* — distinguishing it sharply from classical IT systems.

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b)

**Stuxnet (2010)**

- **Target:** Iranian uranium-enrichment centrifuges at Natanz.
- **Attack vector:** USB-borne worm, propagating via Windows shares and Siemens STEP 7 project files.
- **Layer compromised:** Layers 2 (PLC firmware) and 3 (field-bus commands).
