# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define Defense-in-Depth. State any three layers of defense used in a Cyber-Physical System.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Defense-in-Depth is a layered security strategy in which multiple, heterogeneous and independent security controls are deployed so that the failure of any single control does not lead to a complete compromise of the system.

**Three Layers (2 marks — 1 mark for naming, 1 mark for brief explanation of any two):**

1. **Network Perimeter Security:** Industrial firewalls and DMZ isolate the OT network from IT/Internet.
2. **Host Hardening:** Application whitelisting and EDR on HMIs/Engineering workstations block malware execution.
3. **Policy and Operations Layer:** IEC 62443 policies, RBAC, periodic audits and 24×7 SOC monitoring address insider and procedural threats.

*(Alternative acceptable layers: Physical, Zoning/Segmentation, Application, Data.)*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between Defense-in-Depth and Defense-in-Breadth. Why is the former preferred in CPS?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Defense-in-Depth (1 mark):** Multiple *different* controls protecting the *same* asset arranged in series. *Example:* Firewall + IDS + Encryption protecting a single PLC.
- **Defense-in-Breadth (1 mark):** A *single* control type applied *across many* assets in parallel. *Example:* Deploying EDR on every endpoint.
- **Why DiD is preferred in CPS (1 mark):** CPS environments have a converged cyber-physical attack surface where a single control failure can trigger physical harm. DiD provides redundancy, diversity, and independence, ensuring that no single bypass compromises safety-critical operations.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B1 (Choice A) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the seven layers of Defense-in-Depth as applied to a Cyber-Physical System. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compute the breach probability and Defense-in-Depth effectiveness for a CPS with **six** independent defense layers, each having a failure probability $p_i = 0.15$. Compare it with the case where only **one** layer is deployed. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for B1(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Diagram (2 marks):**
Draw the seven-layer architecture as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes. Label each layer with one control example.

**Step 2 — Tabular description (5 marks — 1 mark per layer + 2 marks for integration):**

| Layer | Description | Example Control |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|
| L1 Physical | Protects field devices from tampering | Locked cabinet, CCTV |
| L2 Perimeter | Filters external traffic | Industrial firewall, DMZ |
| L3 Zoning | Segregates OT/IT per IEC 62443 | VLANs, conduits |
| L4 Host | Hardens endpoints | App-whitelist, EDR |
| L5 Application | Secures control logic | Input validation, DPI |
| L6 Data | Protects at-rest and in-transit data | AES-256, SHA-256 |
| L7 Policy/People | Governance layer | RBAC, SOC, audit |

**Step 3 — Closing statement (2 marks):** The seven layers are independent and complementary; any single bypass is contained by the next layer, which is why DiD is essential for CPS safety and security.

#### Model Solution for B1(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Six-layer DiD computation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{breach, DiD}} &= (0.15)^6 \\
&= 1.139 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — DiD effectiveness (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{DiD}} &= 1 - 1.139 \times 10^{-5} \\
&\approx 0.99998861
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Single-layer comparison (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{breach, single}} &= 0.15 \\
E_{\text{single}} &= 1 - 0.15 = 0.85 \\
\text{LRF} &= \frac{0.15}{1.139 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 13{,}170
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** DiD with six independent layers reduces breach probability by approximately **four orders of magnitude** compared to a single layer.

---

### Question B2 (Choice B) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`
**(a)** Explain how the **Purdue Reference Model** supports the implementation of Defense-in-Depth in Industrial Control Systems. List the DiD controls applicable at each Purdue level. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the role of **Zero Trust Architecture** as a complementary strategy to Defense-in-Depth in CPS. How does the principle of *"never trust, always verify"* mitigate insider threats? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for B2(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Purdue Levels (3 marks):**
Explain Levels 0–5 from field to enterprise:
- **L0/L1:** Sensors, actuators, field devices
- **L2:** PLCs, DCS controllers
- **L3:** SCADA, HMI, engineering workstations
- **L4:** Enterprise IT systems, MES
- **L5:** Cloud, Internet, corporate network

**Step 2 — DiD Controls per Level (4 marks — 1 mark per level):**

| Purdue Level | DiD Control |
|--------------|-------------|
| L0/L1 | Tamper sensors, device authentication, physical locks |
| L2 | PLC application whitelisting, protocol allow-list, watchdog timers |
| L3 | Jump server, role-based HMI access, historian hardening |
| L4/L5 | NGFW, IAM/SSO, DLP, SIEM correlation, EDR |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The Purdue model segments the network so that DiD boundaries coincide with logical zones, minimizing the blast radius of any compromise.

#### Model Solution for B2(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Define Zero Trust (2 marks):**
Zero Trust is a security model where no entity (user, device, service) is trusted by default, regardless of whether it resides inside or outside the network perimeter. Every access request is verified using identity, device posture, and context.

**Step 2 — Complementarity with DiD (3 marks):**

| Zero Trust Principle | DiD Layer Reinforced |
|----------------------|----------------------|
| Continuous authentication (MFA per session) | Layer 7 (Policy) |
| Device posture check before connection | Layer 4 (Host) |
| Microsegmentation per asset | Layer 3 (Zoning) |
| Continuous logging & anomaly detection | Layer 7 (SOC) |
| Least-privilege JIT access | Layer 7 (RBAC) |

**Step 3 — Insider threat mitigation (2 marks):**
Insider threats succeed because of implicit trust. Zero Trust removes that trust: every action is verified against the *current* context (location, time, device health, behaviour baseline). Even a privileged insider cannot move laterally or escalate privileges without re-authentication and posture checks, drastically reducing insider-bypass probability.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in PECPT413 ESE on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing DiD with Defense-in-Breadth.** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the distinction is not made.
> 2. **Forgetting the assumption of independence.** Every probability calculation must explicitly state *"assuming independent, non-overlapping controls."*
> 3. **Ignoring CPS-specific risks.** Answers that read like generic IT-security notes (no mention of SCADA, PLC, Purdue Model, IEC 62443, SIL) lose 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Missing the diagram.** A 14-mark question without a labelled diagram of the layered architecture forfeits 2 marks.
> 5. **Omitting quantitative comparison.** Always include numerical LRF / breach-probability calculations; KTU examiners explicitly look for them in 2024 scheme answers.
> 6. **No mention of Policy/People layer.** Most students list only technical layers; including the *human* layer earns the full 14/14.
> 7. **Skipping a conclusion statement.** Always close with a one-line summary tying cyber and physical safety together.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Defense-in-Depth (DiD)** = layered, redundant, ideally diverse controls protecting the *same* CPS asset.
- **Seven canonical layers (PPHNAPP):** Policy/People, Physical, Host, Network, Application, Protocol, Data.
- **CPS-specific framing:** Always cite **Purdue Model (L0–L5)** and **IEC 62443 zones/conduits**.
- **Core formula:** $P_{\text{breach}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i$ *only* under the independence assumption.
- **Effectiveness:** $E_{\text{DiD}} = 1 - \prod p_i$.
- **Risk model:** $R = T \times V \times I$.
- **Layered Reduction Factor (LRF)** is the key comparative metric — always present it numerically.
- **Differentiate:** DiD (in-series, different controls, one asset) **vs** Defense-in-Breadth (in-parallel, same control, many assets).
- **Zero Trust** complements DiD by removing implicit trust; it strengthens Layers 3, 4, and 7.
- **CPS safety ≠ IT security:** cite **IEC 61508 SIL** and **IEC 62443-3-3 Security Levels (SL 1–4)**.
- **Always include a labelled block diagram** for full marks on 14-mark questions.
- **Mention insider threat, supply-chain risk, and firmware integrity** for bonus marks.
- **Close with a quantitative comparison** (e.g., "DiD reduces breach probability by 4 orders of magnitude").
- **People + Process + Technology** triad must be stated — never treat DiD as a purely technical topic.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Memory Mnemonic for the Exam Hall:**
> *"**C**astle, **C**ontrols, **C**yber-Physical"* → Each **C** has seven layers: **P**hysical, **P**erimeter, **Z**oning, **H**ost, **A**pplication, **D**ata, **P**eople. Memorise this in order before entering the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security by Design Approaches for CPS Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Security by Design Approaches for CPS Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Security by Design (SbD) for Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** is a structured engineering methodology in which protective mechanisms, threat mitigations, and trust boundaries are deliberately embedded into the CPS architecture at every stage of the system development life cycle (SDLC) — from requirements elicitation and conceptual modelling through to deployment, operation, and decommissioning.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, it is formally defined as:

> The proactive, architecture-first integration of confidentiality, integrity, availability, authenticity, and non-repudiation (the **CIA-A-N triad**) into the *physical*, *cyber*, and *cyber-physical* interfaces of a CPS, achieved through layered defences, formal verification, and continuous monitoring, rather than being retrofitted as patches post-deployment.

The reference architectural model used in the KTU PECPT413 syllabus aligns with the **NIST SP 1500-201 (CPS Framework)** and **IEC 62443** industrial automation security standards, which mandate that security controls be intrinsic properties of the system's structural design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Security by Design is *not* a single tool or a firewall. It is a *design philosophy* + a *set of architectural patterns* that ensure every subsystem of the CPS — sensor layer, communication layer, control layer, and application layer — has been engineered to resist, detect, and recover from adversarial actions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault Building" Mental Model

Imagine you are tasked with designing a new bank building to store gold bars.

| Design Choice | Security Analogy | CPS Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Thick concrete walls | Hardens the physical perimeter | Hardened firmware, secure boot |
| Multiple locked doors in sequence | Defence-in-Depth | Layered security controls |
| Biometric checks at each door | Authentication & Authorisation | PKI certificates, RBAC |
| Security cameras inside every room | Continuous monitoring | IDS/IPS, anomaly detection |
| A panic room that seals automatically | Resilience & containment | Isolated safety enclaves, fail-safe states |
| Built-in safe from day 1 (not added later) | Security by Design | Architectural security built-in |

**The crucial insight:** If the architect *first* builds an ordinary glass building and *then* tries to bolt on steel doors, the result is fragile. A *properly engineered* bank (a CPS) is designed with security as a load-bearing pillar from the foundation. This is the essence of **Security by Design** — security is structural, not cosmetic.

---

## 1.3 The CIA-A-N Security Triad for CPS

> [!NOTE]
> **The Five Pillars of CPS Security (Board Favourite Definition)**

1. **Confidentiality** — Sensitive data (sensor readings, control commands) is disclosed only to authorised entities. In CPS, leakage of actuator commands can be as damaging as data theft.
2. **Integrity** — Data and control signals are not tampered with during transmission or processing. A tampered "valve close" command could trigger a physical catastrophe.
3. **Availability** — The CPS must remain operational under attack (e.g., DDoS-resistant, redundant power). A safety-critical system that goes offline is itself a hazard.
4. **Authenticity** — Every actor (sensor, controller, operator) must prove its identity. Mitigates spoofing attacks on sensors (e.g., false temperature readings).
5. **Non-Repudiation** — Actions must be cryptographically traceable to their source, enabling forensic accountability in industrial incident investigations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics used in KTU Board Examinations:**
> - **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD):** Average time between compromise and detection. Target: *seconds to minutes* for safety-critical CPS.
> - **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR):** Average time to contain a detected incident.
> - **Attack Surface Area:** Quantified as the number of exposed interfaces, protocols, and entry points.
> - **Defence-in-Depth Layers (D):** The number of independent security barriers an attacker must bypass.

---

## 1.4 Why Security by Design is Non-Negotiable in CPS

Unlike traditional IT systems, CPS failures cascade into the **physical world** — a single tampered sensor reading can:
- Cause a chemical plant to vent toxic gases
- Trigger a power-grid cascading blackout
- Disable a vehicle's braking subsystem
- Disrupt a hospital's life-support equipment

> [!NOTE]
> **Purdue Model Reference:** KTU's reference architecture maps the CPS into **Levels 0–4** of the Purdue/ISA-95 model. Security by Design demands distinct, formally verified controls at each level boundary.

This is why the **shift-left** principle dominates CPS security pedagogy — vulnerabilities must be *designed out*, not patched in.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Pillars of Security by Design

The KTU 2024 syllabus organises SbD around **six interlocking pillars**. Each pillar maps to specific architectural artefacts in a CPS.

### Pillar 1 — Defence in Depth (DiD)

**Definition:** A layered security strategy in which *independent* defensive controls are deployed at multiple architectural levels so that the failure of one layer does not result in total system compromise.

**Why it works:** An attacker must defeat *multiple, heterogeneous* controls (e.g., cryptographic + physical + procedural), exponentially increasing attack cost.

**How it is applied in CPS:**

| Layer | Architectural Artefact | Threat Mitigated |
|---|---|---|
| **L0 — Physical Process** | Tamper-evident enclosures, lockable cabinets | Physical tampering of sensors/PLCs |
| **L1 — Sensors & Actuators** | Secure boot, hardware RoT (Root of Trust) | Firmware implants, sensor spoofing |
| **L2 — Control Systems (PLC, RTU, DCS)** | Code signing, sandboxed runtimes | Malicious control logic injection |
| **L3 — Supervisory (SCADA, HMI)** | Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), MFA | Operator impersonation |
| **L4 — Enterprise / Cloud** | TLS, micro-segmentation, zero-trust gateways | Eavesdropping, lateral movement |
| **L5 — Operations & Management** | SIEM, SOC, audit logging | Insider threats, post-breach forensics |

---

### Pillar 2 — Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)

**Definition:** A security model where *no entity — internal or external — is trusted by default*. Every request is authenticated, authorised, and logged before access is granted.

**Core Tenets:**
1. **Verify explicitly** — Authenticate based on identity, device posture, location, and behavioural context.
2. **Least-privilege access** — Just-in-Time (JIT) and Just-Enough-Access (JEA) mechanisms.
3. **Assume breach** — Design as if the perimeter is already compromised; segment aggressively.

**Application in CPS:** A SCADA workstation authenticated yesterday cannot issue actuator commands today without re-authentication if its behavioural profile deviates (e.g., unusual command frequency, off-hours activity).

---

### Pillar 3 — Secure-by-Construction Hardware Roots of Trust

Modern CPS edge devices embed a **Hardware Root of Trust (HRoT)** — an immutable, tamper-resistant silicon primitive that anchors the chain of trust from boot to application.

**Components of HRoT:**
- **Immutable Boot ROM** — Initial code executed by the CPU at power-on.
- **Trusted Platform Module (TPM 2.0)** — Cryptographic co-processor for key storage.
- **Secure Enclave / TrustZone** — Hardware-isolated execution environment.
- **Physically Unclonable Function (PUF)** — Generates device-unique cryptographic keys from silicon manufacturing variations.

**Boot Chain Verification Equation:**

$$\text{Trust}_{\text{system}} = \text{Verify}(\text{ROM} \rightarrow \text{Bootloader} \rightarrow \text{OS} \rightarrow \text{App})$$

If any signature verification fails, the device halts or enters a safe-state recovery mode.

---

### Pillar 4 — Network Segmentation & Micro-segmentation

**Definition:** The partitioning of the CPS network into isolated security zones with strictly controlled, audited communication paths between them.

**Architectural Patterns:**

- **DMZ (Demilitarised Zone):** Isolates the OT network from the IT/enterprise network.
- **VLAN-based Segmentation:** Logical separation over shared physical infrastructure.
- **Micro-segmentation:** Per-workload policy enforcement (e.g., one PLC cannot communicate with another without explicit firewall rules).
- **Data Diodes:** One-way physical communication channels for ultra-critical paths (e.g., sensor → historian, never reverse).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Reference:** IEC 62443 defines **Security Level (SL)** 1 to 4, corresponding to threat sophistication from casual to nation-state. Segmentation policies must match the target SL.

---

### Pillar 5 — Cryptographic Protection at Rest and in Transit

| CPS Data State | Cryptographic Mechanism | KTU Board Term |
|---|---|---|
| Data in sensor-to-gateway transit | AES-128/256 + MAC (e.g., ChaCha20-Poly1305) | Link-layer encryption |
| Data in gateway-to-cloud transit | TLS 1.3 with mutual authentication | Transport-layer security |
| Data at rest in historian | AES-XTS full-disk encryption | Storage-layer encryption |
| Firmware updates | RSA-PSS or Ed25519 signatures | Code signing |
| Identity provisioning | X.509 certificates issued by internal CA | PKI infrastructure |

**Confidentiality Guarantee Equation (Information-Theoretic Basis):**

For a symmetric cipher with key length $n$ bits, the probability of an adversary recovering the plaintext by exhaustive key search is:

$$P_{\text{break}} = \frac{1}{2^n}$$

For $n = 256$ bits, this is approximately $1.47 \times 10^{-77}$ — computationally infeasible.

---

### Pillar 6 — Resilience, Fault Tolerance & Safety Interlocks

**Definition:** The ability of the CPS to maintain safe operation (or transition gracefully to a known-safe state) when subjected to attacks, faults, or unexpected inputs.

**Patterns:**
- **Redundancy:** $N$-modular redundancy (NMR) — e.g., triple-modular redundancy (TMR) with majority voting.
- **Watchdog Timers:** Hardware timers that reset the system if the control loop fails to "kick" within $T_{\text{watchdog}}$.
- **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS):** Independent, certified subsystems (per IEC 61508) that override control logic during hazardous conditions.
- **Graceful Degradation:** Reduced functionality (e.g., emergency-only elevator mode) instead of total shutdown.

**TMR Voting Rule:**

$$\text{Output}_{\text{system}} = \text{mode}(\text{Output}_1, \text{Output}_2, \text{Output}_3)$$

Where `mode` is the statistical mode (majority). Tolerates one faulty/compromised module.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Revision Table — Memorise Before Exam**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables | KTU Board Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Exhaustive key search probability | $P_{\text{break}} = 2^{-n}$ | $n$ = key length (bits) | Crypto strength justification |
| 2 | Defence-in-Depth breach probability | $P_{\text{total}} = \prod_{i=1}^{D} p_i$ | $D$ = layers, $p_i$ = breach probability of layer $i$ | Quantifying layered defence |
| 3 | TMR availability | $A_{\text{TMR}} = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)$ | $R$ = single-module reliability | Redundancy design |
| 4 | Attack surface reduction | $\Delta S = S_{\text{before}} - S_{\text{after}}$ | $S$ = number of exposed interfaces | Architectural improvement |
| 5 | Risk score (qualitative) | $R_{\text{score}} = L \times I$ | $L$ = likelihood, $I$ = impact (1–5) | Threat prioritisation |
| 6 | Authentication latency budget | $T_{\text{auth}} \leq T_{\text{control cycle}}$ | Control cycle = ms range in real-time CPS | Real-time constraint |
| 7 | MTTD target for safety-critical | $\text{MTTD} \leq 60\text{ s}$ | — | SLA definition |
| 8 | Zero-trust session timeout | $T_{\text{session}} \leq 15\text{ min}$ | — | Re-authentication policy |
| 9 | Hash integrity (SHA-256 collision resistance) | $P_{\text{collision}} \approx 2^{-128}$ | — | Firmware integrity |
| 10 | RSA minimum key (KTU standard) | $n \geq 2048$ bits | — | PKI design question |

**Critical Note on Formula 2 (DiD breach probability):**

> [!NOTE]
> If each layer has an independent breach probability $p_i = 0.1$ and $D = 5$ layers, then $P_{\text{total}} = 0.1^5 = 10^{-5}$, demonstrating the **multiplicative** strength of layered defence. This is the canonical KTU board derivation.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grids:** SbD prevents false data injection attacks that could destabilise the power network.
- **Medical CPS (e.g., insulin pumps, pacemakers):** Secure boot + authenticated firmware updates prevent life-threatening code injection.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Zero-trust segmentation limits ransomware lateral movement (e.g., lessons from the *Colonial Pipeline* and *Triton/TRISIS* incidents).
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Defence-in-Depth across V2X communication, in-vehicle CAN bus, and cloud telemetry mitigates multi-vector attacks.
- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** Model-based SbD enables formal verification of PLC logic before deployment.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Quantifying Defence-in-Depth Strength

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question):**

A CPS architecture implements $D = 5$ independent security layers. Each layer has a probability of breach $p_i$. Assuming layer independence, derive the expression for the total system breach probability and compute it for $p_1 = 0.05$, $p_2 = 0.08$, $p_3 = 0.10$, $p_4 = 0.12$, $p_5 = 0.15$.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the Event of Total Compromise**

Let $B_i$ be the event that layer $i$ is breached. The total system is compromised only if **all** $D$ layers are breached sequentially (an attacker must defeat each layer to proceed).

**Step 2 — Express Total Breach Probability**

By the definition of conditional probability:

$$P(\text{total breach}) = P(B_1 \cap B_2 \cap B_3 \cap B_4 \cap B_5)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Independence Assumption**

For independent security layers (a core assumption of DiD), the joint probability of intersection equals the product of individual probabilities:

$$P(B_1 \cap B_2 \cap \ldots \cap B_D) = \prod_{i=1}^{D} P(B_i)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Layer Probabilities**

For the general case:

$$P_{\text{total}} = \prod_{i=1}^{D} p_i$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Numerical Result**

Substitute the given values:

$$P_{\text{total}} = 0.05 \times 0.08 \times 0.10 \times 0.12 \times 0.15$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$0.05 \times 0.08 = 0.0040$$

$$0.0040 \times 0.10 = 0.00040$$

$$0.00040 \times 0.12 = 0.0000480$$

$$0.0000480 \times 0.15 = 7.2 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**

$$P_{\text{total}} = 7.2 \times 10^{-6} \approx 1 \text{ in } 138{,}889$$

This represents a **~99.9993% reduction** in compromise probability compared to a single-layer system with $p = 0.15$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The multiplicative effect of DiD is the formal justification for *why more independent security layers* are mandated by IEC 62443 and NIST CPS Framework. KTU board questions often test this exact computation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — TMR Availability Under Attack

**Problem Setup:**

A safety-critical CPS actuator uses Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR). Each module has reliability $R = 0.95$ (probability of correct operation per cycle, including cyber-attack resilience). Derive the system availability $A_{\text{TMR}}$.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 — Define Reliability per Module**

Each of the 3 modules independently produces a correct output with probability $R = 0.95$ and a faulty output with probability $1 - R = 0.05$.

**Step 2 — Enumerate Operational States**

A TMR system produces a correct output in exactly two cases:
- **Case A:** All 3 modules are correct.
- **Case B:** Exactly 2 modules are correct (majority vote still correct).

**Step 3 — Compute Case A Probability**

$$P_A = R^3 = (0.95)^3 = 0.857375$$

**Step 4 — Compute Case B Probability**

Use the binomial coefficient $\binom{3}{2} = 3$ to count the ways to choose exactly 2 correct modules out of 3:

$$P_B = \binom{3}{2} R^2 (1 - R) = 3 \times (0.95)^2 \times (0.05) = 3 \times 0.9025 \times 0.05 = 0.135375$$

**Step 5 — Sum the Probabilities**

$$A_{\text{TMR}} = P_A + P_B = 0.857375 + 0.135375 = 0.99275$$

**Step 6 — Generalised Formula**

$$\boxed{A_{\text{TMR}} = R^3 + 3R^2(1 - R) = 3R^2 - 2R^3}$$

For arbitrary $R$:

$$A_{\text{TMR}}(R) = 3R^2 - 2R^3$$

**Step 7 — Comparison with Single Module**

$$A_{\text{single}} = 0.95 \quad \text{vs.} \quad A_{\text{TMR}} = 0.99275$$

**Availability Gain:**

$$\Delta A = 0.99275 - 0.95 = 0.04275 \text{ (i.e., 4.275 percentage points)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for the Board Exam:** A common student error is to use $A = 1 - (1-R)^3$, which is the *unreliability* of the voter (all-fail case). The correct expression is the sum of the 0-fail and 1-fail binomial terms, as derived above.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Risk Score Computation for CPS Threat

**Problem Setup:**

A KTU board question typically provides a vulnerability with the following qualitative ratings (scale 1–5):
- Likelihood $L = 4$
- Impact $I = 5$

Derive the risk score and the mitigation priority.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply the Risk Formula**

$$R_{\text{score}} = L \times I = 4 \times 5 = 20$$

**Step 2 — Compare Against Risk Matrix Thresholds (IEC 62443 Aligned)**

| Risk Score Range | Classification | Required Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 – 4 | Low | Accept / monitor |
| 5 – 9 | Medium | Mitigate within 90 days |
| 10 – 15 | High | Mitigate within 30 days |
| 16 – 25 | Critical | Immediate action + executive reporting |

**Step 3 — Classify the Vulnerability**

$R_{\text{score}} = 20$ falls in the **Critical** band → immediate mitigation.

**Step 4 — Recommend Security by Design Countermeasures**

1. Add a **firewall rule** at the Purdue Level 3.5 DMZ.
2. Enforce **mutual TLS** with client certificate validation.
3. Deploy **anomaly-based IDS** on the control loop with MTTD < 60 s.
4. Apply **security patching** within 24 hours under change-control.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping SbD Pillars to CPS Layers

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Table — KTU Module 2 Cross-Mapping**

| SbD Pillar | Sensor Layer (L0–L1) | Control Layer (L2) | Supervisory (L3) | Enterprise (L4) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Defence in Depth | Tamper-evident housing | Code signing | RBAC, MFA | DMZ, EDR |
| Zero Trust | Mutual auth per packet | Per-command auth | Re-auth on shift change | Continuous posture checks |
| Hardware RoT | Secure boot on MCU | TPM for PLC | HSM for SCADA servers | Cloud HSM (KMS) |
| Segmentation | One-way data diodes | VLAN isolation per zone | Air-gapped OT network | Micro-segmented cloud VPC |
| Cryptography | AES-CCM on sensor RF | MAC on control frames | TLS 1.3 to historians | TLS + at-rest encryption |
| Resilience | Sensor voting (TMR) | Watchdog timers | Fail-safe interlocks | Multi-region cloud failover |
| Authentication | X.509 device certs | Operator PIN + smartcard | LDAP/RADIUS + MFA | SSO + conditional access |
| Monitoring | Local anomaly detection | PLC logging to SIEM | SCADA event correlation | Cloud-native SIEM |

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Zero-Trust Request Flow in a CPS

This diagram illustrates the **never-trust, always-verify** flow when an operator (or a service) requests an actuator command in a zero-trust CPS.

```mermaid
graph TB
    R["Request Originator: Operator or Service"]:::req
    P1["Step 1: Identity Verification via MFA and Certificate"]:::step
    P2["Step 2: Device Posture Assessment is Endpoint Healthy"]:::step
    P3["Step 3: Context Evaluation: Time Location Behaviour"]:::step
    P4["Step 4: Policy Decision Point PDP Evaluates Request"]:::step
    P5["Step 5: Policy Enforcement Point PEP Grants or Denies"]:::step
    P6["Step 6: Action Logged to SIEM and Audit Trail"]:::step
    P7["Step 7: Command Issued to Actuator with Short Lived Token"]:::step
    P8["Step 8: Token Expires within 15 Minutes Forced Re Auth"]:::step

    R --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8
    P8 -.-> R

    classDef req fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note:** The feedback loop `P8 -.-> R` signifies that after token expiry, *no implicit trust is granted* — every actuator command must re-traverse the full verification chain. This is the operational essence of zero-trust in safety-critical CPS.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Secure Boot Chain-of-Trust (Hardware Root of Trust)

```mermaid
graph TB

    FAIL["Halt and Enter Safe State if Signature Invalid"]:::fail

    PWR["Power On Reset Signal"]:::pwr

    PWR --> ROM
    ROM -- Verifies Signature --> BL
    BL -- Verifies Signature --> OS
    OS -- Verifies Signature --> APP
    ROM -.->|Invalid Sig| FAIL
    BL -.->|Invalid Sig| FAIL
    OS -.->|Invalid Sig| FAIL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** If *any* signature verification in the chain fails, the device must not boot the subsequent stage. KTU board questions often test whether students understand that **the chain is only as strong as its weakest verified link**.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**"Define Security by Design in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. List any FOUR architectural pillars that support it."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Security by Design (SbD) is a CPS engineering paradigm in which security controls — confidentiality, integrity, availability, authenticity, and non-repudiation — are embedded into the architecture from the requirements phase onward, rather than retrofitted after deployment. It mandates that protection mechanisms be a *structural property* of the cyber, physical, and cyber-physical interfaces of the system.
>
> **Four Pillars (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Defence in Depth** — Layered, independent security controls across Purdue Levels 0–4.
> 2. **Zero Trust Architecture** — No implicit trust; continuous verification of every request.
> 3. **Hardware Root of Trust** — Tamper-resistant silicon primitives anchoring the secure boot chain.
> 4. **Resilience and Safety Interlocks** — TMR, watchdogs, and fail-safe states for graceful degradation.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**"Explain the concept of Hardware Root of Trust (HRoT). How does it contribute to the secure boot process in a CPS edge device?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **HRoT Definition (1 Mark):** A Hardware Root of Trust is a set of immutable, tamper-resistant hardware components (Boot ROM, TPM 2.0, Secure Enclave, PUF) that provide a cryptographically verifiable foundation upon which all higher-layer software can be trusted.
>
> **Secure Boot Contribution (2 Marks):** During power-on, the immutable Boot ROM executes first and verifies the cryptographic signature of the Stage 1 Bootloader. If valid, the bootloader verifies the OS image, which in turn verifies the application binary. Each stage's verification is anchored in the previous stage's already-verified key, forming an unbroken **chain of trust**. If any signature fails, the device halts or enters a safe state, preventing malicious firmware from ever executing. This protects CPS edge devices against persistent firmware implants.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**"**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Defence-in-Depth** strategy for a Cyber-Physical System. How does it map to the Purdue Reference Architecture? **(7 Marks)****"

**"**(b)** A CPS implements 5 independent security layers with breach probabilities $p_1 = 0.04$, $p_2 = 0.06$, $p_3 = 0.08$, $p_4 = 0.10$, and $p_5 = 0.12$. Assuming layer independence, calculate the total system breach probability and comment on the result. **(7 Marks)****"

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**1. Definition (1 Mark):**
Defence-in-Depth (DiD) is a layered security strategy in which multiple, independent security controls are deployed at different architectural levels of a CPS such that the failure of any single layer does not lead to total system compromise.

**2. Purdue Level Mapping Table (4 Marks — 0.5 per cell):**

| Purdue Level | CPS Component | Security Control |
|---|---|---|
| Level 0 | Sensors, actuators, physical process | Tamper-evident enclosures, physical access controls |
| Level 1 | PLCs, RTUs, DCS controllers | Secure boot, code signing, sandboxed runtime |
| Level 2 | SCADA, HMI | RBAC, MFA, session recording |
| Level 3 | Historian, MES, operations | DMZ, VLAN segmentation, IDS |
| Level 4 / 5 | Enterprise network, cloud, internet | TLS 1.3, micro-segmentation, SIEM, EDR |

**3. Block Diagram Description (2 Marks):**
*(Draw a stacked-layer pyramid:*

- *Apex (most critical):* Physical process → secured by **physical tamper detection**.
- *Layer 1:* Field devices → secured by **secure boot + signed firmware**.
- *Layer 2:* Control systems → secured by **code signing + sandboxing**.
- *Layer 3:* Supervisory → secured by **RBAC + MFA**.
- *Layer 4:* Enterprise → secured by **firewall + DMZ**.
- *Layer 5:* Cloud/Internet → secured by **TLS + zero-trust gateway**.

*The diagram must show attackers must defeat all layers to compromise the system.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — State the DiD Probability Formula (1 Mark):**

$$P_{\text{total}} = \prod_{i=1}^{D} p_i$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Given Probabilities (1 Mark):**

$$P_{\text{total}} = 0.04 \times 0.06 \times 0.08 \times 0.10 \times 0.12$$

**Step 3 — Perform the Multiplication Step-by-Step (3 Marks):**

$$0.04 \times 0.06 = 0.0024$$

$$0.0024 \times 0.08 = 0.000192$$

$$0.000192 \times 0.10 = 0.0000192$$

$$0.0000192 \times 0.12 = 2.304 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4 — Final Result and Interpretation (2 Marks):**

$$P_{\text{total}} = 2.304 \times 10^{-6} \approx 1 \text{ in } 434{,}028$$

Compared to a single-layer system with $p = 0.12$, the DiD architecture reduces breach probability by a factor of $\sim 52{,}083$. This demonstrates the **multiplicative hardening** effect of layered defences and is the formal quantitative justification for adopting DiD in safety-critical CPS.

---

### Question Choice B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**"**(a)** Explain the **Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)** model and its applicability to CPS. List any FIVE core principles. **(7 Marks)****"

**"**(b)** A safety-critical actuator uses **Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)** with each module having reliability $R = 0.97$. Compute the system availability. Compare it with a single-module system and justify the use of TMR in CPS. **(7 Marks)****"

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**1. ZTA Definition (1 Mark):**
Zero Trust Architecture is a security model in which **no entity — internal or external — is trusted by default**. Every access request is explicitly verified using identity, device posture, behavioural context, and least-privilege policies before access is granted.

**2. Five Core Principles of ZTA (4 Marks — 0.8 each):**

1. **Verify Explicitly** — Authenticate and authorise every request using all available signal data (identity, location, device health, behaviour).
2. **Least-Privilege Access** — Use Just-in-Time (JIT) and Just-Enough-Access (JEA) to minimise standing permissions.
3. **Assume Breach** — Design the system as if the network perimeter is already compromised; segment aggressively.
4. **Continuous Verification** — Re-evaluate trust on every transaction; no long-lived sessions.
5. **Micro-segmentation** — Isolate workloads into small security zones with per-zone policy enforcement.

*(Additional valid principles: encrypt all data in transit, log everything, automate policy enforcement via PDP/PEP.)*

**3. Applicability to CPS (2 Marks):**
In CPS, ZTA ensures that an operator authenticated on Day 1 cannot issue arbitrary commands on Day 2 without re-verification. It prevents lateral movement of ransomware (e.g., the *Triton* attack on a Saudi petrochemical plant) and is essential for IIoT environments where devices may join/leave the network dynamically. ZTA's short-lived tokens (≤ 15 min) align with CPS control-cycle constraints when appropriately sized.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — State the TMR Availability Formula (1 Mark):**

$$A_{\text{TMR}} = R^3 + 3R^2(1 - R)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $R = 0.97$ (1 Mark):**

$$A_{\text{TMR}} = (0.97)^3 + 3(0.97)^2(0.03)$$

**Step 3 — Compute Each Term (3 Marks):**

$$(0.97)^3 = 0.97 \times 0.97 \times 0.97 = 0.912673$$

$$(0.97)^2 = 0.9409$$

$$3 \times 0.9409 \times 0.03 = 0.084681$$

**Step 4 — Sum the Terms (1 Mark):**

$$A_{\text{TMR}} = 0.912673 + 0.084681 = 0.997354$$

**Step 5 — Compare with Single-Module and Justify (1 Mark):**

$$A_{\text{single}} = 0.97 \quad \text{vs.} \quad A_{\text{TMR}} \approx 0.9974$$

The TMR system improves availability by approximately **2.74 percentage points**. In safety-critical CPS (e.g., nuclear reactor shutdown, aircraft flight control, medical infusion pump), this improvement can be the difference between a near-miss and a catastrophic failure. TMR also tolerates one module being compromised by a cyber-attack without affecting correct system behaviour, which justifies its adoption as a *resilience pattern* within the SbD framework.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the independence assumption** in DiD calculations — *always state "assuming independent layers"* explicitly.
> 2. **Using $A = 1 - (1-R)^3$ instead of $A = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)$** for TMR — the former is the unreliability of *all-three-fail*; you need the sum of the *zero-fail* and *one-fail* cases.
> 3. **Confusing TMR with dual modular redundancy (DMR)** — DMR cannot resolve conflicts and is unsuitable for safety-critical applications.
> 4. **Omitting the chain-of-trust explanation** in HRoT questions — boards expect the *boot ROM → bootloader → OS → app* verification sequence.
> 5. **Neglecting to mention the MTTD ≤ 60 s SLA** in real-time CPS IDS answers — without this, the answer appears incomplete.
> 6. **Writing "firewall" alone for segmentation** — boards expect VLAN, DMZ, and micro-segmentation as separate concepts.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorise These Bullets**

- **Security by Design (SbD)** = security as a *structural*, *first-class* design property of the CPS, not a bolt-on.
- **CIA-A-N Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authenticity, Non-repudiation.
- **Six SbD Pillars** = Defence-in-Depth, Zero Trust, Hardware Root of Trust, Network Segmentation, Cryptography, Resilience.
- **Purdue Levels 0–5** = Physical → Control → Supervisory → Operations → Enterprise → External.
- **DiD Probability** = $P_{\text{total}} = \prod p_i$ (multiplicative, hence powerful).
- **TMR Availability** = $A_{\text{TMR}} = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R) = 3R^2 - 2R^3$.
- **Risk Score** = $L \times I$ (Likelihood × Impact, 1–5 scale; 16+ = Critical).
- **Hardware Root of Trust** anchors the chain: **ROM → Bootloader → OS → App**, with halt-on-failure.
- **Zero Trust** = verify explicitly, least privilege, assume breach, micro-segment.
- **TLS 1.3 + mutual auth** is the KTU-default transport security for CPS-to-cloud links.
- **TPM 2.0 / PUF / TrustZone** are canonical HRoT primitives — name them in answers.
- **IEC 62443** = industrial CPS security standard; **NIST SP 1500-201** = CPS Framework.
- **MTTD target for safety-critical CPS** = ≤ 60 seconds.
- **Zero-trust session timeout** = ≤ 15 minutes.
- **AES-256** = $P_{\text{break}} = 2^{-256} \approx 1.47 \times 10^{-77}$.
- **Watchdog timer** + **safety interlock** = mandatory in safety-critical SbD.
- **Data diodes** = one-way physical isolation for ultra-critical OT paths.
- **Micro-segmentation** is preferred over flat VLAN-only designs in modern CPS.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementing Authentication Mechanisms in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Implementing Authentication Mechanisms in CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, *Authentication* is formally defined as the cryptographic and protocol-based process by which a system entity (a sensor, actuator, controller, gateway, or human user) provides irrefutable proof of its claimed identity to a verifier, thereby establishing a trusted communication channel before any control commands, telemetry, or sensitive data are exchanged.

The NIST SP 800-63B (Digital Identity Guidelines) defines authentication as verifying the identity of a user, process, or device, often as a prerequisite to allowing access to resources in an information system. In CPS, this is adapted to the **identity of physical entities and their digital twins**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Authentication in CPS is not merely "login with password." It is a **multi-layered identity proofing mechanism** that must satisfy three CPS-specific constraints: **(1) Real-time latency** (decision in milliseconds for control loops), **(2) Resilience to physical tampering**, and **(3) Scalability across thousands of low-power edge nodes**.

The general authentication function can be abstracted mathematically as:

$$
A: \{E, C, S\} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}
$$

Where:
- $E$ = set of evidence presented by the claimant
- $C$ = set of claimed identities
- $S$ = set of system-side secret stores or trust anchors
- Output = **1 (Authentic)** or **0 (Rejected)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Checkpoint

Imagine **CPS authentication** as the **airport boarding process** for a critical flight:

| CPS Authentication Concept | Airport Analogy | What It Proves |
|---------------------------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Identity Claim** | Boarding pass with your name | "I am who I say I am" |
| **Something You Know (Password/PIN)** | Booking reference number | Secret knowledge |
| **Something You Have (Token/Smart Card)** | Physical boarding pass | Possession of object |
| **Something You Are (Biometric)** | Face scan at gate | Physical trait |
| **Mutual Authentication** | Captain verifies ATC, ATC verifies Captain | Both sides prove identity |
| **Certificate Authority (CA)** | Government passport office | Trusted third party |
| **Session Key Derivation** | Encrypted seat-back communication | Secure channel after boarding |

Just as an airport cannot let a passenger board based on a name alone (anyone could claim any name), a CPS controller cannot accept commands from a sensor that only declares its device ID. Both require **multi-factor proof**, **tamper-resistant credentials**, and a **pre-established trust chain**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary for First-Time Learners:** Authentication is the *bouncer at the door* of your CPS network. It checks IDs (passwords, fingerprints, hardware tokens) before letting any device talk to the controller. If the bouncer is fooled, a fake sensor could feed false data to a nuclear reactor — hence authentication is the *first and most critical* security gate in CPS.

## 1.3 Why Authentication is *Especially Critical* in CPS

Unlike traditional IT networks, CPS authentication failures can cause **physical harm, not just data loss**:

1. **Safety Implications:** A fake sensor reading a patient's heart rate could trigger a fatal insulin pump dose.
2. **Real-Time Constraints:** Authentication must complete in < 100 ms for hard real-time control loops.
3. **Resource Constraints:** Many CPS endpoints (e.g., temperature sensors) have < 32 KB RAM, making heavy cryptographic authentication impossible.
4. **Physical Accessibility:** Attackers can physically steal, clone, or reverse-engineer CPS devices.
5. **Heterogeneity:** A CPS may include PLCs, RTUs, IoT sensors, edge gateways, and cloud analytics — all needing *mutually compatible* authentication.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember the 3 CPS authentication constraints: "L-I-T"**
> **L**atency | **I**ntegrity (tamper-resistance) | **T**hroughput (scalability)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Authentication Factors

Authentication in CPS is built upon **three classical factor classes** (extended to a fourth for CPS):

### Factor 1: Something You Know (Knowledge-Based)
- Examples: Password, PIN, pre-shared key (PSK)
- CPS Use: Operator login to SCADA HMI
- Weakness: Vulnerable to brute-force, shoulder-surfing, keyloggers

### Factor 2: Something You Have (Possession-Based)
- Examples: Smart card, RFID token, hardware security module (HSM), TPM chip
- CPS Use: Technician's RFID badge to access substation
- Weakness: Can be lost, stolen, or cloned

### Factor 3: Something You Are (Biometric)
- Examples: Fingerprint, iris, facial geometry, ECG waveform, voice
- CPS Use: Wearable medical device authenticating the patient
- Weakness: Cannot be revoked if compromised; noisy readings in industrial environments

### Factor 4: Somewhere You Are (Context-Based) — *Unique to CPS*
- Examples: GPS location, network IP, RF fingerprint, time-of-day
- CPS Use: Sensor may only authenticate if its physical location matches registered coordinates
- Weakness: GPS spoofing, IP spoofing

> [!NOTE]
> **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** = combining ≥ 2 of the above factors. The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes MFA as the *de facto standard* for CPS deployments.

## 2.2 The Authentication Protocol Stack in CPS

Authentication in CPS is implemented as a **layered protocol stack**, typically aligned with the OSI/ISO model:

| Layer | Protocol Example | Role in Authentication |
|-------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Application Layer** | OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, SAML | User-level SSO for CPS dashboards |
| **Session Layer** | TLS 1.3 Handshake, DTLS | Mutual auth between gateway and cloud |
| **Network Layer** | IPsec IKEv2, RADIUS | Auth between CPS nodes over IP |
| **Data Link Layer** | IEEE 802.1X, MACsec | Port-level auth for industrial Ethernet |
| **Physical/Field Layer** | PUF, RFID, NFC | Hardware-rooted identity |
| **Cross-Layer** | PKI, X.509 Certificates | Trust anchor chain |

## 2.3 Deep Dive: Cryptographic Authentication Mechanisms

### Mechanism A: Password-Based Authentication (PBA)
- **Algorithm:** Client sends $\text{H}(\text{password} \Vert \text{salt})$ to server, where $\text{H}$ is a hash function (SHA-256, bcrypt, Argon2).
- **CPS Suitability:** Low — passwords are unsuitable for machine-to-machine (M2M) CPS communication.
- **Defensive Augmentations:** PAKE protocols (e.g., SRP, OPAQUE) that resist offline dictionary attacks.

### Mechanism B: Challenge-Response Authentication
- **Protocol Flow:**
  1. Verifier sends random nonce $N_c$.
  2. Prover computes $R = f(K, N_c)$ using shared secret $K$ and function $f$ (e.g., HMAC-SHA256).
  3. Verifier recomputes and compares.
- **CPS Suitability:** High — no password transmission, replay-resistant.
- **Common Variants:** HMAC-based, digital signature-based, PU F-based.

### Mechanism C: Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) Authentication
- **Components:** Certificate Authority (CA), Registration Authority (RA), X.509 certificates.
- **Flow:** Prover presents certificate $\text{Cert}_A$ signed by trusted CA; verifier checks chain to root CA, then uses $A$'s public key to verify a challenge signature.
- **CPS Suitability:** Strong, but requires PKI infrastructure and computational overhead.

### Mechanism D: Physical Unclonable Functions (PUF)
- **Concept:** A PUF exploits **manufacturing micro-variations** in silicon to produce a unique, unpredictable response $R$ to a challenge $C$.
- **Mathematical Model:** $R = PUF(C)$, where $PUF$ is a one-way function bound to the physical device.
- **CPS Suitability:** Excellent — provides hardware-rooted identity without storing secrets in memory (immune to side-channel memory extraction).
- **Challenges:** Environmental noise (temperature, voltage) requires **fuzzy extractors** and **error correction codes (ECC)**.

### Mechanism E: TPM / Secure Element-Based
- **TPM (Trusted Platform Module):** A tamper-resistant chip that stores RSA/ECC keys and performs signing operations internally.
- **CPS Use:** Smart meters, automotive ECUs, industrial controllers.
- **Standard:** ISO/IEC 11889 (TPM 2.0), Common Criteria EAL4+ certified.

### Mechanism F: Lightweight Authentication (For Constrained CPS)
- **Use Case:** 6LoWPAN, RPL-based IoT-CPS networks where nodes have < 50 KB memory.
- **Examples:**
  - **DTLS with ECC** (e.g., Curve25519): ~100× smaller than RSA
  - **Hummingbird-2, PRESENT, or SIMON ciphers**
  - **IETF ACE (Authentication and Authorization for Constrained Environments) framework**
  - **PUF + symmetric key** hybrids

## 2.4 Mutual Authentication in CPS

In CPS, **unilateral authentication is dangerous** because an attacker can impersonate the controller. Therefore, **mutual authentication** is mandatory.

A canonical mutual authentication protocol (simplified ISO/IEC 9798-3 style):

1. $A \rightarrow B: N_A$
2. $A \leftarrow B: N_B, \text{Cert}_B, \text{Sig}_B(N_A \Vert N_B)$
3. $A \rightarrow B: \text{Cert}_A, \text{Sig}_A(N_A \Vert N_B)$
4. Both sides derive session key $K_s = KDF(N_A \oplus N_B, \text{master secret})$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the high-yield reference for KTU ESE 2024. Memorize the units and use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute-value notation to preserve table formatting.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Unit / Domain |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | Authentication Decision Function | $A(E,C,S) \in \{0,1\}$ | Binary accept/reject | Boolean |
| 2 | Password Hash Verification | $V = \text{?} \, H(P \Vert s)$ | $H$=SHA-256, $s$=salt | Hex string |
| 3 | Challenge-Response (HMAC) | $R = \text{HMAC}_K(N)$ | $K$=shared key, $N$=nonce | Bit string |
| 4 | Digital Signature | $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{sk}(M)$ | $sk$=private key | Bit string |
| 5 | PUF Response | $R = PUF(C)$ | Hardware-bound | Bit string |
| 6 | Fuzzy Extractor (PUF) | $(K,P) = \text{Gen}(R); \; K' = \text{Rep}(R',P)$ | $R'$=noisy, $P$=helper data | Bit string |
| 7 | Mutual Auth Key Derivation | $K_s = KDF(N_A \oplus N_B, MK)$ | $KDF$=HKDF, $MK$=master key | Symmetric key |
| 8 | ECDSA Signature Size | $L = 2n$ bits | $n$=curve order in bits | bits |
| 9 | RSA Key Recommendation | $\vert n \vert \geq 2048$ bits | NIST 2024 baseline | bits |
| 10 | ECC vs RSA Equivalent Security | 256-bit ECC $\equiv$ 3072-bit RSA | Symmetric equivalent: 128 bits | bits |
| 11 | Authentication Latency (Target) | $t_{auth} \leq 100$ ms | Hard real-time CPS constraint | milliseconds |
| 12 | False Acceptance Rate (FAR) | $FAR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Biometric security metric | Dimensionless |
| 13 | False Rejection Rate (FRR) | $FRR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | Biometric usability metric | Dimensionless |
| 14 | Equal Error Rate (EER) | $EER = FAR \; @ \; FAR = FRR$ | Operating point selection | Dimensionless |
| 15 | PUF Uniqueness | $U = \frac{2}{L^2}\sum_{i=1}^{L}\sum_{j=1,j\neq i}^{L}\frac{HD(R_i,R_j)}{n}\times 100\%$ | $L$=devices, $n$=response length | Percent (%) |
| 16 | PUF Reliability | $Rel = 1 - \frac{HD(R, R')}{n}$ | Intra-device stability | Percent (%) |
| 17 | Entropy of Secret | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon entropy of key | bits |
| 18 | Birthday Attack Bound | $k \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}$ | For $n$-bit hash collisions | Attempts |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Authentication Use Case | Mechanism Deployed |
|----------|-------------------------|---------------------|
| **Smart Grid (IEC 61850)** | Substation IED mutual authentication | X.509 + TLS 1.3 |
| **Automotive (ISO 21434)** | ECU-to-cloud V2X communication | TPM 2.0 + PKI |
| **Healthcare (IEC 80001-1)** | Insulin pump ↔ glucose monitor | PUF + biometric pairing |
| **Industrial IoT (IEC 62443)** | PLC firmware update authentication | Digital signatures + HSM |
| **Smart Home** | Voice assistant device pairing | Acoustic + cryptographic handshake |
| **Drones / UAVs (FAA Part 107)** | Ground Control Station authentication | Mutual TLS with ECC |
| **Railways (IEC 62290)** | Train-to-wayside communication | ETCS EuroRadio with SIM-based auth |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: PUF-Based Authentication Protocol (Goorden et al. Model)

We derive the **sliding-window PUF authentication protocol** commonly analyzed in CPS security literature.

### Setup
- Prover $P$ holds a PUF with challenge-response pairs $(C_i, R_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$.
- Verifier $V$ stores the responses $R_i$ in a database.
- Goal: $P$ proves possession of the PUF to $V$ without revealing $R_i$.

### Step 1 — Helper Data Generation (Enrollment Phase)
During manufacturing, a fuzzy extractor generates public helper data $P_i$:

$$
P_i = R_i \oplus K_i
$$

where $K_i$ is a randomly chosen key of length $n$ (same as response length). This step **binds the key $K_i$ to the PUF response $R_i$** and stores $P_i$ publicly.

### Step 2 — Authentication Challenge
The verifier randomly picks an index $j$ and sends the challenge $C_j$ to the prover.

### Step 3 — Response Generation
The prover computes:

$$
R_j' = PUF(C_j)
$$

Then recovers the candidate key:

$$
K_j' = R_j' \oplus P_j
$$

### Step 4 — Proof Transmission
The prover sends $H(K_j')$ to the verifier, where $H$ is a cryptographic hash.

### Step 5 — Verification
The verifier, knowing the original $K_j$, checks:

$$
\text{Verify} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } H(K_j') = H(K_j) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The probability that a non-PUF attacker (impersonator) passes is:

$$
P_{succ} = \frac{1}{|\text{Challenge Space}|}
$$

For a $64$-bit challenge space:

$$
P_{succ} = \frac{1}{2^{64}} \approx 5.42 \times 10^{-20}
$$

### Step 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Authentication Mechanism Trade-Offs

| Mechanism | Latency | Energy Cost | Security Level | Scalability | CPS Suitability | Key Weakness |
|-----------|---------|-------------|----------------|-------------|-----------------|--------------|
| Password | Low | Low | Low (brute-force) | High | Low (M2M) | Replay, theft |
| HMAC Challenge-Response | Low ($\sim$10 ms) | Low | Medium-High | Medium | **High** | Key storage risk |
| RSA-2048 Signature | High ($\sim$50 ms) | High | Very High | Medium | Medium | Compute-heavy |
| ECC-256 Signature | Low-Medium ($\sim$15 ms) | Medium | Very High (equiv RSA-3072) | High | **High** | Cert management |
| TPM 2.0 Attestation | Medium | Medium | Very High | Medium | **High** | Hardware cost |
| PUF-Based | Very Low | Very Low | High | High | **Excellent** | Noise, modeling attacks |
| Biometric (Fingerprint) | Medium | High | High (low FAR) | Low | Wearables | Irrevocable |
| Lightweight MAC (e.g., AES-128 CMAC) | Very Low ($\sim$1 ms) | Very Low | High | **Excellent** | **Excellent for sensor nodes** | Symmetric key distribution |
| DTLS + PSK | Medium | Medium | High | Medium | **High** | PSK provisioning |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A CPS uses PUF-based authentication with 128-bit challenge-response pairs. An attacker attempts an impersonation attack. Compute the number of trials needed to achieve a 50% success probability.

**Solution:**

The number of trials $N$ needed for 50% success with random guessing is governed by the coupon-collector approximation:

$$
N = \ln(2) \cdot 2^{128}
$$

$$
N = 0.6931 \times 3.4028 \times 10^{38}
$$

$$
N \approx 2.358 \times 10^{38} \text{ trials}
$$

At an attack rate of $10^9$ attempts per second (GPU farm):

$$
T_{break} = \frac{2.358 \times 10^{38}}{10^9 \times 3.156 \times 10^7 \text{ s/yr}}
$$

$$
T_{break} \approx 7.47 \times 10^{21} \text{ years}
$$

This is **astronomically greater than the age of the universe ($\sim 1.38 \times 10^{10}$ years)**, demonstrating the security strength of PUF-based CPS authentication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Explained:** The denominator uses **1 year = 3.156 × 10⁷ seconds** (i.e., 365.25 × 24 × 3600). This is a standard astronomical conversion. KTU examiners award full marks only when both the conversion factor and the time-unit cancellation are explicitly written.

### 3.4 Derivation: Biometric Authentication FAR/FRR Trade-Off

Given a similarity score $S \in [0,1]$ between two biometric samples, and a decision threshold $T$:

$$
\text{Genuine Pair Accepted iff } S \geq T
$$

$$
\text{Impostor Pair Rejected iff } S < T
$$

As $T$ increases:
- $FAR \downarrow$ (good — fewer impostors accepted)
- $FRR \uparrow$ (bad — more genuine users rejected)

The optimal operating point is at **EER**, where $FAR = FRR$. For a CPS wearable with EER $= 0.001$ ($0.1\%$), over 1 million authentications:

$$
\text{Expected False Accepts} = 10^6 \times 10^{-3} = 1000
$$

$$
\text{Expected False Rejects} = 10^6 \times 10^{-3} = 1000
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing $FAR = \frac{FP}{TP + FP}$ (this is the **False Discovery Rate**, not FAR). The correct formula is $FAR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$, where $FP$ = impostor accepted, $TN$ = impostor rejected. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Multi-Factor Authentication Flow in CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPS Device / User Initiates Login] --> B[Claim Identity: Send Device ID / Username]
    B --> C{Auth Factor 1: Something You Know}
    C -->|Password / PIN / PSK| D[Verify Factor 1]
    C -->|Reject| Z1[Access Denied - Log Event]

    D -->|Pass| E{Auth Factor 2: Something You Have}
    E -->|Smart Card / TPM / Token| F[Verify Factor 2]
    E -->|Reject| Z1

    F -->|Pass| G{Auth Factor 3: Something You Are}
    G -->|Biometric / PUF| H[Verify Factor 3]
    G -->|Reject| Z1

    H -->|Pass| I{Auth Factor 4: Context}
    I -->|GPS / Time / RF Fingerprint| J[Verify Context]
    I -->|Skip Optional| K

    J -->|Pass| K[Session Key Negotiation]
    J -->|Fail| Z1

    K --> L[Mutual Authentication: Both Sides Prove Identity]
    L --> M[Derive Encrypted Session Key]
    M --> N[Grant Access to CPS Network]
    N --> O[Continuous Monitoring and Re-auth]
    O --> P[Session Timeout / Revocation]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: PUF-Based Authentication Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENROLL[Enrollment Phase - Secure Factory]
        E1[PUF Hardware Unit] --> E2[Generate Challenge Response Pairs]
        E2 --> E3[Store in Verifier Database]
        E3 --> E4[Generate Helper Data Pi]
    end

    subgraph AUTH[Authentication Phase - Field Deployment]
        F1[Verifier Sends Random Challenge Cj] --> F2[Prover Device Receives Cj]
        F2 --> F3[PUF Computes Response Rj]
        F3 --> F4[Recover Key Kj from Helper Data]
        F4 --> F5[Send Hash of Kj to Verifier]
        F5 --> F6[Verifier Compares with Stored Hash]
        F6 -->|Match| F7[Authentication Success]
        F6 -->|No Match| F8[Authentication Failure]
    end

    ENROLL --> AUTH
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: CPS Authentication Protocol Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Layer: OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect]
    L2[Session / Transport Layer: TLS 1.3 or DTLS 1.3]
    L3[Network Layer: IPsec IKEv2 / RADIUS]
    L4[Data Link Layer: IEEE 802.1X / MACsec]
    L5[Physical Hardware Layer: PUF / TPM 2.0 / Secure Element]
    L6[Trust Anchor Layer: PKI / X.509 / CA Hierarchy]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Authentication Failure Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Authentication Attempt] --> Q1{Identity Claimed Correctly?}
    Q1 -->|No| R1[Reject - Unknown Identity]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{All Factors Valid?}
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Reject - Factor Mismatch]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Certificate Valid and Not Revoked?}
    Q3 -->|No| R3[Reject - Cert Expired or Revoked]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Within Operational Context?}
    Q4 -->|No| R4[Reject - Anomalous Location or Time]
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q5{Mutual Authentication Complete?}
    Q5 -->|No| R5[Reject - One-Sided Auth Detected]
    Q5 -->|Yes| A1[Grant Access and Issue Session Token]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Token-Based Authentication in CPS Cloud Gateway

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as CPS Edge Device
    participant IdP as Identity Provider IdP
    participant GW as Cloud Gateway
    participant Res as Protected Resource PLC

    Dev->>IdP: Request Access Token with Client Credentials
    IdP-->>Dev: Issue JWT Signed Access Token TTL 15 min
    Dev->>GW: Send JWT in Authorization Header
    GW->>GW: Verify JWT Signature using IdP Public Key
    GW->>Res: Forward Authenticated Command
    Res-->>GW: Acknowledge Command
    GW-->>Dev: Return Response with Status
    Note over Dev,Res: Refresh Token Used Every 15 min to Renew Access
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]
**Differentiate between authentication and authorization in the context of a Cyber-Physical System. Give one CPS-specific example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Authentication | Authorization |
|--------|----------------|---------------|
| Purpose | Verifies **"Who are you?"** | Verifies **"What are you allowed to do?"** |
| Mechanism | Password, biometric, certificate, PUF | ACLs, RBAC, capability tokens |
| CPS Example | A temperature sensor proves its identity to the SCADA server using a TPM-signed certificate | The authenticated sensor is permitted to write only the `temperature` register but not the `actuator_command` register |

**Authentication precedes authorization.** In CPS, the sequence is: **(1) Authenticate device identity → (2) Authorize specific register/command access → (3) Audit action.** A sensor that authenticates successfully but is not authorized to actuate a valve is correctly behaving in a least-privilege model.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for difference, 1 for CPS example)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]
**What is a Physical Unclonable Function (PUF)? Mention any two advantages of PUF-based authentication in CPS.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Physical Unclonable Function (PUF)** is a hardware security primitive that exploits **inherent, uncontrollable manufacturing variations** in physical structures (e.g., SRAM power-up states, optical fiber scattering, arbiter delay) to produce a unique, device-specific response $R$ to a given challenge $C$. The function is unclonable because the exact physical variations cannot be replicated even by the original manufacturer.

**Two Advantages in CPS:**
1. **No persistent secret storage required** — the "secret" is the device's physical microstructure, eliminating memory-extraction attacks (e.g., cold-boot, JTAG probing).
2. **Lightweight and low-power** — ideal for resource-constrained CPS endpoints such as wireless sensor nodes and RFID tags, with response generation in nanoseconds.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for two advantages)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three-factor authentication model** in detail. With a neat diagram, describe how **multi-factor authentication (MFA)** is implemented in a typical Industrial CPS (e.g., a smart manufacturing cell). List **three attacks** that MFA defends against.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **password-based, challenge-response, and PUF-based** authentication mechanisms in CPS. Your comparison must cover: security strength, computational overhead, CPS suitability, and one key vulnerability of each.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **architecture of a PKI-based authentication system** for CPS. Explain the role of **Certificate Authority (CA), Registration Authority (RA), and X.509 certificates** in establishing trust between a sensor node and a cloud analytics platform.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With suitable diagrams and protocol steps, explain **mutual authentication using a challenge-response protocol with public key cryptography**. Derive the expression for the session key and discuss how this protocol defends against **replay attacks** and **man-in-the-middle attacks**.

---

## 5.3 Detailed Model Solutions

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Three-Factor Authentication Model

The **three-factor authentication model** in CPS is based on verifying the claimant using three independent categories of evidence:

1. **Factor 1 — Knowledge (Something You Know):**
   - Operator password to access the SCADA Human-Machine Interface (HMI).
   - Pre-shared symmetric key between two CPS controllers.

2. **Factor 2 — Possession (Something You Have):**
   - Smart card issued to maintenance technicians.
   - TPM 2.0 chip embedded in a PLC for hardware-rooted identity.

3. **Factor 3 — Inherence (Something You Are):**
   - Fingerprint scanner for biometric login at the control station.
   - Voice authentication for hands-free industrial environments.

**Implementation in Smart Manufacturing Cell — Flow:**

```
[Technician] → Inserts [Smart Card] in HMI reader
            → Enters [PIN] on keypad
            → Places [Fingerprint] on biometric sensor
            → MFA Verifier checks all three factors
            → Grants access to robotic arm controller
            → Logs action in audit trail (IEC 62443 compliance)
```

**Three Attacks Defended by MFA:**

| # | Attack | How MFA Defends |
|---|--------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Password Brute-Force** | Even if password is cracked, attacker lacks the smart card and biometric |
| 2 | **Stolen Smart Card** | Card alone is useless without the PIN and fingerprint |
| 3 | **Biometric Spoofing** | Fake fingerprint fails when combined with stolen card + unknown PIN |

---

#### Part (b) — Comparison of Authentication Mechanisms

| Parameter | Password-Based | Challenge-Response | PUF-Based |
|-----------|----------------|--------------------|-----------|
| **Security Strength** | Low — vulnerable to dictionary, rainbow tables | High — fresh nonce per session, replay-resistant | High — hardware-bound, unclonable |
| **Computational Overhead** | Very Low (one hash) | Low-Medium (one HMAC or signature) | Very Low (single hardware query) |
| **CPS Suitability** | Poor for M2M; OK for human HMI | Excellent for M2M | Excellent for resource-constrained edge |
| **Key Vulnerability** | Offline dictionary attack; phishing; reuse | Stolen pre-shared key; side-channel on HMAC | Modeling attacks using machine learning on CRPs |
| **Latency** | < 1 ms | 5–20 ms | < 1 ms |
| **Revocability** | Easy (reset password) | Easy (rotate key) | Difficult (PUF is physical) |
| **Storage Cost** | One hash entry | Shared secret in NVM | Helper data only (~hundreds of bytes) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "authentication" with "encryption":** Authentication proves identity; encryption protects data confidentiality. They are *not the same* and KTU deducts 2 marks for the swap.
> 2. **Omitting the CPS-specific constraint:** Any answer that describes only IT authentication (web login) without addressing **latency, real-time, or physical safety** is treated as incomplete and capped at 60% of allotted marks.
> 3. **Not mentioning replay-attack defense:** A challenge-response answer that does not explain the role of the **nonce** in preventing replay is marked down by 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to cite a CPS standard:** Valid references to **IEC 62443, ISO 21434, NIST SP 800-63B** earn 1 bonus mark in KTU valuation.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — PKI Architecture for CPS

A **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** is the trust framework that issues, manages, and revokes digital certificates to bind cryptographic key pairs to real-world CPS entities.

**Components:**

1. **Certificate Authority (CA):** The root of trust. Signs X.509 certificates. In CPS, the CA is often operated by the plant owner or a regulatory body.
2. **Registration Authority (RA):** Verifies the identity of the requesting CPS device before the CA issues a certificate.
3. **X.509 Certificate:** A digital document binding the device's public key to its identity (device ID, manufacturer, role). Contains: serial number, validity period, issuer DN, subject DN, public key, and CA's digital signature.
4. **Certificate Repository:** Public directory (e.g., LDAP) where certificates and Certificate Revocation Lists (CRL) are published.
5. **Certificate Revocation List (CRL) / OCSP Responder:** Tracks compromised or decommissioned certificates.

**Trust Flow in CPS:**

```
[Sensor Node] → submits CSR (Certificate Signing Request) to [RA]
            → RA verifies device credentials
            → RA forwards CSR to [CA]
            → CA issues X.509 certificate
            → Sensor uses certificate for mutual TLS with [Cloud Platform]
            → Cloud validates certificate chain up to trusted root CA
            → Encrypted + authenticated session established
```

---

#### Part (b) — Mutual Authentication Protocol Derivation

**Protocol (ISO/IEC 9798-3 Inspired):**

1. $A \rightarrow B: \; N_A$ (Alice sends fresh nonce)
2. $A \leftarrow B: \; N_B, \text{Cert}_B, \sigma_B = \text{Sign}_{sk_B}(N_A \Vert N_B)$ (Bob replies with his nonce, certificate, and signature)
3. $A \rightarrow B: \; \text{Cert}_A, \sigma_A = \text{Sign}_{sk_A}(N_A \Vert N_B)$ (Alice proves possession of her private key)
4. $A, B: \; K_s = \text{KDF}(N_A \oplus N_B, \text{MasterKey})$ (Both derive the same session key)

**Session Key Derivation (Detailed):**

$$
K_s = \text{HKDF-Extract}(\text{salt}, N_A \oplus N_B)
$$

$$
K_s' = \text{HKDF-Expand}(K_s, \text{info} = \text{"CPS-Session-2024"})
$$

where HKDF is the HMAC-based Key Derivation Function (RFC 5869).

**Replay Attack Defense:**
Each protocol run uses **freshly generated nonces** $N_A, N_B$. An attacker who replays a previously captured message $M$ will fail because the verifier expects a nonce that was *never used before* and is not present in the replayed message.

**Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Defense:**
The **digital signatures $\sigma_A, \sigma_B$** bind each message to the *signer's private key*. Since the attacker does not possess $sk_A$ or $sk_B$, they cannot forge valid signatures. The **certificate chain** ensures both parties know each other's legitimate public keys.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Property Summary:** Mutual authentication with nonces provides **mutual entity authentication**, **implicit key authentication**, **key freshness**, and **resistance to replay and MITM** — the four pillars of CPS session security.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Authentication** answers *"Who are you?"*; **Authorization** answers *"What can you do?"* — never confuse them.
- ✅ CPS authentication must satisfy **L-I-T**: **L**atency, **I**ntegrity (tamper-resistance), **T**hroughput.
- ✅ **Three classical factors:** Knowledge (password), Possession (smart card/TPM), Inherence (biometric).
- ✅ **Fourth CPS-specific factor:** Context (location, time, RF fingerprint).
- ✅ **Mutual authentication** is mandatory in CPS; unilateral auth is insecure.
- ✅ **Nonces** in challenge-response prevent replay attacks.
- ✅ **PUF** provides hardware-rooted identity without persistent secret storage; immune to memory-extraction attacks.
- ✅ **TPM 2.0** is the industrial standard for hardware security modules (ISO/IEC 11889).
- ✅ **PKI** uses X.509 certificates and a CA hierarchy; suitable for large-scale CPS deployments.
- ✅ **Lightweight authentication** (DTLS, ECC, AES-CMAC) is required for constrained CPS devices (< 50 KB RAM).
- ✅ **FAR = FP / (FP + TN)**, **FRR = FN / (FN + TP)**, **EER** is the operating point where FAR = FRR.
- ✅ **ECC-256 ≈ RSA-3072** in security strength but ~100× faster and smaller.
- ✅ **Standards to cite:** IEC 62443 (Industrial), ISO 21434 (Automotive), NIST SP 800-63B (Identity), ISO/IEC 11889 (TPM), RFC 5869 (HKDF).
- ✅ **Authentication decision** is binary: $A: \{E, C, S\} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}$.
- ✅ **Fuzzy extractor** $(K, P) = \text{Gen}(R)$ enables reliable PUF key recovery despite environmental noise.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line KTU Exam Punchline (memorize for 14-mark questions):**
> *"In Cyber-Physical Systems, authentication is not a luxury but a life-safety requirement; it must be mutual, multi-factor, low-latency, and hardware-rooted to defend against the uniquely CPS threats of physical spoofing, command injection, and cascading control failures."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and Other Access Control Models in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and Other Access Control Models in CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)?

**Formal Definition (NIST Standard INCITS 359-2012):**
> *Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is a security model in which permissions to perform operations on a system are associated with roles, rather than directly with individual users. Users acquire permissions by being assigned to one or more roles, and access to resources is granted or denied based on the permissions assigned to the activated role(s) within an active session.*

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** — which integrate computation, networking, and physical processes (e.g., smart grids, industrial control systems, autonomous vehicles, medical devices) — RBAC is the dominant access control paradigm because it scales naturally to large operator populations and aligns with organizational structure (engineer, operator, auditor, supervisor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** RBAC is the de-facto access control model for industrial CPS, SCADA systems, and IoT platforms. The NIST RBAC model defines **four incremental levels** — RBAC₀ (Core), RBAC₁ (Hierarchical), RBAC₂ (Constrained), and RBAC₃ (Symmetric/Combined).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Hospital Access Card

Imagine a large hospital:
- **Users** = doctors, nurses, receptionists, janitors, surgeons.
- **Roles** = "Cardiologist", "Nurse-On-Duty", "Receptionist", "Surgeon", "Janitor".
- **Permissions** = "Open ICU Door", "View Patient Records", "Prescribe Medicine", "Operate MRI", "Clean Ward 3".

A cardiologist is **assigned** the "Cardiologist" role. That role is **authorized** to "View Cardiac Records" and "Open ICU Door". The cardiologist is **not** assigned the "Janitor" role, so they cannot "Clean Ward 3". When the cardiologist logs in, a **session** is created where the role is activated, and the permissions become usable.

Now replace the hospital with a **power plant SCADA system**:
- **Users** = Control room operators, field engineers, vendors, system administrators.
- **Roles** = "Control-Operator", "Field-Engineer", "Vendor-Read-Only", "Admin".
- **Permissions** = "Open Breaker #3", "Read Turbine RPM", "Modify PLC Ladder Logic", "Acknowledge Alarm".

Same model — same security principle — just applied to cyber-physical actuators and sensors.

## 1.2 Other Access Control Models in CPS — Quick Overview

| Model | Full Name | Core Idea | Typical CPS Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DAC** | Discretionary Access Control | Owner of resource decides who accesses it | File systems in engineering workstations |
| **MAC** | Mandatory Access Control | System-enforced labels (security levels) dictate access | Military-grade CPS, classified ICS networks |
| **RBAC** | Role-Based Access Control | Permissions tied to job roles | Industrial control, smart grid, hospital CPS |
| **ABAC** | Attribute-Based Access Control | Decisions based on attributes of user, resource, environment | Dynamic IoT, context-aware smart cities |
| **IBAC** | Identity-Based Access Control | Per-user permission lists | Small-scale, high-security enclaves |
| **CapBAC** | Capability-Based Access Control | Possession of a token (capability) grants access | Distributed IoT, blockchain-CPS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** The difference between RBAC and ABAC is the **basis of decision**. RBAC uses *role membership*; ABAC uses *policy evaluation over attributes* (e.g., time, location, device-trust-score).
