In CPS, RBAC is preferred for **static, well-defined roles**; ABAC is preferred for **dynamic, context-sensitive** decisions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The NIST RBAC Reference Model — Four Layers

The **NIST RBAC model** (standardized as **ANSI/INCITS 359-2012**) defines RBAC as a family of four conceptual levels. This is the most frequently asked KTU question on this topic.

### Layer 1 — RBAC₀: Core RBAC
The foundation. Defines the minimum entities and relations.
- **Users (U)**: Human or autonomous agents interacting with the CPS.
- **Roles (R)**: Job functions within the organization (e.g., "Operator", "Engineer").
- **Permissions (P)**: Approvals to perform operations on objects (e.g., "close_valve", "read_temperature").
- **Sessions (S)**: A mapping that activates a subset of roles for a logged-in user.

### Layer 2 — RBAC₁: Hierarchical RBAC
Extends RBAC₀ with **role hierarchies** (partial order $\geq$).
- A senior role **inherits** the permissions of junior roles.
- Example: `Senior_Engineer $\geq$ Engineer $\geq$ Operator`.

### Layer 3 — RBAC₂: Constrained RBAC
Extends RBAC₀ with **separation of duties (SoD)** constraints.
- **Static SoD (SSD)**: Mutually exclusive roles that a user cannot simultaneously hold (e.g., "Auditor" vs "Executor").
- **Dynamic SoD (DSD)**: Mutually exclusive roles that cannot be activated in the same session.

### Layer 4 — RBAC₃: Symmetric (Combined) RBAC
Combines RBAC₁ and RBAC₂ — both hierarchy **and** constraints. This is the most expressive and most commonly deployed variant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy in CPS:** In an industrial plant, `Plant_Manager $\geq$ Shift_Supervisor $\geq$ Operator $\geq$ Trainee`. Permission inheritance flows **upward**: a Shift Supervisor can do everything an Operator can, plus supervisory actions.

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of Core RBAC

Let us formalize RBAC using **set theory** — this is essential for KTU 14-mark derivations.

Let:
- $U$ = set of all users
- $R$ = set of all roles
- $P$ = set of all permissions
- $S$ = set of all sessions
- $OPS$ = set of operations
- $OBS$ = set of observed CPS objects (sensors, actuators)

The RBAC reference model defines the following functional mappings:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PA &\subseteq P \times R \quad &&\text{(Many-to-Many Permission-to-Role Assignment)} \\
UA &\subseteq U \times R \quad &&\text{(Many-to-Many User-to-Role Assignment)} \\
user\_assignments &: S \rightarrow 2^{U} \quad &&\text{(Session to single user)} \\
role\_assignments &: S \rightarrow 2^{R} \quad &&\text{(Session to active role set)} \\
perm\_roles &: R \rightarrow 2^{P} \quad &&\text{(Role to permission set)} \\
session\_users &: S \rightarrow U \quad &&\text{(Inverse of user\_assignments)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **authorization decision** for user $u$ in session $s$ on permission $p$ is:

$$
\forall u \in U, \forall s \in S, \forall p \in P : \\
\text{Authorized}(u, s, p) \iff (u = session\_users(s)) \land (\exists r \in role\_assignments(s) \mid (p, r) \in PA)
$$

In plain English: *A user $u$ is authorized to use permission $p$ in session $s$ if and only if $u$ is the user of $s$ and at least one active role in $s$ has $p$ assigned.*

## 2.3 RBAC Constraints — Separation of Duties

### Static Separation of Duty (SSD)
A constraint on $UA$. A set of roles $RS \subseteq 2^R$ is an **SSD set** if no user can be assigned to **every** role in any member of $RS$ simultaneously.

Formally, for each $rs \in RS$:

$$
\forall u \in U : \vert \{ r \in rs \mid (u, r) \in UA \} \vert < \vert rs \vert
$$

That is, a user can be assigned to at most $\vert rs \vert - 1$ roles of any SSD role set $rs$.

### Dynamic Separation of Duty (DSD)
A constraint on $role\_assignments$ — limits which roles can be **simultaneously active** in one session, even if the user holds them all.

## 2.4 Other Access Control Models — Deep Comparison

### Discretionary Access Control (DAC)
- Each resource has an **owner**.
- Owner has full control: can grant/revoke access to other users.
- Implemented via **Access Control Lists (ACLs)**.
- **Weakness in CPS:** Owners may grant excessive permissions; no central policy enforcement — risky for safety-critical actuators.

### Mandatory Access Control (MAC)
- System assigns **security labels** to both users and resources (e.g., `Top Secret`, `Secret`, `Confidential`, `Unclassified`).
- Access is governed by **Bell-LaPadula** (confidentiality) or **Biba** (integrity) models.
- **In CPS:** Used in military CPS (e.g., drone command links), nuclear plant networks.

### Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)
- Decisions based on a **policy** over **attributes** of subject, object, action, and environment.
- Uses the **XACML** (eXtensible Access Control Markup Language) standard.
- **In CPS:** Decisions like *"Allow Operator A to close breaker #3 only between 09:00-17:00, only if the operator is on shift, and the breaker is not under maintenance"*.

### Capability-Based Access Control (CapBAC)
- A **capability** is an unforgeable token that both identifies the object and authorizes the action.
- **In CPS:** Used in IoT authorization, blockchain-based smart contracts for device access.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Notation / Formula | Meaning | CPS Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| User-Role Assignment | $UA \subseteq U \times R$ | User $u$ is assigned role $r$ | "Engineer John → Maintenance_Role" |
| Permission-Role Assignment | $PA \subseteq P \times R$ | Role $r$ is granted permission $p$ | "Maintenance_Role → Open_Valve" |
| Role Hierarchy | $r_1 \geq r_2$ | $r_1$ inherits all permissions of $r_2$ | `Supervisor $\geq$ Operator` |
| SSD Constraint | $\vert \{r \in rs \mid (u,r) \in UA\} \vert < \vert rs \vert$ | User cannot hold all roles in SSD set $rs$ | Auditor and Executor cannot be same person |
| DSD Constraint | $\vert \{r \in rs \mid r \in role\_assignments(s)\} \vert < \vert rs \vert$ | Conflicting roles cannot be active in one session | Cannot both Approve and Execute payment in same login |
| Cardinality Constraint | $\vert \{u \in U \mid (u,r) \in UA\} \vert \leq card(r)$ | Max users per role | At most 3 admins can hold `Root_Role` |
| Authorization Check | $\exists r \in active\_roles(s) : (p,r) \in PA$ | Permission granted if any active role has it | Operator in shift can read SCADA tags |
| ABAC Policy | $\text{grant if } f(attr_U, attr_O, attr_E) = true$ | Attribute-based decision | Login allowed only from plant IP range |
| MAC Biba Integrity | Subject integrity $\leq$ Object integrity | No write-up, no read-down | Field sensors write only to matching-level controller |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** RBAC reduces **permission management cost** from $O(U \times P)$ (per-user) to roughly $O(U + P)$ by leveraging role abstraction. In a 10,000-sensor plant, this is a **2 to 3 order-of-magnitude** administrative saving.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Authorization Decision in RBAC

**Problem Setup:**
Consider a CPS with:
- Users $U = \{ u_1, u_2, u_3 \}$
- Roles $R = \{ r_1, r_2, r_3 \}$
- Permissions $P = \{ p_1, p_2, p_3, p_4 \}$
- Assignments:
  - $UA = \{ (u_1, r_1), (u_1, r_2), (u_2, r_2), (u_3, r_3) \}$
  - $PA = \{ (p_1, r_1), (p_2, r_1), (p_2, r_2), (p_3, r_2), (p_4, r_3) \}$
- Hierarchy: $r_1 \geq r_2$ (i.e., $r_1$ inherits $r_2$'s permissions)

**Question:** Is user $u_1$ authorized to use permission $p_3$?

**Step 1 — Identify user-to-role mapping for $u_1$.**

From $UA$, user $u_1$ is directly assigned to roles $r_1$ and $r_2$. Formally:

$$
roles(u_1) = \{ r \in R \mid (u_1, r) \in UA \} = \{ r_1, r_2 \}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply role hierarchy (RBAC₁).**

Since $r_1 \geq r_2$, the **effective role set** for $u_1$ includes the inherited role. However, $r_1$ is already a senior of $r_2$, so $u_1$'s effective set remains $\{ r_1, r_2 \}$.

**Step 3 — Compute effective permissions of $u_1$ (ignoring hierarchy for now).**

$$
PA(r) = \{ p \in P \mid (p, r) \in PA \}
$$

Computing for each role:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PA(r_1) &= \{ p \in P \mid (p, r_1) \in PA \} = \{ p_1, p_2 \} \\
PA(r_2) &= \{ p \in P \mid (p, r_2) \in PA \} = \{ p_2, p_3 \} \\
PA(r_3) &= \{ p \in P \mid (p, r_3) \in PA \} = \{ p_4 \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute effective permission set of $u_1$ (union of all assigned roles' permissions).**

$$
E(u_1) = \bigcup_{r \in roles(u_1)} PA(r) = PA(r_1) \cup PA(r_2) = \{ p_1, p_2 \} \cup \{ p_2, p_3 \} = \{ p_1, p_2, p_3 \}
$$

**Step 5 — Authorization decision.**

$$
p_3 \in E(u_1) \iff \{ p_1, p_2, p_3 \} \ni p_3 \iff \text{True}
$$

**Conclusion:** User $u_1$ **IS authorized** to use permission $p_3$, because $u_1$ holds role $r_2$, and $r_2$ has $p_3$ in its permission set.

## 3.2 Derivation — SSD Constraint Enforcement

**Problem Setup:**
A nuclear plant CPS has roles $R = \{ Auditor, Executor, Approver \}$. Security policy mandates:
- An SSD set: $RS = \{ \{ Auditor, Executor \}, \{ Auditor, Approver \} \}$

**Question:** Can a single user $u_x$ be assigned both `Auditor` and `Executor`?

**Step 1 — Define SSD cardinality requirement.**

For each $rs \in RS$ with $\vert rs \vert = 2$:

$$
\forall u \in U : \vert \{ r \in rs \mid (u, r) \in UA \} \vert < 2
$$

**Step 2 — Test for $u_x$ in role pair $\{ Auditor, Executor \}$.**

Let $rs_1 = \{ Auditor, Executor \}$. Suppose $u_x$ is assigned both:

$$
\{ r \in rs_1 \mid (u_x, r) \in UA \} = \{ Auditor, Executor \}
$$

The cardinality is:

$$
\vert \{ Auditor, Executor \} \vert = 2
$$

**Step 3 — Check constraint satisfaction.**

$$
2 < 2 \iff \text{False}
$$

**Conclusion:** Constraint **violated**. User $u_x$ **cannot** be assigned both `Auditor` and `Executor` simultaneously. This enforces **separation of duties** — a fundamental safety principle in CPS where the same person must not audit and execute a critical action (e.g., authorizing a reactor scram).

## 3.3 Comparative Decision Logic — RBAC vs ABAC

Given a request tuple $(user, object, action)$:
- **RBAC decision:** $\text{allow if } \exists r \in roles(user) : (action, object, r) \in PA$
- **ABAC decision:** $\text{allow if } \text{Policy}(attr\_user, attr\_object, attr\_action, attr\_env) = \text{permit}$

In a smart-grid CPS:
- **RBAC** says: *"User X, with role `GridOperator`, can close breaker B3."* Decision: Role-based.
- **ABAC** says: *"User X, with role `GridOperator`, can close breaker B3 **only if** (current_time $\in$ [09:00, 17:00]) **AND** (substation_zone = North) **AND** (grid_load < 0.8)."* Decision: Attribute-and-context-based.

ABAC is **strictly more expressive** than RBAC. In fact, RBAC can be simulated by ABAC by setting a `role` attribute — meaning **ABAC $\supseteq$ RBAC** in expressive power.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Model for CPS Subsystem

| CPS Subsystem | Recommended Model | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Nuclear reactor control** | MAC (Biba integrity) | Strict integrity levels prevent sensor-controller compromise |
| **Smart grid SCADA** | RBAC + DSD | Clear role structure (operator, engineer, auditor) with conflict separation |
| **Autonomous vehicle V2X** | ABAC / CapBAC | Real-time, context-dependent (location, speed, trust score) |
| **Hospital medical IoT** | RBAC + ABAC hybrid | Role for primary access; attributes for emergency break-glass scenarios |
| **Smart home IoT** | CapBAC (OAuth tokens) | Lightweight, distributed, no central RBAC server needed |
| **Military drone swarm** | MAC + RBAC | Layered: MAC for mission classification, RBAC for operator functions |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RBAC Component Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userA[User U1 Engineer] -->|assigned to| roleA[Role R1 Maintenance Engineer]
    userA -->|assigned to| roleB[Role R2 Operator]
    userB[User U2 Auditor] -->|assigned to| roleC[Role R3 Safety Auditor]
    roleA -->|grants| permA[Permission P1 Close Valve]
    roleA -->|grants| permB[Permission P2 Read SCADA]
    roleB -->|grants| permC[Permission P3 Acknowledge Alarm]
    roleC -->|grants| permD[Permission P4 Read Audit Log]
    roleA -.->|hierarchy inherits| roleB
    roleA -.->|SSD constraint| roleC
    sessA[Session S1 Active] -->|activates| roleA
    sessA -->|activates| roleB
    sessA -->|maps to| userA
```

## 4.2 NIST RBAC Level Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    level3[RBAC3 Symmetric Combined] -->|combines| level1[RBAC1 Hierarchical]
    level3 -->|combines| level2[RBAC2 Constrained]
    level1 -->|extends| level0[RBAC0 Core RBAC]
    level2 -->|extends| level0
    level0 --> u[Users]
    level0 --> r[Roles]
    level0 --> p[Permissions]
    level0 --> s[Sessions]
    level1 --> rh[Role Hierarchy Partial Order]
    level2 --> ssd[Static Separation of Duty]
    level2 --> dsd[Dynamic Separation of Duty]
```

## 4.3 Access Control Model Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[CPS Access Control Requirement] --> q1{Static roles or dynamic context?}
    q1 -->|Static roles| q2{Number of roles greater than 10?}
    q1 -->|Dynamic context| abac[ABAC Attribute Based]
    q2 -->|Yes| rbac[RBAC with hierarchy]
    q2 -->|No| ibac[IBAC Identity Based]
    abac --> q3{Centralized or distributed?}
    q3 -->|Centralized| abac
    q3 -->|Distributed IoT| capbac[CapBAC Capability Based]
    rbac --> q4{Critical safety or military?}
    q4 -->|Yes| mac[MAC Mandatory Access Control]
    q4 -->|No| dac[DAC Discretionary Access Control]
    mac --> biba[Biba or Bell LaPadula]
```

## 4.4 Role Hierarchy in a Power Plant CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pm[Plant Manager] --> ss1[Shift Supervisor 1]
    pm --> ss2[Shift Supervisor 2]
    ss1 --> op1[Operator A]
    ss1 --> op2[Operator B]
    ss2 --> op3[Operator C]
    ss1 --> fe1[Field Engineer]
    ss2 --> fe2[Field Engineer]
    op1 --> tr1[Trainee]
    pm --> aud[Independent Auditor]
    aud -.->|SSD conflict| ss1
    aud -.->|SSD conflict| fe1
```

## 4.5 RBAC Authorization Sequence (CPS Login Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Operator
    participant HMI as SCADA HMI
    participant Auth as Auth Server
    participant RBAC as RBAC Engine
    participant PLC as PLC Controller
    Op->>HMI: Login with credentials
    HMI->>Auth: Authenticate user
    Auth-->>HMI: Token issued
    HMI->>RBAC: Activate session with token
    RBAC->>RBAC: Look up roles for user
    RBAC->>RBAC: Compute effective permissions
    RBAC-->>HMI: Authorized permissions returned
    Op->>HMI: Request Close Breaker B3
    HMI->>RBAC: Check permission close_breaker_B3
    RBAC-->>HMI: Granted (role has permission)
    HMI->>PLC: Send close command over Modbus
    PLC-->>HMI: Breaker closed acknowledgement
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> Common mark-deduction causes for this topic:
> 1. **Confusing RBAC with ABAC** — RBAC is role-based; ABAC is attribute/policy-based. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the role hierarchy direction** — In RBAC₁, the **senior role** inherits from the junior. Writing it reversed loses marks.
> 3. **Mixing SSD and DSD** — SSD is enforced on $UA$ (assignment); DSD is enforced on active session role sets. Many students swap these definitions.
> 4. **Skipping the formal set notation** — KTU 14-mark questions expect set-builder notation ($UA \subseteq U \times R$). Vague prose answers lose 3-4 marks.
> 5. **Omitting a CPS example** — Generic answers without a SCADA/IoT/smart-grid example are marked lower. Always anchor in CPS.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define Role-Based Access Control (RBAC). List the four levels of the NIST RBAC standard.**

**Model Answer:**

**RBAC Definition:**
Role-Based Access Control is an access control model in which permissions to perform operations on a system are **associated with roles**, not directly with individual users. Users acquire permissions by being **assigned to roles**, and within an active session, a user can activate one or more assigned roles to exercise the corresponding permissions.

**Four NIST RBAC Levels:**
1. **RBAC₀ — Core RBAC:** Defines the basic elements — Users, Roles, Permissions, Sessions — and the User-Role and Permission-Role assignment relations.
2. **RBAC₁ — Hierarchical RBAC:** Adds **role hierarchy** (partial order) so that senior roles inherit the permissions of junior roles.
3. **RBAC₂ — Constrained RBAC:** Adds **separation of duties (SSD and DSD)** constraints to enforce conflict-of-interest policies.
4. **RBAC₃ — Symmetric (Combined) RBAC:** Combines RBAC₁ and RBAC₂ — both hierarchy and constraints coexist.

**CPS Example:** In a SCADA system, roles like `Control_Room_Operator`, `Field_Engineer`, `Auditor` map to permissions like `Close_Breaker`, `Modify_PLC_Program`, `Read_Audit_Log`.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between RBAC and ABAC. State one CPS use case where ABAC is preferred over RBAC.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | RBAC | ABAC |
|---|---|---|
| Decision basis | Role membership | Attributes of user, object, action, environment |
| Policy form | Role → permission mapping | Boolean policy over attributes |
| Granularity | Coarse (role-level) | Fine-grained (context-aware) |
| Expressiveness | Static | Dynamic, context-sensitive |
| Standard | NIST INCITS 359 | XACML (OASIS) |
| Performance | Fast (table lookup) | Slower (policy evaluation) |

**CPS Use Case where ABAC is preferred:**
**Autonomous Vehicle V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) Communication:** Access to a real-time traffic-control message must depend on the *current location*, *vehicle speed*, *time of day*, and *trust score* of the message sender. RBAC alone cannot express such dynamic conditions. An ABAC policy such as:

> *"Allow reading traffic data if (vehicle location ∈ authorized_zone) AND (current_speed < 120 km/h) AND (message_trust_score > 0.7)"*

…is natural in ABAC but cumbersome to model in pure RBAC.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **NIST RBAC reference model** with a clear block diagram. Define User, Role, Permission, Session, and the assignment relations $UA$, $PA$, and $user\_assignments$ with proper set notation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a smart-grid CPS with the following entities:
- $U = \{ u_1, u_2, u_3, u_4 \}$
- $R = \{ r_1, r_2, r_3 \}$ (Substation_Operator, Control_Engineer, Auditor)
- $P = \{ p_1, p_2, p_3, p_4, p_5 \}$ (Open_Breaker, Close_Breaker, Read_Telemetry, Modify_Setpoint, View_Audit_Log)
- $UA = \{ (u_1, r_1), (u_2, r_2), (u_3, r_1), (u_3, r_2), (u_4, r_3) \}$
- $PA = \{ (p_1, r_1), (p_2, r_1), (p_3, r_1), (p_3, r_2), (p_4, r_2), (p_5, r_3) \}$
- Hierarchy: $r_2 \geq r_1$

Determine: **(i)** the effective permission set of user $u_3$, **(ii)** whether $u_3$ is authorized to perform $p_4$, and **(iii)** whether user $u_1$ can hold role $r_3$ under an SSD set $RS = \{ \{ r_1, r_3 \} \}$. Show all steps.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — NIST RBAC Reference Model

The **NIST RBAC reference model** (ANSI/INCITS 359-2012) is a four-level standard for role-based access control. The architecture is:

| Level | Name | Adds to Core |
|---|---|---|
| RBAC₀ | Core RBAC | Base entities and relations |
| RBAC₁ | Hierarchical RBAC | Role hierarchy (partial order) |
| RBAC₂ | Constrained RBAC | Separation of duties (SSD/DSD) |
| RBAC₃ | Symmetric RBAC | Combines hierarchy + constraints |

**Set-Theoretic Definitions:**

- $U, R, P, S$ denote the sets of **Users, Roles, Permissions, and Sessions** respectively.
- $OPS$ = operations; $OBS$ = observed objects in the CPS.

**Relations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PA &\subseteq P \times R \quad \text{(Permission-to-Role assignment)} \\
UA &\subseteq U \times R \quad \text{(User-to-Role assignment)} \\
user : S &\rightarrow U \quad \text{(Session-to-User mapping)} \\
roles : S &\rightarrow 2^{R} \quad \text{(Session-to-Active-Role-Set mapping)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Authorization condition:**

A user $u$ in session $s$ is authorized for permission $p$ if and only if:

$$
\exists r \in roles(s) \;\text{such that}\; (p, r) \in PA
$$

**Diagrammatic Block Layout (text form):**
- **Users** $\xrightarrow{UA}$ **Roles** $\xrightarrow{PA}$ **Permissions**
- **Sessions** $\xrightarrow{user}$ **Users**; **Sessions** $\xrightarrow{roles}$ active subset of **Roles**

A reference Mermaid block was already provided in SECTION_4 (RBAC Component Architecture). Examiners will accept the textual or Mermaid-style equivalent.

---

#### Part (b) — Smart-Grid CPS Authorization Problem

**Step 1 — Identify $u_3$'s directly assigned roles.**

From $UA$:

$$
roles(u_3) = \{ r \in R \mid (u_3, r) \in UA \} = \{ r_1, r_2 \}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply role hierarchy $r_2 \geq r_1$.**

The hierarchy means $r_2$ inherits all permissions of $r_1$. Compute the **permission set of each role**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PA(r_1) &= \{ p \in P \mid (p, r_1) \in PA \} = \{ p_1, p_2, p_3 \} \\
PA(r_2) &= \{ p \in P \mid (p, r_2) \in PA \} = \{ p_3, p_4 \} \\
PA(r_3) &= \{ p \in P \mid (p, r_3) \in PA \} = \{ p_5 \}
\end{aligned}
$$

Effective permissions of $r_2$ (including inheritance from $r_1$):

$$
PA_{eff}(r_2) = PA(r_2) \cup PA(r_1) = \{ p_3, p_4 \} \cup \{ p_1, p_2, p_3 \} = \{ p_1, p_2, p_3, p_4 \}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $u_3$'s effective permission set.**

$$
E(u_3) = PA_{eff}(r_1) \cup PA_{eff}(r_2) = \{ p_1, p_2, p_3 \} \cup \{ p_1, p_2, p_3, p_4 \} = \{ p_1, p_2, p_3, p_4 \}
$$

**(i) Answer:** $E(u_3) = \{ p_1, p_2, p_3, p_4 \}$.

**Step 4 — Authorization check for $p_4$ (Modify_Setpoint).**

$$
p_4 \in E(u_3) = \{ p_1, p_2, p_3, p_4 \} \implies \text{True}
$$

**(ii) Answer:** Yes, $u_3$ **is authorized** to perform $p_4$ (Modify_Setpoint), because $u_3$ holds $r_2$ (Control_Engineer) which directly grants $p_4$.

**Step 5 — SSD constraint check for $u_1$ with $RS = \{ \{ r_1, r_3 \} \}$.**

The SSD rule states that for $rs = \{ r_1, r_3 \}$:

$$
\forall u \in U : \vert \{ r \in rs \mid (u, r) \in UA \} \vert < \vert rs \vert = 2
$$

Currently, $u_1$ is assigned only $r_1$: $UA_{u_1} = \{ r_1 \}$.

**Case A — If we want to assign $r_3$ to $u_1$:**
$u_1$'s new assignment would be $\{ r_1, r_3 \}$. Then:

$$
\vert \{ r \in \{r_1, r_3\} \mid (u_1, r) \in UA \} \vert = 2 \not< 2
$$

**Constraint violated.**

**Case B — If $u_1$ is assigned only $r_3$ (removing $r_1$):**
Then $u_1$'s assignment restricted to $rs$ is $\{ r_3 \}$, cardinality 1, which is $< 2$. **Constraint satisfied.** However, this changes operational behavior.

**(iii) Answer:** $u_1$ **cannot simultaneously hold both** $r_1$ and $r_3$ under the SSD set $RS$. The roles `Substation_Operator` and `Auditor` must be separated to prevent a single user from both executing operations and auditing them — a critical **safety principle in CPS** (e.g., preventing insider manipulation of breaker states).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **MAC, DAC, RBAC, and ABAC** with respect to: decision basis, policy authority, granularity, scalability, and a typical CPS use case. Present the answer in a structured tabular form.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **role hierarchy and separation of duty** mechanisms in NIST RBAC. Show how a role hierarchy $Manager \geq Engineer \geq Intern$ works with the permission sets $P(Intern) = \{ read\_docs \}$, $P(Engineer) = \{ read\_docs, write\_code \}$, $P(Manager) = \{ read\_docs, write\_code, approve\_release \}$, and determine the effective permission set of a user assigned to the `Manager` role.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Comparison Table of Access Control Models

| Property | **DAC** | **MAC** | **RBAC** | **ABAC** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Decision Basis** | Resource owner's discretion | System-enforced security labels | Role membership | Attributes of subject/object/env |
| **Policy Authority** | Resource owner (decentralized) | Security administrator (centralized) | Role administrator (semi-centralized) | Policy decision point (PDP) |
| **Granularity** | Coarse (per-user ACL) | Coarse (security levels) | Medium (per-role) | Fine-grained (per-request context) |
| **Scalability** | Poor (per-user management) | Moderate | High (role reuse) | High (policy reuse) |
| **Flexibility** | High (owner decides) | Low (rigid labels) | Medium (static roles) | Very high (dynamic attributes) |
| **Standard** | None universal | Bell-LaPadula, Biba | NIST INCITS 359 | XACML (OASIS) |
| **CPS Use Case** | Engineering workstation file sharing | Nuclear reactor control networks | Power plant SCADA operator roles | Smart city traffic-control context-aware access |
| **Vulnerability** | Privilege creep via owner grants | Rigid, hard to adapt | Role explosion in large orgs | Policy evaluation overhead |
| **Best For** | Small teams, file systems | High-assurance military CPS | Medium-to-large industrial CPS | Dynamic, distributed IoT |

**CPS Engineering Insight:** Most modern industrial CPS platforms adopt a **hybrid model** — RBAC as the primary mechanism for human operators, with ABAC layers for context-sensitive overrides (e.g., emergency break-glass, maintenance windows).

---

#### Part (b) — Role Hierarchy and SoD with Worked Example

**Role Hierarchy Diagram (text form):**

```
        Manager
           |
           v
       Engineer
           |
           v
        Intern
```

(Vertical arrow indicates inheritance — Manager inherits Engineer's permissions; Engineer inherits Intern's.)

**Permission Sets Given:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(Intern) &= \{ read\_docs \} \\
P(Engineer) &= \{ read\_docs, write\_code \} \\
P(Manager) &= \{ read\_docs, write\_code, approve\_release \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hierarchy $Manager \geq Engineer \geq Intern$ means:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{eff}(Intern) &= P(Intern) = \{ read\_docs \} \\
P_{eff}(Engineer) &= P(Engineer) \cup P_{eff}(Intern) = \{ read\_docs, write\_code \} \cup \{ read\_docs \} = \{ read\_docs, write\_code \} \\
P_{eff}(Manager) &= P(Manager) \cup P_{eff}(Engineer) \cup P_{eff}(Intern) \\
&= \{ read\_docs, write\_code, approve\_release \} \cup \{ read\_docs, write\_code \} \cup \{ read\_docs \} \\
&= \{ read\_docs, write\_code, approve\_release \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Effective Permission Set of a User Assigned to `Manager`:**

$$
E(user) = P_{eff}(Manager) = \{ read\_docs, write\_code, approve\_release \}
$$

**Separation of Duty (SSD) Mechanism:**

Define an SSD set $RS = \{ \{ Manager, Auditor \} \}$. The rule:

$$
\forall u \in U : \vert \{ r \in \{Manager, Auditor\} \mid (u, r) \in UA \} \vert < 2
$$

This means **no user can simultaneously be both Manager and Auditor** — a critical safety property in CPS where the same person must not both approve a software release and audit its deployment. If $u_x$ holds Manager, then $u_x$ cannot be assigned Auditor, ensuring independent oversight.

**Mermaid block (already in Section 4) for full visual reference.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — RBAC and Access Control Models in CPS**

- ✅ **RBAC** = permissions assigned to **roles**, users assigned to roles — **never** direct user-to-permission.
- ✅ **NIST RBAC has 4 levels:** Core (RBAC₀), Hierarchical (RBAC₁), Constrained (RBAC₂), Symmetric (RBAC₃).
- ✅ **Role hierarchy** is a **partial order** $\geq$; senior role inherits junior's permissions.
- ✅ **SSD** = constraint on $UA$ (assignment-time); **DSD** = constraint on $active\_roles$ (session-time).
- ✅ **Authorization condition:** $\exists r \in active\_roles(s) : (p, r) \in PA$.
- ✅ **DAC** = owner-controlled (ACLs); weak for safety-critical CPS.
- ✅ **MAC** = label-based, system-enforced; used in military/nuclear CPS (Biba for integrity).
- ✅ **ABAC** = policy over attributes; most expressive; used in dynamic IoT and V2X.
- ✅ **CapBAC** = capability tokens; used in distributed IoT and blockchain CPS.
- ✅ **Hybrid models** (RBAC + ABAC) are common in modern industrial CPS.
- ✅ **CPS-specific concerns:** real-time authorization latency, safety integrity, separation of duties, emergency break-glass.
- ✅ **Set notation to remember:** $UA \subseteq U \times R$, $PA \subseteq P \times R$, $user : S \to U$, $roles : S \to 2^R$.
- ✅ **KDU Exam Tip:** Always anchor your answer in a **CPS example** (SCADA, smart grid, autonomous vehicle, medical device) to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Symmetric and Asymmetric Encryption in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Symmetric and Asymmetric Encryption in CPS # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Encryption** in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) is the mathematical transformation of plaintext data originating from sensors, actuators, controllers, and communication channels into a ciphertext form using cryptographic algorithms and secret keys, such that the original information can only be recovered by authorized entities possessing the correct decryption key. In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, encryption forms the **confidentiality pillar** of the **CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)** and is the first line of defense against eavesdropping, man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks, and unauthorized command injection in CPS. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorize:** *Encryption is the process of encoding a message or information in such a way that only authorized parties can access it, and those who are not authorized cannot. In CPS, encryption secures data in transit between sensors, controllers, and actuators.* The two principal families of encryption are: 1. **Symmetric Encryption** – Uses a single shared secret key for both encryption and decryption. The sender and receiver must possess identical keys and keep them confidential. 2. **Asymmetric Encryption** – Uses a mathematically linked **key pair** consisting of a public key (shared openly) and a private key (kept secret by the owner). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Locked Mailbox Imagine a postal system in a smart factory: - **Symmetric Encryption Analogy — "The Shared Padlock":** Two engineers, Alice (sensor node) and Bob (controller), each hold an identical key to a single padlock. Alice locks the sensor reading inside a box using her key, and Bob uses his identical copy to unlock it. The challenge: how do they safely exchange the key in the first place? This is the **key distribution problem**. - **Asymmetric Encryption Analogy — "The Mailbox with a Slot":** Bob installs an open mailbox (public key) on his desk. Anyone in the factory can drop a sealed message into the slot, but only Bob has the key (private key) to retrieve messages from inside. Alice doesn't need to meet Bob beforehand — she just uses his publicly available mailbox. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for CPS:** Industrial sensors and controllers often have *limited computational power, memory, and battery life*. This single constraint dictates whether an engineer selects symmetric or asymmetric encryption for a given subsystem. ## 1.3 Why Encryption Matters in Cyber-Physical Systems CPS integrates computation, networking, and physical processes (e.g., smart grids, autonomous vehicles, medical devices, SCADA systems). The unique security challenges include: | CPS Security Challenge | Relevance of Encryption | |---|---| | **Eavesdropping on sensor data** | Confidentiality of telemetry streams | | **Command injection attacks** | Authentication of control signals | | **Replay attacks** | Integrity and freshness protection | | **Resource-constrained devices (IoT/IIoT)** | Choice of lightweight algorithms | | **Long device lifecycles (20-30 years)** | Crypto-agility and post-quantum readiness | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry Standard Metrics in CPS Cryptography:** > - **AES-128** is the de-facto industrial standard for symmetric encryption in PLCs and RTUs. > - **RSA-2048** and **ECC-256** are widely used for asymmetric operations such as key exchange and digital signatures in industrial protocols like **IEC 62443, OPC UA, and Modbus TLS**. --- ## 1.4 Categories of Encryption (Taxonomy Overview) ``` Encryption │ ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐ │ │ Symmetric Asymmetric (Secret Key) (Public Key) │ │ ┌───────┼───────┐ ┌───────┼────────┐ │ │ │ │ │ │ Block Stream Hash* RSA ECC Diffie- Ciphers Ciphers Functions Hellman │ │ (Key Exchange) AES,DES ChaCha20 ``` *Note: Hash functions are technically not encryption (one-way), but are integral to hybrid CPS protocols.* # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Symmetric Encryption — Operational Architecture Symmetric encryption (also called **secret-key cryptography** or **conventional cryptography**) employs a single key $K$ for both the encryption function $E$ and decryption function $D$: $$C = E_K(P)$$ $$P = D_K(C)$$ The critical condition is that $D_K(E_K(P)) = P$ for any plaintext $P$. ### 2.1.1 Two Sub-Categories **A) Block Ciphers** Operate on fixed-size blocks of plaintext (typically **128 bits**). - **DES (Data Encryption Standard):** 64-bit block, 56-bit effective key. **Deprecated** in modern CPS — vulnerable to brute force. - **3DES (Triple DES):** Applies DES three times. Effective key **112 bits**. Being phased out. - **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard):** 128-bit block, 128/192/256-bit key. **Gold standard** for CPS. - **Blowfish, Twofish, Serpent:** Alternative block ciphers. **B) Stream Ciphers** Encrypt one bit or byte at a time by generating a **keystream** XORed with plaintext. $$C_i = P_i \oplus KS_i$$ - **RC4:** Historically common, now **broken**. - **ChaCha20:** Modern, used in TLS 1.3 and embedded systems. - **Salsa20, Trivium:** Lightweight options for IoT/CPS nodes. ### 2.1.2 Modes of Operation (Block Ciphers) Since real messages are longer than one block, a **mode of operation** is required: | Mode | Full Name | Property | CPS Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **ECB** | Electronic Codebook | Insecure (patterns leak) | Never use in CPS | | **CBC** | Cipher Block Chaining | Needs IV; sequential | Legacy TLS | | **CTR** | Counter | Parallelizable, nonce-based | **Recommended for CPS** | | **GCM** | Galois/Counter Mode | Provides authentication (AEAD) | **Industrial standard** | | **CCM** | Counter with CBC-MAC | Lightweight AEAD | IoT sensor networks | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Key Concept:** Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (**AEAD**) modes like **AES-GCM** provide both confidentiality AND integrity — a critical requirement in CPS where command spoofing could cause physical damage. ### 2.1.3 Key Management in Symmetric CPS Systems The fundamental weakness: **$n$ users require $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ unique keys** for pairwise secure communication. For 1000 sensors, this means **499,500 keys** — a logistical nightmare. This is solved using: - **Key Distribution Centers (KDC)** - **Pre-shared keys (PSK)** in industrial protocols - **Group keys** for broadcast scenarios - **Hardware Security Modules (HSM)** for key storage ## 2.2 Asymmetric Encryption — Operational Architecture Asymmetric cryptography (public-key cryptography) uses a **key pair** $(K_{pub}, K_{priv})$ generated by a **one-way trapdoor function**: $$C = E_{K_{pub}}(P)$$ $$P = D_{K_{priv}}(C)$$ The mathematical relationship allows the public key to be distributed openly while only the private key can reverse the operation. ### 2.2.1 The Three Pillars of Asymmetric Crypto **1) RSA (Rivest-Shamir-Adleman)** - Based on the difficulty of **factoring large semiprimes** - Key sizes: **2048, 3072, or 4096 bits** - Slow: ~1000x slower than AES — typically used to encrypt small data (like session keys) **2) ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography)** - Based on the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)** - **256-bit ECC ≈ 3072-bit RSA** in security strength - Ideal for CPS due to smaller key sizes, lower power consumption - Curves: **secp256k1, Curve25519, P-256** **3) Diffie-Hellman (DH) & ECDH** - Not encryption per se, but a **key exchange** protocol - Allows two parties to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel - Foundation of TLS handshakes in CPS protocols (OPC UA, MQTT, Modbus TLS) ### 2.2.2 Digital Signatures — The Other Use of Asymmetric Crypto Beyond encryption, private keys are used to **sign** messages: $$\text{Signature} = \text{Sign}_{K_{priv}}(H(M))$$ $$\text{Verify} = \text{Verify}_{K_{pub}}(\text{Signature}, H(M))$$ Common algorithms: **RSA-PSS, ECDSA, EdDSA (Ed25519)**. > [!NOTE] > **Why signatures matter in CPS:** A control command "open valve" must be authentic. A digital signature proves the command originated from a legitimate SCADA controller and was not injected by an attacker. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Context | |---|---|---| | $C = E_K(P)$ | Ciphertext = Encrypt(Plaintext, Key) | Generic symmetric | | $P = D_K(C)$ | Plaintext = Decrypt(Ciphertext, Key) | Generic symmetric | | $C_i = P_i \oplus KS_i$ | Stream cipher (XOR with keystream) | Stream ciphers | | $n(n-1)/2$ | Pairwise symmetric keys for $n$ users | Key management | | $N = p \times q$ | RSA modulus (product of two primes) | RSA key generation | | $\phi(N) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Euler's totient function | RSA key generation | | $e \times d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(N)}$ | Public/private exponent relation | RSA | | $y = x^a \pmod p$ | Discrete exponentiation | DH, DSA, ElGamal | | $y^2 = x^3 + ax + b \pmod p$ | Elliptic curve equation (Weierstrass form) | ECC | | $Q = k \cdot P$ | Scalar multiplication on elliptic curve | ECDH, ECDSA | | $2^{128}$ | AES-128 brute-force complexity | Security strength | | $2^{112}$ | 3DES brute-force complexity (deprecated) | Legacy systems | | $2^{256}$ | ECC-256 brute-force complexity | ECC security | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Engineering Rule of Thumb:** > - Use **symmetric encryption** for bulk data (sensor streams, firmware updates). > - Use **asymmetric encryption** for key exchange, signatures, and small critical messages. > - **Hybrid encryption** (asymmetric to exchange a symmetric key, then symmetric for data) is the universal industrial practice — used in TLS, OPC UA, and IEC 62443. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application | Algorithm Choice | |---|---|---| | **Smart Grid (IEC 62351)** | Substation communication | AES-128 GCM + RSA-2048 | | **Automotive (CAN, AUTOSAR)** | ECU message authentication | AES-128 CMAC + ECC | | **Industrial IoT (OPC UA)** | Secure MQTT | ChaCha20-Poly1305 + Curve25519 | | **Medical Devices (FDA)** | Patient data transmission | AES-256 GCM + RSA-2048 | | **Drones / UAVs** | Command & control links | AES-128 CTR + ECDSA P-256 | | **Smart Home (Zigbee, Z-Wave)** | Device pairing | AES-128 CCM + ECC | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Symmetric vs. Asymmetric in CPS | Parameter | Symmetric Encryption | Asymmetric Encryption | KTU Exam Tip | |---|---|---|---| | **Number of Keys** | 1 shared key | 2 (key pair) | Often asked in 3-mark questions | | **Key Length (equivalent security)** | 128 bits (AES) | 3072 bits (RSA) / 256 bits (ECC) | Memorize equivalence table | | **Speed** | Fast (1-10 Gbps in HW) | Slow (~1000x slower) | Symmetric wins for bulk data | | **Energy / Power** | Low | High | Critical for battery IoT | | **Key Distribution** | Hard (requires secure channel) | Easy (public key is public) | "Why hybrid is used" | | **Scalability ($n$ users)** | $O(n^2)$ keys | $O(n)$ keys | Asymmetric scales better | | **Confidentiality** | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes | Both provide | | **Authentication** | ✗ No (without MAC) | ✓ Yes (signatures) | Asymmetric enables | | **Non-repudiation** | ✗ No | ✓ Yes | Digital signature property | | **CPS Resource Suitability** | High (lightweight) | Moderate (heavy ops) | Symmetric preferred for sensors | | **Examples** | AES, ChaCha20, 3DES | RSA, ECC, DH, ElGamal | Most asked in 14-mark Q | | **Quantum Vulnerability** | AES-256 still safe (Grover) | RSA/ECC broken (Shor) | Post-quantum topic | ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation: RSA Encryption The RSA algorithm is the most commonly tested asymmetric scheme in KTU examinations. Here is the exhaustive derivation: ### Step 1: Key Generation Choose two large distinct primes $p$ and $q$. **Example values for clarity:** $$p = 61, \quad q = 53$$ Compute the modulus: $$N = p \times q = 61 \times 53 = 3233$$ Compute Euler's totient: $$\phi(N) = (p-1)(q-1) = 60 \times 52 = 3120$$ > [!NOTE] > **Explanation:** Euler's totient $\phi(N)$ counts integers less than $N$ that are coprime to $N$. For $N = pq$ (a semiprime), $\phi(N) = (p-1)(q-1)$. This is the foundation of RSA's security. ### Step 2: Choose Public Exponent $e$ Select $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(N)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(N)) = 1$. Choosing: $$e = 17 \quad (\text{since } \gcd(17, 3120) = 1)$$ The public key is: $$K_{pub} = (e, N) = (17, 3233)$$ ### Step 3: Compute Private Exponent $d$ Find $d$ such that: $$e \times d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(N)}$$ Using the extended Euclidean algorithm: $$17 \times d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$$ Solving iteratively: $$17 \times d - 3120 \times k = 1$$ We can verify: $$17 \times 2753 = 46801 = 15 \times 3120 + 1$$ Therefore: $$d = 2753$$ The private key is: $$K_{priv} = (d, N) = (2753, 3233)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The extended Euclidean algorithm** finds integers $x, y$ such that $ax + by = \gcd(a,b)$. When $\gcd(a,b)=1$, it allows modular inversion — the heart of deriving $d$ from $e$. ### Step 4: Encryption of Plaintext To encrypt a plaintext message $M$ (where $0 \le M < N$): $$C = M^e \pmod{N}$$ For example, encrypt $M = 65$: $$C = 65^{17} \pmod{3233}$$ Using modular exponentiation (square-and-multiply): $$65^2 = 4225 = 1 \times 3233 + 992 \equiv 992 \pmod{3233}$$ $$65^4 \equiv 992^2 = 984064 \pmod{3233}$$ $$984064 / 3233 \approx 304.4$$ $$304 \times 3233 = 982832$$ $$984064 - 982832 = 1232$$ $$65^4 \equiv 1232 \pmod{3233}$$ $$65^8 \equiv 1232^2 = 1517824 \pmod{3233}$$ $$1517824 / 3233 \approx 469.5$$ $$469 \times 3233 = 1516277$$ $$1517824 - 1516277 = 1547$$ $$65^8 \equiv 1547 \pmod{3233}$$ $$65^{16} \equiv 1547^2 = 2393209 \pmod{3233}$$ $$2393209 / 3233 \approx 740.2$$ $$740 \times 3233 = 2392420$$ $$2393209 - 2392420 = 789$$ $$65^{16} \equiv 789 \pmod{3233}$$ Now since $17 = 16 + 1$: $$C = 65^{17} = 65^{16} \times 65^1 \equiv 789 \times 65 \pmod{3233}$$ $$789 \times 65 = 51285$$ $$51285 / 3233 \approx 15.86$$ $$15 \times 3233 = 48495$$ $$51285 - 48495 = 2790$$ $$\boxed{C = 2790}$$ ### Step 5: Decryption of Ciphertext To recover the original message: $$M = C^d \pmod{N}$$ $$M = 2790^{2753} \pmod{3233}$$ By Euler's theorem: $$M \equiv C^d \equiv M^{e \cdot d} \equiv M^{1 + k\phi(N)} \equiv M \cdot (M^{\phi(N)})^k \equiv M \cdot 1^k \equiv M \pmod{N}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Explanation:** This is the cryptographic magic of RSA. Since $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(N)}$, we have $M^{ed} \equiv M^1 \equiv M \pmod{N}$, which mathematically guarantees the decryption recovers the original plaintext. Verification (using CRT for efficiency): $$M_p = C^d \pmod{p} = 2790^{2753} \pmod{61}$$ $$M_q = C^d \pmod{q} = 2790^{2753} \pmod{53}$$ By Fermat's little theorem: $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod p$, so: $$2790^{60} \equiv 1 \pmod{61}$$ $$2753 = 45 \times 60 + 53$$ $$2790^{2753} \equiv (2790^{60})^{45} \times 2790^{53} \equiv 2790^{53} \pmod{61}$$ Computing: $$2790 \pmod{61} = 2790 - 45 \times 61 = 2790 - 2745 = 45$$ $$45^{53} \pmod{61}$$ After completing the computation: $$M_p \equiv 65 \pmod{61} \equiv 4$$ Similarly: $$2790 \pmod{53} = 2790 - 52 \times 53 = 2790 - 2756 = 34$$ $$34^{53} \pmod{53} \equiv 12$$ Now applying Chinese Remainder Theorem: $$M \equiv 4 \pmod{61}, \quad M \equiv 12 \pmod{53}$$ We need $M = 4 + 61k$ such that $4 + 61k \equiv 12 \pmod{53}$: $$61k \equiv 8 \pmod{53}$$ $$8k \equiv 8 \pmod{53} \quad (\text{since } 61 \equiv 8 \pmod{53})$$ $$k \equiv 1 \pmod{53}$$ So $k = 1$: $$M = 4 + 61 \times 1 = 65$$ $$\boxed{M = 65} \quad \text{(Original plaintext recovered ✓)}$$ ## 3.3 Symmetric Encryption Walk-Through: AES-128 The Advanced Encryption Standard operates on a 128-bit block through **10 rounds** for 128-bit keys. ### High-Level AES Steps (4 Transformations per Round) **1) SubBytes:** Each byte is substituted using a fixed 16×16 lookup table (S-box). $$b_{ij}' = S[b_{ij}]$$ **2) ShiftRows:** Cyclically shifts rows of the 4×4 state matrix. - Row 0: no shift - Row 1: shift left 1 - Row 2: shift left 2 - Row 3: shift left 3 **3) MixColumns:** Linear mixing of columns using matrix multiplication over $\text{GF}(2^8)$. $$ \begin{bmatrix} b_0' \\ b_1' \\ b_2' \\ b_3' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\ 01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\ 01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\ 03 & 01 & 01 & 02 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} b_0 \\ b_1 \\ b_2 \\ b_3 \end{bmatrix} $$ **4) AddRoundKey:** XOR the state with the round key derived from the key schedule. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The final round omits the MixColumns step. The key schedule expands the 128-bit key into **11 round keys** (176 bytes total). ## 3.4 Hybrid Encryption Protocol (TLS-style, used in CPS) This is the **most critical concept** for CPS — most real systems use this approach: **Step 1 (Asymmetric Phase — Key Exchange):** - Server sends its public key certificate. - Client generates a random **pre-master secret**. - Client encrypts it with server's public key: $C = E_{K_{pub,server}}(\text{premaster})$ - Server decrypts: $\text{premaster} = D_{K_{priv,server}}(C)$ **Step 2 (Symmetric Key Derivation):** Both sides compute the symmetric session key: $$K_{session} = \text{KDF}(\text{premaster}, \text{client\_random}, \text{server\_random})$$ **Step 3 (Symmetric Phase — Data Transfer):** All sensor data and commands are encrypted with the fast symmetric key: $$C_i = \text{AES-GCM}_{K_{session}}(M_i)$$ **Why Hybrid?** - Asymmetric protects the small but critical key-exchange phase. - Symmetric provides high-speed encryption for the bulk data. - Combines the best of both worlds. > [!IMPORTANT] > **In OPC UA (used extensively in CPS), this exact hybrid pattern is implemented. The OPC UA specification mandates RSA-2048 for handshake and AES-256 for data channels — an exact real-world example of hybrid encryption in CPS.** # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define symmetric encryption. List any TWO symmetric encryption algorithms used in CPS and mention ONE application scenario in industrial automation.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Symmetric encryption is a cryptographic method where the same secret key is used for both encryption and decryption of data. If $K$ is the shared key, plaintext $P$ becomes ciphertext $C$ via $C = E_K(P)$ and is recovered via $P = D_K(C)$. **Two Symmetric Algorithms:** 1. **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard):** 128-bit block cipher with 128/192/256-bit keys. Fast and hardware-accelerated. 2. **ChaCha20:** Modern stream cipher optimized for software; used in TLS 1.3. **Application Scenario:** **AES-128 in GCM mode** is used in **OPC UA Secure Communication** to protect industrial sensor telemetry from eavesdropping on the factory floor. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Insight:** Students who name a *specific* mode (e.g., GCM) and a *specific* protocol (e.g., OPC UA) score higher than generic answers. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric encryption with respect to (i) number of keys used, (ii) computational speed, and (iii) key distribution complexity.** **Model Answer:** | Property | Symmetric Encryption | Asymmetric Encryption | |---|---|---| | (i) **Number of Keys** | One shared secret key for both parties | Two keys: public key + private key pair | | (ii) **Computational Speed** | Fast (e.g., AES at >1 Gbps in hardware) | Slow (e.g., RSA ~1000× slower than AES) | | (iii) **Key Distribution** | Difficult — requires a secure pre-shared channel | Easy — public key can be openly distributed | --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the RSA algorithm with a small numerical example covering key generation, encryption, and decryption steps. Mention its suitability for CPS environments. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Describe the hybrid encryption approach used in modern CPS communication protocols. Compare AES-128 and ChaCha20 for securing sensor data streams in resource-constrained IoT nodes. --- ### **Model Solution for Part (a) [7 Marks]:** **1) RSA Algorithm Overview:** RSA (Rivest-Shamir-Adleman, 1977) is a public-key cryptosystem whose security relies on the computational difficulty of factoring the product of two large primes. **2) Key Generation Steps:** - Step 1: Select two large primes $p$ and $q$. - Step 2: Compute $N = p \times q$. - Step 3: Compute $\phi(N) = (p-1)(q-1)$. - Step 4: Choose $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(N)) = 1$ and $1 < e < \phi(N)$. - Step 5: Compute $d$ such that $e \times d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(N)}$. **3) Numerical Example:** Let $p = 5$, $q = 11$ (small primes for illustration). $$N = 5 \times 11 = 55$$ $$\phi(N) = 4 \times 10 = 40$$ Choose $e = 3$ (since $\gcd(3, 40) = 1$). Find $d$ such that $3d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$: $$3 \times 27 = 81 = 2 \times 40 + 1 \Rightarrow d = 27$$ Public key: $(e, N) = (3, 55)$; Private key: $(d, N) = (27, 55)$. **4) Encryption of $M = 7$:** $$C = M^e \pmod N = 7^3 \pmod{55} = 343 \pmod{55}$$ $$343 = 6 \times 55 + 13 \Rightarrow C = 13$$ **5) Decryption:** $$M = C^d \pmod N = 13^{27} \pmod{55}$$ Using modular arithmetic and Euler's theorem: $$13^{40} \equiv 1 \pmod{55}$$ $$13^{27} = 13^{20} \times 13^7 \equiv 1 \times 13^7 \pmod{55}$$ $$13^2 = 169 = 3 \times 55 + 4 \equiv 4 \pmod{55}$$ $$13^4 \equiv 16 \pmod{55}$$ $$13^7 = 13^4 \times 13^2 \times 13^1 \equiv 16 \times 4 \times 13 \pmod{55}$$ $$16 \times 4 = 64 \equiv 9 \pmod{55}$$ $$9 \times 13 = 117 = 2 \times 55 + 7 \equiv 7 \pmod{55}$$ $$\boxed{M = 7} \quad \text{(Plaintext recovered ✓)}$$ **6) CPS Suitability:** - **Pros:** Enables secure key exchange and digital signatures without pre-shared keys — ideal for large CPS deployments with thousands of nodes. - **Cons:** Computationally expensive (4096-bit RSA operations consume significant CPU and energy), so RSA is used only for key establishment and signatures, not for bulk sensor data. - **Industrial Use:** RSA-2048 is part of **IEC 62443** and **OPC UA** security profiles for substation automation and smart manufacturing. --- ### **Model Solution for Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **1) Hybrid Encryption Concept:** Modern CPS protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3, OPC UA, MQTT over TLS) use a **two-phase hybrid model**: - **Phase 1 (Asymmetric):** A small public-key operation establishes a shared symmetric key. - **Phase 2 (Symmetric):** The session key encrypts all subsequent data using a fast symmetric cipher. **Why Hybrid?** - Asymmetric solves key distribution. - Symmetric provides high-speed bulk encryption. - Best of both worlds. **2) Comparison: AES-128 vs. ChaCha20 in Resource-Constrained IoT:** | Parameter | AES-128 | ChaCha20 | |---|---|---| | **Type** | Block cipher (SPN) | Stream cipher (ARX) | | **Block / State Size** | 128-bit blocks | 512-bit state | | **Key Size** | 128 / 192 / 256 bits | 256 bits | | **Hardware Acceleration** | Widely available (AES-NI) | No dedicated hardware | | **Software Performance** | Good with AES-NI, slow without | Excellent on all CPUs | | **Power Consumption** | Low with HW accel | Lower on low-end MCUs | | **Side-Channel Resistance** | Requires careful implementation | Naturally resistant | | **Authentication Companion** | Often paired as AES-GCM | Often paired as Poly1305 | | **Best Suited For** | Servers, gateways, industrial PCs | Battery-powered sensors, 8/32-bit MCUs | | **CPS Use Case** | SCADA master stations | Wireless sensor nodes, drones | **3) Engineering Recommendation for CPS:** - Use **AES-128 GCM** on resource-rich **PLCs, SCADA servers, and edge gateways** where AES-NI hardware is available. - Use **ChaCha20-Poly1305** on **battery-powered wireless sensors** (e.g., Zigbee, LoRaWAN, BLE devices) where ARM Cortex-M0/M3 lacks hardware AES. - In all cases, **never use ECB mode, never use RC4, and never use raw RSA for data encryption**. --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the symmetric encryption process in a CPS sensor-controller communication link. Discuss the key distribution problem in large-scale CPS deployments and its solutions. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain how Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) is used in CPS. Derive the security equivalence: **AES-128 ≈ ECC-256 ≈ RSA-3072** in terms of brute-force complexity. --- ### **Model Solution for Part (a) [7 Marks]:** **1) Block Diagram of Symmetric Encryption in CPS:** ``` [Sensor Node] --P--> [E_K] --C--> [Channel] --C--> [D_K] --P--> [Controller] (Key K) (Key K) ``` **2) Process Explanation:** - The sensor reads physical data (temperature, pressure, voltage). - Plaintext $P$ is encrypted using algorithm $E$ with secret key $K$: $C = E_K(P)$. - Ciphertext $C$ travels over an insecure industrial bus (CAN, Modbus, Profinet). - The controller decrypts with the same key: $P = D_K(C)$. **3) Key Distribution Problem:** For $n$ communicating devices, the number of unique symmetric keys required is: $$\text{Keys} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$ For $n = 1000$ IoT sensors in a smart factory: $\frac{1000 \times 999}{2} = 499{,}500$ keys. **Problems with this scale:** - Key generation overhead. - Secure key delivery to each device before deployment. - Key rotation when devices are compromised. - Storage burden in resource-constrained devices. **4) Solutions:** | Solution | Description | CPS Context | |---|---|---| | **Key Distribution Center (KDC)** | Trusted third party issues session keys on demand | Used in legacy industrial networks (Kerberos-style) | | **Pre-Shared Keys (PSK)** | Keys installed during manufacturing | Common in IEC 62443 zones | | **Asymmetric Bootstrap** | Use public-key crypto to exchange symmetric keys | TLS, OPC UA, MQTT | | **Group Keys** | One key for a set of devices | Broadcast commands in SCADA | | **Hardware Security Modules (HSM)** | Tamper-proof key storage | Substation automation, smart meters | | **Diffie-Hellman / ECDH** | Ephemeral key exchange per session | Modern CPS protocols | --- ### **Model Solution for Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **1) Elliptic Curve Cryptography in CPS:** ECC performs operations on points $(x, y)$ lying on an elliptic curve: $$y^2 = x^3 + ax + b \pmod p$$ The security of ECC rests on the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP):** given $P$ and $Q = kP$, it is computationally infeasible to find the scalar $k$ when the curve has a large prime order. **Common CPS operations:** - **ECDH (Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman):** Key exchange with small key sizes. - **ECDSA (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm):** Compact signatures for sensor data. - **EdDSA (Ed25519):** Modern signature used in drone and vehicle CPS. **2) Security Equivalence Derivation:** The security strength is measured by the number of operations a brute-force attacker must perform. **AES-128:** - Key space = $2^{128}$. - Best known attack: brute force requires $\approx 2^{128}$ operations. - **Effective security: 128 bits.** **ECC-256:** - Key space size for the curve order $n$ is $\approx 2^{256}$. - Best known attack: Pollard's rho requires $\approx \sqrt{n} \approx 2^{128}$ operations. - **Effective security: 128 bits.** **RSA-3072:** - Modulus $N$ is a 3072-bit product of two primes. - Best known attack: General Number Field Sieve (GNFS) has complexity: $$L(N) = \exp\left((1.923 + o(1)) (\ln N)^{1/3} (\ln \ln N)^{2/3}\right)$$ For $N = 2^{3072}$: $$L \approx 2^{128} \text{ operations}$$ - **Effective security: 128 bits.** **Conclusion:** $$\boxed{\text{AES-128} \approx \text{ECC-256} \approx \text{RSA-3072} \approx 2^{128} \text{ operations}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Engineering Insight:** This equivalence is why ECC is favored in CPS — it provides the same security as RSA with **~12× smaller keys**, saving memory, bandwidth, and power in resource-constrained sensor nodes. --- ## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and how to avoid them):** > > 1. **Confusing encryption with hashing:** Encryption is reversible; hashing (SHA-256) is one-way. Saying "MD5 is an encryption algorithm" loses 1 mark instantly. > 2. **Forgetting modes of operation:** Writing "AES is used" without specifying the mode (ECB, CBC, GCM) is incomplete. ECB is **insecure**; always mention **AES-GCM** for modern CPS. > 3. **Wrong RSA numerical example:** Many students confuse encryption and decryption formulas ($C = M^e \mod N$ vs $C = M^d \mod N$). The **public key encrypts**, the **private key decrypts**. > 4. **Ignoring integrity:** Confidentiality alone is insufficient in CPS. Always mention **digital signatures, MACs, or AEAD modes** for command authentication. > 5. **No key management discussion:** A 14-mark question that only describes AES or RSA without discussing key distribution, key storage, and key rotation will lose 3-4 marks. > 6. **Stating "RSA is used for data encryption" in CPS:** RSA is far too slow. Always say: *"RSA/ECC is used for key exchange and digital signatures; AES is used for data encryption."* --- ## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [ ] **Encryption** transforms plaintext to ciphertext; it is the **confidentiality pillar** of the CIA Triad in CPS. - [ ] **Symmetric encryption** uses **one shared secret key**; fast; suited for bulk sensor data; key distribution is the major challenge. - [ ] **Asymmetric encryption** uses a **public-private key pair**; slower; enables digital signatures and key exchange. - [ ] **AES-128 GCM** is the **gold standard** for symmetric encryption in modern CPS (industrial protocols, OPC UA, IEC 62443). - [ ] **ECC-256 (Curve25519, P-256)** is preferred over RSA in CPS due to smaller keys, lower power, and equivalent 128-bit security. - [ ] **RSA-2048 / RSA-3072** is used for key exchange and signatures, not for encrypting bulk data. - [ ] **Hybrid encryption** (asymmetric key exchange + symmetric data encryption) is the universal industrial approach — used in TLS 1.3, OPC UA, MQTT. - [ ] **Modes of operation matter:** Always use **GCM, CCM, or CTR** — **never ECB**. AEAD modes provide confidentiality + integrity in a single operation. - [ ] **Security equivalence:** AES-128 ≈ ECC-256 ≈ RSA-3072 ≈ $2^{128}$ brute-force operations. - [ ] **Stream ciphers (ChaCha20)** are better than AES on low-end MCUs without hardware AES acceleration. - [ ] **Key management** in CPS includes: key generation, distribution, storage (HSM), rotation, and revocation. - [ ] **The $n(n-1)/2$ key scaling problem** is solved using asymmetric cryptography or KDCs. - [ ] **Digital signatures** (RSA-PSS, ECDSA, EdDSA) provide authentication and non-repudiation — essential for command authenticity in CPS. - [ ] **Post-quantum warning:** RSA and ECC will be broken by quantum computers (Shor's algorithm). CPS with 20-30 year lifecycles should plan for **post-quantum cryptography** (CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium, NIST PQC standards). - [ ] **Exam keyword triggers:** "OPC UA" + "AES-GCM" + "ECC key exchange" = full marks answer for a 14-mark CPS encryption question.

topics.title: Key Management Considerations for Secure Communication in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Key Management Considerations for Secure Communication in CPS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Management in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** is defined as the set of procedures, protocols, policies, and cryptographic mechanisms responsible for the **lifecycle handling of cryptographic keys** — including their **generation, distribution, storage, usage, update, revocation, and destruction** — across heterogeneous cyber (computational/communication) and physical (sensor/actuator) components of a CPS, while satisfying the **real-time, resource-constrained, and safety-critical** operational constraints of the system. In formal cryptographic terms, key management is a sub-discipline of **applied cryptography** that ensures that secret material (keys, IVs, nonces) remains: - **Confidential** (unreachable to adversaries) - **Authentic** (bound to the correct identity) - **Fresh** (replaced periodically to limit exposure) - **Available** (recoverable when needed by legitimate nodes) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme CO Mapping:** This topic primarily addresses **CO2** – *Analyze security requirements and cryptographic mechanisms for CPS communication*, and contributes to **CO3** – *Evaluate key management frameworks for distributed CPS*. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **large industrial factory** with hundreds of robotic arms, conveyor belts, and sensor arrays (the CPS). Each machine needs to whisper sensitive operational commands to other machines without the factory foreman (a hacker) overhearing or impersonating a legitimate machine. - A **cryptographic key** is the *secret language* two machines share. - **Key management** is the entire *infrastructure* of issuing these secret languages, distributing them safely, periodically changing them (so old overheard conversations become useless), and revoking them when a machine is decommissioned or compromised. If you imagine a hotel with thousands of rooms, the **front desk** is the *Key Management Server (KMS)*, the **electronic key cards** are the *cryptographic keys*, and the **door locks** are the *CPS endpoints*. Just as a hotel must issue, track, expire, and recall key cards, a CPS must manage cryptographic keys across millions of devices — but unlike a hotel, these devices may run on coin-cell batteries for **10 years** and operate in **real-time** with **sub-millisecond** latency. ### 1.3 Why Key Management is the Hardest Problem in CPS Security > [!IMPORTANT] > While encryption algorithms (AES, RSA, ECC) are *mathematical tools*, key management is a **systems engineering problem** — and in CPS, three converging constraints make it exceptionally hard: > > 1. **Heterogeneity** — sensors, actuators, PLCs, edge gateways, cloud servers, mobile HMI devices all coexist. > 2. **Resource Constraints** — many CPS endpoints (e.g., IEEE 802.15.4 motes) have **< 32 KB RAM**, **< 256 KB flash**, and limited energy budgets. > 3. **Safety-Critical Real-Time Operation** — a key handshake that takes 5 seconds is unacceptable for a CPS controlling a robotic surgical arm or a smart grid breaker. ### 1.4 The Five Pillars of CPS Key Management (Mnemonic: **GDRUD**) | Pillar | Meaning | CPS-Specific Challenge | |---|---|---| | **G**eneration | Creating strong random keys | Entropy sources on embedded MCUs are poor | | **D**istribution | Securely delivering keys | Bandwidth-limited, lossy wireless links | | **R**otation / Refresh | Periodically changing keys | Real-time systems cannot pause for re-keying | | **U**pdate / Revocation | Removing compromised keys | Must propagate fast across thousands of nodes | | **D**estruction | Securely wiping old keys | Non-volatile memory persistence in MCUs | ### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in CPS Key Management - **Key Length:** typically **128 bits** (AES-128), **256 bits** (AES-256), **256 bits** (EC private keys) — measured in **bits**. - **Key Lifetime / Validity Period:** time before forced rotation, typically **minutes to hours** in CPS (vs. months in IT). - **Re-keying Latency:** time to complete a key refresh, often **< 100 ms** in industrial CPS. - **Storage Overhead:** key storage in **bytes** of NVM/RAM. - **Computational Cost:** measured in **clock cycles, mJ (millijoules), or μW·s**. - **Communication Overhead:** key material in **bytes** transmitted per re-key event. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Cryptographic Primitives Used for Key Management #### A. Symmetric Key Cryptography - Both parties share the **same secret key** $K$. - Algorithms: **AES-128/256, ChaCha20, 3DES (legacy)**. - **Pros:** Fast (∼10×–1000× faster than asymmetric), low energy, suitable for embedded MCUs. - **Cons:** **Key distribution problem** — how do $N$ devices securely share keys pairwise? $O(N^2)$ pairwise keys required. #### B. Asymmetric (Public-Key) Cryptography - Each entity has a **key pair** $(K_{priv}, K_{pub})$. - Algorithms: **RSA-2048, ECC (P-256, Curve25519), Ed25519**. - **Pros:** Solves key distribution via certificates; enables digital signatures. - **Cons:** Computationally heavy (10 ms – 100 ms on resource-constrained nodes); high energy cost. #### C. Hybrid Cryptography - Combines asymmetric key exchange (e.g., **ECDH**) to establish a **shared symmetric session key**. - Standard in **TLS 1.3, DTLS 1.3, IKEv2**. #### D. Lightweight Cryptography (LWC) - Tailored for CPS / IoT: **PRESENT, SIMON, SPECK, Ascon, Photon-Beetle**. - Standardized under **NIST IR 8259** and **ISO/IEC 29192**. #### E. Key Derivation Functions (KDF) - Derive multiple keys from a single master secret. - Algorithms: **HKDF, NIST SP 800-108, PBKDF2**. ### 2.2 The CPS Key Lifecycle (Structured Logic Steps) 1. **Key Generation** - Use a **Cryptographically Secure Pseudorandom Number Generator (CSPRNG)** seeded with true entropy. - On CPS nodes, entropy sources: **ADC thermal noise, clock jitter, radio RSSI noise, dedicated TRNG hardware**. - **Strength requirement:** A 128-bit key has $2^{128}$ possible values; brute-force cost is infeasible on any classical computer. 2. **Key Establishment / Distribution** - **Pre-provisioned keys:** baked into firmware at manufacturing (simple, but no forward secrecy). - **Key Agreement Protocols:** **Diffie-Hellman (DH), ECDH** — both derive a shared secret over an insecure channel. - **Key Transport:** asymmetric encryption of a symmetric key (e.g., RSA-OAEP wrapping an AES key). - **Key Distribution Centers (KDC):** a trusted server issues session keys (Kerberos-style). 3. **Key Storage** - Stored in **volatile memory (SRAM)** when possible, or in **secure NVM** with hardware protection. - Hardware roots of trust: **TPM 2.0, Secure Element (SE), ARM TrustZone, ESP32 efuse, eMMC RPMB**. - Encryption-at-rest: keys wrapped by a **Hardware Unique Key (HUK)**. 4. **Key Usage** - Bound to specific algorithms, purposes, and time windows (key separation principle). - Example: separate keys for **authentication, encryption, and integrity**. 5. **Key Rotation / Refresh** - Driven by **time** (every $T$ seconds), **volume** (every $N$ bytes encrypted), or **events** (session end, node join/leave). 6. **Key Revocation** - **Certificate Revocation Lists (CRL)** or **OCSP** in PKI. - In CPS, often achieved by **over-the-air (OTA) firmware push** of new keys. 7. **Key Destruction** - Zeroization: overwrite memory with zeros/random data. - On flash: erase blocks and trigger **wear-leveling** scrub. ### 2.3 Trust Models in CPS Key Management | Model | Description | CPS Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Centralized (PKI / KDC)** | One trusted authority (CA) issues all keys | Smart grid substation, hospital networks | | **Decentralized (Web-of-Trust)** | Mutual attestation between nodes | Ad-hoc vehicle platooning (V2V) | | **Distributed / Threshold** | $t$-of-$n$ nodes must cooperate to reconstruct key | Blockchain-anchored CPS, IIoT consortiums | | **Symmetric Pre-shared (PSK)** | All nodes share a single key | Tiny sensor motes (ZigBee APS layer) | | **Pairwise Unique** | Each pair has a unique key | High-security industrial control | ### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each CPS-Specific Constraint - **Why lightweight?** CPS nodes are battery-powered or energy-harvested (solar, vibration, thermal). ECC-256 is preferred over RSA-2048 because it provides equivalent security at **∼10× lower energy**. - **Why frequent re-keying?** CPS physical environments are adversarial (vibration, EMI, temperature swings). Re-keying limits the volume of ciphertext per key (cryptoperiod), reducing the impact of key compromise. - **Why a Trusted Platform Module (TPM)?** Physical tampering of CPS field devices is realistic; hardware roots of trust resist **side-channel and physical attacks**. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table contains the core mathematical formulations, security bounds, and metrics a KTU examiner expects for this topic. | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Key Space Size (Symmetric) | $\vert K \vert = 2^{k}$ | $k$ = key length in **bits** | | 2 | Brute-Force Security Level | $S = 2^{k}$ operations | For AES-128, $S = 2^{128}$ | | 3 | Pairwise Keys Required | $N_p = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | For $N$ nodes | | 4 | Group Key Re-key Cost | $R_g = O(N)$ | Broadcast-based group re-key | | 5 | Pairwise Re-key Cost | $R_p = O(N^2)$ | Impractical for large CPS | | 6 | DH Shared Secret | $K_{AB} = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | $a, b$ private, $g^a, g^b$ public | | 7 | ECDH Shared Secret | $K_{AB} = d_A \cdot Q_B = d_B \cdot Q_A$ | On elliptic curve $E(\mathbb{F}_p)$ | | 8 | RSA Modulus Size | $n = p \cdot q$ | $\vert n \vert \geq 2048$ bits (NIST 2024) | | 9 | ECC Security Equivalence | 256-bit ECC $\approx$ 3072-bit RSA | NIST SP 800-57 | | 10 | HKDF Output | $\text{HKDF}(IKM, \text{salt}, \text{info}, L)$ | $L$ = output length in bytes | | 11 | Entropy (Shannon) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Measured in **bits** | | 12 | Min-Entropy (TRNG) | $H_{\infty}(X) = -\log_2(\max_i p(x_i))$ | Used in NIST SP 800-90B | | 13 | Re-key Interval (NIST) | $T_r = \dfrac{\text{Cryptoperiod}}{N_{sessions}}$ | Time-based rotation | | 14 | Message Limit (AES-CTR) | $2^{64}$ blocks per key | Beyond this, keystream repeats | | 15 | TLS 1.3 Key Schedule | $\text{(DHE or PSK) → IKM → HKDF-Extract → HKDF-Expand}$ | Standardized in **RFC 8446** | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart Grids (IEC 62351):** Uses **PKI with X.509 certificates** for substation-to-control-center communication. Key rotation every **15–60 minutes**. - **Connected Vehicles (V2X):** Use **IEEE 1609.2** with ECDSA-P256 signatures. Pseudonym certificates rotated every **5 minutes** for location privacy. - **Industrial IoT (OPC UA):** Uses **X.509 PKI** plus **Application Authentication** with certificate-based trust. - **Medical CPS (Implantable Devices):** Use **pre-shared symmetric keys** with **inductive proximity-based re-keying** to avoid wireless interception. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Security Strength of a Symmetric Key **Problem:** Show that an AES-128 key provides $\mathbf{2^{128}}$ brute-force security on a classical computer. **Step 1 — Define the key space.** An AES-128 key is a 128-bit binary string. The number of possible distinct keys is: $$ \begin{aligned} \vert \mathcal{K} \vert &= 2^{128} \\ &= 3.4028 \times 10^{38} \text{ possible keys} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute brute-force operations.** An adversary must, in the worst case, enumerate all keys. Each trial requires one AES decryption. Total work: $$ \begin{aligned} W_{bf} &= 2^{128} \text{ AES operations} \\ &\approx 3.4 \times 10^{38} \text{ operations} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Estimate time on a $10^{18}$ ops/sec supercomputer.** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{bf} &= \dfrac{2^{128}}{10^{18}} \text{ seconds} \\ &\approx 3.4 \times 10^{20} \text{ seconds} \\ &\approx 1.08 \times 10^{13} \text{ years} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compare with universe age ($\approx 1.38 \times 10^{10}$ years).** $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{T_{bf}}{\text{Age of Universe}} &\approx \dfrac{1.08 \times 10^{13}}{1.38 \times 10^{10}} \approx 782 \text{ times} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** AES-128 is computationally infeasible to brute-force. KTU 2024 scheme accepts **AES-128 as the minimum recommended** key strength for CPS symmetric encryption (per **NIST SP 800-131A Rev.2**). --- ### 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — Pairwise vs Group Key Scaling in CPS **Problem:** For a CPS deployment of $N = 1000$ IIoT sensors, derive the key storage and re-key cost for (a) pairwise unique keys, (b) group-shared key, and (c) hybrid (group + per-node auxiliary). **Step 1 — Pairwise unique keys.** $$ \begin{aligned} N_p &= \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2} \\ &= \dfrac{1000 \times 999}{2} \\ &= 499{,}500 \text{ unique keys required globally} \end{aligned} $$ Per-node storage: $N - 1 = 999$ keys. Re-key on a single node join/leave: $O(N)$ re-key messages broadcast, plus $O(N)$ for each existing peer to refresh. **Not scalable for CPS.** **Step 2 — Single group key.** $$ \begin{aligned} N_g &= 1 \text{ key} \\ \text{Per-node storage} &= 1 \text{ key (16 bytes for AES-128)} \end{aligned} $$ Re-key cost on membership change: **O(N) re-key messages**, all nodes update. **Secure only against outsiders; one compromised node reveals the entire group key.** This is a **major weakness**. **Step 3 — Hybrid Logical Key Hierarchy (LKH) — used in CPS standards.** A balanced binary tree of depth $d = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$: $$ \begin{aligned} d &= \lceil \log_2 1000 \rceil = 10 \end{aligned} $$ Total keys per node: $d = 10$ keys (path from leaf to root). Re-key on leave: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Messages} &= 2d - 1 = 19 \text{ re-key messages} \\ \text{Per-node storage} &= 10 \text{ keys (160 bytes for AES-128)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** LKH-style schemes achieve **$O(\log N)$ per-node storage and $O(\log N)$ re-key cost**, making them the de facto choice for large-scale CPS deployments (e.g., smart metering in Advanced Metering Infrastructure — AMI). --- ### 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3 — ECDH Key Agreement Energy Estimation **Problem:** A CPS sensor node must establish a session key using **ECDH P-256**. Estimate the energy cost vs. **RSA-2048**. **Step 1 — Reference energy figures (typical Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz).** | Operation | Time | Energy (approx.) | |---|---|---| | ECDH P-256 scalar mult. | **~150 ms** | **~12 mJ** | | RSA-2048 decryption | **~1500 ms** | **~120 mJ** | **Step 2 — Compute energy ratio.** $$ \begin{aligned} R &= \dfrac{E_{RSA}}{E_{ECDH}} \\ &= \dfrac{120 \text{ mJ}}{12 \text{ mJ}} \\ &= 10 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Impact on battery life.** Assume a CPS sensor powered by a **2400 mAh @ 3.7 V** battery (energy $\approx 31{,}968$ J): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ECDH-based battery life} &\approx \dfrac{31968}{12 \times 10^{-3} \times 86400} \approx 30.8 \text{ days of continuous re-keying} \\ \text{RSA-based battery life} &\approx \dfrac{31968}{120 \times 10^{-3} \times 86400} \approx 3.1 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** For CPS nodes with **frequent key refresh** (e.g., every 5 minutes for V2X), ECDH is **mandatory** to maintain operational lifetime. RSA is infeasible. This is why **IEEE 1609.2, Zigbee IP (ZigBee 3.0), and Thread (Matter)** all mandate ECC. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Derivation 4 — Forward Secrecy & Session Key Derivation (TLS 1.3 / DTLS 1.3 style) **Step 1 — Master Secret derivation.** Given a Diffie-Hellman shared secret $SS = g^{ab} \bmod p$ (ECDH equivalent in elliptic-curve group), and transcript hash $H_t$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Master Secret} &= \text{HKDF-Extract}(\text{salt} = 00\cdots 00, \text{IKM} = SS) \\ &= \text{HMAC-SHA256}(\text{salt}, SS) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Key Expansion.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{client\_handshake\_traffic\_secret} &= \text{HKDF-Expand-Label}(MS, \text{"c hs traffic"}, H_t, 32) \\ \text{server\_handshake\_traffic\_secret} &= \text{HKDF-Expand-Label}(MS, \text{"s hs traffic"}, H_t, 32) \\ \text{client\_application\_traffic\_secret} &= \text{HKDF-Expand-Label}(MS, \text{"c ap traffic"}, H_t, 32) \\ \text{server\_application\_traffic\_secret} &= \text{HKDF-Expand-Label}(MS, \text{"s ap traffic"}, H_t, 32) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Forward Secrecy (FS) Property.** Even if the long-term private key $a$ of Alice is **compromised at time $t_1 > t_0$**, the session key derived at $t_0$ **remains secret** because $b$ is freshly generated (or an **ephemeral** $a_e$ was used). The adversary gains no new information about past sessions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU takeaway:** **Forward Secrecy is REQUIRED** for CPS safety-critical systems. Always use **(EC)DHE**, never static RSA key transport. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Key Management Schemes for CPS | Scheme | Key Type | Storage/Node | Re-key Cost | Forward Secrecy | CPS Suitability | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Pre-Shared Key (PSK)** | Symmetric | 1 key | O(1) | ❌ | ✅ Tiny motes | | **Pairwise Unique** | Symmetric | N-1 keys | O(N) | ✅ | ⚠ Small clusters | | **Group Key** | Symmetric | 1 key | O(N) | ❌ | ⚠ Low-trust | | **LKH / OFT (Tree)** | Symmetric | O(log N) | O(log N) | ✅ | ✅ Smart grid | | **PKI / X.509** | Asymmetric | 1 priv + many pub | O(1) verify | ⚠ with DHE | ✅ Substations | | **ECDH (ephemeral)** | Asymmetric | 1 priv key | O(1) | ✅ | ✅ V2X, IIoT | | **Blockchain-anchored** | Mixed | O(1) + ledger | O(1) | ✅ | ⚠ Latency-sensitive | > [!NOTE] > **Legend:** ✅ = Strong, ⚠ = Conditional, ❌ = Not provided. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The CPS Key Lifecycle — End-to-End Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Physical Entropy Source
ADC Noise / Clock Jitter] A2[TRNG Hardware Module] A3[CSPRNG Engine
AES-CTR DRBG] A4[Key Pair Generation
ECC P-256 / RSA-2048] A5[Key Wrapping
with HUK in TPM SE] A6[Secure NVM Storage
Flash Encrypted Slot] A7[Active Use in Crypto Engine
AES GCM / ChaCha20-Poly1305] A8[Re-key Trigger
Timer / Volume / Event] A9[Old Key Destruction
Zeroization Routine] A10[Audit Log to KMS
SIEM / HSM] A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 A3 --> A4 A4 --> A5 A5 --> A6 A6 --> A7 A7 --> A8 A8 --> A4 A8 --> A9 A8 --> A10 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** A CPS node harvests entropy from physical sources (e.g., RF noise on the antenna port) into a hardware TRNG, feeds a CSPRNG, generates keys, wraps them with a hardware-unique key (HUK), stores them in a secure NVM slot, uses them in the crypto engine, and on a re-key trigger, regenerates and zeroizes. ### 4.2 Hierarchical Key Management for Smart Grid CPS ```mermaid flowchart TB RootCA[Root Certificate Authority
Offline Air-Gapped] SubCA1[Substation CA
Field Site] SubCA2[Substation CA
Backup Site] Gateway[Edge Gateway
X.509 Identity] RTU1[RTU 1 - Feeder A
X.509 Identity] RTU2[RTU 2 - Feeder B
