X.509 Identity] Sensor1[Smart Meter Cluster 1] Sensor2[Smart Meter Cluster 2] Sensor3[Smart Meter Cluster 3] RootCA --> SubCA1 RootCA --> SubCA2 SubCA1 --> Gateway SubCA1 --> RTU1 SubCA1 --> RTU2 Gateway --> Sensor1 Gateway --> Sensor2 Gateway --> Sensor3 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Architecture Insight:** The two-level PKI hierarchy (offline Root CA + online Substation CA) is a **defense-in-depth pattern** used in real **IEC 61850**-compliant smart grids. Compromise of one substation CA does not affect the rest of the grid. ### 4.3 Logical Key Hierarchy (LKH) for Group Re-keying ```mermaid flowchart TB K0[Group Root Key K0] K1[Subgroup Key K1 - Left Half] K2[Subgroup Key K2 - Right Half] K3[Node Key K3 - Leaf 1] K4[Node Key K4 - Leaf 2] K5[Node Key K5 - Leaf 3] K6[Node Key K6 - Leaf 4] N1((Node 1)) N2((Node 2)) N3((Node 3)) N4((Node 4)) K0 --> K1 K0 --> K2 K1 --> K3 K1 --> K4 K2 --> K5 K2 --> K6 K3 --> N1 K4 --> N2 K5 --> N3 K6 --> N4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the LKH tree:** When **Node 3 leaves** the group, only the keys **K2 and K5** need to be updated and redistributed. All other nodes retain their existing key material. This achieves **$O(\log N)$** re-key cost. ### 4.4 CPS Key Management Threat Surface ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Adversary] --> B1[Side-Channel Attack
Power / EM / Timing] A --> B2[Physical Tampering
JTAG / Chip-off] A --> B3[Network Interception
MITM on Re-key] A --> B4[Insider Threat
Compromised Node] A --> B5[Replay Attack
Old Key Reuse] B1 --> C1[Mitigation: Constant-Time
Crypto / Masking] B2 --> C2[Mitigation: TPM 2.0
Secure Boot] B3 --> C3[Mitigation: Mutual Auth
Channel Binding] B4 --> C4[Mitigation: Forward Secrecy
Ephemeral ECDH] B5 --> C5[Mitigation: Key Freshness
Nonces / Timestamps] ``` ### 4.5 Key Establishment Protocol Stack for CPS (Layered View) ```mermaid flowchart TD L1[Application Layer
OPC UA / IEC 61850 / MQTT-SN] L2[Session Security Layer
TLS 1.3 / DTLS 1.3] L3[Key Agreement Layer
ECDH P-256 + HKDF] L4[Authentication Layer
X.509 v3 / PSK / EDH] L5[Crypto Primitive Layer
AES-128-GCM / SHA-256] L6[Hardware Root of Trust
TPM 2.0 / SE / TrustZone] L7[Physical Layer
IEEE 802.15.4 / WirelessHART / 5G URLLC] L7 --> L6 L6 --> L5 L5 --> L4 L4 --> L3 L3 --> L2 L2 --> L1 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Each layer abstracts a *specific* key management concern. The Hardware Root of Trust is the **cryptographic anchor** — without it, all upper-layer guarantees are reduced to software-only trust. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECPT413]` **Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric key management in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. List two CPS-specific scenarios where each is preferred.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Symmetric | Asymmetric | |---|---|---| | Key | Single shared secret | Public + private key pair | | Speed | Fast (AES: ∼100 Mbps) | Slow (RSA: ∼1–10 Kbps) | | Energy | Low (∼12 mJ/key) | High (∼120 mJ/key) | | Distribution | Hard (KDC needed) | Easy (public broadcast) | | Forward Secrecy | Needs re-key logic | Native with (EC)DHE | **CPS Scenarios:** - **Symmetric preferred:** Tiny **IEEE 802.15.4 motes** in wireless sensor networks; **RFID tags**; **smart card-based access control**. - **Asymmetric preferred:** **Substation-to-control-center** SCADA links (X.509 PKI); **V2V beacon signing** (ECDSA P-256); **firmware OTA updates** (signed by manufacturer's private key). *(3 marks: 1 for differentiation, 1 for each scenario.)* --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECPT413]` **What is a Hardware Root of Trust (HRoT)? Why is it essential for CPS key management?** **Model Answer:** A **Hardware Root of Trust (HRoT)** is a tamper-resistant hardware module (e.g., **TPM 2.0, Secure Element, ARM TrustZone, HSM**) that provides a **trustworthy foundation** for all cryptographic operations in a CPS node. It performs: - **Secure key generation** (true random sources) - **Hardware key storage** (memory inaccessible to main CPU) - **Cryptographic acceleration** (offloads AES/SHA/RSA/ECC) - **Attestation** (proof of device integrity) **Why essential for CPS:** 1. CPS field devices are physically accessible — an adversary can perform **chip-off attacks** to extract firmware keys. HRoT resists this via **tamper detection and key zeroization**. 2. **Boot-time attestation** ensures the firmware (and thus the key handling code) has not been modified. 3. Provides a **Hardware Unique Key (HUK)** to wrap all higher-level keys, creating a **chain of trust**. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for properties, 1 for CPS-specific justification.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2 of PECPT413 has an internal choice between Question A and Question B for the 14-mark slot. Both are presented below with full solutions. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECPT413]`** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete lifecycle of cryptographic keys** in a CPS node. Explain each phase with respect to CPS-specific constraints. **(b) [7 Marks]** A CPS deployment of $N = 500$ smart meters uses a **Logical Key Hierarchy (LKH)** group key management scheme. Compute: 1. The depth of the LKH tree. 2. The number of keys each meter must store. 3. The number of re-key messages required when **one meter leaves** the group. 4. Comment on the **scalability** vs. a pairwise key scheme. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the full lifecycle diagram. The phases are: 1. **Key Generation (1 mark):** Use of CSPRNG (e.g., **AES-CTR DRBG, NIST SP 800-90A**) seeded by a hardware TRNG. CPS constraint: limited entropy; reuse of predictable seeds breaks security. 2. **Key Establishment (1 mark):** ECDH-based handshake (e.g., **TLS 1.3 ECDHE**), or pre-provisioning during manufacturing. CPS constraint: low-bandwidth wireless links; minimal handshake rounds. 3. **Key Storage (1 mark):** Wrapped with a Hardware Unique Key inside a **TPM 2.0 / Secure Element**. CPS constraint: tamper resistance in field-deployed devices. 4. **Key Usage (1 mark):** Bound to specific algorithms and purposes (key separation). Example: separate keys for **MAC, encryption, key wrapping**. 5. **Key Rotation (1 mark):** Time-based (every $T$ seconds) or volume-based (every $2^{48}$ blocks). CPS constraint: must complete in **< 100 ms**. 6. **Key Revocation (1 mark):** Use **Certificate Revocation Lists (CRL)** or **OCSP**; broadcast new group key via LKH. CPS constraint: must propagate fast across the network. 7. **Key Destruction (1 mark):** Zeroization of NVM slots; trigger **wear-leveling** to overwrite physical flash cells. CPS constraint: flash memory persistence requires multi-pass overwrite. **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — Tree depth.** $$ \begin{aligned} d &= \lceil \log_2 N \rceil \\ &= \lceil \log_2 500 \rceil \\ &= \lceil 8.97 \rceil \\ &= 9 \end{aligned} $$ **(1 mark)** **Step 2 — Keys per meter.** In a balanced binary LKH, each leaf-to-root path has $d + 1$ nodes (including the leaf). Hence: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Keys per node} &= d + 1 = 10 \text{ keys} \\ \text{Storage} &= 10 \times 16 \text{ bytes} = 160 \text{ bytes (for AES-128)} \end{aligned} $$ **(1 mark)** **Step 3 — Re-key messages on a single leave.** For a balanced LKH, when a leaf departs, all keys on its path must be refreshed. The number of re-key messages broadcast by the Group Controller is: $$ \begin{aligned} M &= 2d - 1 \\ &= 2(9) - 1 \\ &= 17 \text{ messages} \end{aligned} $$ **(1 mark)** **Step 4 — Comparison with pairwise scheme.** | Metric | LKH | Pairwise | |---|---|---| | Storage per node | $O(\log N) = 10$ keys | $O(N) = 499$ keys | | Total keys in system | $O(N) = 2N - 1 = 999$ | $O(N^2) = 124{,}750$ | | Re-key on leave | $O(\log N) = 17$ msgs | $O(N) = 499$ msgs | | Forward Secrecy | Yes (key refresh) | Yes | | Scalability | ✅ Excellent for $N \leq 10^6$ | ❌ Infeasible for $N > 100$ | **(4 marks: 2 for pairwise analysis, 1 for comparison table, 1 for scalability comment.)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECPT413]`** **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **PKI-based** and **symmetric pre-shared key (PSK)** key management approaches for CPS. For each, discuss the **trust model, scalability, CPS use cases, and limitations**. **(b) [7 Marks]** A CPS sensor node uses **ECDH P-256** for session key establishment. The two parties' private keys are $d_A = 7$ and $d_B = 11$ on the curve $E: y^2 = x^3 + 2x + 3 \pmod{17}$ with generator $G = (5, 1)$. Compute: 1. The public keys $Q_A$ and $Q_B$. 2. The shared secret $K_{AB}$ from both sides and verify equality. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks** | Aspect | PKI-based | Symmetric PSK | |---|---|---| | **Trust Model** | Hierarchical — relies on **Certificate Authorities (CAs)** | Single shared secret — all nodes trust each other | | **Key Storage** | 1 private key per node | 1 shared key per group | | **Scalability** | High — public keys can be distributed openly | Low — O(N) re-key on a single node compromise | | **Computation** | Expensive (ECDH, RSA, ECDSA) | Cheap (AES, HMAC) | | **CPS Use Case** | Substation gateways, V2X, smart meter AMI | Tiny sensor motes, RFID, body-area networks | | **Limitations** | CA compromise = total system failure; certificate revocation overhead | One node compromise = entire group key exposed; no forward secrecy | | **Standards** | IEC 62351, IEEE 1609.2, OPC UA | ZigBee APS, LoRaWAN, Bluetooth LE Legacy | **(7 marks: 2 for trust model + scalability, 2 for CPS use cases, 2 for limitations, 1 for standard mapping.)** **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — Compute public key $Q_A = d_A \cdot G = 7 \cdot (5, 1)$.** Use the elliptic curve point-doubling and addition formulas on $E: y^2 = x^3 + 2x + 3$ over $\mathbb{F}_{17}$. Point doubling for $G = (5, 1)$: $$ \begin{aligned} s &= \dfrac{3x_1^2 + a}{2y_1} \bmod 17 \\ &= \dfrac{3 \cdot 25 + 2}{2} \bmod 17 \\ &= \dfrac{77}{2} \bmod 17 \\ &= 77 \cdot 2^{-1} \bmod 17 \\ &= 77 \cdot 9 \bmod 17 \quad (\text{since } 2 \cdot 9 = 18 \equiv 1) \\ &= 693 \bmod 17 \\ &= 693 - 40 \cdot 17 = 693 - 680 = 13 \end{aligned} $$ So $2G = (x_3, y_3)$ with: $$ \begin{aligned} x_3 &= s^2 - 2x_1 \bmod 17 = 169 - 10 \bmod 17 = 159 \bmod 17 = 6 \\ y_3 &= s(x_1 - x_3) - y_1 \bmod 17 = 13 \cdot (5 - 6) - 1 = -13 - 1 = -14 \bmod 17 = 3 \end{aligned} $$ Hence $2G = (6, 3)$. Continue $7G = 7 \cdot G$ using **double-and-add**: $$ \begin{aligned} 4G &= 2 \cdot 2G \\ 2G &= (6, 3) \Rightarrow 4G: \\ s &= \dfrac{3 \cdot 36 + 2}{2 \cdot 3} = \dfrac{110}{6} = 110 \cdot 6^{-1} \bmod 17 \\ 6^{-1} &\equiv 3 \bmod 17 \quad (\text{since } 6 \cdot 3 = 18 \equiv 1) \\ s &= 110 \cdot 3 = 330 \bmod 17 = 330 - 19 \cdot 17 = 330 - 323 = 7 \\ x_5 &= 49 - 12 \bmod 17 = 37 \bmod 17 = 3 \\ y_5 &= 7(6 - 3) - 3 = 21 - 3 = 18 \bmod 17 = 1 \\ 4G &= (3, 1) \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} 3G &= 2G + G = (6,3) + (5,1) \\ s &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \dfrac{3 - 1}{6 - 5} = 2 \cdot 1^{-1} = 2 \bmod 17 \\ x &= 4 - 11 = -7 \bmod 17 = 10 \\ y &= 2(5 - 10) - 1 = -10 - 1 = -11 \bmod 17 = 6 \\ 3G &= (10, 6) \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} 7G &= 4G + 3G = (3,1) + (10,6) \\ s &= \dfrac{6 - 1}{10 - 3} = \dfrac{5}{7} = 5 \cdot 7^{-1} \bmod 17 \\ 7^{-1} &\equiv 5 \bmod 17 \quad (\text{since } 7 \cdot 5 = 35 \equiv 1) \\ s &= 5 \cdot 5 = 25 \bmod 17 = 8 \\ x &= 64 - 13 \bmod 17 = 51 \bmod 17 = 0 \\ y &= 8(3 - 0) - 1 = 24 - 1 = 23 \bmod 17 = 6 \\ Q_A = 7G &= (0, 6) \end{aligned} $$ **(2 marks)** **Step 2 — Compute $Q_B = 11 \cdot G$.** Using the precomputed values $G, 2G, 3G, 4G$: $$ \begin{aligned} 11G &= 8G + 3G \\ 8G &= 2 \cdot 4G = 2 \cdot (3, 1) \\ s &= \dfrac{3 \cdot 9 + 2}{2} = \dfrac{29}{2} = 29 \cdot 9 \bmod 17 = 261 \bmod 17 = 6 \\ x &= 36 - 6 = 30 \bmod 17 = 13 \\ y &= 6(3 - 13) - 1 = -60 - 1 = -61 \bmod 17 = 7 \\ 8G &= (13, 7) \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} 11G &= 8G + 3G = (13,7) + (10,6) \\ s &= \dfrac{6 - 7}{10 - 13} = \dfrac{-1}{-3} = 1 \cdot 3^{-1} \bmod 17 \\ 3^{-1} &\equiv 6 \bmod 17 \\ s &= 6 \\ x &= 36 - 13 \bmod 17 = 23 \bmod 17 = 6 \\ y &= 6(13 - 6) - 7 = 42 - 7 = 35 \bmod 17 = 1 \\ Q_B = 11G &= (6, 1) \end{aligned} $$ **(2 marks)** **Step 3 — Compute shared secret from Alice's side: $K_{AB} = d_A \cdot Q_B = 7 \cdot (6, 1)$.** $$ \begin{aligned} 7 \cdot (6,1) &= 4(6,1) + 2(6,1) + (6,1) \\ 2 \cdot (6,1): s &= \dfrac{3 \cdot 36 + 2}{2} = 55 \cdot 9 \bmod 17 = 495 \bmod 17 = 2 \\ x &= 4 - 12 = -8 \bmod 17 = 9 \\ y &= 2(6-9) - 1 = -7 \bmod 17 = 10 \\ 2(6,1) &= (9,10) \\ 4(6,1) &= 2(9,10) \\ s &= \dfrac{3 \cdot 81 + 2}{20} = \dfrac{245}{20} = 245 \cdot (20^{-1}) \bmod 17 \\ 20 &\equiv 3 \bmod 17 \Rightarrow 3^{-1} \equiv 6 \\ s &= 245 \cdot 6 = 1470 \bmod 17 = 1470 - 86 \cdot 17 = 1470 - 1462 = 8 \\ x &= 64 - 18 = 46 \bmod 17 = 12 \\ y &= 8(9-12) - 10 = -24 - 10 = -34 \bmod 17 = 0 \\ 4(6,1) &= (12, 0) \end{aligned} $$ Now $7(6,1) = 4(6,1) + 2(6,1) + (6,1) = (12,0) + (9,10) + (6,1)$: $$ \begin{aligned} (12,0) + (9,10): s &= \dfrac{10 - 0}{9 - 12} = \dfrac{10}{-3} = 10 \cdot 6 \bmod 17 = 60 \bmod 17 = 9 \\ x &= 81 - 21 = 60 \bmod 17 = 9 \\ y &= 9(12 - 9) - 0 = 27 \bmod 17 = 10 \\ (12,0) + (9,10) &= (9, 10) \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} (9,10) + (6,1): s &= \dfrac{1 - 10}{6 - 9} = \dfrac{-9}{-3} = 9 \cdot 6 \bmod 17 = 54 \bmod 17 = 3 \\ x &= 9 - 15 = -6 \bmod 17 = 11 \\ y &= 3(9 - 11) - 10 = -6 - 10 = -16 \bmod 17 = 1 \\ K_{AB} &= (11, 1) \end{aligned} $$ **(2 marks)** **Step 4 — Verify from Bob's side: $K_{AB}' = d_B \cdot Q_A = 11 \cdot (0, 6)$.** By the **associativity of elliptic curve scalar multiplication**, $11 \cdot (0, 6) = 11 \cdot 7G = 77G = 7 \cdot 11G = 7Q_B = K_{AB}$. Hence: $$ K_{AB}' = K_{AB} = (11, 1) \quad \blacksquare $$ **(1 mark for verification/justification)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Modular inverse errors** — many students forget to compute $a^{-1} \bmod p$ and divide directly. *Loss: 2–3 marks.* > 2. **Skipping the verify step** — KTU explicitly tests whether you confirm that both sides arrive at the **same shared secret**. *Loss: 1 mark.* > 3. **Not reducing the final point** — always reduce both $x$ and $y$ modulo $p$. *Loss: 0.5–1 mark.* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Key Management in CPS** - ✅ **Key Management = Lifecycle (GDRUD):** Generation → Distribution → Rotation → Update/Revocation → Destruction. Memorize this acronym. - ✅ **Minimum key sizes (NIST SP 800-131A, 2024):** AES-128, RSA-2048, ECC-256, SHA-256. - ✅ **Forward Secrecy is mandatory for safety-critical CPS** — use **(EC)DHE**, not static RSA. - ✅ **Pairwise keys:** $N(N-1)/2$ total — **not scalable** beyond $N \approx 100$. - ✅ **LKH tree:** depth $= \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$, keys per node $= d+1$, re-key messages on leave $= 2d - 1$. - ✅ **CPS node hardware:** Always assume **TPM 2.0 / Secure Element** for storing root keys; software-only key storage is **insecure** in field-deployed CPS. - ✅ **ECDH P-256** consumes **~10× less energy** than RSA-2048 — preferred for battery-powered CPS sensors. - ✅ **Cryptoperiod for CPS:** typically **minutes to hours** (not months) due to physical exposure and safety-criticality. - ✅ **TLS 1.3 / DTLS 1.3** is the de facto secure-communication protocol for CPS gateways and edge devices — enforces (EC)DHE, AEAD ciphers, and forward secrecy by default. - ✅ **Standards to remember:** **IEC 62351** (smart grid), **IEEE 1609.2** (V2X), **OPC UA Security** (IIoT), **IEC 62443** (industrial automation), **NIST SP 800-82 Rev.3** (CPS/ICS guide). - ✅ **Lightweight Crypto (NIST IR 8259):** For ultra-constrained CPS nodes, consider **Ascon, Photon-Beetle, PRESENT, SIMON/SPECK**. - ✅ **Key separation principle:** Never use the same key for **encryption, authentication, and key wrapping** — derive separate subkeys via HKDF. - ✅ **AES-CTR limit:** $2^{64}$ blocks per key — beyond this, keystream repeats catastrophically. - ✅ **Side-channel mitigations:** Constant-time implementations, masking, hiding — required for tamper-resistant CPS hardware. - ✅ **CRL vs OCSP:** CRL is bandwidth-heavy (push the whole list); OCSP is lightweight (query per certificate). For CPS, **stapled OCSP** or **short-lived certificates** are preferred. - ✅ **Blockchain-anchored key management** is emerging for **multi-domain CPS** (e.g., federated industrial clusters), but the consensus latency limits real-time applicability.

topics.title: Secure Coding Guidelines for CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Secure Coding Guidelines for CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Secure Coding Guidelines for Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** are a formally documented set of engineering rules, coding conventions, defensive programming techniques, and architectural best practices enforced during the software development lifecycle (SDLC) to eliminate exploitable vulnerabilities in software that directly interacts with physical processes, sensors, actuators, and industrial control networks.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – PECPT413**, secure coding guidelines constitute the *pragmatic bridge* between abstract security policy (defined in Module 1: CPS Architecture & Threat Models) and operational trustworthiness. Unlike generic IT software, CPS code controls real-world physical state—voltage, torque, temperature, valve position, vehicle trajectory—and therefore the *integrity*, *availability*, and *determinism* of code are non-negotiable. A single buffer overflow in a PLC ladder-logic equivalent or in embedded C running on a RTOS can cause physical destruction, not just data loss.

The guidelines are typically derived from industry-recognized standards:
- **CERT C/C++ Secure Coding Standards**
- **MISRA-C / MISRA-C++** (Motor Industry Software Reliability Association)
- **IEC 62443-4-1 / IEC 62443-4-2** (Industrial communication network security)
- **ISO/SAE 21434** (Road vehicles – Cybersecurity engineering)
- **NIST SP 800-218 (SSDF)** – Secure Software Development Framework
- **OWASP Secure Coding Guidelines**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Surgeon’s Checklist:** Think of writing CPS code as performing open-heart surgery on a running patient. A web-developer mistake (a dropped SQL query) causes a webpage glitch; a CPS-developer mistake causes the patient to flatline. Secure coding guidelines are the **pre-operative checklist**—sterilize the inputs, double-check the doses, never trust the monitor blindly, watch for hemorrhage (memory leaks), and always have a kill-switch (fail-safe) wired independently of the main system. The patient (the physical process) cannot be rebooted if the software surgeon makes an error.

**Why Secure Coding is *Harder* in CPS than in IT:**
- **Safety-Coupling:** Bugs can be lethal (Stuxnet centrifuges, pacemaker recalls).
- **Real-Time Constraints:** Security checks cannot violate hard deadlines (e.g., airbag deployment within **5 ms**).
- **Long Lifecycles:** A SCADA RTU deployed today may run 20+ years unpatched.
- **Resource-Constrained Devices:** No room for full TLS, antivirus, or HIDS.
- **Legacy Code:** ICS protocols like Modbus, DNP3, Profinet have **no built-in security**—security must be retrofitted at the application layer.

### 1.3 Core Properties of "Secure CPS Code" (The Pillars)

| Pillar | Definition | Engineering Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Protect sensed data & control logic from eavesdropping | % encrypted channels |
| **Integrity** | Code & data are not tampered with | Code-signing, CRC, hash |
| **Availability** | Control loops execute on time, every time | MTTF, deadline-miss rate |
| **Authenticity** | Only legitimate firmware/commands execute | Signed firmware, MAC |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Audit trail of operator actions | Log immutability |
| **Determinism** | Bounded execution time, no heap fragmentation | WCET analysis |
| **Safety** | Fail to a known safe state | SIL (Safety Integrity Level) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Board Exam Tip: Always link each secure coding rule to **at least one of the CIA+ triad pillars** in your answers. Examiners reward this explicit mapping.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Pillars of Secure CPS Coding (The C-I-A-F-S-A-N Framework)

Every secure coding guideline for CPS can be classified into one of seven logical families. Understanding *why* a rule exists (its threat model) is the key to retaining it for the exam.

#### Pillar I – **Input Validation & Sanitization** (The Bouncer at the Door)

The single most exploited vulnerability in CPS is *untrusted input*—telemetry from spoofed sensors, commands from compromised HMIs, packets from rogue engineering laptops. **Whitelisting** (allow known-good) is mandatory; **blacklisting** (block known-bad) fails because attackers invent new payloads daily.

```c
// UNSAFE: Blacklist approach (bypassable)
if (input == "admin") reject;
// SAFE: Whitelist approach with bounded parsing
if (read_uint16(&input) > MAX_PRESSURE_PSI) {
    raise_alarm(SENSOR_OUT_OF_RANGE);
    actuate_safe_state();
}
```

* **Why it matters in CPS:** A malformed Modbus packet with a length field of `0xFFFF` will overflow naive stack buffers in legacy RTUs.
* **How it works:** Use strict type-checking, range-checking, length-prefixed protocols (TLV – Type-Length-Value), and **defensive parsing libraries** (e.g., Protocol Buffers, CBOR with schema validation).

#### Pillar II – **Memory Safety** (The Foundation of Trustworthy C/C++)

Most CPS firmware is written in **C** for determinism and zero-cost abstractions. C's manual memory model is the #1 source of CVEs. The two governing principles:

$$
\text{No Code Path} = f(\text{undefined behavior})
$$

- **Stack canaries** (GCC `-fstack-protector-strong`) place a random sentinel between the return address and local variables; corruption triggers `__stack_chk_fail`.
- **AddressSanitizer (ASan)** instruments load/store to detect out-of-bounds and use-after-free at runtime.
- **Static analysis** (Coverity, Clang Static Analyzer, Klocwork) finds bugs *before* deployment.
- **Use safe dialects:** MISRA-C forbids `gets()`, dynamic `malloc` after init, recursion in hard real-time tasks.

#### Pillar III – **Authentication & Authorization** (Who Are You, and What May You Do?)

CPS uses **role-based access control (RBAC)** mapped to physical consequences:
- **Operator:** Read telemetry, send setpoints within limits.
- **Engineer:** Modify configuration, upload logic.
- **Maintenance:** Update firmware (requires dual-control / two-person rule).
- **Auditor:** Read-only access to logs.

Use **hardware roots of trust** (TPM 2.0, Secure Element, HSM) to store keys. Never hard-code credentials—use **PUFs (Physically Unclonable Functions)** or secure key provisioning.

#### Pillar IV – **Cryptography Done Right** (The Right Tool, The Right Way)

- **Symmetric:** AES-128/256-GCM for telemetry bulk encryption.
- **Asymmetric:** ECDSA (P-256) for firmware signatures; Ed25519 for modern devices.
- **Hashing:** SHA-256 minimum; avoid MD5/SHA-1 in CPS.
- **Key exchange:** ECDH (X25519) for session setup.
- **Never roll your own crypto.** Use **mbedTLS, WolfSSL, OpenSSL (FIPS 140-2 validated)**, or vendor SDKs.

$$
\text{Key Length Trade-off: } \quad T_{\text{break}} = f(2^n / R_{\text{attack}})
$$

where $n$ = key bits, $R_{\text{attack}}$ = attacker rate (attempts/sec). At $R = 10^{12}$/sec, AES-128 gives **>100 years** of security.

#### Pillar V – **Firmware Integrity & Secure Boot** (Trust Starts at Power-On)

The **Chain of Trust:**
1. ROM bootloader (immutable) verifies signature of Stage-1 bootloader.
2. Stage-1 verifies RTOS image.
3. RTOS verifies application code.
4. Application verifies configuration / PLC logic downloads.

> **Physical Unclonable Function (PUF):**
> $$K = f(\text{Manufacturing Variations of SRAM power-on values})$$
> A unique per-device key generated from silicon imperfections—impossible to clone, never stored in NVM.

#### Pillar VI – **Fail-Safe Design & Watchdog Enforcement** (The "Kill Switch")

A CPS must **fail to a known safe state**, not fail silently. Software mechanisms:
- **Hardware Watchdog Timer (WDT):** A separate counter that the main firmware must "pet" (reset) every cycle. If firmware hangs, WDT expires and resets the MCU to a safe bootloader.
- **Deadman Timer:** Independent timing for sensor signal plausibility (e.g., if a temperature sensor reads identically for 60 s in a furnace, declare it failed).
- **Diversity / Redundancy:** Two dissimilar implementations (e.g., one C, one Simulink-generated) vote on the control output—dissimilarity defeats common-mode bugs.

#### Pillar VII – **Auditability, Logging & Tamper-Evidence** (The Black Box)

Every command, setpoint change, login, and alarm must be logged to **tamper-evident storage** (append-only, hash-chained, optionally signed).
- **Syslog over TLS 1.3** to a central SIEM.
- **Local circular buffer** with hash chain:
$$H_{n+1} = \text{SHA-256}(H_n \Vert \text{Event}_n)$$
- **Time synchronization** via PTP (IEEE 1588) or NTP-with-auth (RFC 5905 + NTS).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Standards Table

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Memory Alignment | $\text{struct size} \equiv 0 \pmod{\text{sizeof(largest member)}}$ | Prevent bus faults on ARM Cortex-M |
| Watchdog Period | $T_{\text{WDT}} > T_{\text{worst-case loop}} + T_{\text{slack}}$ | Typical: 1.5× longest task |
| CRC-32 (Ethernet) | $G(x) = x^{32} + x^{26} + x^{23} + \dots + 1$ | Modbus TCP integrity |
| RSA Strength | $n \geq 2048$ bits (legacy), $n \geq 3072$ bits (2030+) | Key sizing |
| ECDSA | 256-bit key ≈ 3072-bit RSA | Constrained devices |
| Control Loop Jitter | $J = T_{\max} - T_{\min}$ | Must be < 1% of period |
| Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) | $\text{PFD} = \lambda_{\text{DU}} \times T_{\text{Proof}}$ | SIL-2: $10^{-7} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-6}$ |
| Cipher Suite Strength | AES-256-GCM $\equiv$ 256-bit security level | TLS 1.3 mandate |
| Buffer Length Check | $\text{strlen}(s) < n$ **before** `strcpy` | Defensive C |
| Rate Limit | $R_{\text{max}} = \frac{\text{Bucket Size}}{\text{Refill Rate}}$ | DoS protection |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility – Why This Matters Beyond Exams

Secure CPS coding is the **operational backbone of Industry 4.0, smart grids, autonomous vehicles, medical IoT, and critical infrastructure**. A graduate who masters these guidelines is employable as an Embedded Security Engineer, ICS/SCADA Security Analyst, or Functional Safety Engineer. The demand in India's growing smart-manufacturing corridors (Kerala's KINFRA parks, Bengaluru's electronics city, Pune's automotive belt) for IEC 62443 / ISO 21434 certified engineers is doubling annually.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Watchdog Timer (WDT) Sizing for a Hard Real-Time CPS Task

**Problem Statement:**
A CPS controller runs a periodic control task on an RTOS with a worst-case execution time (WCET) of $C = 35$ ms, period $T = 100$ ms, and a hardware watchdog timer (WDT) that must trigger a safe reset if the firmware hangs. The WDT has a fixed prescaler of $1:256$ from a 16 MHz clock. Derive a watchdog reload value that prevents spurious resets but catches genuine hangs within 2 task periods.

---

#### **Step 1 — Identify the Governing Constraint**

To prevent spurious resets, the WDT must outlive the longest legitimate critical section, including:
- The longest task WCET, $C_{\max}$
- Interrupt service routines (ISRs) of priority higher than the control task
- RTOS context switch overhead, $T_{\text{cs}}$

$$
T_{\text{WDT}} > C_{\max} + \sum_{i} C_{\text{ISR},i} + T_{\text{cs}}
$$

Given:
- $C_{\max} = 35$ ms
- Two high-priority ISRs: 2 ms and 3 ms
- Context switch: $T_{\text{cs}} = 0.5$ ms

$$
T_{\text{WDT}} > 35 + 2 + 3 + 0.5 = 40.5 \text{ ms}
$$

#### **Step 2 — Add Engineering Safety Margin (Typically 1.5×)**

The 1.5× factor accounts for clock drift, cache misses, and worst-case bus contention:

$$
T_{\text{WDT,min}} = 1.5 \times 40.5 = 60.75 \text{ ms}
$$

#### **Step 3 — Convert Time to Timer Ticks**

The WDT clock source after prescaler:

$$
f_{\text{WDT}} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{PRESCALER}} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{256} = 62500 \text{ Hz}
$$

Time per tick:

$$
T_{\text{tick}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{WDT}}} = \frac{1}{62500} = 16 \text{ μs}
$$

Reload value (counting from reload down to 0):

$$
N_{\text{reload}} = \left\lceil \frac{T_{\text{WDT,min}}}{T_{\text{tick}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{60.75 \times 10^{-3}}{16 \times 10^{-6}} \right\rceil
$$

$$
N_{\text{reload}} = \left\lceil 3796.875 \right\rceil = 3797 \text{ ticks}
$$

#### **Step 4 — Verify Upper Bound (Hang Detection within 2 Periods)**

The WDT must also fire before the *next legitimate* task would have run:

$$
T_{\text{WDT,max}} = 2 \times T = 2 \times 100 = 200 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
N_{\max} = \left\lfloor \frac{200 \times 10^{-3}}{16 \times 10^{-6}} \right\rfloor = 12500 \text{ ticks}
$$

Our computed $N_{\text{reload}} = 3797$ satisfies $1 \le N_{\text{reload}} \le 12500$. ✓

#### **Step 5 — Code Implementation in C (MISRA-C Compliant Snippet)**

```c
#include <stdint.h>

#define WDT_PRESCALER     256U
#define CLK_FREQ_HZ       16000000UL
#define TICK_FREQ_HZ      (CLK_FREQ_HZ / WDT_PRESCALER)  /* 62500 */
#define WDT_TIMEOUT_MS    61U                            /* rounded up */

static const uint16_t wdt_reload = 
    (uint16_t)(WDT_TIMEOUT_MS * (TICK_FREQ_HZ / 1000U));

void kick_watchdog(void)
{
    /* Pet the dog: write reload value to WDT counter register */
    WDT_REG = wdt_reload;
}

void main_control_loop(void)
{
    init_hardware();
    init_safety_state();   /* Fail-safe: all actuators OFF */
    for (;;) {
        sample_sensors();
        compute_control_law();
        actuate_outputs();
        kick_watchdog();   /* Reset the countdown */
        vTaskDelayUntil(&last_wake_time, TASK_PERIOD_TICKS);
    }
}
```

#### **Step 6 — Analytical Result Matrix**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clock frequency | $f_{\text{clk}}$ | 16 000 000 | Hz |
| Prescaler | — | 256 | — |
| WDT tick frequency | $f_{\text{WDT}}$ | 62 500 | Hz |
| Tick period | $T_{\text{tick}}$ | 16 | μs |
| Minimum WDT period | $T_{\text{WDT,min}}$ | 60.75 | ms |
| Computed reload | $N_{\text{reload}}$ | 3797 | ticks |
| Maximum allowed (2 periods) | $T_{\text{WDT,max}}$ | 200 | ms |
| Safety factor applied | $k$ | 1.5 | — |
| **Verdict** | — | **PASS** | — |

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Mapping Vulnerability → Guideline → Defense

| # | Common CPS Vulnerability | CWE ID | Secure Coding Guideline | Defense Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stack buffer overflow | CWE-121 | Validate all length fields; use safe string libs | `-fstack-protector-strong`, `strncpy_s` |
| 2 | Integer overflow in setpoint | CWE-190 | Range-check before arithmetic | Saturating arithmetic, `__builtin_add_overflow` |
| 3 | Hard-coded credentials | CWE-798 | Externalize secrets to HSM/PUF | TPM 2.0, secure provisioning |
| 4 | Missing firmware signature | CWE-494 | Verify signature at every boot | Secure boot chain |
| 5 | Unauthenticated Modbus command | CWE-306 | Add challenge-response | MAC (CMAC-AES) per IEC 62351 |
| 6 | Command injection via HMI | CWE-77 | Whitelist allowed commands | Schema-based protocol parser |
| 7 | Insecure default configuration | CWE-1188 | Factory-reset to deny-all | Principle of least privilege |
| 8 | Timing side-channel in key compare | CWE-208 | Constant-time comparison | `CRYPTO_memcmp` |
| 9 | Replay of control command | CWE-294 | Add monotonic counter / nonce | Timestamp + session ID |
| 10 | Use-after-free in RTOS task | CWE-416 | Static analysis, ownership discipline | RAII (C++), manual ownership docs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matrix matters for KTU exams:** A 14-mark question will often ask *"List and explain any 5 secure coding practices for CPS."* The above matrix is your gold-mine—you can pick any row, expand it into 2-3 sentences, and earn full marks. Examiners reward **mapping** (vulnerability → rule → mechanism), not just listing.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Secure CPS Software Development Lifecycle (Secure SDLC for CPS)

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the iterative, gated process by which CPS firmware is conceived, coded, verified, deployed, and maintained under secure-coding discipline. Each gate has explicit *exit criteria* that must be met before progression.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Requirements and Threat Modeling STRIDE] --> B[Secure Architecture Design IEC 62443 Zones]
    B --> C[Coding with MISRA C and CERT Rules]
    C --> D[Static Analysis Coverity, Clang SA]
    D --> E{D Static Analysis Pass}
    E -- No --> C
    E -- Yes --> F[Unit Testing with Fault Injection]
    F --> G[Code Review Dual Author]
    G --> H{Review Approved}
    H -- No --> C
    H -- Yes --> I[Dynamic Analysis ASan, UBSan on Target]
    I --> J[Penetration Testing on HIL Bench]
    J --> K{Pen Test Clean}
    K -- No --> L[Remediation and CVE Tracking]
    L --> C
    K -- Yes --> M[Secure Boot Signing and Provisioning]
    M --> N[Field Deployment with OTA Update Channel]
    N --> O[Runtime Monitoring SIEM and WDT]
    O --> P{Vulnerabilities Disclosed}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Patch and Re-sign Firmware]
    Q --> N
    P -- No --> O
```

**Reading the diagram:** Note the *four mandatory exit gates* (D, H, K, P). A KTU examiner will award partial marks for drawing this loop and explicitly labeling the gates—students often forget to mention the *iterative feedback loops* and lose 2-3 marks.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Secure CPS Node

This block diagram illustrates the layered security architecture of a typical CPS edge device (e.g., a smart grid RTU, an automotive ECU, or a PLC). The layers enforce **defense-in-depth**—no single layer failure compromises the system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PHY["Physical and Sensing Layer"]
        SENSORS[Analog Sensors Temperature, Pressure, Flow]
        ACTUATORS[Actuators Valves, Motors, Relays]
    end

    subgraph SECBOOT["Layer 0: Hardware Root of Trust"]
        ROMBL[ROM Bootloader Immutable]
        PUF[PUF Derived Device Key]
        TPM[TPM 2.0 Module]
    end

    subgraph RTOS["Layer 1: Hard RTOS Kernel"]
        SCHED[Deterministic Scheduler]
        WDT[Independent Watchdog Timer]
        MPU[Memory Protection Unit Isolation per Task]
    end

    subgraph APP["Layer 2: Application Logic"]
        CTRL[Control Algorithms PID, MPC]
        VALID[Input Validation Parser Schema Based]
        FSM[Safety Finite State Machine]
    end

    subgraph SECAPP["Layer 3: Security Services"]
        CRYPTO[Crypto Library mbedTLS Wrapper]
        AUTHN[Authentication RBAC Engine]
        AUDIT[Tamper Evident Audit Log]
    end

    subgraph COMM["Layer 4: Secure Communication"]
        TLS[TLS 1.3 or DTLS Channel]
        FWUP[Secure OTA Update Endpoint]
    end

    SENSORS --> VALID
    VALID --> CTRL
    CTRL --> FSM
    FSM --> ACTUATORS
    CTRL --> WDT
    WDT --> ROMBL
    ROMBL --> TPM
    PUF --> CRYPTO
    CRYPTO --> TLS
    TLS --> FWUP
    CTRL --> AUTHN
    CTRL --> AUDIT
```

**Engineering interpretation:** Notice the **vertical trust anchors**—the ROM bootloader, the PUF, and the independent WDT form a *trust triad* that cannot be subverted even if the application is fully compromised. This is a classic exam-winning insight.

### 4.3 Decision Flow – How to Choose a Cryptographic Primitive in CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Need Cryptographic Function] --> Q1{Real Time Budget}
    Q1 -- Under 1 ms --> SYM[Use Symmetric AES GCM]
    Q1 -- Over 1 ms acceptable --> Q2{Asymmetric Required}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Key Size Constraint}
    Q3 -- Under 4 KB flash --> ECC[Use ECDSA P 256 or Ed25519]
    Q3 -- Larger flash --> RSA[RSA 2048 with CRT Optimization]
    Q2 -- No --> SYM
    SYM --> Q4{Authenticated}
    Q4 -- Yes --> GCM[AES 128 or 256 GCM]
    Q4 -- No --> CTR[AES CTR with Separate HMAC]
    ECC --> SIG[Digital Signature for Firmware]
    RSA --> SIG
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:** Students routinely lose 2-4 marks by (1) confusing **CIA** (security) with **SIL** (safety) — they are *complementary but distinct*; (2) forgetting to **map a guideline to a specific CPS threat** (e.g., "input validation prevents fuzzing attacks on Modbus"); (3) writing "use encryption" without specifying **algorithm, mode, and key length**; (4) ignoring the **fail-safe** requirement; (5) using the phrase "strong password" in a CPS context (CPS often has *no* passwords, only certificates and HSM keys). **Always show the HOW, not just the WHAT.**

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define the term "Secure Coding Guidelines" in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. List any four families of such guidelines.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Secure coding guidelines for CPS are a documented set of programming rules, defensive techniques, and best practices applied during software development to eliminate exploitable vulnerabilities in code that interacts with physical processes. They ensure **confidentiality, integrity, availability, authenticity, and safety** of the CPS.

Four families:
1. **Input validation and sanitization** (whitelisting, range checks).
2. **Memory safety** (no overflow, use of safe libs, static analysis).
3. **Authentication, authorization, and access control** (RBAC, HSM).
4. **Cryptographic hygiene** (correct algorithm selection, key management, no hard-coded secrets).
*(Alternatively, secure boot, fail-safe design, audit logging are also acceptable.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*Differentiate between a hardware watchdog timer (WDT) and a deadman timer in CPS. Why is having both considered a best practice?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Hardware WDT:** A separate, independent timer that the main firmware must periodically *pet*. If firmware hangs, the WDT expires and triggers a system reset. Detects *control flow* failures.
- **Deadman Timer:** Monitors *data plausibility* (e.g., a sensor value stuck, communication lost for N seconds). Detects *semantic* failures where the firmware is alive but operating on bad data.

**Why both?** They are *complementary*. WDT alone misses cases where the firmware runs but produces nonsensical output. Deadman alone misses cases where firmware hangs entirely. Together they form a **defense-in-depth** check on both timing and semantic correctness — essential for safety-critical CPS.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **seven pillars of secure CPS coding** with one example guideline per pillar. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design a **secure boot chain** for a CPS edge device. List the entities involved, the cryptographic primitives, and explain the **chain-of-trust** concept. Justify why the ROM bootloader must be **immutable**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Seven Pillars (7 marks – 1 mark per pillar):**

1. **Input Validation & Sanitization:** All external inputs (sensor data, HMI commands, network packets) are validated against a strict whitelist of expected format, range, and length. *Example:* Reject Modbus packets with length field > 260 bytes.

2. **Memory Safety:** Use of safe C dialects (MISRA-C), bounds-checked functions, stack canaries, ASan instrumentation. *Example:* Replace `strcpy` with `strncpy_s(dst, n, src, n)`.

3. **Authentication & Authorization:** Role-Based Access Control. Only authenticated, authorized operators may issue setpoint changes. *Example:* Engineer role requires dual-person approval for PLC logic download.

4. **Cryptographic Hygiene:** Use of vetted libraries (mbedTLS, OpenSSL FIPS); no custom crypto; AES-GCM for confidentiality + integrity; ECDSA-P256 for signatures. *Example:* Sign firmware with Ed25519 before OTA deployment.

5. **Firmware Integrity & Secure Boot:** Cryptographic verification of every boot stage. *Example:* ROM bootloader verifies Stage-1 image signature before execution.

6. **Fail-Safe & Watchdog:** Independent hardware WDT; fail-to-safe-state on anomaly. *Example:* If pressure sensor is silent for 60 s, vent valve opens.

7. **Auditability & Tamper-Evidence:** Hash-chained append-only logs. *Example:* Every operator action hashed with previous log entry using SHA-256.

**(b) Secure Boot Chain (7 marks):**

**Entities:**
- **ROM Bootloader (BL0):** Hard-coded, immutable, in mask ROM.
- **Stage-1 Bootloader (BL1):** Verifies and loads BL2.
- **RTOS Image (BL2 / OS):** Verified before execution.
- **Application Code (APP):** Verified by the OS loader.
- **Hardware Root of Trust:** PUF or TPM 2.0 storing the *root public key* $K_{\text{root}}$.

**Cryptographic Primitives:**
- **Hash:** SHA-256.
- **Signature:** ECDSA-P256 (or Ed25519) over the image.
- **Key Storage:** PUF-generated device key, fused root public key in OTP.

**Chain of Trust Logic:**

$$
\text{BL0 executes unconditionally (immutable ROM)}.
$$
$$
\text{BL0 verifies: } \text{ECDSA\_Verify}(K_{\text{root}}, \text{SHA-256}(BL1), \sigma_{BL1}) \stackrel{?}{=} \text{True}.
$$
$$
\text{If True} \rightarrow \text{Execute BL1}.
$$
$$
\text{BL1 verifies BL2/OS} \rightarrow \text{BL2 verifies APP}.
$$

If **any** verification fails, the bootloader enters **recovery mode**: accepts only a *signed* recovery image from a physically-authenticated channel (e.g., service-tool with certificate).

**Why ROM must be immutable:** ROM (mask ROM, eFuse, OTP) is *physically* non-rewritable. An attacker who compromises Flash/EEPROM cannot modify the ROM, so the *first link* of the chain remains trustworthy. Without immutability, the attacker could substitute a malicious BL0 that skips all signature checks — defeating the entire chain.

**Engineering Justification:** This architecture is used in **STM32 Secure Boot**, **NXP i.MX High Assurance Boot (HAB)**, and **TI MSP432 bootloader**. It defends against persistent firmware implants like **VPNFilter, Triton/TRISIS, and Industroyer**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the **OWASP / CERT secure coding rules** that are most critical for CPS. Compare and contrast **MISRA-C** and **CERT C** as coding standards. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A CPS uses **Modbus TCP** for field-device communication. The legacy protocol has no authentication or integrity protection. Propose a **defense-in-depth secure coding strategy** to mitigate this, including cryptographic wrapping, key management, and rate-limiting. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Critical OWASP/CERT Rules & Standard Comparison (7 marks):**

**Top CERT C rules for CPS:**
| Rule ID | Title | Why it matters in CPS |
|---|---|---|
| **ARR30-C** | Do not use floating-point variables as loop counters | Determinism in control loops |
| **ERR33-C** | Detect and handle errors at function boundaries | Fault propagation in RTOS |
| **INT32-C** | Ensure operations on signed integers do not result in overflow | Setpoint arithmetic |
| **MEM30-C** | Do not access freed memory | RTOS task lifetime mgmt |
| **STR31-C** | Guarantee that storage for strings has sufficient space | Buffer overflows in HMI parsers |
| **FIO30-C** | Exclude user input from format strings | Command injection |
| **MSC30-C** | Do not use the rand() function for security | Predictable session tokens |

**MISRA-C vs CERT C — Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | MISRA-C | CERT C |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Automotive (UK) | Software security (US, SEI/CMU) |
| **Goal** | Functional safety (ISO 26262) | Cybersecurity |
| **Coverage** | Style, language subset, undefined behavior | Vulnerabilities (memory, crypto, input) |
| **Enforcement** | Strict, often required by OEMs | Advisory, priority-tagged |
| **C Standard** | MISRA-C:2012 (C99) targets | C11/C17 broader |
| **Use in CPS** | Mandatory for SIL-rated systems | Mandatory for security-rated systems |
| **Overlap** | ~30% rules identical | ~30% rules identical |

**Verdict:** For safety-critical CPS (vehicles, avionics), **MISRA-C is king**. For security-critical CPS (smart grid, medical), **CERT C is king**. Best practice: **adopt both**, with MISRA providing the safety substrate and CERT providing the security overlay.

**(b) Defense-in-Depth for Modbus TCP (7 marks):**

Modbus TCP (port 502) was designed in 1979 with **no security** — it is plaintext, unauthenticated, and uses a simple 16-bit CRC. The following layered strategy secures it without breaking compatibility with legacy field devices.

**Layer 1 — Network Segmentation & Firewalling**
- Place Modbus devices in an **IEC 62443 Security Zone** (e.g., Zone 1: Field Devices) with controlled Conduits.
- Industrial firewall (e.g., Cisco ISA3000, Fortinet) denies any Modbus traffic originating outside the control LAN.
- VLAN + ACL on managed switches.

**Layer 2 — Cryptographic Wrapping with IEC 62351-3 (TLS)**
- Tunnel Modbus TCP inside **TLS 1.3** (DTLS for UDP variants).
- **Mutual authentication:** Both client (HMI) and server (RTU) present X.509 certificates issued by the plant PKI.
- Cipher suite mandate: `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384` (256-bit security level).
- Session resumption: tickets or session IDs to limit handshake cost.

**Layer 3 — Application-Layer Message Authentication (IEC 62351-6)**
- For devices too constrained for full TLS, append a **CMAC-AES-128** tag computed over the Modbus PDU using a pre-shared session key.
- Sequence number inside the tag prevents **replay** attacks.

**Layer 4 — Rate Limiting & DoS Mitigation**
- Token-bucket rate limit per source IP:

$$
R_{\max} = \frac{B_{\text{bucket}}}{T_{\text{refill}}} = \frac{100 \text{ pkts}}{1 \text{ s}} = 100 \text{ pps}
$$

- Exceeding limit → alarm + temporary blackhole (operator review).
- Per-MB-function-code whitelist (e.g., function code `0x06` Write Single Register disabled for non-engineer roles).

**Layer 5 — Audit Logging & Anomaly Detection**
- Every Modbus transaction logged to SIEM with timestamp, source, function code, register, value.
- Baseline of "normal" traffic; ML/rule-based detection of out-of-band setpoints (e.g., a normally-closed valve commanded to open at 03:00).

**Layer 6 — Code-Level Hardening in the Modbus Library**
- Use a fuzzed, hardened Modbus library (e.g., **libmodbus with STRIDE-reviewed patches**).
- Compile with `-fstack-protector-strong -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 -fPIE -pie`.
- Static analysis with Coverity/CodeQL in CI/CD.

**Layer 7 — Safety Interlock Independent of Modbus**
- A hard-wired safety relay that **physically** disables the actuator if an out-of-range setpoint is detected at the I/O layer — *cannot be subverted by Modbus compromise*.

> **Resulting Defense-in-Depth Model:** An attacker must simultaneously defeat: (i) the firewall, (ii) TLS, (iii) the CMAC tag, (iv) the rate limiter, (v) the safety interlock. The probability of full compromise drops from **~0.95** (single-layer) to **< 0.001** (seven layers), assuming independent failure modes.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

- ✅ **Definition:** Secure coding guidelines = documented rules to eliminate vulnerabilities in CPS software.
- ✅ **The 7 Pillars:** Input validation, Memory safety, AuthN/AuthZ, Crypto hygiene, Secure boot, Fail-safe, Auditability.
- ✅ **Memory Safety Tools:** `-fstack-protector-strong`, ASan, MISRA-C, static analyzers.
- ✅ **Crypto Mandate:** AES-128/256-GCM, ECDSA-P256, SHA-256 minimum; **never roll your own**; **never hard-code keys**.
- ✅ **WDT Sizing Rule:** $T_{\text{WDT}} > 1.5 \times (C_{\max} + \text{ISRs} + \text{CS})$ AND $T_{\text{WDT}} < 2T_{\text{period}}$.
- ✅ **Secure Boot Chain:** ROM → BL1 → OS → APP, each verifies next via ECDSA, root key in PUF/TPM.
- ✅ **Standards to Name in Answers:** MISRA-C, CERT C/C++, IEC 62443-4-1/2, IEC 62351, ISO/SAE 21434, NIST SSDF.
- ✅ **Modbus Defense Layers:** Segmentation + TLS (IEC 62351-3) + CMAC (IEC 62351-6) + Rate-Limit + Audit + Hardening + Safety Interlock.
- ✅ **Key Distinction:** *Safety* (fail-safe, SIL, ISO 26262) is **not the same** as *Security* (CIA, IEC 62443); CPS needs **both**.
- ✅ **Watchdog ≠ Deadman:** WDT detects *hangs*; Deadman detects *plausibility failure* of data.
- ✅ **PUF:** Hardware-derived device key from silicon imperfections; unclonable.
- ✅ **Hash-Chained Logs:** $H_{n+1} = \text{SHA-256}(H_n \| \text{Event}_n)$ — tamper-evidence.
- ✅ **Exam Mantra:** Always answer with **Threat → Rule → Mechanism → Metric** structure.

**Final Study Tip:** The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to demonstrate *engineering judgment*, not just rote recall. For any secure-coding question, frame your answer in the **STRIDE** threat-model vocabulary (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege). This single word — *STRIDE* — used correctly 2-3 times in a 14-mark answer, signals examiner-level thinking and consistently earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing Methodologies (Unit Testing, Integration Testing) for Secure CPS Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Testing Methodologies (Unit Testing, Integration Testing) for Secure CPS Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Testing Methodologies in CPS — Formal Definition

A **Testing Methodology** is a systematic, formally defined engineering strategy used to validate the correctness, reliability, safety, and security of a software/hardware system by executing the system under controlled conditions and comparing observed behavior against expected outcomes. In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, testing methodologies refer to the structured procedures—unit, integration, system, and acceptance testing—that verify both the *cyber* (computational, networking, control logic) and *physical* (sensor, actuator, process) layers, while actively probing for **vulnerabilities, fault-tolerance, and adversarial resilience**.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme PECPT413 syllabus**, testing for secure CPS is not a passive verification activity—it is an **active adversarial probing process** where tests are designed to break assumptions and uncover hidden failure modes that could be exploited in real-world deployments (e.g., a malicious actor injecting false sensor data into an industrial PLC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Testing methodologies for CPS differ from traditional IT testing because **safety, timing, and physical-world consequences** are non-negotiable constraints. A bug in a CPS is not just a downtime event—it can cause physical damage, injury, or loss of life.

## 1.2 The Two Foundational Layers: Unit Testing vs. Integration Testing

### 🔬 Unit Testing
**Unit Testing** is the lowest level of software testing in which *individual, isolated units* of source code (functions, methods, classes, or hardware abstraction modules) are tested independently to validate that each performs as designed. For secure CPS, a "unit" often includes:
- A single control algorithm (e.g., a PID controller function)
- A sensor data parsing routine
- An authentication handshake function
- A cryptographic primitive (e.g., AES encrypt/decrypt wrapper)

### 🔗 Integration Testing
**Integration Testing** is the phase where *multiple units are combined and tested as a group* to expose faults in the **interaction, communication, data flow, timing, and security boundaries** between integrated components. For CPS, integration testing must also verify the **cyber–physical handshake**: that the controller correctly receives sensor data, makes decisions, and sends validated commands to actuators without race conditions, data corruption, or injection attacks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction in CPS Context:** Unit testing asks *"Does this code module work correctly in isolation?"* — Integration testing asks *"Do the modules talk to each other safely, securely, and in real-time when connected to the physical world?"*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Automotive ECU Factory"

Imagine a **modern car's Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** being assembled:

- **Unit Testing** = testing each *individual car part* on a bench:
  - Does the brake pedal sensor correctly output 0–5V?
  - Does the airbag detonation function trigger within 10 ms?
  - Does the cryptographic signing function produce valid HMAC-SHA256 outputs?
  
  Each part is tested **standalone** before being installed.

- **Integration Testing** = assembling parts and testing their *interaction*:
  - When the brake pedal is pressed, does the sensor → ECU → brake actuator chain respond in **< 100 ms**?
  - Can a malicious CAN bus message trick the ECU into applying brakes when none were commanded?
  - Do the ABS controller and stability control module exchange data without timing collisions?

- **Why Security?** = Imagine a hacker tries to inject a fake "stop engine" message. **Unit testing alone won't catch this**—only integration testing, with adversarial test cases, will reveal the vulnerability.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** When asked in KTU exams about the *difference* between unit and integration testing, always emphasize the **interaction boundary**. A common student mistake is to say "integration tests more features"—the correct framing is that integration tests the **interfaces, data contracts, and timing behavior** *between* units.

## 1.4 Why Security-Specific Testing for CPS?

Traditional testing aims for **functional correctness**. **Secure CPS testing** additionally requires:

| Traditional Testing Concern | Secure CPS Extension |
|---|---|
| Does the code work? | Does the code work under **adversarial inputs**? |
| Is the output correct? | Is the output **authentic and tamper-proof**? |
| Does it meet timing? | Does it meet timing **even under DoS/replay attacks**? |
| Does it integrate cleanly? | Does it integrate **without privilege escalation paths**? |

The standard testing goal is **verification**; the secure CPS testing goal is **verification + adversarial validation + safety assurance**.

# 🔍 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Testing Pyramid for Secure CPS

Modern secure CPS testing follows a **layered pyramid** (analogous to Mike Cohn's test pyramid), adapted with a *security overlay*:

1. **Static Analysis Layer (base)** — SAST tools, linting, formal verification, model checking.
2. **Unit Testing Layer** — Isolation tests of individual modules (controllers, crypto, parsers).
3. **Integration Testing Layer** — Combined modules with mocked/simulated physical interfaces.
4. **System/HIL Testing Layer** — Hardware-in-the-loop with real sensors/actuators.
5. **Acceptance / Penetration Testing Layer (apex)** — Real-world deployment validation, red-team adversarial testing.

## 2.2 Unit Testing — Operational Breakdown

Unit testing for secure CPS proceeds through the following structured phases:

- **Identification of Units:** Decompose CPS software into atomic testable modules. A typical CPS unit may correspond to:
  - A single task in a real-time operating system (RTOS).
  - A state-machine transition in a PLC.
  - A cryptographic primitive (AES block, HMAC computation).
  - A Kalman filter step.
- **Test Case Design:** Each unit must have **positive tests** (expected inputs → expected outputs), **negative tests** (invalid/malicious inputs → safe failure), and **boundary tests** (min/max sensor values, timing deadlines).
- **Mocking Physical Interfaces:** CPS units interact with sensors/actuators. In unit tests, these are replaced with **mocks/stubs** (e.g., simulating a temperature sensor reading 150°C when normal range is 0–100°C).
- **Execution & Assertion:** Run the unit against test cases; assert outputs match expected behavior, including exception handling.
- **Code Coverage Analysis:** Measure the percentage of code paths exercised by tests.
- **Regression Re-execution:** On every code change, re-run all unit tests to ensure no regressions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Security Enhancement at Unit Level:** Each unit test for CPS must include at least one **adversarial input** case. Example: feeding a parser with a malformed CAN frame, oversized payload, or a packet with corrupted HMAC to ensure the unit *rejects and does not crash*.

## 2.3 Integration Testing — Operational Breakdown

Integration testing for CPS has **four recognized strategies**:

- **Big-Bang Integration:** All units combined at once, tested as a whole. *Fast but fault localization is difficult.*
- **Top-Down Integration:** Start from top-level controllers, use stubs for lower-level units. *Good for validating high-level control logic first.*
- **Bottom-Up Integration:** Start from sensor/actuator drivers, work upward. *Good for CPS where physical layer reliability is paramount.*
- **Sandwich / Hybrid Integration:** Combines top-down and bottom-up; uses a target layer in the middle. *Most practical for real CPS projects.*

For **secure CPS**, integration testing also includes:

- **Interface Contract Testing:** Verifies that data exchanged between units meets expected schema, range, and authentication (e.g., signed sensor readings).
- **Timing & Real-Time Testing:** Verifies that the integrated chain meets **deadline constraints** under both normal and adversarial load (e.g., 10 ms control loop maintained even during simulated DoS).
- **Fault Injection Testing:** Deliberately inject sensor failures, communication dropouts, or bit-flips to verify graceful degradation.
- **Adversarial Integration Testing:** Simulate an attacker injecting malicious commands at the integration boundary and verify the system rejects them.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following metrics and formulas are essential for KTU 2024 Scheme exam answers on this topic.

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Range | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Statement Coverage (SC)** | $SC = \frac{\text{Executed Statements}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100\%$ | Percent (0–100) | Weakest coverage metric; required by KTU as baseline |
| **Branch Coverage (BC)** | $BC = \frac{\text{Executed Branches}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100\%$ | Percent (0–100) | Also called Decision Coverage; stronger than SC |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity (V(G))** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Integer $\geq 1$ | $E$=edges, $N$=nodes, $P$=connected components in CFG |
| **McCabe's Alternate Formula** | $V(G) = \pi + 1$ | Integer $\geq 1$ | $\pi$ = number of predicate nodes in flow graph |
| **Path Coverage Bound** | $\text{Min Test Cases} \geq V(G)$ | Integer | Minimum tests needed to cover all independent paths |
| **Modified Condition/Decision Coverage (MC/DC)** | Every condition must independently affect the decision outcome | Boolean per condition | Required in **DO-178C** (aerospace) and **ISO 26262** (automotive) |
| **Mutation Score (MS)** | $MS = \frac{\text{Killed Mutants}}{\text{Total Mutants}} \times 100\%$ | Percent (0–100) | Tests the *quality* of the test suite itself |
| **Fault Detection Ratio (FDR)** | $FDR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \times 100\%$ | Percent (0–100) | $TP$=true positives, $FN$=false negatives |
| **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** | $MTTD = \frac{\sum \text{Detection Times}}{\text{Number of Faults}}$ | Time units | Critical for secure CPS incident response |
| **Integration Test Coverage (ITC)** | $ITC = \frac{\text{Integrated Paths Tested}}{\text{Total Integrated Paths}} \times 100\%$ | Percent (0–100) | Distinct from unit coverage |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These testing strategies and metrics are mandated across regulated CPS industries:

- **Aviation:** DO-178C requires **MC/DC coverage at Level A** (catastrophic failure).
- **Automotive:** ISO 26262 mandates ASIL-D systems to achieve ≥ **100% statement + branch + MC/DC** coverage.
- **Medical Devices:** IEC 62304 requires documented unit, integration, and system testing for Class III devices (e.g., pacemakers, infusion pumps).
- **Industrial Control:** IEC 62443 (industrial cybersecurity) requires security testing at every integration boundary.
- **Smart Grid:** NISTIR 7628 mandates penetration testing and fault injection for all PMU/smart meter integrations.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cyclomatic Complexity from a Control Flow Graph

**Cyclomatic complexity** $V(G)$ is one of the most testable KTU exam derivations. Below is the exhaustive, step-by-step derivation using graph theory.

### Step 1 — Define the Control Flow Graph (CFG)
A program is modeled as a **directed graph** $G = (N, E)$ where:
- $N$ = set of nodes representing program statements / basic blocks.
- $E$ = set of edges representing control flow between statements.
- $P$ = number of connected components (for a single program, $P = 1$).

### Step 2 — State the Formal Definition
Cyclomatic complexity is defined as the number of **linearly independent paths** through the program's source code. Mathematically, it is the size of a **cyclomatic set** of paths.

### Step 3 — Derive Using Euler's Formula for Planar Graphs
For a planar connected graph, Euler's formula states:

$$V - E + F = 2$$

where $F$ is the number of faces (regions) enclosed by the graph.

### Step 4 — Map to Program Structure
In the program control flow graph, the number of faces $F$ equals the number of bounded regions *plus* the outer (unbounded) region. Each bounded region corresponds to a **loop or conditional** in the program. Therefore, for a connected CFG, the number of independent paths $V(G)$ equals the number of bounded regions plus 1.

### Step 5 — Algebraic Rearrangement
Rearranging Euler's formula:

$$F = 2 - V + E = E - V + 2$$

For a connected program ($P = 1$), the number of independent cycles (paths) is:

$$V(G) = E - N + 2$$

### Step 6 — Generalization for Disconnected Graphs
If the CFG is not connected (e.g., multiple functions analyzed together), $P$ represents the number of connected components. The general formula becomes:

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

### Step 7 — Alternate Form via Predicate Nodes
Each predicate (decision point: `if`, `while`, `for`, `case`, `&&`, `||`) adds one independent path. For a single connected graph:

$$V(G) = \pi + 1$$

where $\pi$ is the number of predicate nodes.

### Step 8 — Worked Example

Consider the following pseudo-CPS code (a simplified temperature controller):

```
int tempCtl(int currentTemp, int threshold) {
    if (currentTemp > threshold) {        // predicate 1
        if (currentTemp > threshold + 10) // predicate 2
            return EMERGENCY_SHUTDOWN;
        else
            return ACTIVATE_COOLING;
    } else {
        return IDLE;
    }
}
```

Constructing the CFG:
- Nodes (basic blocks): `N = 6` (entry, p1-true, p2-true, p2-false, p1-false, exit)
- Edges (control flows): `E = 8`
- Connected components: `P = 1`
- Predicate nodes: `p1`, `p2` → $\pi = 2$

Applying the formulas:

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P = 8 - 6 + 2(1) = 4$$

$$V(G) = \pi + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reconciliation:** When CFG modeling accounts for the implicit "merge" edges at function boundaries, the value 4 emerges. When using the **predicate count** method strictly (which counts only explicit decision points), the value is 3. For KTU exams, **state both** and clarify the modeling assumption.

### Step 9 — Engineering Interpretation
A cyclomatic complexity of **3** means at least **3 independent unit test cases** are needed to traverse every linearly independent path. For secure CPS, this is the *minimum*; **adversarial tests for each path** are then layered on top.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical: Mutation Score Calculation

A CPS security testing team writes a test suite and applies mutation testing with **50 mutants** injected into the controller module. After running tests, **42 mutants are killed** (i.e., at least one test fails for each).

### Step 1 — Identify Known Quantities
Total mutants: $M_{total} = 50$
Killed mutants: $M_{killed} = 42$

### Step 2 — Apply Mutation Score Formula

$$MS = \frac{M_{killed}}{M_{total}} \times 100\%$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and Compute

$$MS = \frac{42}{50} \times 100\% = 0.84 \times 100\% = 84\%$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

An $84\%$ mutation score is **acceptable** for many commercial systems, but **insufficient for safety-critical CPS** (ISO 26262 typically requires ≥ **95%** mutation score for ASIL-D components). The remaining 8 surviving mutants indicate the test suite has **detection gaps** — potentially for security-critical edge cases.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Testing Strategy

The following matrix helps select the appropriate testing strategy for a CPS component based on its **safety criticality** and **security exposure**.

| Component Type | Safety Class | Security Exposure | Recommended Primary Testing | Minimum Coverage Target |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cruise control algorithm | ASIL-B | Moderate | Unit + Integration + HIL | 100% statement, 100% branch |
| Airbag detonation logic | ASIL-D | Critical | Unit + Integration + Fault Injection + Adversarial | 100% MC/DC, 95% mutation |
| Crypto authentication module | ASIL-D | Critical | Unit + Integration + Penetration | 100% branch, 100% mutation |
| Dashboard display | QM (Quality Managed) | Low | Unit + System | 80% statement |
| V2X communication handler | ASIL-B | High | Unit + Integration + Adversarial | 100% branch, 90% mutation |
| Telemetry logger | QM | Low | Unit + Integration | 70% statement |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Security-Aware Unit Test Case Design

Let us design unit test cases for the AES-128 encryption function used in a CPS CAN bus message authenticator.

### Step 1 — Identify the Unit Under Test (UUT)
Function: `aes128_encrypt(plaintext[16], key[16]) → ciphertext[16]`

### Step 2 — Define Test Categories

**Category A — Functional Correctness:**
- TC-A1: Known-answer test (NIST FIPS-197 test vector #1).
- TC-A2: Known-answer test (NIST FIPS-197 test vector #2).

**Category B — Boundary Inputs:**
- TC-B1: All-zero plaintext, all-zero key.
- TC-B2: All-0xFF plaintext, all-0xFF key.

**Category C — Adversarial / Security Tests:**
- TC-C1: Plaintext with high Hamming weight (potential side-channel leak detector).
- TC-C2: Plaintext identical to key (known plaintext attack vector).
- TC-C3: Malformed input length (should be safely rejected, not crash).
- TC-C4: Key reuse detection across 1000 calls (should still produce distinct ciphertexts via proper IV).

### Step 3 — Execute and Measure

| Test Category | # Tests | Coverage Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| Functional (A) | 2 | Statement: ~60% |
| Boundary (B) | 2 | Statement: ~80% |
| Adversarial (C) | 4 | Statement: ~95%, Branch: 100% |

### Step 4 — Iterate
Add additional tests until the **branch coverage = 100%** and **mutation score ≥ 95%** are achieved for safety-critical crypto units.
