   - Mahalanobis distance: $S(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$.
   - Reconstruction error: $S(\mathbf{x}) = \Vert \mathbf{x} - D(E(\mathbf{x})) \Vert_2^2$.
   - Negative log-likelihood: $S(\mathbf{x}) = -\log p_{\mathcal{M}}(\mathbf{x})$.
4. **Threshold Decision:** Compare $S(\mathbf{x})$ against an operating threshold $\tau$:
   $$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{ALERT (Attack)} & \text{if } S(\mathbf{x}) > \tau \\ \text{NORMAL} & \text{if } S(\mathbf{x}) \leq \tau \end{cases}$$
5. **Model Update:** Periodically retrain or adapt $\mathcal{M}$ to handle *concept drift* (e.g., seasonal changes in power demand).

> [!NOTE]
> In CPS, the choice of $\tau$ is driven by an **acceptable False Alarm Rate** constraint dictated by the plant's safety margin — for a nuclear reactor, $\tau$ is set extremely high (rare alarms, but each must be true), whereas for an HVAC system, $\tau$ may be relaxed to catch more anomalies.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **mandatory formula kit** for any numerical or analytical question on this topic in the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE). All boundary conditions and units are exam-relevant.

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Detection Probability (TPR / Recall) | $P_D = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq P_D \leq 1$ |
| 2 | False Alarm Rate (FPR) | $P_{FA} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq P_{FA} \leq 1$ |
| 3 | Miss Rate | $P_M = 1 - P_D = \dfrac{FN}{TP + FN}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Precision (Positive Predictive Value) | $\text{PPV} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq \text{PPV} \leq 1$ |
| 5 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{PPV} \cdot P_D}{\text{PPV} + P_D}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq F_1 \leq 1$ |
| 6 | Bayes' Posterior (Alert given Observation) | $P(A \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid A)\, P(A)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$ | Probability density in $p(\mathbf{x})$ |
| 7 | Mahalanobis Anomaly Score | $S(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$ | $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ must be PD; $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 8 | Multivariate Gaussian Likelihood | $p(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2} S(\mathbf{x})^2\right)$ | $\vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert$ is the determinant |
| 9 | Neyman–Pearson Decision Rule | Choose $\tau$ to maximize $P_D$ subject to $P_{FA} \leq \alpha$ | $\alpha$ is the FPR budget |
| 10 | Detection Latency | $L_d = t_{\text{alert}} - t_{\text{attack onset}}$ | Seconds / milliseconds (CPS-critical) |
| 11 | ROC–AUC Integral | $\text{AUC} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{1} P_D(P_{FA})\, dP_{FA}$ | $0.5 \leq \text{AUC} \leq 1$ for a useful detector |
| 12 | Autoencoder Reconstruction Error | $S(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$ | Mean Squared Error per feature |
| 13 | Concept Drift (Page–Hinkley) | $U_T = \sum_{t=1}^{T} (x_t - \bar{x}_T - \delta)$, alarm if $U_T > \lambda$ | $\delta$ = tolerance, $\lambda$ = threshold |
| 14 | Information-Theoretic Surprise | $S(\mathbf{x}) = -\log_2 p(\mathbf{x})$ | Measured in **bits** (Shannon) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** If a question asks you to *compare* the two paradigms, you **must** cite formulas (1)–(5) and (9)–(10) by name. Writing only prose loses 2–3 marks in a 14-mark question.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| CPS Domain | Preferred Paradigm | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **SCADA / Substation Automation** | Hybrid (SBD for known worms like Havex/BlackEnergy + ABD for process anomalies) | Protocol-bound signatures are effective; ABD catches load-altering attacks |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | ABD (with HMM/LSTM on CAN bus) | Zero-day CAN-injection attacks cannot be signature-matched |
| **Medical Devices (Pacemaker, Insulin Pump)** | ABD (one-class models on vital signs) | Patient safety → minimize $P_M$ even at cost of $P_{FA}$ |
| **Smart Home IoT** | SBD (lightweight, resource-constrained) | Compute budget is too small for ML inference |
| **Industrial Robotics** | ABD (kinematic model-based) | Unusual joint torques/velocities indicate attacks or faults |
| **Smart Grid Demand Response** | ABD (time-series forecasting) | Load profiles have strong temporal patterns exploitable by ABD |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

This section provides fully expanded derivations that may be asked in **Part B (14-mark)** analytical questions. **All algebraic transitions are explicitly shown**; no step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: F1-Score from First Principles

**Starting point:** F1 is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, defined as:

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{\text{PPV}} + \frac{1}{P_D}}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the definitions of PPV and $P_D$:**

$$\text{PPV} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}, \qquad P_D = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**Step 2 — Invert both:**

$$\frac{1}{\text{PPV}} = \frac{TP + FP}{TP} = 1 + \frac{FP}{TP}$$

$$\frac{1}{P_D} = \frac{TP + FN}{TP} = 1 + \frac{FN}{TP}$$

**Step 3 — Add the inverses:**

$$\frac{1}{\text{PPV}} + \frac{1}{P_D} = 2 + \frac{FP + FN}{TP}$$

**Step 4 — Invert the sum to obtain $F_1$:**

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{2 + \frac{FP + FN}{TP}} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by 1/(TP + FN):**

Using $TP + FN + FP + TN = N$ (total samples), and noting that $F_1$ can also be written as:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

> This is the **closed-form expression** of F1 directly in terms of the confusion matrix entries. KTU examiners accept either the harmonic-mean form or this form.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mahalanobis Distance as an Anomaly Score

**Setup:** Let the normal CPS state be modeled as a multivariate Gaussian $\mathbf{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$, where $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is positive-definite.

**Step 1 — Write the joint Gaussian PDF:**

$$p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2}\, \vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) \right)$$

**Step 2 — Take the negative log-likelihood (this becomes our score $S(\mathbf{x})$):**

$$-\log p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi) + \frac{1}{2}\log \vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert + \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})$$

**Step 3 — Drop the constants (which do not depend on $\mathbf{x}$):**

$$S(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})$$

**Step 4 — Identify the quadratic form as the squared Mahalanobis distance:**

$$S(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}\, d_M^2(\mathbf{x})$$

$$d_M(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$$

**Step 5 — Decision rule under Neyman–Pearson:** For a given false-alarm budget $\alpha$, set the threshold $\tau$ such that:

$$P(S(\mathbf{x}) > \tau \mid H_0) = \alpha$$

where $H_0$ is the "no-attack" hypothesis. For the Gaussian model, $d_M^2(\mathbf{x}) \sim \chi^2_n$ (chi-squared with $n$ degrees of freedom), so:

$$\tau = \frac{1}{2} \chi^2_{n,\, 1-\alpha}$$

This is the **operational threshold** in closed form. In a KTU 14-mark question, deriving $\tau$ from the chi-squared distribution earns full marks under the "analytical reasoning" CO.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bayes' Posterior for CPS Alert Decision

**Step 1 — State the prior probabilities.** Let $A$ = "attack in progress" and $\bar{A}$ = "no attack".

$$P(A) = \pi_1, \qquad P(\bar{A}) = \pi_0, \qquad \pi_0 + \pi_1 = 1$$

**Step 2 — State the class-conditional densities of the observation $\mathbf{x}$:**

$$p(\mathbf{x} \mid A) = f_1(\mathbf{x}), \qquad p(\mathbf{x} \mid \bar{A}) = f_0(\mathbf{x})$$

**Step 3 — Write Bayes' theorem for the posterior probability of attack given $\mathbf{x}$:**

$$P(A \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{f_1(\mathbf{x})\, \pi_1}{f_1(\mathbf{x})\, \pi_1 + f_0(\mathbf{x})\, \pi_0}$$

**Step 4 — Define the likelihood ratio:**

$$\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{f_1(\mathbf{x})}{f_0(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Step 5 — Express the decision in terms of $\Lambda(\mathbf{x})$:**

$$P(A \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{\Lambda(\mathbf{x})\, \pi_1}{\Lambda(\mathbf{x})\, \pi_1 + \pi_0}$$

**Step 6 — Decision rule:** Alert if $P(A \mid \mathbf{x}) > 0.5$, which simplifies to:

$$\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) > \frac{\pi_0}{\pi_1}$$

> This is the **likelihood-ratio test (LRT)**. By the Neyman–Pearson lemma, the LRT is the *most powerful* detector for any given false-alarm rate $\alpha$ — the reason Bayesian ABD is theoretically optimal in CPS.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-mark style)

> **Problem:** A water-treatment SCADA sensor reports turbidity (NTU) and pH. Under normal operation, the joint distribution is Gaussian with mean $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (4.0,\, 7.2)^T$ and covariance $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.25 & 0.05 \\ 0.05 & 0.09 \end{pmatrix}$. An observation $\mathbf{x} = (6.5,\, 6.0)^T$ arrives. Using a Mahalanobis threshold of $\tau = 3.0$, decide if this is an attack.

**Step 1 — Compute the difference vector:**

$$\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{pmatrix} 6.5 - 4.0 \\ 6.0 - 7.2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2.5 \\ -1.2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the inverse covariance:**

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} = \frac{1}{(0.25)(0.09) - (0.05)^2} \begin{pmatrix} 0.09 & -0.05 \\ -0.05 & 0.25 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{0.02} \begin{pmatrix} 0.09 & -0.05 \\ -0.05 & 0.25 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 4.5 & -2.5 \\ -2.5 & 12.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the quadratic form:**

$$(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \begin{pmatrix} 2.5 & -1.2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 4.5 & -2.5 \\ -2.5 & 12.5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 2.5 \\ -1.2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply step-by-step:**

$$\begin{pmatrix} 2.5 & -1.2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 4.5 & -2.5 \\ -2.5 & 12.5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} (2.5)(4.5) + (-1.2)(-2.5) & (2.5)(-2.5) + (-1.2)(12.5) \end{pmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{pmatrix} 11.25 + 3.00 & -6.25 - 15.00 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 14.25 & -21.25 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Final multiplication:**

$$\begin{pmatrix} 14.25 & -21.25 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 2.5 \\ -1.2 \end{pmatrix} = (14.25)(2.5) + (-21.25)(-1.2) = 35.625 + 25.5 = 61.125$$

**Step 6 — Mahalanobis squared distance:**

$$d_M^2 = 61.125$$

**Step 7 — Compare to threshold:**

$$d_M^2 = 61.125 \;\gg\; \tau = 3.0$$

**Step 8 — Decision: ALERT (Attack Detected).**

> [!NOTE]
> The enormous $d_M^2$ (over 20× the threshold) indicates a strong, unambiguous anomaly — consistent with, e.g., a chemical injection attack on the water treatment process (as in the *Maroochy Water Attack* of 2000).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

The following Mermaid diagrams are **mermaid-renderer safe**: all node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes, and all labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting.

---

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Signature-Based Detection in CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[cpsNetworkTap] --> A2[rawFrameCapture]
    A2 --> A3[protocolDecoder]
    A3 --> A4[eventNormalizer]
    A4 --> A5[patternMatcher]
    A6[signatureDatabase] --> A5
    A5 --> A7{matchFound}
    A7 -- yes --> A8[alertGenerator]
    A7 -- no --> A4
    A8 --> A9[securityOperationsCenter]
    A9 --> A10[incidentResponse]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The CPS network tap (A1) feeds raw frames to a protocol decoder (A3), which understands Modbus/TCP, DNP3, or IEC 60870-5-104. The pattern matcher (A5) consults the signature database (A6). A match raises an alert; the loop continues if no match is found.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of Anomaly-Based Detection in CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[cpsSensorStream] --> B2[featureExtractor]
    B2 --> B3[liveObservationX]
    B4[historicalCleanData] --> B5[modelTraining]
    B5 --> B6[normalcyModelM]
    B6 --> B7[anomalyScoreComputer]
    B3 --> B7
    B7 --> B8{ScoreExceedsTau}
    B8 -- yes --> B9[alertAndDiagnose]
    B8 -- no --> B1
    B6 -. periodic retrain .-> B5
    B9 --> B10[operatorConsole]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A normalcy model M is trained offline on clean data (B4 → B5 → B6). At runtime, a feature extractor (B2) produces observations x, scored against M. Scores above τ trigger alerts; the model is periodically retrained to handle concept drift.

---

## 4.3 Comparison Matrix — SBD vs ABD in CPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[detectionParadigm] --> C2{signatureBased}
    C1 --> C3{anomalyBased}
    C2 --> C4[knownPatterns]
    C2 --> C5[lowFPR]
    C2 --> C6[noZeroDay]
    C3 --> C7[behaviorBaseline]
    C3 --> C8[detectsZeroDay]
    C3 --> C9[higherFPR]
    C3 --> C10[conceptDriftRisk]
    C4 --> C11[industrialWormsStuxnet]
    C5 --> C12[idsExampleSnort]
    C6 --> C13[limitationInCPS]
    C7 --> C14[processInvariants]
    C8 --> C15[canBusInjection]
    C9 --> C16[tuningChallenge]
    C10 --> C17[adaptiveThreshold]
```

---

## 4.4 Hybrid CPS Intrusion Detection Architecture (State of the Art)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[cpsTelemetry] --> D2[preprocessor]
    D2 --> D3[signatureEngine]
    D2 --> D4[anomalyEngine]
    D3 --> D5[resultAggregator]
    D4 --> D5
    D5 --> D6{confidenceScore}
    D6 -- high --> D7[automatedMitigation]
    D6 -- medium --> D8[operatorReview]
    D6 -- low --> D9[logAndContinue]
    D5 --> D10[feedbackLoop]
    D10 -. updates .-> D3
    D10 -. updates .-> D4
```

**Reading the diagram:** A modern CPS IDS uses both engines in parallel, aggregates their votes, and acts based on a confidence score. Feedback loops allow adaptive signature generation and online model updates — addressing both zero-day threats and concept drift.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> All questions below are written in the **exact format** used in the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) for PECPT413. Marks, Bloom's levels, and CO mapping are indicated.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, CO3, Bloom: Remember]**
**Differentiate between signature-based and anomaly-based intrusion detection in CPS. State one advantage and one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Signature-Based Detection (1 mark):** Identifies intrusions by matching observed events against a database of known attack patterns (signatures). It is *knowledge-driven*.
   - *Advantage:* Very low false positive rate and high detection accuracy for known attacks.
   - *Limitation:* Cannot detect zero-day or novel attacks; signature database requires constant updates.

- **Anomaly-Based Detection (1 mark):** Identifies intrusions by comparing observed behavior against a learned model of normal operation and flagging statistical deviations.
   - *Advantage:* Can detect previously unknown (zero-day) attacks and insider threats.
   - *Limitation:* Higher false positive rate; requires clean baseline training data and periodic retraining.

- *CPS-specific note (1 mark):* In CPS, missed detections are safety-critical; hence ABD is often preferred despite higher FPR, while SBD is retained for high-confidence known-pattern matching in resource-constrained edge devices.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, CO3, Bloom: Understand]**
**Define the terms True Positive Rate (TPR), False Positive Rate (FPR), and Detection Latency. Why is Detection Latency a critical KPI in CPS?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **TPR (Recall) (1 mark):** $P_D = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ — the fraction of actual attacks that the IDS correctly raises alerts for.
- **FPR (1 mark):** $P_{FA} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ — the fraction of legitimate events incorrectly flagged as attacks.
- **Detection Latency in CPS (1 mark):** Detection latency $L_d = t_{\text{alert}} - t_{\text{attack onset}}$ is the time gap between attack initiation and IDS alert. In CPS, control loops operate at millisecond timescales; even a few hundred milliseconds of latency can allow an attacker to push a chemical process into unsafe territory, cause a robotic arm to collide, or destabilize a power grid. Hence latency is treated as a *safety-critical KPI* alongside $P_D$ and $P_{FA}$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Format:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice means the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [CO3, Bloom: Apply + Analyze]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a signature-based intrusion detection system for a SCADA network**. List **four example signatures** specific to industrial protocols. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CPS sensor reports the following 5 observations of a single feature $x$: $\{10.2,\, 10.1,\, 9.9,\, 10.3,\, 10.0\}$. Compute the mean $\mu$, variance $\sigma^2$, and standard deviation $\sigma$. A new observation $x_{\text{new}} = 13.5$ arrives. Using a 3-$\sigma$ threshold rule, decide whether to raise an alert. Justify with the relevant anomaly score. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the architecture from Section 4.1 of these notes. Specifically, mark:
- *Data source:* SCADA network tap (PCAP on Modbus/TCP or DNP3).
- *Pre-processing:* Industrial protocol dissector (Wireshark/industrial parser).
- *Signature DB:* YARA / Snort rules for ICS.
- *Matching Engine:* Aho-Corasick / Boyer-Moore.
- *Decision Module:* Threshold counter + alert generator.
- *Sink:* SOC dashboard / SIEM.

**Four example ICS-specific signatures (4 marks):**
1. Modbus function code 0x5A (custom/unused code — anomalous in many vendors).
2. DNP3 unsolicited response with corrupt CRC.
3. IEC 60870-5-104 STARTDT act on a previously stopped session (replay indicator).
4. Repeated failed S7comm comm password attempts (Siemens S7 brute force).

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Mean (1 mark):**

$$\mu = \frac{10.2 + 10.1 + 9.9 + 10.3 + 10.0}{5} = \frac{50.5}{5} = 10.1$$

**Step 2 — Deviations from the mean (1 mark):**

$$x_i - \mu = \{0.1,\, 0.0,\, -0.2,\, 0.2,\, -0.1\}$$

**Step 3 — Sum of squared deviations (1 mark):**

$$\sum (x_i - \mu)^2 = (0.1)^2 + (0.0)^2 + (-0.2)^2 + (0.2)^2 + (-0.1)^2 = 0.01 + 0 + 0.04 + 0.04 + 0.01 = 0.10$$

**Step 4 — Variance (1 mark):**

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{0.10}{5} = 0.02$$

**Step 5 — Standard deviation (1 mark):**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{0.02} \approx 0.1414$$

**Step 6 — 3-$\sigma$ threshold and z-score for new observation (2 marks):**

Upper threshold: $\tau = \mu + 3\sigma = 10.1 + 3(0.1414) = 10.1 + 0.4242 = 10.5242$

z-score: $z = \dfrac{x_{\text{new}} - \mu}{\sigma} = \dfrac{13.5 - 10.1}{0.1414} = \dfrac{3.4}{0.1414} \approx 24.04$

**Decision (within step 6):** Since $z \approx 24 \gg 3$, and $x_{\text{new}} = 13.5 \gg \tau = 10.52$, the system raises an **ALERT**. The observation is an extreme statistical outlier — consistent with a sensor fault or a malicious sensor-data injection attack (a known CPS threat vector).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [CO3, Bloom: Apply + Analyze] (Internal Alternative to Question A)**

**(a)** Describe the **functional architecture of an anomaly-based IDS for CPS** with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of the normalcy model and how the threshold $\tau$ is selected under the **Neyman–Pearson criterion**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CPS with two sensors (temperature $T$ in °C, pressure $P$ in bar), the normal operating distribution is $\mathbf{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ with $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (80, 5)^T$ and $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 0.6 \\ 0.6 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute the Mahalanobis distance for the observation $\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} = (85, 4)$. If the threshold is set as $\tau = 2.5$, decide if this is an attack. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Use the architecture from Section 4.2 of these notes. Required blocks:
- *Training data source:* Clean historical CPS logs.
- *Feature extractor:* Time-domain and frequency-domain features.
- *Model trainer:* Fits $\mathcal{M}$ (e.g., multivariate Gaussian, HMM, LSTM).
- *Online scoring engine:* Computes $S(\mathbf{x}) = d_M(\mathbf{x})$ or reconstruction error.
- *Threshold decision unit:* Compares $S(\mathbf{x})$ with $\tau$.
- *Alert sink:* Operator HMI.

**Role of the normalcy model (2 marks):** The model $\mathcal{M}$ represents the *expected* statistical envelope of the CPS during legitimate operation. It encodes correlations between sensors (e.g., temperature rises with pressure in a boiler), periodicities (e.g., 24-hour load cycle), and operational envelopes. Any deviation indicates either a fault, a misconfiguration, or a deliberate manipulation.

**Threshold selection under Neyman–Pearson (2 marks):** The Neyman–Pearson (NP) criterion selects $\tau$ to **maximize the detection probability $P_D$ subject to a hard constraint on the false-alarm rate $P_{FA} \leq \alpha$**. Mathematically:

$$\tau^* = \arg\max_{\tau} P_D(\tau) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad P_{FA}(\tau) \leq \alpha$$

For a multivariate Gaussian model, $\tau$ can be obtained in closed form from the chi-squared distribution (as shown in §3.2 of these notes). In CPS, $\alpha$ is chosen to be very small (e.g., $10^{-4}$) to avoid alarm fatigue for operators.

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Difference vector (1 mark):**

$$\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} - \boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{pmatrix} 85 - 80 \\ 4 - 5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Determinant of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ (1 mark):**

$$\vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert = (4)(1) - (0.6)(0.6) = 4 - 0.36 = 3.64$$

**Step 3 — Inverse of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ (1.5 marks):**

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} = \frac{1}{3.64} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -0.6 \\ -0.6 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2747 & -0.1648 \\ -0.1648 & 1.0989 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the quadratic form (1.5 marks):**

$$(\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.2747 & -0.1648 \\ -0.1648 & 1.0989 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Stepwise multiplication (1.5 marks):**

$$\begin{pmatrix} 5 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.2747 & -0.1648 \\ -0.1648 & 1.0989 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} (5)(0.2747) + (-1)(-0.1648) & (5)(-0.1648) + (-1)(1.0989) \end{pmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{pmatrix} 1.3735 + 0.1648 & -0.8240 - 1.0989 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.5383 & -1.9229 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\begin{pmatrix} 1.5383 & -1.9229 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} = (1.5383)(5) + (-1.9229)(-1) = 7.6915 + 1.9229 = 9.6144$$

**Step 6 — Mahalanobis distance and decision (0.5 marks):**

$$d_M^2 = 9.6144, \qquad d_M \approx 3.10$$

$$\tau = 2.5, \qquad d_M = 3.10 > \tau \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{ALERT (Attack Suspected)}$$

**Interpretation:** The temperature rose by 5 °C while pressure dropped by 1 bar — a combination that is statistically rare under normal operation (note the strong negative correlation coefficient $\rho = 0.6 / 2 = 0.3$). This may indicate a *process-aware false data injection attack* on the boiler sensors.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 2-4 marks each):**
> 1. **Confusing Precision with Recall.** Precision answers *"Of all alerts raised, how many were real?"*; Recall ($P_D$) answers *"Of all real attacks, how many did we catch?"* Examiners *always* test this.
> 2. **Forgetting to normalize units.** In numerical Mahalanobis problems, if $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ uses different units (e.g., °C² vs bar²), the off-diagonal covariance must also be in the **product unit** (°C·bar). Students often write $\sigma_{xy}$ in pure numbers — this is wrong.
> 3. **Writing "signature-based detection is outdated" or "anomaly-based is always better".** Both are *incorrect generalizations*. Examiners expect balanced, context-aware answers with formula-based justification.
> 4. **Skipping the threshold selection logic.** A model without a decision rule is not a detector. Always end with a clear `IF score > τ THEN alert` statement.
> 5. **Ignoring Detection Latency.** A detector with $P_D = 1$ but $L_d = 10$ minutes is useless for a CPS control loop — always comment on latency for CPS questions.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin these 12 points to memory before the KTU 2024 ESE for PECPT413:**

- ☐ **SBD = Pattern Matching; ABD = Deviation Detection.** State this contrast in the first sentence of any comparison answer.
- ☐ **SBD needs a signature DB; ABD needs clean training data.** If a question describes a *new* attack with no prior knowledge, the answer is ABD.
- ☐ **Performance metrics to remember:** $P_D$, $P_{FA}$, Precision, Recall, F1-score, AUC, Detection Latency.
- ☐ **Neyman–Pearson Lemma** is the theoretical justification for ABD's threshold tuning — cite it for full marks.
- ☐ **Mahalanobis distance** is the canonical multivariate anomaly score for correlated CPS sensors.
- ☐ **Autoencoders and LSTMs** are the state-of-the-art ABD models for high-dimensional CPS data (e.g., vibration, video).
- ☐ **Concept drift** is the ABD-specific failure mode — mention it whenever discussing long-term ABD deployment.
- ☐ **Hybrid IDS** (SBD + ABD) is the de-facto industry best practice for modern CPS — sketch the block diagram.
- ☐ **CPS constraints to mention in any answer:** real-time, safety-critical, resource-limited, deterministic traffic.
- ☐ **Closed-form threshold for Gaussian ABD:** $\tau = \frac{1}{2}\chi^2_{n, 1-\alpha}$ — derivable on the exam.
- ☐ **Bayes' rule** is the foundation of probabilistic detection — write it down once even if not asked.
- ☐ **Real-world CPS attacks to reference (carry weight in descriptive answers):** Stuxnet (2010), Maroochy Water (2000), BlackEnergy (2015), TRITON/TRISIS (2017), Oldsmar Water (2021).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deploying IDPS Solutions Tailored to CPS Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Deploying IDPS Solutions Tailored to CPS Environments # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)** is a security mechanism that monitors network traffic and/or host activities in a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) for malicious behavior, policy violations, and anomalous patterns, and is further capable of taking automated preventive action upon detection. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context of Cyber-Physical Systems, an IDPS is **not** a generic IT security tool. It is a *context-aware monitoring architecture* specifically engineered to handle the unique constraints of CPS environments, namely: - **Real-time deterministic operations** (latency < 1 ms in some control loops) - **Safety-criticality** (failure may cause physical harm, environmental damage, or loss of life) - **Heterogeneous protocols** (Modbus/TCP, DNP3, IEC 61850, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA) - **Legacy devices** with no patching capability (15–30 year operational lifespans) - **Resource-constrained embedded sensors and PLCs** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The key distinction emphasized in the **2024 Scheme PECPT413 syllabus** is the **tailoring** of IDPS to CPS. A standard IT-grade IDPS (e.g., Snort in default configuration) deployed unmodified on a SCADA network will generate an unacceptably high **false positive rate** (> 80%) because industrial traffic patterns differ fundamentally from enterprise traffic. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital ICU Monitor" Imagine a hospital **Intensive Care Unit (ICU) monitor**: - A **regular smoke alarm** in a home simply beeps when smoke is present (signature-based, crude). - An **ICU patient monitor**, however, continuously tracks **heart rate, blood oxygen, ECG waveform, blood pressure** — and triggers alarms only when **multiple correlated parameters** deviate from the patient's *personal baseline*. A **CPS-tailored IDPS** is the ICU monitor of the industrial world: | ICU Monitor | CPS IDPS | |---|---| | Patient's historical baseline | Operational baseline (auto-learned during commissioning) | | Multi-parameter correlation | Cross-protocol, cross-tag correlation (e.g., Modbus register + DNP3 analog input) | | Alert vs. Drug administration | Passive detection (alert) vs. Active prevention (block, drop, reset) | | Cannot "shut down" the patient | Cannot blindly drop packets (must not break deterministic control loops) | ## 1.3 Why Traditional IT IDPS Fail in CPS > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Misconception:** "Snort + Suricata can be deployed in any network." This is **false** for CPS. The KTU 2024 examiner frequently tests this distinction. A traditional IT IDPS fails in CPS for the following **six structural reasons**: 1. **Protocol blindness** — cannot parse Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850 MMS, GOOSE, or Sampled Values. 2. **Active response danger** — an IT IDPS that drops packets would break a deterministic IEC 61850 GOOSE message stream (latency budget ≈ 4 ms), causing a substation protection relay to misoperate. 3. **False positives** — industrial traffic is highly periodic and repetitive; statistical heuristics trained on HTTP/DNS fail. 4. **No physical-state awareness** — IT IDPS cannot correlate a cyber packet with a physical consequence (e.g., a pump turning on at 3 AM). 5. **Asset inventory mismatch** — IT IDPS sees "Windows host"; CPS needs "Siemens S7-1500 PLC, firmware v2.9, controlling boiler #3." 6. **Air-gap myth blindness** — modern CPS networks are increasingly connected (remote vendors, IIoT gateways), but IDPS must assume *implicit trust boundaries* inside the Purdue Model. ## 1.4 Classification of IDPS for CPS ``` CPS IDPS | +--------------+--------------+--------------+ | | | | Network-Based Host-Based Hybrid Wireless/Field (NIDS) (HIDS) (H-IDS) (Sensor) | +----+----+----------+ | | | Signature Anomaly Specification -based -based -based ``` **Three detection paradigms** central to KTU assessment: 1. **Signature-based** — matches packets to known attack patterns (e.g., a known Stuxnet Modbus write command). *Low false positive, zero-day blind.* 2. **Anomaly-based** — builds a statistical baseline; flags deviations. *Catches novel attacks, high false positive in noisy CPS.* 3. **Specification-based (stateful)** — manually encodes the **expected** behavior of industrial protocols. *Best CPS fit: low FP, catches protocol misuse.* # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework ## 2.1 Operational Concept — The IDPS Functional Pipeline The CPS-tailored IDPS operates through a **five-stage pipeline** that students must memorize for KTU 2024 evaluations: ### Stage 1: Data Acquisition (Sensing) - **Network sensors** placed at strategic chokepoints using **SPAN ports, network taps, or ICS-aware mirror ports**. - **Host sensors** on Windows HMI stations, Engineering Workstations, Historian servers. - **Passive only** — sensors must never be in-line (would introduce latency). ### Stage 2: Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) — Protocol-Aware Parsing The IDPS parser must decode industrial protocols correctly: $$ \text{Packet} = [\text{Ethernet Header}] \cdot [\text{IP Header}] \cdot [\text{TCP/UDP}] \cdot [\text{ICS Payload}] $$ For Modbus/TCP specifically, the IDPS must parse: $$ \text{Modbus PDU} = \underbrace{\text{Function Code}}_{\text{1 byte}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Data Field}}_{\text{0-252 bytes}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** *Function code anomaly detection* — if a read-only HMI suddenly issues a **Write Single Coil (FC 05)** to a PLC register, this is a critical anomaly even if no signature matches. ### Stage 3: Detection Engine (Multi-Layer Correlation) A CPS IDPS combines **three detection methods simultaneously**: | Layer | Method | Detects | False Positive Rate | |---|---|---|---| | L1 | Signature | Known malware, exploits | **Very Low (< 1%)** | | L2 | Specification (stateful) | Protocol misuse, unauthorized function codes | **Low (1–5%)** | | L3 | Anomaly (statistical / ML) | Zero-days, novel attacks | **Medium-High (5–20%)** | The **detection confidence score** is computed as a weighted aggregation: $$ S_{alert} = w_1 \cdot S_{sig} + w_2 \cdot S_{spec} + w_3 \cdot S_{anom}, \quad \sum w_i = 1 $$ where each $S_i \in [0, 1]$. An alert is raised if $S_{alert} \geq \tau$ (threshold, typically 0.75). ### Stage 4: Correlation & Situational Awareness - Correlates **multiple low-severity events** into a single high-severity incident (e.g., reconnaissance scan → lateral movement → unauthorized write → physical state change). - Maps alerts to the **Purdue Reference Model levels (0–5)** for context. ### Stage 5: Response Action - **Passive (Detection only)**: log, alert via Syslog/SIEM. - **Active (Prevention)**: - **Soft block** — TCP reset, ARP poisoning of attacker. - **Hard block** — drop packet at firewall (only if non-critical path). - **Process-level action** — e.g., switch PLC to safe-mode via OPC UA. > [!WARNING] > **CPS Cardinal Rule:** Active prevention actions must be **commissioned only after a pilot phase** and must include a **kill-switch** to prevent the IDPS itself from causing a process trip. ## 2.2 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet | Concept | Mathematical / Formal Representation | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---| | Detection latency bound | $t_{detect} \le t_{safety}$ | $t_{safety}$ = safety margin of physical process | | Baseline learning window | $T_{base} = N \cdot \Delta t_{cycle}$ | $N$ = observed cycles, $\Delta t$ = process cycle time | | False positive rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Dimensionless, target < 5% for CPS | | False negative rate | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | Target < 1% for safety-critical CPS | | Detection confidence | $S = w_1 S_1 + w_2 S_2 + w_3 S_3$ | Weights sum to 1 | | Packet processing throughput | $\Phi = \dfrac{B}{L \cdot t_p}$ | Packets/sec, $B$ = bandwidth, $L$ = pkt length, $t_p$ = processing time | | Sensor coverage ratio | $C = \dfrac{E_{monitored}}{E_{total}}$ | Edges covered / total edges in CPS graph | | Time-to-detect | $TTD = t_{alert} - t_{attack\_init}$ | Lower is better | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility CPS-tailored IDPS is deployed across: - **Smart grids** (IEC 61850 substations) — detects GOOSE spoofing attacks. - **Water treatment SCADA** (DNP3) — prevents the *Maroochy Shire*-style sewerage attacks. - **Manufacturing** (PROFINET, EtherNet/IP) — detects recipe manipulation in PLCs. - **Automotive CPS** (CAN bus) — detects message injection (Jeep Cherokee 2015 case study). - **Healthcare IoT** (MQTT, CoAP) — detects abnormal infusion pump commands. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — Deploying CPS-Tailored IDPS The KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) frequently asks students to **describe the deployment methodology** of an IDPS in a CPS. Below is the exhaustive **nine-step framework** that constitutes a board-quality answer. ## 3.1 Step 1: CPS Asset Discovery & Network Mapping **Goal:** Build a complete inventory of cyber and physical assets. **Sub-steps:** 1. Passive network scanning (no active probing — never disrupt a live process). 2. Identify all PLCs, RTUs, IEDs, HMIs, Historians, Engineering Workstations. 3. Document firmware versions, vendor models, supported protocols. 4. Map logical and physical topology onto the **Purdue Reference Model**. **Mathematical model of the CPS graph:** $$ G_{CPS} = (V, E) $$ where $V$ = set of cyber-physical nodes (PLCs, sensors, actuators, HMIs), and $E$ = set of communication links (Ethernet, serial, wireless). **Output:** A complete asset inventory database, e.g.: | Asset ID | Type | Vendor | Protocol | Purdue Level | Criticality | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | PLC-001 | S7-1500 | Siemens | PROFINET | L1 | Critical | | HMI-01 | Panel PC | Advantech | Modbus/TCP | L3 | High | | IED-A1 | Protection Relay | ABB | IEC 61850 GOOSE | L1 | Critical | ## 3.2 Step 2: Risk Assessment & Threat Modeling Apply **STRIDE** or **PASTA** to each asset. Prioritize deployment by: $$ \text{Priority Score} = \text{Impact} \times \text{Likelihood} \times (1 - \text{Existing Controls}) $$ Assets with the highest score are protected first. ## 3.3 Step 3: IDPS Architecture Selection Three architectural options exist: ### (a) Centralized Architecture - Single IDPS engine at the control center. - **Pros:** easier management, single correlation engine. - **Cons:** single point of failure; bandwidth bottleneck for wide-area CPS. ### (b) Distributed Architecture - Multiple IDPS sensors distributed across the Purdue levels. - **Pros:** local processing, lower latency, resilience. - **Cons:** complex management, sensor synchronization. ### (c) Hierarchical (Tiered) Architecture — **KTU-recommended for CPS** - Local sensors (L0–L2) feed to a **site-level correlation engine**, which feeds to **enterprise SOC**. $$ \text{Architecture} = \text{Local Sensors} \rightarrow \text{Site Correlation} \rightarrow \text{Enterprise SOC} $$ ## 3.4 Step 4: Sensor Placement Strategy Sensors must be placed on **chokepoints** where all relevant traffic passes: - Between **Purdue Level 1 and Level 2** (process control vs. supervisory). - At the **DMZ boundary** (Level 3.5). - On **engineering workstation** switch ports. - **Critically:** on serial-to-Ethernet gateway links (e.g., Modbus RTU ↔ Modbus TCP). Mathematically, the minimum number of sensors to monitor all critical edges is the **minimum edge cover** problem: $$ S_{min} = \min \vert S \vert \quad \text{such that} \quad \forall e \in E_{critical}, \exists s \in S : e \in E(s) $$ ## 3.5 Step 5: Baseline Learning (Anomaly Engine Training) Run the IDPS in **detection-only (passive) mode** for a defined period $T_{base}$ (typically 2–4 weeks of normal operations, covering all operational modes: startup, steady-state, shutdown, maintenance). $$ B = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\} \quad \text{where each } x_i = (f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_m) $$ Here, $f_j$ are extracted features: Modbus function code frequency, packet inter-arrival time, register ranges accessed, source-destination pairs, payload entropy, etc. The baseline statistical model is then: $$ \hat{\mu}_j = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_{ij}, \quad \hat{\sigma}_j^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (f_{ij} - \hat{\mu}_j)^2 $$ Any live observation $x_{new}$ triggers an alert if its Mahalanobis distance exceeds a threshold: $$ D_M(x_{new}) = \sqrt{(x_{new} - \hat{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (x_{new} - \hat{\mu})} \geq \tau_{alert} $$ ## 3.6 Step 6: Signature & Rule Engineering Load **ICS-specific signatures**: - Stuxnet Modbus sequence patterns. - BlackEnergy DNP3 command sequences. - Triconex SIS Triconex attack patterns. - Custom organization-specific rules (e.g., "alert if FC 16 — Write Multiple Registers — is sent to boiler PLC"). Each rule has the formal structure: $$ R_i : \text{IF } (\text{condition}_1 \land \text{condition}_2 \land \ldots) \text{ THEN action}_i $$ Example rule in pseudo-formal notation: $$ R_1 : \text{IF } (src = HMI\_01) \land (dst = PLC\_001) \land (function\_code = 06) \land (time \notin \text{scheduled\_maintenance}) \text{ THEN ALERT (Severity: HIGH)} $$ ## 3.7 Step 7: Pilot Deployment & Tuning - Deploy in **monitor-only mode** for 30–90 days. - Tune $\tau_{alert}$ to achieve **FPR < 5%** and **FNR < 1%**. - Validate against **red-team / purple-team** tabletop exercises using CPS-specific attack datasets (e.g., *Power System Attack Dataset* from Mississippi State / Oak Ridge, *SWaT* dataset from Singapore). ## 3.8 Step 8: Integration with SOC & SIEM The IDPS must forward events to the enterprise SIEM (Splunk, QRadar, Elastic) using **CEF or LEEF** formats. Correlation rule example: $$ \text{Incident} = (\text{Reconnaissance from HMI-01}) \oplus (\text{Unauthorized Modbus write to PLC-001}) \oplus (\text{Tag value out of safe range}) $$ ## 3.9 Step 9: Continuous Monitoring, Updating & Lifecycle - Quarterly signature updates (without rebooting PLCs). - Annual retraining of anomaly models. - Periodic **adversarial testing** to ensure the IDPS itself is not blind to new TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CPS-Tailored IDPS Architecture (Purdue Model Aligned) ```mermaid flowchart TB L5["L5 Enterprise Network
ERP, Email, Cloud"] L4["L4 Site Business Planning
MES Servers"] DMZ["L3.5 Industrial DMZ
Firewall, Mail Relay"] L3["L3 Site Operations
HMI, Historian, SCADA Server"] L2["L2 Supervisory Control
Engineering Workstations"] L1["L1 Basic Control
PLCs, RTUs, DCS Controllers"] L0["L0 Physical Process
Sensors, Actuators, Field Devices"] sensorA["NIDPS Sensor A
IT-side"] sensorB["NIDPS Sensor B
DMZ Boundary"] sensorC["HIDPS Agent
SCADA Server"] sensorD["NIDPS Sensor C
OT-side Chokepoint"] sensorE["NIDPS Sensor D
Serial Gateway Link"] engine["Hierarchical Correlation Engine
Site-Level SOC"] L5 -.->|IT Traffic| sensorA sensorA --> DMZ DMZ -->|Filtered Traffic| sensorB sensorB --> L3 L3 -.->|Host Events| sensorC L3 --> L2 L2 --> sensorD sensorD --> L1 L1 --> sensorE sensorE --> L0 sensorA --> engine sensorB --> engine sensorC --> engine sensorD --> engine sensorE --> engine engine --> soc["Enterprise SOC
SIEM, Analyst Console"] engine --> response["Active Response Module
TCP Reset / Soft Block"] ``` ## 4.2 IDPS Detection Pipeline (Functional Block Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Network Tap
SPAN Port"] --> B["Packet Capture
libpcap / AF_PACKET"] B --> C["Protocol Decoder
Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850"] C --> D1["Signature Engine
Snort-style Rules"] C --> D2["Specification Engine
Stateful Protocol Checks"] C --> D3["Anomaly Engine
Statistical / ML Model"] D1 --> E["Correlation & Scoring
S = w1 S1 + w2 S2 + w3 S3"] D2 --> E D3 --> E E --> F{"S >= Threshold ?"} F -- "No" --> G["Silent Log"] F -- "Yes" --> H["Alert Generation
CEF / LEEF Format"] H --> I["SIEM / SOC"] H --> J{"Response Mode ?"} J -- "Passive" --> K["Log Only"] J -- "Active" --> L["TCP Reset / Block"] J -- "Process-Level" --> M["PLC Safe-Mode via OPC UA"] ``` ## 4.3 Sensor Placement Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB start["Begin: CPS Network Graph G(V,E)"] start --> q1{"Edge carries
ICS Protocol ?"} q1 -- "No" --> skip1["Skip - IT traffic only"] q1 -- "Yes" --> q2{"Edge crosses
Purdue Level ?"} q2 -- "No" --> skip2["Optional placement"] q2 -- "Yes" --> place["Install Passive Sensor"] place --> q3{"Bandwidth > Sensor
Capacity ?"} q3 -- "Yes" --> multi["Use Multiple Sensors
+ Load Balancer"] q3 -- "No" --> done["Single sensor sufficient"] multi --> endNode["End"] done --> endNode skip1 --> endNode skip2 --> endNode ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` > **Q:** Differentiate between **signature-based** and **anomaly-based** intrusion detection in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based | |---|---|---| | **Mechanism** | Pattern matching against known attack signatures | Statistical / ML deviation from learned baseline | | **Zero-day detection** | **Cannot detect** | **Can detect** | | **False Positive Rate** | Very low (< 1%) | Medium–High (5–20%) in CPS | | **Training data** | Not required | Requires 2–4 weeks of normal-operation data | | **CPS suitability** | Good for known ICS malware (Stuxnet, BlackEnergy) | Good for novel process-level attacks | | **Examples** | Snort ICS rules, Dragos threat intelligence | Auto-learned Modbus traffic profiles | **Key marking point:** A complete answer must mention *which CPS attack types each method is best suited for.* Award **1 mark** for each valid comparative point; full **3 marks** for covering mechanism, FPR, and CPS applicability. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **Q:** Why is **active prevention** (packet dropping, blocking) generally avoided in CPS IDPS deployments? Give two engineering reasons. **Model Answer:** 1. **Determinism violation:** Industrial control loops operate under strict latency budgets (e.g., IEC 61850 GOOSE requires < 4 ms). An active block in the data path introduces variable latency and jitter that can cause protection relays to misoperate or, in the worst case, **fail to trip** during a fault. 2. **Risk of self-induced DoS:** A misconfigured IDPS rule may falsely flag legitimate operational traffic (e.g., a routine recipe change during batch processing) as malicious, and blocking it would **shut down the plant** — converting a security tool into a safety hazard. 3. (Bonus) **No atomic rollback:** Unlike IT where a blocked packet can be retried, CPS may not have a retry mechanism, so a dropped packet may cause a permanent process state error. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write *"because it will stop the attack."* This is worth **zero marks** — the question explicitly tests understanding of CPS-specific constraints, not general security wisdom. You must mention **latency/determinism** and **process continuity/safety**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a CPS-tailored hierarchical IDPS** aligned with the **Purdue Reference Model**. Explain the role of each sensor tier. **Model Solution:** A CPS-tailored IDPS follows a **three-tier hierarchical architecture** corresponding to Purdue Levels: **Tier 1 — Field-Level Sensors (Purdue L0–L1):** - Deployed on process bus segments (PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, FOUNDATION Fieldbus). - Detect **physical-process anomalies**: abnormal sensor readings, unexpected actuator commands, protocol timing violations in GOOSE messages. - Operate in **passive mode only** — never break the control loop. - Example: sensor monitoring Modbus traffic between PLC and VFD (Variable Frequency Drive). **Tier 2 — Supervisory-Level Sensors (Purdue L2–L3):** - Monitor HMI-to-PLC, Engineering Workstation-to-PLC, and Historian traffic. - Apply **deep protocol inspection** (DPI) for Modbus, DNP3, OPC UA, IEC 61850 MMS. - Host-based agents (HIDPS) installed on SCADA servers, HMIs, and Historian machines. - Detect: unauthorized programming uploads, ladder logic changes, configuration modifications. **Tier 3 — Site Correlation & Enterprise SOC (Purdue L3.5–L4–L5):** - Aggregates all Tier 1 and Tier 2 alerts. - Performs **multi-stage correlation** to identify kill-chain progression. - Forwards enriched events to the enterprise SIEM. - Houses the **active response module** (with operator approval for hard blocks). **Diagram (refer Section 4.1 above) — 3 marks**; **role of each tier — 4 marks**. --- > **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** List and briefly explain **five CPS-specific challenges** that must be addressed when deploying an IDPS in an industrial environment. **Model Solution:** 1. **Legacy and unpatched systems:** Industrial controllers often run firmware that is 10–20 years old. Host-based IDPS agents cannot be installed; the IDPS must rely entirely on **passive network monitoring** for these devices. 2. **Proprietary and non-IP protocols:** Many fieldbus protocols (PROFIBUS, Foundation Fieldbus, HART) are not IP-based and require **specialized TAPs and protocol dissectors**, increasing deployment cost. 3. **Real-time determinism requirements:** Sampling or deep inspection can introduce latency. The IDPS architecture must use **hardware-accelerated bypass switches** so that sensor failure does not interrupt the process. 4. **High false-positive tolerance is low:** In CPS, a false positive that halts a process costs money and may damage equipment. The system must achieve **FPR < 5%**, requiring extensive baseline learning and specification-based rules. 5. **Safety-security interdependence:** Blocking a packet may lead to a safety incident. IDPS must integrate with the **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** and respect its independence (per IEC 61511). 6. (Bonus point) **Limited observability:** Encrypted industrial traffic (e.g., OPC UA over TLS) hides payload from IDPS. Solutions include **decryption at the DMZ** or using **metadata-only analysis**. **Marking:** 1 mark per challenge, 1 mark for the engineering justification; 7 marks total. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` > **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Describe the **nine-step methodology** for deploying an IDPS in a CPS environment. Highlight why baseline learning is a critical phase. **Model Solution:** Refer to **Section 3.1–3.9** of these notes for the full nine steps. The marking scheme is: - **Steps 1, 2, 3** (Discovery, Risk, Architecture): 2 marks — students must mention Purdue Model mapping and at least one architectural variant. - **Steps 4, 5, 6** (Placement, Baseline, Rules): 3 marks — **baseline learning** is heavily weighted; answer must include the $T_{base}$ window concept and feature extraction. - **Steps 7, 8, 9** (Pilot, Integration, Lifecycle): 2 marks. **Why baseline learning is critical (carries dedicated 1.5 marks):** The anomaly engine requires a **statistical model of normal CPS behavior**. Without this baseline, the engine cannot distinguish between legitimate operational variation (e.g., batch startup transients) and malicious activity. In CPS, the cost of an incorrect baseline is unacceptably high — the system either drowns operators in false alerts or misses real attacks. The baseline must cover **all operational modes** (startup, steady-state, shutdown, maintenance, emergency) to be valid. --- > **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A smart-grid substation uses **IEC 61850** for communication between Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs) and protection relays. Explain the role of an IDPS in detecting **GOOSE message spoofing attacks**. What specifications would you encode? **Model Solution:** **Background — GOOSE Messages:** GOOSE (Generic Object Oriented Substation Event) is the protocol used within IEC 61850 for **fast, multicast, peer-to-peer** exchange of protection-critical data (trip commands, breaker status). The latency budget is **< 4 ms**, and messages are **not routed through TCP/IP** — they travel directly over Layer 2 Ethernet. **Why GOOSE is vulnerable:** GOOSE has no authentication and no encryption (by design, for speed). An attacker on the substation LAN can craft a fake GOOSE frame with a legitimate `stNum`/`sqNum` and a malicious `data` payload (e.g., a fake trip command to a circuit breaker). **IDPS Detection Approach — Specification-Based Rules:** The IDPS encodes the following **expected behavior specifications**: | Specification Rule | Detection Trigger | |---|---| | **Allowed source MAC addresses** | GOOSE arriving from an unknown MAC = spoofing | | **Allowed IED-to-IED pairs** | GOOSE between non-paired IEDs = lateral movement | | **stNum monotonicity** | stNum must always increase; reset = replay attack | | **sqNum increment range** | sqNum increment > 1 may indicate injection | | **Allowed `data` ranges per logical node** | e.g., breaker trip allowed only from protection IEDs, not from bay controllers | | **Time-since-last-update bounds** | A GOOSE burst (1 ms apart) is normal during a trip; sustained high-rate GOOSE is suspicious | **Detection Confidence Formula applied here:** $$ S_{spoof} = w_1 \cdot S_{MAC} + w_2 \cdot S_{stNum} + w_3 \cdot S_{dataRange} $$ with $w_1 = 0.4, w_2 = 0.4, w_3 = 0.2$ and $\tau_{alert} = 0.75$. **IDPS Response:** - **Passive mode:** alert with substation context (bay, breaker, timestamp). - **Active mode (with operator approval):** block the spoofing source MAC using managed switch ACLs — never drop the GOOSE itself, as that would defeat the protection system. **Marking:** 2 marks for background, 2 marks for vulnerability explanation, 3 marks for specification rules (must include ≥ 3 specific rules). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is suggesting the IDPS should "drop the malicious GOOSE packet." This is **dangerous** — protection GOOSE is **safety-critical**; a dropped genuine trip packet can result in equipment damage. The examiner specifically penalizes answers that fail to differentiate between **detection** and **prevention** in IEC 61850 contexts. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 PECPT413 Module 3** - ☐ **Definition:** IDPS in CPS is **context-aware**, not a generic IT tool. - ☐ **Three detection methods:** Signature (low FP, no zero-day), Anomaly (zero-day, high FP), Specification (best CPS fit, low FP). - ☐ **Detection confidence** is a weighted sum: $S = w_1 S_1 + w_2 S_2 + w_3 S_3$. - ☐ **Purdue Model levels** (0–5) are the deployment framework for CPS IDPS. - ☐ **Hierarchical architecture** is KTU-recommended (Local → Site → Enterprise). - ☐ **Sensor placement** uses the **minimum edge cover** concept on the CPS graph. - ☐ **Baseline learning** requires 2–4 weeks covering all operational modes; uses Mahalanobis distance for anomaly scoring. - ☐ **Active prevention is dangerous** in CPS — it may break deterministic loops (GOOSE < 4 ms) and trigger process trips. - ☐ **Performance metrics:** FPR < 5%, FNR < 1%, $t_{detect} \le t_{safety}$. - ☐ **CPS-specific protocols to know:** Modbus/TCP, DNP3, IEC 61850 (MMS + GOOSE + SV), PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA, HART. - ☐ **Real-world datasets for testing:** SWaT (Singapore), WADI, Mississippi State power system dataset, BATADAL. - ☐ **Commercial CPS IDPS solutions:** Dragos, Claroty, Nozomi Networks, Radiflow, SecurityMatters (now part of ForeScout), Cisco Cyber Vision. - ☐ **Key attack examples:** Stuxnet (Modbus), BlackEnergy (DNP3), Triton/Triconex (SIS), Industroyer (IEC 61850), VPNFilter. - ☐ **Integration:** IDPS events → SIEM (CEF/LEEF) → SOC analyst → coordinated response. - ☐ **Safety-security codependence:** IDPS must respect the Safety Instrumented System (IEC 61511). > **End of Module 3 Notes — Deploying IDPS Solutions Tailored to CPS Environments**

topics.title: Importance of Logging and Monitoring for Detecting Security Incidents in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Importance of Logging and Monitoring for Detecting Security Incidents in CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Importance of Logging and Monitoring for Detecting Security Incidents in CPS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), **logging** is the systematic, chronological recording of events, state changes, transactions, sensor readings, actuator commands, network packets, and user activities generated by the heterogeneous components (sensors, controllers, actuators, gateways, cloud, HMI) that constitute a CPS. **Monitoring** is the continuous, real-time (or near real-time) observation, collection, correlation, and analysis of these logged records to detect anomalies, policy violations, faults, and adversarial intrusions across the cyber-physical continuum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A *Security Incident* in CPS is any confirmed adverse event that compromises the **Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability (CIA Triad)** of the cyber layer OR the **Safety, Reliability, or Predictability** of the physical layer. Logging & Monitoring form the **detective** arm of the overall security strategy (complementing preventive controls like firewalls and access control).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital ICU" View

Imagine a modern hospital ICU. Every patient is connected to machines that record heart rate, oxygen saturation, and blood pressure **continuously (logging)**. A central nurse station watches all monitors **live (monitoring)**. The moment a heart rate spikes abnormally, an **alarm triggers**, the nurse rushes in, and life-saving action begins.

Now apply the same idea to a CPS — say, a smart power grid or an autonomous vehicle fleet:

| Hospital ICU Element | CPS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient vital monitor | PLC / RTU / Sensor logger |
| Nurse station | SIEM / SOC dashboard |
| Heart-rate alert | Intrusion Detection System (IDS) alert |
| Patient medical record | Immutable log file / audit trail |
| ICU alarm threshold | Baseline behavioral profile |
| Code-blue response | Incident Response (IR) playbook |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy matters for KTU exams:** Examiners often frame questions around the *visibility* problem in CPS — operators literally cannot see a cyber-physical breach unless they have a logging + monitoring pipeline. Without logs, you are operating "blind."

### 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics (Must Remember)

- **MTTD — Mean Time To Detect:** Average time between incident occurrence and detection. Industry target for critical CPS: **< 5 minutes**.
- **MTTR — Mean Time To Respond/Recover:** Average time from detection to containment/recovery.
- **FPR — False Positive Rate:** Legitimate events incorrectly flagged as incidents.
- **FNR — False Negative Rate:** Actual incidents missed by the monitoring system (**most dangerous in CPS**).
- **Log Retention Period:** Minimum **90 days** for operational logs, **1 year** for security audit logs (per IEC 62443 and NIST SP 800-82).
- **SIEM — Security Information and Event Management:** Centralized platform that aggregates logs and correlates events.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of CPS Visibility

A CPS is **inherently multi-domain**: it spans sensors (physical), communication networks (cyber), control logic (cyber), and actuators (physical). A security incident in CPS almost always produces a *cross-domain footprint*. Effective logging & monitoring must therefore capture all three pillars:

1. **Cyber-layer logs** — packet captures, authentication attempts, firewall denies, firmware changes, VPN tunnels, SCADA protocol messages (Modbus/TCP, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, OPC UA).
2. **Physical-layer logs** — sensor values, actuator commands, setpoint changes, equipment vibration, temperature curves, pressure readings.
3. **Process/logical-layer logs** — PLC ladder logic downloads, HMI screen interactions, engineering workstation sessions, recipe/batch modifications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A unique CPS attack signature is the **cyber-physical correlation anomaly** — e.g., a cyber event (unauthorized login) occurring **seconds before** a physical event (valve opens, motor stalls). This *temporal correlation* is what distinguishes CPS intrusion detection from classical IT intrusion detection.

## 2.2 The Detection Lifecycle (Operational Concept)

```
[Event Generated] → [Log Captured] → [Log Transported] → [Log Stored]
        ↓
[Correlation Engine] → [Anomaly/Rule Match] → [Alert Raised]
        ↓
[Triage by Analyst] → [Incident Confirmed] → [Response Action]
```

Each step has measurable engineering parameters that KTU questions frequently test.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The table below consolidates every quantitative expression a KTU 2024 examiner can ask on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean Time To Detect | $\text{MTTD} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{\text{detect},i} - t_{\text{occur},i})$ | $t$ in minutes; $N$ = # incidents | SOC efficiency KPI |
| 2 | Mean Time To Respond | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{\text{contain},i} - t_{\text{detect},i})$ | $t$ in minutes | IR team efficiency |
| 3 | Detection Rate | $D = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = 1 - \text{FNR}$ | dimensionless | Quality of monitoring system |
| 4 | False Positive Rate | $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | dimensionless | Alert fatigue metric |
| 5 | Log Throughput | $\Lambda = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{T_{\text{window}}}$ | Events per Second (EPS) | SIEM capacity planning |
| 6 | Storage Requirement | $S = \Lambda \times R \times t_{\text{retention}}$ | Bytes; $R$ = avg record size | Disk / cloud sizing |
| 7 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sum P_{\text{true}}}{\sum P_{\text{false}}}\right)$ | decibels (dB) | Rule quality |
| 8 | Availability of CPS | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | dimensionless | Resilience metric |
| 9 | Baseline Deviation (z-score) | $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | dimensionless | Anomaly detection |
| 10 | Information Entropy of Logs | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p(x_i)\log_{2} p(x_i)$ | bits | Log diversity analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Absolute Values in Tables:** All set/magnitude notations have been written using inline LaTeX (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practice, logging & monitoring enables:

- **Forensic reconstruction** of attacks (e.g., the *Stuxnet* investigation relied on reconstructed logs from Natanz).
- **Regulatory compliance** with IEC 62443, NIST SP 800-82, NERC-CIP (power sector), and ISA-99.
- **Safety interlocks** — e.g., detecting an unauthorized setpoint change before a turbine overspeed event.
- **Predictive maintenance** by spotting gradual drift in sensor logs.
- **Auditability** for insurance and legal post-incident proceedings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Computing Storage Requirement for a CPS Log Pipeline

**Problem statement (typical KTU style):** A substation CPS generates logs from **4 sources**: PMU (Phasor Measurement Unit), IED (Intelligent Electronic Device), Gateway, and HMI. The average EPS (events per second) and average record size per source are:

| Source | EPS ($\lambda_i$) | Avg Record Size ($R_i$) |
|---|---|---|
| PMU | 50 | 256 B |
| IED | 20 | 512 B |
| Gateway | 10 | 1024 B |
| HMI | 5 | 2048 B |

If retention $t_{\text{ret}} = 90$ days, compute total storage $S$ required. Assume 30% overhead for indexing & compression metadata.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Per-source throughput:**

$$
\Lambda_{\text{PMU}} = 50 \text{ EPS} \times 256 \text{ B/E} = 12{,}800 \text{ B/s}
$$

$$
\Lambda_{\text{IED}} = 20 \text{ EPS} \times 512 \text{ B/E} = 10{,}240 \text{ B/s}
$$

$$
\Lambda_{\text{GW}} = 10 \text{ EPS} \times 1024 \text{ B/E} = 10{,}240 \text{ B/s}
$$

$$
\Lambda_{\text{HMI}} = 5 \text{ EPS} \times 2048 \text{ B/E} = 10{,}240 \text{ B/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate throughput:**

$$
\Lambda_{\text{total}} = 12{,}800 + 10{,}240 + 10{,}240 + 10{,}240 = 43{,}520 \text{ B/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to seconds in 90 days:**

$$
T_{\text{sec}} = 90 \times 24 \times 3600 = 7{,}776{,}000 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Raw storage:**

$$
S_{\text{raw}} = \Lambda_{\text{total}} \times T_{\text{sec}} = 43{,}520 \times 7{,}776{,}000
$$

$$
S_{\text{raw}} = 3.3841 \times 10^{11} \text{ B} \approx 338.41 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply 30% overhead:**

$$
S_{\text{final}} = S_{\text{raw}} \times 1.30 \approx 439.93 \text{ GB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A single substation needs roughly **440 GB of log storage for 90 days** — this justifies why SIEM vendors use tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) and why CPS operators often sample rather than log every packet.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Detection Quality (Confusion Matrix for CPS IDS)

Consider a CPS Intrusion Detection System evaluated on a test dataset of **10,000 events**, with the following outcomes:

| | Actual Intrusion | Actual Normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Flagged as Intrusion** | 180 (TP) | 220 (FP) |
| **Flagged as Normal** | 20 (FN) | 9,580 (TN) |

**Step 1 — Compute Detection Rate (Recall):**

$$
D = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 90\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute False Positive Rate:**

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{220}{220 + 9{,}580} = \frac{220}{9{,}800} \approx 0.02245 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 2.245\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Precision:**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 220} = \frac{180}{400} = 0.45 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 45\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot D}{P + D} = \frac{2 \times 0.45 \times 0.90}{0.45 + 0.90} = \frac{0.81}{1.35} = 0.60
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A **low FNR (high detection rate)** is *non-negotiable* in safety-critical CPS (nuclear, medical, aviation). A high FPR is annoying but tolerable; a high FNR can be catastrophic. This is the *asymmetric risk profile* unique to CPS.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When Does Each Logging Strategy Win?

| Scenario | Best Strategy | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Real-time valve control (millisecond loop) | In-memory circular buffer logging | Disk I/O too slow |
| Daily batch process reporting | Write-ahead file logging | High durability, low cost |
| Long-term forensic analysis | Immutable WORM storage + blockchain anchoring | Tamper-evidence |
| Distributed fleet of drones | Edge-local logs + compressed sync to cloud | Bandwidth-constrained |
| Air-gapped nuclear plant | Local SIEM + physical log book | No outbound connectivity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End CPS Logging & Monitoring Architecture

The block diagram below maps every architectural layer an examiner expects you to know. Each node ID is purely alphanumeric per the engine's safeguard rule.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PHYSLAYER["PHYSICAL LAYER SENSORS AND ACTUATORS"]

    subgraph COLLECT["LOG COLLECTION AND TRANSPORT"]

    A1 --> B1

    D3 --> E1
