
## 4.2 Cyber-Physical Correlation Detection Flow

This diagram illustrates how a true CPS incident (e.g., unauthorized PLC program change followed by abnormal actuator behavior) is detected through *temporal correlation* of cyber and physical logs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["CYBER EVENT UNAUTHORIZED LOGIN DETECTED"] --> stepB["TIMESTAMP T0"]
    stepB --> stepC["WINDOW COMPARISON T0 MINUS 60S TO T0 PLUS 60S"]
    stepC --> stepD{"PHYSICAL EVENT IN WINDOW?"}
    stepD -- YES --> stepE["ALERT CRITICAL CORRELATED INCIDENT"]
    stepD -- NO --> stepF["LOG AS LOW SEVERITY"]
    stepE --> stepG["TRIGGER IR PLAYBOOK"]
    stepG --> stepH["LOG TO IMMUTABLE AUDIT"]
```

## 4.3 Detection Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stratA["CHOOSE DETECTION METHOD"] --> stratB{"KNOWN SIGNATURE?"}
    stratB -- YES --> stratC["USE SIGNATURE BASED IDS"]
    stratB -- NO --> stratD{"BASELINE AVAILABLE?"}
    stratD -- YES --> stratE["USE ANOMALY BASED DETECTION"]
    stratD -- NO --> stratF["USE HYBRID ML APPROACH"]
    stratC --> stratG["DEPLOY RULES TO SIEM"]
    stratE --> stratG
    stratF --> stratG
    stratG --> stratH["CONTINUOUS TUNING"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Why is logging alone insufficient for detecting security incidents in a CPS? Explain with an example."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Logging is a *passive* activity — it records events to persistent storage but does not analyze them. A log file alone is just a "black box" with no real-time interpretation. Without an active **monitoring** layer (e.g., SIEM correlation engine, anomaly detector, or SOC analyst), critical incidents can sit undetected in logs for days.

**Example:** In the *Stuxnet* attack on Iranian centrifuges, raw logs existed but were not monitored in real-time; the malicious PLC code modifications went unnoticed for months, allowing centrifuges to be destroyed gradually. Logs without monitoring = delayed or missed detection.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "logs help detect attacks" and stop. KTU examiners specifically award the **third mark** only when you contrast *logging vs. monitoring* explicitly. Always state that logs need an *active analyzer* to be useful.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"List any six sources of logs in a typical SCADA-based CPS."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — any six accepted):**

1. PLC / RTU event logs (program changes, fault codes)
2. HMI / SCADA server logs (operator actions, screen captures)
3. Network firewall and IDS logs
4. Authentication server logs (RADIUS / AD / TACACS+)
5. Engineering workstation logs (project file modifications)
6. Historian database logs (setpoint changes, recipe edits)
7. Field device firmware logs
8. Communication protocol logs (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Pattern]** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers **internal choice** between two questions. Students must answer ONE.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **CIA Triad** in the context of CPS and describe how a breach in each component manifests in a power-grid CPS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **end-to-end CPS logging and monitoring architecture**. Explain the role of SIEM and the importance of **log correlation** in detecting cross-domain attacks. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **CIA Triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — extended to CPS:

| CIA Pillar | Meaning in IT | CPS-Specific Manifestation |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Prevent unauthorized data disclosure | Disclosure of grid load schedules → market manipulation |
| **Integrity** | Prevent unauthorized data modification | Tampering with PMU measurements → false protection relay tripping |
| **Availability** | Ensure timely access to data/services | Denial-of-service on substation gateway → blackout |

In a **power grid CPS**:

- **Confidentiality breach:** An attacker reads customer consumption data, violating privacy regulations.
- **Integrity breach:** A malicious actor modifies a circuit breaker's open/close command, causing cascading failure (as in the *Ukraine 2015* attack).
- **Availability breach:** A ransomware attack encrypts the Energy Management System (EMS), preventing grid operators from issuing control commands during peak load.

**Key CPS-specific extension:** Beyond CIA, safety-critical CPS also require **Safety, Reliability, Predictability** — sometimes called the *CISA+* model.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Refer to **Diagram 4.1** above for the end-to-end architecture.

**Role of SIEM (Security Information and Event Management):**

The SIEM is the **central nervous system** of CPS monitoring. It performs:

1. **Aggregation** — collects logs from PLCs, RTUs, firewalls, IDS, HMI, historians.
2. **Normalization** — converts heterogeneous log formats (Modbus, DNP3, syslog) into a common schema.
3. **Correlation** — links related events using time, source, and rule logic.
4. **Alerting** — raises prioritized alerts to the SOC.
5. **Retention** — stores logs for forensic and compliance use.

**Importance of Log Correlation in Cross-Domain Attacks:**

A single log line (e.g., "user X logged in") is rarely malicious. However, when **correlated** with a subsequent event ("user X uploaded new ladder logic to PLC-7") and then **physical state change** ("PLC-7 commanded valve V-12 to open beyond safe limit"), the chain reveals an attack. This *multi-domain temporal correlation* is the cornerstone of CPS-specific detection and is what differentiates it from traditional IT SIEM deployments.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Define **MTTD** and **MTTR**. A CPS operator recorded the following incident data over a quarter. Compute the **MTTD** and **MTTR**. Identify which process requires urgent improvement. **(7 marks)**

| Incident | $t_{\text{occur}}$ | $t_{\text{detect}}$ | $t_{\text{contain}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| I1 | 09:00 | 09:14 | 10:05 |
| I2 | 11:30 | 11:33 | 12:20 |
| I3 | 14:15 | 15:00 | 15:10 |
| I4 | 16:45 | 16:46 | 17:30 |

**(b)** Discuss **four challenges** unique to logging and monitoring in CPS compared to traditional IT environments. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Formulas:**

$$
\text{MTTD} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{\text{detect},i} - t_{\text{occur},i})
$$

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{\text{contain},i} - t_{\text{detect},i})
$$

**Step 1 — Detection Delays (minutes):**

- I1: $09{:}14 - 09{:}00 = 14$
- I2: $11{:}33 - 11{:}30 = 3$
- I3: $15{:}00 - 14{:}15 = 45$
- I4: $16{:}46 - 16{:}45 = 1$

**Step 2 — MTTD:**

$$
\text{MTTD} = \frac{14 + 3 + 45 + 1}{4} = \frac{63}{4} = 15.75 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Response Delays (minutes):**

- I1: $10{:}05 - 09{:}14 = 51$
- I2: $12{:}20 - 11{:}33 = 47$
- I3: $15{:}10 - 15{:}00 = 10$
- I4: $17{:}30 - 16{:}46 = 44$

**Step 4 — MTTR:**

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{51 + 47 + 10 + 44}{4} = \frac{152}{4} = 38.0 \text{ min}
$$

**Conclusion — Improvement Required:** The **MTTR (38 min)** is significantly higher than MTTD (15.75 min), and the *containment* process is the bottleneck. For a CPS, slow containment is dangerous because the attacker may already have caused physical damage. Therefore, the **incident response / containment playbooks** require urgent improvement (better automation, predefined runbooks, faster forensic handoff).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Four CPS-Unique Logging & Monitoring Challenges:**

1. **Heterogeneous Protocols & Clock Skew:** CPS uses Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850, OPC UA, PROFIBUS — none natively synchronized. NTP is often unavailable in air-gapped systems, making temporal correlation unreliable.

2. **Resource-Constrained Devices:** Sensors and embedded RTUs cannot run heavy logging agents; logs are often sampled or aggregated at gateways, losing fidelity.

3. **Real-Time Constraints vs. Logging Overhead:** Writing every packet to disk may exceed the millisecond control loop budget. Engineers must choose what to log without breaking the control cycle.

4. **Safety vs. Security Trade-off:** A logging system that *only* detects intrusions may inadvertently delay a safety shutdown. CPS monitoring must distinguish "attack" from "fault" without compromising the *Safety Integrity Level (SIL)*.

5. *(Bonus)* **Air-Gapped Networks** prevent cloud SIEM uploads, forcing on-premise solutions with limited scalability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Read Before Exam):**
> - ❌ **Do NOT** confuse *logging* (storage) with *monitoring* (analysis) — this distinction alone is worth 2–3 marks per question.
> - ❌ **Do NOT** give only IT-centric examples (e.g., SQL injection) — always bring in a *physical* consequence (motor burnout, valve stuck, sensor spoofed).
> - ❌ **Do NOT** forget to mention **IEC 62443 / NIST SP 800-82** in long answers; examiners award bonus credit for standards awareness.
> - ✅ **Do** draw the architecture diagram even if not asked — it is a 1–2 mark differentiator.
> - ✅ **Do** show intermediate steps in numericals; KTU's step-marking policy rewards every correct intermediate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Logging** = recording events; **Monitoring** = analyzing them in (near) real-time. Both are required.
- CPS attacks are **cross-domain**; detection requires **cyber-physical temporal correlation**.
- **CIA Triad** extends to CPS with **Safety, Reliability, Predictability** (CISA+ model).
- **MTTD** = detect time − occur time; **MTTR** = contain time − detect time. Lower is better.
- **FNR is more dangerous than FPR** in CPS (asymmetric risk).
- SIEM is the **central correlation engine**; it aggregates, normalizes, correlates, alerts, retains.
- **Key log sources:** PLC, RTU, HMI, Historian, Firewall, Auth Server, Engineering Workstation, IDS, Field Devices.
- **Standards to cite:** IEC 62443, NIST SP 800-82, NERC-CIP, ISA-99.
- **Storage formula:** $S = \Lambda \times R \times t_{\text{ret}} \times (1 + \text{overhead})$.
- **CPS-unique challenges:** protocol heterogeneity, clock skew, resource constraints, real-time vs. logging trade-off, safety-security conflict.
- **Famous case studies:** Stuxnet (Iran, 2010), Ukraine Power Grid (2015), Triton/TRISIS (Saudi Arabia, 2017), Colonial Pipeline (2021).
- **Always** end answers with a *practical engineering recommendation* — examiners reward applied thinking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools and Techniques for Effective Monitoring in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tools and Techniques for Effective Monitoring in CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **CPS Monitoring** is the continuous, real-time observation, measurement, and analysis of the cyber, physical, and hybrid processes within a Cyber-Physical System to ensure operational integrity, detect anomalies, identify security threats, and maintain safety, reliability, and performance guarantees.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme PECPT413 syllabus**, effective monitoring in CPS involves the systematic deployment of hardware sensors, software agents, network probes, and analytical engines that collectively generate telemetry data from:
- **Process-level signals** (temperature, pressure, flow, voltage, current, vibration)
- **Network-level traffic** (MODBUS/TCP, DNP3, IEC-60870-5-104, PROFINET, EtherCAT, OPC-UA)
- **Host-level events** (system calls, log entries, firmware integrity, file modifications)
- **Application-layer transactions** (HMI commands, PLC ladder logic changes, SCADA tag updates)

The primary goal is to achieve **Continuous Diagnostics and Mitigation (CDM)** — a concept formally adopted by U.S. DHS / NIST (SP 800-137) and now standard in industrial cybersecurity frameworks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPS Monitoring ≠ IT Monitoring.** In CPS, the **physical consequence** of a missed alert can be loss of life, environmental damage, or catastrophic equipment failure. Therefore, monitoring must satisfy **hard real-time constraints** (typically **sub-millisecond to sub-second response windows**), unlike traditional IT monitoring.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Industrial ICU

Imagine a modern **Neonatal Intensive Care Unit (NICU)** in a hospital:

- **Infant (CPS process)** continuously emits vital signs — heart rate, oxygen saturation, blood pressure.
- **Patient monitor (SIEM / IDS)** collects these vitals through leads (sensors) attached to the body.
- **Alarm thresholds (anomaly detection rules)** are pre-set: if SpO₂ drops below **90%**, the system alerts immediately.
- **Nursing station dashboard (SCADA HMI / SOC console)** shows the consolidated view of all monitored parameters.
- **An attending physician (security analyst / control engineer)** validates alarms and prescribes corrective action.

Now, replace the infant with a **steam turbine, a chemical reactor, a smart grid substation, or an autonomous vehicle fleet** — and you have CPS monitoring.

| NICU Element | CPS Equivalent |
|--------------|---------------|
| Patient vitals | Process sensor data (PT, TT, FT, LT) |
| Patient monitor | IDS / IPS / SIEM |
| Alarm threshold | Anomaly rule (e.g., temperature > 120°C) |
| Nurse station | SCADA HMI / SOC Dashboard |
| Physician | Control engineer / SOC analyst |
| ICU ventilator | Safety Instrumented System (SIS) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question asks *"Why is monitoring critical in CPS?"*, always mention the **convergence of safety, security, and reliability (S-S-R)** — three properties that are independently guaranteed in classical systems but jointly required in CPS.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of CPS Monitoring (CSDM Model)

A widely accepted classification decomposes CPS monitoring into:

1. **Cyber Monitoring** — Observation of digital artefacts (packets, logs, binaries, configurations).
2. **Physical Monitoring** — Observation of physical phenomena (sensor readings, actuator states, energy flow).
3. **Cross-Domain Monitoring** — Correlation between cyber events and physical consequences (e.g., a network packet causes a valve to open).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in CPS Monitoring

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bolded metrics** are the most frequently tested in KTU university examinations.

- **MTTD (Mean Time To Detect):** Average time between an event's occurrence and its detection. Target: **< 1 second** for safety-critical CPS.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Respond):** Average time between detection and containment.
- **MTTC (Mean Time To Contain):** Time from detection to isolation of the threat.
- **FPR (False Positive Rate):** Probability that a benign event is flagged as malicious.
- **FNR (False Negative Rate):** Probability that a malicious event is missed.
- **Detection Accuracy:** Ratio of correctly classified events to total events.
- **Precision (P)** and **Recall (R):** Standard information-retrieval metrics adapted to intrusion detection.
- **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall.
- **AUC-ROC:** Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve, summarizing classifier performance.
- **Latency (τ):** End-to-end delay from event occurrence to alert generation. Hard real-time CPS requires **τ ≤ 100 ms** for safety loops.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of CPS Monitoring Tools

The KTU 2024 scheme organizes CPS monitoring tools along **three orthogonal axes**: (a) the layer they observe, (b) the methodology they use, and (c) the deployment model.

### 2.1.1 Axis 1 — Observation Layer

- **Network Layer Monitoring**
  - **Passive network taps / SPAN ports** — non-intrusive mirroring of industrial traffic.
  - **Protocol analyzers** (Wireshark, tcpdump, Savvius, Claroty Continuous Threat Detection) — decode OT protocols like MODBUS, DNP3, IEC-61850, EtherNet/IP.
  - **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** engines — parse industrial payload beyond headers.

- **Host Layer Monitoring**
  - **Host-based IDS (HIDS)** — Tripwire, OSSEC, Wazuh.
  - **Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)** — CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint.
  - **File Integrity Monitoring (FIM)** — detect unauthorized changes to PLC firmware, HMI projects, engineering workstations.

- **Process / Physical Layer Monitoring**
  - **Process Historians** — OSIsoft PI System, AVEVA Historian, InfluxDB (open-source).
  - **Anomaly detectors on sensor streams** — autoencoders, statistical CUSUM, ARIMA, LSTM.
  - **Physics-based observers** — Kalman filters, state observers that compare predicted vs. measured states.

- **Application Layer Monitoring**
  - **HMI/SCADA event logs** (WinCC, FactoryTalk, Citect).
  - **Engineering workstation activity** (Studio 5000, TIA Portal change logs).
  - **PLC programming audit trails**.

### 2.1.2 Axis 2 — Detection Methodology

| Methodology | How It Works | Strength | Limitation |
|------------|-------------|----------|------------|
| **Signature-based** | Matches event to a known pattern (Snort rule, YARA rule) | Very low FPR, fast | Cannot detect zero-day attacks |
| **Anomaly-based** | Builds a model of "normal" and flags deviations | Detects novel attacks | High FPR, retraining needed |
| **Stateful / Specification-based** | Enforces a formal model of expected system behavior | No training required, deterministic | Manual rule authoring |
| **Behavioral (UEBA)** | Per-entity baseline of user/device behavior | Detects insider threats | Cold-start problem |
| **Hybrid (Multi-method)** | Combines 2 or more methods | Balanced precision and recall | Computational overhead |

### 2.1.3 Axis 3 — Deployment Model

- **Inline (in-path) monitoring** — IPS placed in series with traffic; can actively block.
- **Passive (out-of-path) monitoring** — Taps SPAN ports; observation only, no interference (preferred for OT to avoid latency/jitter).
- **Agent-based** — Software installed on endpoint.
- **Agentless** — Polls via standard protocols (SNMP, WMI, MODBUS).
- **Centralized SIEM** — All logs to a single correlation engine.
- **Distributed / Edge monitoring** — Each cell has local analytics; reduces bandwidth and central SPOF.

## 2.2 Core Tools and Their Functional Roles

### 2.2.1 SIEM (Security Information and Event Management)

A SIEM performs **log aggregation, normalization, correlation, and alerting**. For CPS, the leading SIEM platforms are:

- **Splunk Enterprise Security** — widely used; supports custom OT parsers.
- **IBM QRadar** — strong correlation engine.
- **Microsoft Sentinel** — cloud-native; integrates with Defender for IoT.
- **Elastic Security (ELK Stack)** — open-source; popular in academic setups.
- **Nozomi Networks Guardian / ArcSight** — OT-native SIEMs with built-in ICS protocol parsers.

### 2.2.2 IDS / IPS for CPS

- **Snort / Suricata** — Open-source NIDS with OT rule sets (e.g., `scada:protocol:modbus`).
- **Zeek (formerly Bro)** — Network analysis framework; generates structured logs (conn.log, modbus.log).
- **Claroty, Nozomi, Dragos, Tenable.ot, CyberX** — Commercial ICS-aware NDR (Network Detection & Response) platforms.
- **OSIsoft / AVEVA Edge** — Combines historian with anomaly detection.

### 2.2.3 Network Monitoring and Visibility

- **Wireshark** — De facto packet analyzer; supports MODBUS, DNP3, IEC-60870, GOOSE, SV, PROFINET.
- **tcpdump** — CLI packet capture.
- **ntopng** — NetFlow/IPFIX analyzer for traffic profiling.
- **Grafana + Prometheus** — Open-source time-series visualization for process metrics.
- **Scada-LTS, OpenSCADA, Ignition (trial)** — Open SCADA HMI for lab setups.

### 2.2.4 Specialized CPS Tools

- **GRFICS (Graphical Realism Framework for Industrial Control Systems)** — SANS Institute's open-source ICS simulation/sandbox for training and testing monitoring tools.
- **Conpot** — Low-interaction ICS honeypot mimicking MODBUS/S7comm servers.
- **Snap7** — Library to communicate with Siemens S7 PLCs (used in research for monitoring).
- **pymodbus** — Python MODBUS simulator and monitor.
- **Kali ICS tools** — `modbus-cli`, `s7scan`, `plcscan`.

### 2.2.5 Log and Event Management

- **Syslog-ng / rsyslog** — Forwarding and storage of syslog messages.
- **Graylog** — Open-source log management with correlation rules.
- **The ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana)** — The most popular open-source stack for CPS log analysis.

### 2.2.6 Threat Intelligence Platforms (TIPs)

- **MISP (Malware Information Sharing Platform)** — Open-source; widely used in OT-ISACs.
- **Anomali ThreatStream** — Commercial TIP.
- **AlienVault OTX** — Open threat exchange.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative formula tested in KTU board examinations on this topic. **Avoid the `|` character inside tables** — we use `\vert` for absolute value to keep the markdown safe.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | FP = False Positives, TN = True Negatives | Dimensionless, range [0,1] |
| 2 | False Negative Rate | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | FN = False Negatives, TP = True Positives | Dimensionless, range [0,1] |
| 3 | True Positive Rate (Recall / Sensitivity) | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = 1 - FNR$ | Same as above | Dimensionless, range [0,1] |
| 4 | True Negative Rate (Specificity) | $TNR = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP} = 1 - FPR$ | — | Dimensionless, range [0,1] |
| 5 | Precision (Positive Predictive Value) | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | — | Dimensionless, range [0,1] |
| 6 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | R = Recall = TPR | Dimensionless, range [0,1] |
| 7 | Detection Accuracy | $A = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | — | Dimensionless, range [0,1] |
| 8 | Matthews Correlation Coefficient | $MCC = \dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Robust to class imbalance | Range [-1, +1] |
| 9 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (sensor stream) | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} \right)$ | Power ratio in dB | Typically 40–80 dB in industrial sensors |
| 10 | Mean Time To Detect (MTTD) | $MTTD = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{detect,i} - t_{event,i})$ | Average over N incidents | Seconds |
| 11 | Mean Time To Respond (MTTR) | $MTTR = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{resolve,i} - t_{detect,i})$ | — | Seconds to minutes |
| 12 | Throughput (monitoring engine) | $\lambda = \dfrac{P_{processed}}{T_{window}}$ | Events processed per second | Events/sec (eps) |
| 13 | Anomaly Score (statistical z-score) | $z_i = \dfrac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Distance from mean in σ units | Threshold $\vert z \vert \geq 3$ for alarm |
| 14 | CUSUM Decision Statistic | $S_t = \max(0, S_{t-1} + z_t - k)$ | $k$ = reference value, alarm when $S_t \geq h$ | h chosen for given ARL₀ |
| 15 | Log-likelihood ratio (CUSUM) | $\Lambda_t = \log \dfrac{f_\theta(x_t)}{f_0(x_t)}$ | Density under attack vs. normal | Threshold $\Lambda_t \geq \eta$ |
| 16 | ROC AUC | $AUC = \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$ | Area under ROC curve | 1.0 = perfect, 0.5 = random |
| 17 | Information Entropy (stream randomness) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon entropy in bits | Used for detecting encrypted exfiltration |
| 18 | Shannon Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling frequency | $f_s$ ≥ 2× signal bandwidth |
| 19 | End-to-end Monitoring Latency | $\tau = \tau_{sense} + \tau_{trans} + \tau_{queue} + \tau_{proc} + \tau_{alert}$ | Sum of all sub-delays | Must be ≤ 100 ms for safety loops |
| 20 | Availability (operational) | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures | 0.99999 (five 9s) for safety systems |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic exercises — they drive real procurement decisions:

- **Nuclear power plants** require **$A \geq 0.99999$** for safety monitoring (IEC 61508 SIL-3 / SIL-4).
- **Smart grid SCADA** demands **$FPR \leq 0.01$** to avoid operator alarm fatigue.
- **Autonomous vehicles** require **$\tau \leq 10 \text{ ms}$** for obstacle-detection monitoring loops.
- **Pharmaceutical batch control** uses **$P \geq 0.999$** and **$R \geq 0.999$** (F1 ≥ 0.999) to comply with FDA 21 CFR Part 11.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of F1-Score from First Principles (Begin with the Confusion Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> The F1-Score is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU 2024 scheme ESE papers. The KTU board expects an exhaustive, multi-line derivation that explicitly links the confusion matrix to the harmonic mean.

### Step 1 — Establish the Confusion Matrix

For a binary classifier that distinguishes "Normal" (N) from "Attack" (A) on a CPS event stream:

|  | **Predicted N** | **Predicted A** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual N** | TN | FP |
| **Actual A** | FN | TP |

- **TP** = True Positives — actual attacks correctly flagged.
- **TN** = True Negatives — actual normals correctly ignored.
- **FP** = False Positives (Type I error) — normals wrongly flagged.
- **FN** = False Negatives (Type II error) — attacks missed (most dangerous in CPS).

### Step 2 — Express Precision and Recall

Precision (P) is the fraction of *alarms raised* that are *real attacks*:

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

Recall (R) is the fraction of *real attacks* that are *correctly raised*:

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

### Step 3 — The Harmonic Mean Motivation

The arithmetic mean of P and R would be inflated whenever one of them is high and the other is low. To penalise such imbalances, the **harmonic mean** is used because it is dominated by the smaller of the two values.

Define the harmonic mean $F_1$ of two positive numbers $P$ and $R$ as:

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}$$

### Step 4 — Algebraic Simplification

Multiply numerator and denominator by $P \cdot R$:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{R + P}$$

This is the standard form taught in KTU classes.

### Step 5 — Express F1 in Terms of Confusion Matrix Counts

Substitute the expressions for P and R:

$$\frac{1}{P} = \frac{TP + FP}{TP}, \quad \frac{1}{R} = \frac{TP + FN}{TP}$$

Hence:

$$\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R} = \frac{TP + FP}{TP} + \frac{TP + FN}{TP} = \frac{2\,TP + FP + FN}{TP}$$

Substituting back:

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{2\,TP + FP + FN}{TP}} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2\,TP + FP + FN}$$

$$\boxed{F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2\,TP + FP + FN}}$$

This form is **directly usable** when only the confusion matrix is given.

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

Suppose a CPS monitoring system processes **1000 events** in one day with the following confusion matrix:

|  | Pred N | Pred A |
|---|---|---|
| **Act N** | 880 | 20 |
| **Act A** | 10 | 90 |

Calculate F1, accuracy, and FPR.

**Solution:**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{90}{90 + 20} = \frac{90}{110} = 0.8182$$

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{90}{90 + 10} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.9000$$

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.8182 \times 0.9000}{0.8182 + 0.9000} = \frac{1.4727}{1.7182} \approx 0.8571$$

$$A = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{90 + 880}{1000} = 0.9700$$

$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{20}{20 + 880} = \frac{20}{900} \approx 0.0222$$

**Interpretation:** The system detects 90% of attacks (Recall = 0.90) with moderate precision (0.82). Accuracy is 97%, but FPR is 2.22% — which would generate **~20 false alarms per day**, a likely cause of operator fatigue.

## 3.2 Derivation of the CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) Decision Statistic

The **CUSUM algorithm** is the workhorse anomaly detector for slow-drift attacks (e.g., a stealthy pressure change in a chemical reactor). The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly includes CUSUM in Module 3.

### Step 1 — Define the Log-Likelihood Ratio

Let $x_t$ be the observed sensor value at time $t$. We have two hypotheses:

- $H_0$: $x_t$ follows a normal distribution $f_0(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \sigma^2)$.
- $H_1$: $x_t$ follows a shifted distribution $f_1(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_0 + \delta, \sigma^2)$ where $\delta$ is the attack-induced drift.

The log-likelihood ratio (LLR) is:

$$z_t = \log \frac{f_1(x_t)}{f_0(x_t)} = \frac{(x_t - \mu_0)\delta}{\sigma^2} - \frac{\delta^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

### Step 2 — Define the Drift Reference $k$

The term $\frac{\delta^2}{2\sigma^2}$ is constant; we define a reference value $k$ to centre the statistic:

$$k = \frac{\delta^2}{2\sigma^2} \quad \text{(sometimes also written as } k = \frac{\delta}{2} \text{ in simplified form)}$$

### Step 3 — The Recursive CUSUM Statistic

The cumulative decision statistic is the running sum of "excess" LLRs, floored at zero (so it does not decrease when the system returns to normal):

$$S_t = \max\left(0, \; S_{t-1} + z_t - k\right)$$

### Step 4 — Decision Rule

The alarm is raised when:

$$S_t \geq h$$

where $h$ is a threshold chosen to achieve a target **Average Run Length under $H_0$ (ARL₀)** — typically 1000 to 10000 samples.

### Step 5 — Steady-State Behaviour Proof (Skipped Step Justification)

If the process is truly under $H_0$, $E[z_t] = -k$ (because the constant term dominates), so the expected increment of $S_t$ is non-positive. The floored recursion ensures $S_t$ stays near 0. Conversely, under $H_1$, $E[z_t] > k$, so $S_t$ increases on average, eventually crossing $h$. This validates the rule.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Detection Methodologies

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is a high-yield revision tool. KTU examiners often give 3–5 marks just for drawing a comparison.

| Criterion | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based | Specification-Based | Behavioral (UEBA) |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------|--------------------|------------------|
| Training required | No | Yes (large) | No (manual rules) | Yes (per entity) |
| Zero-day detection | Poor | Excellent | Moderate (if model is comprehensive) | Good |
| False Positive Rate | Very Low | High | Very Low | Medium |
| Computational cost | Low | High | Low | Medium |
| Adaptation to new attacks | None | High (via retraining) | Manual update | High |
| Suitability for CPS (real-time) | Excellent | Moderate | Excellent | Moderate |
| Operator trust | High (explainable) | Low (black-box) | High (deterministic) | Medium |
| Example tool | Snort, Suricata | Isolation Forest, LSTM | Bro/Zeek policy scripts | Exabeam, Securonix |

## 3.4 Numerical Derivation — Sampling Theorem Boundary

A pressure transmitter in a pipeline must detect pressure transients. The fastest transient has bandwidth $f_{max} = 250 \text{ Hz}$.

By the **Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem**:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 250 = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

To provide a guard band (typical industrial practice is **5× oversampling**), choose $f_s = 2.5 \text{ kHz}$.

This sampling frequency determines the **monitoring engine's minimum processing throughput** $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = f_s \times N_{channels} = 2500 \times 32 = 80{,}000 \text{ events/sec}$$

If the chosen SIEM cannot sustain this rate, the system violates the real-time CPS monitoring requirement.


**Reading the diagram:**
- **Levels 0–1 (Process & I/O):** physical phenomena and field devices. Monitoring is via process historians and physics-based observers.
- **Levels 2–3 (Control & Operations):** SCADA, HMI, engineering workstations. Monitored by NIDS (Suricata/Zeek) and HIDS (Wazuh/OSSEC).
- **Level 4 (Enterprise IT):** corporate network, ERP, email. Monitored by EDR and SIEM.
- **Passive taps** feed network traffic to the NIDS without inserting latency.
- **SIEM** is the correlation hub that fuses cyber + physical + behavioural signals.
- **Alerts** can trigger the **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** to perform a protective shutdown.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting.**
> - **Always mention the convergence of safety + security + reliability** when explaining the *importance* of CPS monitoring. Students who treat it like IT monitoring lose 2 marks.
> - **Always cite a specific tool and its role.** Vague answers like "we use a SIEM" are not enough — write "Splunk ES / ELK Stack aggregates logs from Wireshark captures and Suricata alerts and applies correlation rules".
> - **When asked about "anomaly detection", you must explain the limitation** (high FPR) and the **mitigation** (hybrid with signature-based detection). Omitting this loses 2 marks.
> - **Do not confuse SIEM, IDS, IPS, EDR, NDR.** Memorize the acronym meanings and the layer each operates on.
> - **Numerical questions always test the confusion matrix → F1 / FPR / accuracy chain.** Practice the derivation in Section 3.1.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define CPS monitoring. How is it different from traditional IT monitoring?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
CPS monitoring is the continuous observation of cyber, physical, and cross-domain signals in a Cyber-Physical System to ensure safety, security, and reliability.
It differs from IT monitoring in three ways:
1. **Real-time constraints** — sub-second response; IT monitoring tolerates minutes.
2. **Safety implications** — missed alerts cause physical harm; IT misses cause data loss.
3. **Protocol awareness** — CPS uses MODBUS/DNP3/IEC-61850; IT uses HTTP/SMTP.
*(Full marks require all three differences.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**List any three signature-based intrusion detection tools used in CPS monitoring and state their key feature.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Snort** — open-source NIDS with OT-specific rulesets; lightweight.
2. **Suricata** — multi-threaded NIDS; supports MODBUS/DNP3 decoding.
3. **Zeek (Bro)** — generates structured logs (conn.log, modbus.log) suitable for SIEM ingestion.
*(Each tool with one feature ⇒ 1 mark; full marks = 3 tools.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four-level architecture of CPS monitoring** as applied to an industrial SCADA system. Discuss the role of passive network taps, SIEM, and process historians. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Define **Precision, Recall, F1-score, FPR, and Accuracy**. For a CPS anomaly detection system that processed 2000 events with TP = 180, FP = 25, FN = 20, TN = 1775, compute each metric and comment on the suitability of the system for a safety-critical loop. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A CPS monitoring architecture for a SCADA plant is best organized in **four levels**, derived from the **Purdue Reference Model**:

1. **Level 0 — Physical Process:** Pumps, valves, motors, reactors. *Monitoring artefact:* field sensors (pressure, temperature, flow, vibration). These generate raw analogue/digital signals.

2. **Level 1 — Sensors, Actuators, and PLCs:** Direct control loop. *Monitoring tools:* **Process Historians** (e.g., OSIsoft PI) that store high-fidelity time-series data; **physics-based observers** (Kalman filter) that flag deviations between predicted and observed states.

3. **Level 2 — SCADA HMI and Engineering Workstations:** Supervisory control. *Monitoring tools:* **HIDS** (Wazuh, OSSEC) for file-integrity and host logs; **NIDS** (Suricata, Zeek) fed by **passive SPAN taps** that mirror the OT network traffic without inserting latency.

4. **Level 3 — Operations DMZ and Enterprise IT (Level 3.5/4):** Business network. *Monitoring tools:* **EDR** (CrowdStrike) and **SIEM** (Splunk, QRadar, Sentinel) that aggregate events from Levels 0–3 and apply correlation rules.

**Role of key components:**
- **Passive Network Tap / SPAN Port:** A hardware splitter that copies every packet on an industrial segment to the NIDS without altering the live traffic. This is critical in OT because any in-line device can introduce jitter that violates real-time guarantees.
- **SIEM:** The central correlation engine. It normalizes logs from heterogeneous sources, applies correlation rules (e.g., "three failed logins on HMI within 60 s AND a subsequent MODBUS write to a critical register"), and raises prioritized alerts.
- **Process Historian:** A high-resolution time-series database (sub-second granularity) that enables forensic reconstruction of the physical process, supports anomaly detection on slow-drift variables (using CUSUM or LSTM), and provides regulatory audit trails.

**Block diagram:** *(Refer to the Mermaid architecture in Section 4.1.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Given: TP = 180, FP = 25, FN = 20, TN = 1775, Total = 2000.

**Precision:**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 25} = \frac{180}{205} \approx 0.8780$$

**Recall:**

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.9000$$

**F1-Score:**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.8780 \times 0.9000}{0.8780 + 0.9000} = \frac{1.5804}{1.7780} \approx 0.8889$$

**False Positive Rate:**

$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{25}{25 + 1775} = \frac{25}{1800} \approx 0.0139$$

**Accuracy:**

$$A = \frac{TP + TN}{Total} = \frac{180 + 1775}{2000} = \frac{1955}{2000} = 0.9775$$

**Comment on suitability for safety-critical loop:**
- **Recall = 0.90** is good but **not sufficient** for a SIL-3 / SIL-4 safety loop (typically needs ≥ 0.999).
- **FPR = 1.39%** is acceptable but will still generate ~28 false alarms per day in a 2000-event stream — a contributor to operator fatigue.
- **Recommendation:** Augment with a **signature-based** or **specification-based** detector to push Recall > 0.999 and FPR < 0.001 before deploying in a safety-critical application.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **three detection methodologies** (signature, anomaly, specification) used in CPS monitoring. Compare them on at least four criteria using a table. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **F1-score** starting from its definition as the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. Express it in terms of the confusion matrix entries (TP, FP, FN, TN). Compute F1 for TP = 95, FP = 10, FN = 5, TN = 890. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The three detection methodologies form the **detection spine** of any CPS monitoring stack.

1. **Signature-Based Detection:**
   - Compares each event to a database of known attack patterns (rules, hashes, YARA patterns, Snort signatures).
   - *Example:* A MODBUS packet with function code 90 (undefined) flagged because the registered list contains only codes 1–8, 15–16, 23.
   - *Strength:* Extremely low FPR; deterministic; explainable.
   - *Weakness:* Zero-day blind; signature database must be constantly updated.

2. **Anomaly-Based Detection:**
   - Learns a statistical or ML model of "normal" behaviour and flags deviations.
   - *Example:* LSTM autoencoder on pressure sensor; reconstruction error > 3σ triggers an alarm.
   - *Strength:* Detects novel / zero-day attacks.
   - *Weakness:* High FPR; requires extensive training data; may be slow.

3. **Specification-Based (Stateful) Detection:**
   - Encodes a formal model of expected system behaviour (state machines, invariants) and flags violations.
   - *Example:* A rule that an HMI cannot issue more than 5 write commands per minute to a specific register.
   - *Strength:* No training needed; very low FPR; highly explainable; deterministic.
   - *Weakness:* Manual rule authoring is labour-intensive; incomplete specifications can miss attacks.

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Signature | Anomaly | Specification |
|-----------|-----------|---------|---------------|
| Detection of zero-days | Poor | Excellent | Moderate |
| False Positive Rate | Very Low | High | Very Low |
| Training data needed | No | Yes (large) | No (but rules) |
| Computational cost | Low | High | Low |
| Operator trust | High | Low | High |
| Maintenance | Update signatures | Retrain model | Update specs |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Definition of F1 as the harmonic mean:**

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute P and R in terms of confusion matrix entries:**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}, \quad R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{1}{P} = \frac{TP + FP}{TP}, \quad \frac{1}{R} = \frac{TP + FN}{TP}$$

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R} = \frac{(TP + FP) + (TP + FN)}{TP} = \frac{2\,TP + FP + FN}{TP}$$

**Step 4 — Invert and multiply by 2:**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2\,TP + FP + FN}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation (TP = 95, FP = 10, FN = 5, TN = 890):**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \times 95}{2 \times 95 + 10 + 5} = \frac{190}{190 + 15} = \frac{190}{205} \approx 0.9268$$

**Step 6 — Verification via Precision and Recall:**

$$P = \frac{95}{95 + 10} = 0.9048, \quad R = \frac{95}{95 + 5} = 0.9500$$

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.9048 \times 0.9500}{0.9048 + 0.9500} = \frac{1.7191}{1.8548} \approx 0.9268 \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3 (Monitoring Tools and Techniques in CPS)**

- ✅ **Definition:** CPS monitoring = continuous, real-time observation of cyber + physical + cross-domain signals.
- ✅ **Key difference from IT monitoring:** Hard real-time, safety-critical, protocol-aware (MODBUS, DNP3, IEC-61850).
- ✅ **Three layers of monitoring:** Cyber (NIDS/HIDS), Physical (historians, observers), Cross-domain (correlation).
- ✅ **Three detection methods:** Signature (Snort, YARA), Anomaly (LSTM, Isolation Forest, CUSUM), Specification (state-machine rules).
- ✅ **Essential tools to memorize:**
  - *Network:* Wireshark, tcpdump, Zeek, Suricata, Snort, ntopng.
  - *Host:* OSSEC, Wazuh, Tripwire, CrowdStrike (EDR).
  - *SIEM:* Splunk ES, QRadar, Sentinel, ELK, Graylog.
  - *OT-native:* Claroty, Nozomi, Dragos, Tenable.ot, CyberX.
  - *Specialized:* Conpot (honeypot), GRFICS (sandbox), pymodbus, Snap7.
  - *Visualization:* Grafana, Prometheus, OSIsoft PI.
- ✅ **Confusion matrix formulas:** Precision, Recall, F1, FPR, FNR, Accuracy, MCC — all must be derivable from scratch.
- ✅ **F1 derived form:** $F_1 = \dfrac{2\,TP}{2\,TP + FP + FN}$ — the KTU-favourite direct formula.
- ✅ **CUSUM statistic:** $S_t = \max(0, S_{t-1} + z_t - k)$; alarm when $S_t \geq h$.
- ✅ **z-score threshold:** $\vert z \vert \geq 3$ is the standard industrial anomaly trigger.
- ✅ **Sampling theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; practice the 5× oversampling rule.
- ✅ **MTTD, MTTR, MTTC:** Definitions and target values for CPS (sub-second for safety loops).
- ✅ **Latency equation:** $\tau = \tau_{sense} + \tau_{trans} + \tau_{queue} + \tau_{proc} + \tau_{alert}$; must be ≤ 100 ms for safety-critical monitoring.
- ✅ **Passive vs. Inline monitoring:** OT prefers **passive taps** to avoid introducing latency/jitter.
- ✅ **Purdue Model levels** (0–5) and the location of each monitoring tool within them.
- ✅ **Top real-world frameworks:** NIST SP 800-137 (CDM), ISA/IEC 62443, NERC-CIP, NIS2 Directive.
- ✅ **Examiner favourite 14-marker:** Confusion matrix numerical + monitoring architecture diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Developing Incident Response Plans (IRPs) Specific to CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Developing Incident Response Plans (IRPs) Specific to CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS) Incident Response Plan (IRP)** is a structured, pre-documented set of procedures, roles, communication protocols, and technical actions that an organization executes when a cyber attack, operational anomaly, or safety event compromises the confidentiality, integrity, or availability (the classic **CIA Triad** extended with **Safety** as the *fourth pillar* in CPS) of integrated computational and physical process-control assets.

Per the **NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2** framework (Computer Security Incident Handling Guide), an IRP is a *living document* that prescribes the six canonical lifecycle phases: **Preparation → Detection & Analysis → Containment, Eradication & Recovery → Post-Incident Activity**, adapted here for Operational Technology (OT) and Industrial Control System (ICS) environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPS-Specific Definition (KTU Board Exam Definition):**
> "An Incident Response Plan for Cyber-Physical Systems is a coordinated, multi-disciplinary strategy that integrates IT cybersecurity protocols with OT operational continuity and engineering safety constraints, ensuring that during a cyber-induced physical process compromise, response actions preserve human life, environmental integrity, and physical asset safety *before* pursuing traditional cyber forensics or system restoration goals."

## 1.2 The CPS-IRP Triad (Four Pillars)

Unlike purely IT environments, a CPS IRP must defend four competing priorities, often expressed as the **AIC-S + Safety** model:

| Priority | Description |
|---|---|
| **Availability** | Continuous operation of physical processes (e.g., power grid, water treatment) |
| **Integrity** | Correctness of sensor readings, control commands, and actuator outputs |
| **Confidentiality** | Protection of process parameters, recipes, and proprietary control logic |
| **Safety** | Prevention of physical harm to humans, environment, and infrastructure |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hospital Emergency Room (ER) Analogy:**
> Imagine a hospital ER handling two types of emergencies:
> 1. **Medical emergencies** (heart attacks, injuries) → analogous to **physical process faults**
> 2. **Cyber attacks on hospital IT** (ransomware on patient records) → analogous to **IT-only incidents**
>
> A normal hospital has separate protocols for each. But what if a hacker simultaneously corrupts the *heart-rate monitors* AND turns off the *ventilator controls*? Now you have a **CPS incident** — a cyber attack with *physical consequences*.
>
> A CPS IRP is like a **joint IT+Medical disaster protocol**:
> - The *IT team* must isolate the network
> - The *medical team* must keep the patient alive using backup/manual equipment
> - The *forensic team* must preserve evidence without touching critical systems
> - The *safety officer* has **veto power** over any action that could harm the patient
>
> In a CPS IRP, **Safety ALWAYS outranks Security**, and **Physical Continuity ALWAYS outranks Digital Forensics**.

## 1.4 Why Traditional IT IRPs Fail in CPS

Standard IT incident playbooks (e.g., "pull the network cable," "wipe and reimage the server") can be **catastrophically dangerous** in CPS:

| IT IRP Action | CPS Consequence |
|---|---|
| Power off compromised server | Physical process loses controller → plant shutdown, equipment damage |
| Quarantine host from network | SCADA master loses visibility → operator "blind flying" |
| Wipe and reimage PLC | Loss of proprietary ladder logic, calibration constants |
| Block all incoming traffic | Legitimate sensor telemetry blocked → control loops destabilize |
| Force password reset on all accounts | Operator stations locked out during critical operation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Exam Pitfall:** Students often answer CPS security questions by copying IT playbooks. The examiner explicitly deducts marks for "IT-centric solutions applied to OT without safety justification."
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Phase CPS Incident Response Lifecycle (NIST SP 800-61 Adapted)

### Phase 1: PREPARATION (Pre-Incident Baseline)

This phase builds organizational readiness **before** an incident occurs. In CPS, this is the *most critical* phase because downtime during a physical-process compromise can cause irreversible harm.

**Key Activities:**
- Asset inventory (Purdue Model Levels 0–5)
- Network segmentation diagrams (DMZ, IDMZ, Safety Instrumented Systems zone)
- Baseline of normal process behavior (mean, variance of sensor data)
- Establishment of IR team roles (CISO, OT lead, Safety officer, Legal, PR)
- Pre-authorized "break-glass" emergency actions
- Tabletop exercises and red-team/blue-team drills
- Backup and recovery of PLC ladder logic, HMI configurations, DCS recipes

### Phase 2: DETECTION & ANALYSIS (Identification)

**CPS-Specific Detection Vectors:**
- **Cyber indicators:** SIEM alerts, IDS/IPS signatures, anomalous logins
- **Physical indicators:** Out-of-range sensor values, unexpected actuator behavior, alarm storms
- **Hybrid indicators:** "Cyber-physical correlation" — e.g., a network spike coincides with a process upset

**Analysis Hierarchy:**
1. Triage (real incident vs. false positive vs. sensor fault)
2. Scope assessment (which controllers, which processes, which zones)
3. Impact assessment (safety risk? production loss? environmental release?)
4. Attacker attribution (less critical than in IT — focus on *containment first*)

### Phase 3: CONTAINMENT, ERADICATION & RECOVERY

This is the **operational heart** of a CPS IRP. Containment in CPS has three sub-strategies:

- **Short-term containment:** Isolate compromised zone, switch to manual/backup control
- **System-level containment:** Disconnect IT-OT bridge, enable read-only historian mode
- **Long-term containment:** Maintain operations on degraded/legacy systems while remediation occurs

### Phase 4: POST-INCIDENT ACTIVITY (Lessons Learned)

Mandatory after-action review within **72 hours**, including:
- Root cause analysis (5-Whys, Fishbone/Ishikawa)
- Timeline reconstruction
- Forensics preservation and chain-of-custody
- Update of IRP, signatures, baselines
- Regulatory disclosure (CIRCIA, NIS2, sector-specific)

## 2.2 The CPS-IRP Maturity Model (Adapted from CERT/CC)

| Maturity Level | Characteristics |
|---|---|
| **Level 1 — Ad Hoc** | No formal plan, reactive firefighting, IT-only focus |
| **Level 2 — Defined** | Documented plan, basic roles, no CPS-specific testing |
| **Level 3 — Managed** | CPS-tailored playbooks, OT-IT joint drills, tabletop exercises |
| **Level 4 — Quantitatively Controlled** | Metrics-driven (MTTD, MTTR), continuous improvement, automation |
| **Level 5 — Optimizing** | Predictive analytics, threat intelligence integration, autonomous containment |

## 2.3 MITRE ATT&CK for ICS — The CPS Threat Knowledge Base

Unlike IT environments, CPS incidents are mapped against the **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS matrix**, which catalogues adversary tactics specific to industrial environments:

| Tactic | CPS-Specific Technique Example |
|---|---|
| **Initial Access** | Spearphishing targeting control engineers |
| **Execution** | Manipulating PLC function blocks |
| **Persistence** | Modifying firmware on RTUs |
| **Evasion** | Spoofing sensor telemetry to mask attack |
| **Discovery** | Enumerating Modbus/Profibus registers |
| **Impair Process Control** | T0830 (Adversary causes physical damage) |
| **Impact** | Denial of Control, Manipulation of View, Damage to Property |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metric Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are central to evaluating CPS IRP effectiveness. **All formulas are mathematically verifiable.**

**1. Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)**

$$\text{MTTD} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{\text{detect},i} - t_{\text{occur},i})}{N}$$

Where:
- $t_{\text{detect},i}$ = timestamp when incident $i$ was detected
- $t_{\text{occur},i}$ = estimated timestamp when incident $i$ actually occurred
- $N$ = total number of incidents in measurement period

**2. Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)**

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{\text{resolve},i} - t_{\text{detect},i})}{N}$$

Where $t_{\text{resolve},i}$ = timestamp when incident $i$ was fully contained and recovered.

**3. Mean Time to Contain (MTTC)**

$$\text{MTTC} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{\text{contain},i} - t_{\text{detect},i})}{N}$$

**4. Cyber-Physical Impact Index (CPII)**

$$\text{CPII} = \alpha \cdot S + \beta \cdot E + \gamma \cdot F + \delta \cdot R$$

Where:
- $S$ = Safety impact score (0–10)
- $E$ = Environmental impact score (0–10)
- $F$ = Financial impact score (0–10)
- $R$ = Reputational impact score (0–10)
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta$ = organizational weighting coefficients, where $\alpha + \beta + \gamma + \delta = 1$
- $\alpha \geq 0.4$ is recommended to enforce safety priority

**5. Incident Severity Classification (ISA/IEC 62443 Zone-Based)**

$$\text{Severity} = \max(\text{SL}_T, \text{SL}_A, \text{SL}_I, \text{SL}_C) + \text{Context}$$

Where:
- $\text{SL}_T$ = Target Security Level (design intent)
- $\text{SL}_A$ = Achieved Security Level (current state)
- $\text{SL}_I$ = Indication of actual capability
- $\text{SL}_C$ = Capability Security Level of adversary
- Context = physical proximity to Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These metrics and frameworks are used in:
- **Power utilities:** NERC CIP-008-5 mandates IRPs for bulk electric systems
- **Oil & Gas:** API STD 1164 pipeline SCADA security
- **Manufacturing:** IEC 62443-2-4 service provider requirements
- **Healthcare:** FDA premarket cybersecurity guidance for medical devices
- **Transportation:** TSA Pipeline Security Directive (post-Colonial Pipeline)

---

## 2.6 KTU Quick-Reference Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| Concept | Definition / Value |
|---|---|
| **Primary CPS IRP Standard** | NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2 |
| **CPS-Specific IRP Standard** | NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3 (Guide to ICS Security) |
| **ICS Threat Framework** | MITRE ATT&CK for ICS |
| **Industry Standard** | ISA/IEC 62443 (Zones & Conduits) |
| **Number of IR Lifecycle Phases** | **6** (in NIST SP 800-61) |
| **CPS Priority Order** | Safety > Availability > Integrity > Confidentiality |
| **CPS Asset Levels (Purdue)** | **6 levels** (0–5) |
| **ATT&CK for ICS Tactics** | **11** (vs. 14 in Enterprise) |
| **Default Containment Priority** | Isolate IT-OT bridge *before* disconnecting OT zone |
| **Safety Override Rule** | Safety officer authority is *absolute*; cannot be overridden by CISO |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Derivation

## 3.1 Derivation: The Safety-First Containment Decision Tree

A core analytical task in CPS IRP design is determining the **correct containment action** when an incident is detected. The decision must weight safety against security objectives. Below is a complete, step-by-step derivation of the containment priority algorithm.

### Step 1: Define the Incident State Vector

Let the incident state at time $t$ be represented by the vector $\mathbf{I}(t)$:

$$\mathbf{I}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} S(t) \\ C(t) \\ P(t) \\ N(t) \end{bmatrix}$$

Where:
- $S(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ = Safety violation status (1 = active threat to life/environment)
- $C(t) \in \{0, 1, 2\}$ = Cyber compromise level (0 = clean, 1 = suspicious, 2 = confirmed)
- $P(t) \in [0, 10]$ = Process deviation magnitude (0 = nominal, 10 = catastrophic)
- $N(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ = Network reachability to safety-critical assets

### Step 2: Compute the Composite Risk Score

$$\text{Risk}(t) = w_1 \cdot S(t) + w_2 \cdot P(t) + w_3 \cdot C(t) + w_4 \cdot N(t)$$

Recommended weights for CPS environments:
- $w_1 = 0.50$ (safety dominance)
- $w_2 = 0.25$ (process deviation)
- $w_3 = 0.15$ (cyber compromise)
- $w_4 = 0.10$ (network reachability)

**Constraint:**
$$w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1.00$$

### Step 3: Apply the Containment Decision Function

$$\text{Action}(t) = \begin{cases} \text{Emergency Shutdown (ESD)} & \text{if } \text{Risk}(t) \geq 8.0 \\ \text{Isolate IT-OT Bridge} & \text{if } 5.0 \leq \text{Risk}(t) < 8.0 \\ \text{Manual Control Fallback} & \text{if } 3.0 \leq \text{Risk}(t) < 5.0 \\ \text{Monitor \& Log} & \text{if } \text{Risk}(t) < 3.0 \end{cases}$$

### Worked Example

**Scenario:** A refinery detects anomalous Modbus traffic from the engineering workstation to a compressor PLC. Sensor data shows compressor outlet pressure rising.

**Given:**
- $S(t) = 1$ (overpressure risks rupture → safety violation imminent)
- $C(t) = 2$ (confirmed malicious command observed)
- $P(t) = 6$ (pressure at 85% of safety relief setpoint)
- $N(t) = 1$ (workstation can reach SIS)

**Compute:**

$$\text{Risk}(t) = (0.50)(1) + (0.25)(6) + (0.15)(2) + (0.10)(1)$$

$$\text{Risk}(t) = 0.50 + 1.50 + 0.30 + 0.10 = 2.40$$

Wait — this gives 2.40. Re-check by adding the magnitudes: the formula gives a weighted sum. Recalculating with corrected weighting for this specific scenario:

$$\text{Risk}(t) = 0.50 \cdot 1 + 0.25 \cdot 6 + 0.15 \cdot 2 + 0.10 \cdot 1$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Risk}(t) &= 0.50 + 1.50 + 0.30 + 0.10 \\ \text{Risk}(t) &= 2.40 \end{aligned}$$

This is below the 8.0 threshold, but safety override applies: since $S(t) = 1$ and $P(t) > 5$, the **Safety Override Clause** automatically escalates to ESD.

### Step 4: Apply the Safety Override Clause

$$\text{Final Action} = \begin{cases} \text{ESD} & \text{if } S(t) = 1 \text{ OR } \text{Risk}(t) \geq 8.0 \\ \text{Decision Function} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Final Decision:** Emergency Shutdown, regardless of computed Risk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Safety Override Clause embodies the legal and ethical principle that **no cyber-recovery objective can justify physical harm**. This is codified in standards like IEC 61511 (Functional Safety) and is a key differentiator between CPS IRPs and IT IRPs.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: CPS IRP vs IT IRP

| Dimension | IT IRP | CPS IRP |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Data confidentiality, service restoration | Human safety, physical continuity |
| **Decision Authority** | CISO leads | Safety officer has final say |
| **Time Pressure** | Hours to days | Seconds to minutes |
| **Containment Tools** | Network isolation, reimaging | Manual valve shutdown, SIS activation |
| **Forensics Priority** | High (legal evidence) | Low during incident; deferred |
| **Recovery Method** | Restore from backup | Return to safe state, then restore |
| **Failure Mode** | Data loss | Physical destruction, casualties |
| **Testing** | Tabletop, red team | Live drills with *safety observers* |
| **Documentation** | Cyber incident ticket | Process safety + cyber incident report |
| **Stakeholders** | IT, Legal, PR | Operations, Engineering, Safety, IT, Legal, PR, Regulators |

## 3.3 The CPS IRP Document Structure (Mandatory Sections)

A KTU-compliant CPS IRP document should contain **12 mandatory sections**:

1. **Plan Identification & Version Control**
2. **Scope, Objectives, and Assumptions**
3. **Stakeholders & Roles (RACI Matrix)**
4. **Asset Inventory (Purdue Model Mapped)**
5. **Severity Classification Matrix**
6. **Communication Plan & Escalation Tree**
7. **Containment Playbooks (per asset class)**
8. **Eradication Procedures**
9. **Recovery Procedures (Safe-State First)**
10. **Forensics & Chain of Custody**
11. **Post-Incident Review Template**
12. **Compliance & Regulatory Mapping (NERC CIP, NIS2, etc.)**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CPS Incident Response Lifecycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    prepA[Preparation] --> detA[Detection and Analysis]
    detA --> contA[Containment]
    contA --> eraA[Eradication]
    eraA --> recA[Recovery]
    recA --> postA[Post Incident Activity]
    postA -.Lessons Learned Update.-> prepA

    prepA:::prepNode
    detA:::detNode
    contA:::contNode
    eraA:::eraNode
    recA:::recNode
    postA:::postNode

    classDef prepNode fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef detNode fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef contNode fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef eraNode fill:#d62728,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef recNode fill:#9467bd,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef postNode fill:#8c564b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 CPS Containment Decision Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Incident Detected] --> assessA{Check Safety Violation S equals 1}
    assessA -- YES --> esdA[EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN]
    assessA -- NO --> riskA{Compute Risk Score}
    riskA -- Greater Equal 8.0 --> esdA
    riskA -- Between 5.0 and 8.0 --> isoA[Isolate IT OT Bridge]
    riskA -- Between 3.0 and 5.0 --> manA[Manual Control Fallback]
    riskA -- Below 3.0 --> monA[Monitor and Log]
    esdA --> safeA[Transition to Safe State]
    isoA --> safeA
    manA --> safeA
    monA --> safeA
    safeA --> forenA[Deferred Forensics]

    esdA:::critNode
    isoA:::warnNode
    manA:::safeNode
    monA:::okNode
    safeA:::recovNode

    classDef critNode fill:#b22222,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef warnNode fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef safeNode fill:#daa520,stroke:#fff,color:#000
    classDef okNode fill:#228b22,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef recovNode fill:#4169e1,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 CPS Incident Response RACI Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cisoA[CISO] -->|Reports To| irtA[IR Team Lead]
    otlA[OT Lead] -->|Reports To| irtA
    safA[Safety Officer] -->|VETO POWER| irtA
    legA[Legal] -->|Advises| irtA
    praA[Public Relations] -->|Advises| irtA
    engA[Control Engineer] -->|Executes| irtA
    irtA --> planA[Activate IRP Playbook]
    planA --> exeA[Execute Containment]
    exeA --> commA[Notify Regulators]

    safA:::vetoNode
    irtA:::leadNode
    exeA:::execNode

    classDef vetoNode fill:#dc143c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef leadNode fill:#1e90ff,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef execNode fill:#3cb371,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Purdue Model & CPS IRP Zone Mapping (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    lvl5A[Level 5: Enterprise Network] --> lvl4A
    lvl4A[Level 4: Site Business Planning] --> lvl3A
    lvl3A[Level 3: Site Operations DMZ] --> lvl2A
    lvl2A[Level 2: Supervisory Control SCADA HMI] --> lvl1A
    lvl1A[Level 1: Basic Control PLC DCS] --> lvl0A
    lvl0A[Level 0: Physical Process Sensors Actuators]

    lvl5A:::entNode
    lvl4A:::entNode
    lvl3A:::dmzNode
    lvl2A:::scadaNode
    lvl1A:::ctrlNode
    lvl0A:::physNode

    classDef entNode fill:#6495ed,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef dmzNode fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef scadaNode fill:#ff6347,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef ctrlNode fill:#dc143c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef physNode fill:#2f4f4f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*Explain why a traditional IT Incident Response Plan cannot be directly applied to a Cyber-Physical System. Provide two specific reasons with examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A traditional IT Incident Response Plan (IRP) cannot be directly applied to a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) due to the following reasons:

**Reason 1: Safety vs Confidentiality Priority Mismatch (1½ marks)**
In IT environments, the primary objective is preserving data confidentiality and integrity. In CPS, the primary objective is **human safety and physical process continuity**. Example: An IT IRP would prioritize isolating a compromised server; in a CPS, isolating the controller of a chemical reactor could cause an uncontrolled pressure buildup and physical harm.

**Reason 2: Time-Criticality and Availability Requirements (1½ marks)**
IT systems can tolerate hours of downtime during incident response, but CPS processes (e.g., power grids, water treatment, medical devices) must operate continuously or transition safely to a shutdown state. Example: Wiping and reimaging a PLC — a routine IT action — could destroy proprietary ladder logic and cause a multi-day plant outage.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*List the six phases of the NIST Incident Response Lifecycle as adapted for CPS, and state the most critical phase for CPS environments.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six phases of the NIST SP 800-61 Incident Response Lifecycle adapted for CPS are:

1. **Preparation** (1/2 mark)
2. **Detection and Analysis** (1/2 mark)
3. **Containment** (1/2 mark)
4. **Eradication** (1/2 mark)
5. **Recovery** (1/2 mark)
6. **Post-Incident Activity** (1/2 mark)

The most critical phase for CPS environments is **Preparation** (½ mark), because pre-incident planning — including baseline process behavior, asset inventory, backup of PLC logic, and pre-authorized safety actions — determines whether the response can be executed within the tight safety time windows of physical processes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]`**

**(a)** Design a CPS-specific Incident Response Plan for a **smart water treatment plant** consisting of: (i) Level 0 sensors (pH, turbidity, flow meters), (ii) Level 1 PLCs controlling chemical dosing pumps, (iii) Level 2 SCADA HMI, and (iv) Level 3 historian server connected to corporate network. Identify the IR team roles and their authority hierarchy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During normal operation, the SCADA HMI suddenly displays an erroneous pH reading of 14 (maximum scale), while the physical pH sensor at Level 0 reads a normal value of 7.2. Simultaneously, the engineering workstation shows suspicious outbound network traffic. Apply the Safety-First Containment Decision Framework to determine the correct response action, justifying each step. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Asset Inventory & Zone Mapping (2 marks):**
- Zone A (Safety-Critical): Level 1 PLCs controlling chlorine dosing — chlorination overdose is a public health hazard
- Zone B (Operational): Level 2 SCADA HMI and Level 3 Historian
- Zone C (Corporate): Level 4–5 enterprise network

**IR Team Roles & Authority Hierarchy (5 marks):**

| Role | Authority Level | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| **Plant Safety Officer** | **Absolute Veto** | Final say on any action affecting human/public safety; can override CISO |
| **Plant Operations Manager** | **Operational Command** | Authorizes manual control fallback and safe shutdown |
| **OT/ICS Lead Engineer** | **Technical Command** | Executes playbook, isolates zones, restores from backup |
| **CISO** | **Advisory** | Coordinates with IT, manages cyber forensics, regulatory reporting |
| **Control Engineer** | **Execution** | Implements manual setpoints, calibrates sensors |
| **Legal Counsel** | **Advisory** | Manages regulatory disclosure (EPA, state health dept.) |
| **Public Relations** | **Advisory** | Manages public communication if water quality affected |

**Key Principle (½ mark):** The Safety Officer's authority supersedes the CISO's authority in all matters where physical harm is plausible.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Identify the Incident State Vector (2 marks)**
- $S(t) = 1$ — Display of pH=14 could trigger automatic emergency response, possibly initiating unsafe chemical injection
- $C(t) = 2$ — Suspicious outbound traffic confirms cyber compromise
- $P(t) = 1$ — Physical sensor reads 7.2 (normal), so no actual process deviation
- $N(t) = 1$ — Compromised workstation is in engineering network, which can reach Level 2 SCADA via management network

**Step 2: Compute Risk Score (2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Risk}(t) &= w_1 \cdot S(t) + w_2 \cdot P(t) + w_3 \cdot C(t) + w_4 \cdot N(t) \\ &= (0.50)(1) + (0.25)(1) + (0.15)(2) + (0.10)(1) \\ &= 0.50 + 0.25 + 0.30 + 0.10 \\ &= 1.15 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Apply Decision Function (1 mark)**
Computed Risk = 1.15 < 3.0, which would normally trigger "Monitor & Log." However:

**Step 4: Apply Safety Override Clause (2 marks)**
Since $S(t) = 1$, the Safety Override Clause mandates **Emergency Response**.

**Final Correct Action:** 
1. **Immediate:** Manually verify pH at independent sensor location (sanity check)
2. **Containment:** Isolate IT-OT bridge at Level 3 DMZ; block outbound traffic
3. **Safe State:** Switch chlorination control to **manual mode** with operator-set fixed dosing; engage Safety Instrumented System (SIS) to prevent over-dosing
4. **Forensics:** Preserve SCADA logs, HMI screenshots, network captures (deferred)
5. **Recovery:** Restore SCADA display from golden image after root-cause analysis

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]`**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** framework with the **MITRE ATT&CK for Enterprise** framework. Highlight three CPS-specific tactics present only in the ICS matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A power utility substation experiences a cyber-physical attack in which the attacker manipulates sensor readings to mask an unauthorized breaker operation. Develop the **post-incident activity** (lessons learned) workflow, including forensic considerations unique to OT environments. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Dimension | ATT&CK Enterprise | ATT&CK for ICS |
|---|---|---|
| **Target Environment** | IT systems, cloud, mobile | Industrial Control Systems (ICS) |
| **Tactics Count** | 14 | **11** |
| **Focus** | Data theft, espionage, ransomware | Physical process manipulation, equipment damage |
| **Techniques** | ~200 techniques | ~80 techniques |
| **Impact** | Mostly virtual | **Physical** (equipment damage, casualties) |
| **Time-Critical** | Days | Seconds to minutes |

**Three CPS-Specific Tactics Present ONLY in ATT&CK for ICS (3 marks):**

1. **Impair Process Control (TA0106)** — Adversary actively manipulates control setpoints to drive the physical process into unsafe states. *Example:* Changing the setpoint on a pressure relief valve.

2. **Damage to Property (TA0107)** — Adversary causes deliberate physical destruction of equipment or infrastructure. *Example:* Stuxnet-style centrifuge over-spinning.

3. **Manipulation of View (TA0104)** — Adversary hides physical events by spoofing the operator's view (HMI display shows normal values while physical process is being damaged). *Example:* HMI shows normal pH while acid tank is actually overflowing.

*(Other valid ICS-only tactics include "Denial of Control" and "Theft of Operational Information.")*

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Post-Incident Activity Workflow for CPS:**

**Step 1: Immediate After-Action Review within 72 hours (1 mark)**
- Convene IR team: Safety officer, OT lead, CISO, Legal, Engineering
- Goal: *Operational learning*, not blame assignment

**Step 2: Root Cause Analysis using 5-Whys (2 marks)**
Example:
- *Why did the breaker operate unauthorized?* — Attacker sent valid DNP3 command.
- *Why was the command accepted as valid?* — Authentication keys were not rotated.
- *Why were keys not rotated?* — Rotation procedure was not in IRP playbook.
- *Why was procedure missing?* — IRP did not include DNP3-specific guidance.
- *Why did IRP omit DNP3?* — No coordination between IT security and OT engineering.

**Step 3: OT-Specific Forensics (2 marks)**
- **Volatile data first:** Capture running PLC memory, RTU registers, HMI session state
- **Non-disruptive imaging:** Use out-of-band forensic tools; **never** power-cycle ICS equipment
- **Process log correlation:** Align cyber logs with process historian data (sub-second timestamps)
- **Chain of custody:** Use write-blockers; preserve physical evidence (compromised HMI, network taps)
- **Specialized tools:** Wireshark with ICS dissectors (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104), Dragos Platform, Claroty

**Step 4: Update IRP, Baselines, and Playbooks (1 mark)**
- Add DNP3 authentication rotation to playbook
- Update MITRE ATT&CK for ICS mapping
- Conduct tabletop exercise within 30 days

**Step 5: Regulatory & External Reporting (1 mark)**
- NERC CIP-008-5 reporting (if North American bulk electric system)
- Sector-specific regulators (FERC, CISA, TSA Pipeline if applicable)
- Information sharing via ISAC (E-ISAC for energy sector)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in CPS IRP Questions:**
>
> 1. **Citing only IT frameworks (–2 marks):** Stating "follow NIST SP 800-61" without adapting to CPS. Always mention NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3, IEC 62443, or MITRE ATT&CK for ICS.
>
> 2. **Ignoring the Safety Override (–3 marks):** Designing an IRP where the CISO has final authority. Examiners explicitly check for the Safety Officer's veto power.
>
> 3. **Recommending destructive containment (–2 marks):** Suggesting "power off," "wipe and reimage PLC," or "factory reset" without safety justification.
>
> 4. **Missing Purdue Model mapping (–1 mark):** Failing to identify which Purdue Level is affected, which invalidates the containment logic.
>
> 5. **Conflating IT forensics with OT forensics (–2 marks):** Suggesting live disk imaging of a running PLC. Always specify *non-disruptive* capture.
>
> 6. **Omitting regulatory reporting (–1 mark):** CPS incidents are subject to mandatory disclosure (NERC CIP-008, NIS2, CIRCIA); failing to mention this loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — CPS Incident Response Plans:**

- ✅ A CPS IRP is built on **NIST SP 800-61** (general) + **NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3** (CPS-specific)
- ✅ The **six lifecycle phases** are: Preparation → Detection → Containment → Eradication → Recovery → Post-Incident Activity
- ✅ **Preparation is the most critical phase** in CPS due to time-criticality
- ✅ **Priority order in CPS:** Safety > Availability > Integrity > Confidentiality
- ✅ The **Safety Officer has veto power** over all containment decisions
- ✅ Traditional IT actions (power off, wipe, isolate) can be **catastrophically dangerous** in CPS
- ✅ Containment strategies: short-term (manual fallback), system-level (zone isolation), long-term (legacy degraded operation)
- ✅ **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** has 11 tactics (vs. 14 in Enterprise), with CPS-specific tactics like *Impair Process Control*, *Damage to Property*, *Manipulation of View*
- ✅ **Purdue Model Levels 0–5** must be mapped in the asset inventory section
- ✅ **OT forensics must be non-disruptive:** never power-cycle ICS equipment during evidence capture
- ✅ Mandatory IRP document sections: 12 minimum (scope, roles, asset inventory, severity matrix, communication plan, playbooks, etc.)
- ✅ **Key metrics:** MTTD, MTTR, MTTC, and the CPS-specific CPII (Cyber-Physical Impact Index)
- ✅ **Safety Override Clause:** If $S(t) = 1$, trigger Emergency Shutdown regardless of computed risk score
- ✅ **Regulatory frameworks:** NERC CIP-008-5 (energy), IEC 62443 (industrial), NIS2 (EU), CIRCIA (US reporting)
- ✅ **Forensic timeline alignment:** Sub-second correlation between cyber logs and process historian data is essential
- ✅ **Recovery principle:** Always return to a **safe state first**, then restore normal operation — never the reverse
- ✅ **Testing requirement:** CPS IRPs must be tested with **live drills under safety officer supervision**, not just tabletop exercises
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incident Handling and Escalation Procedures in CPS Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Incident Handling and Escalation Procedures in CPS Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Incident Handling (IH)** in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) refers to the **systematic, pre-defined set of procedural and technical actions** taken by an organization to **prepare for, detect, analyze, contain, eradicate, recover from, and post-process** security events that compromise the **confidentiality, integrity, or availability (CIA triad)** of the integrated computational and physical process control layers.

**Escalation Procedures** are the **structured, role-based protocols** that dictate **when, how, to whom, and under what authority** an incident must be elevated through organizational tiers (Tier 1 SOC Analyst → Tier 2 Incident Responder → CISO → Executive/Legal/Regulatory bodies) as severity, scope, or impact increases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In CPS, incident handling diverges from traditional IT incident response because **safety-of-life**, **environmental damage**, and **physical process disruption** are first-class concerns — often **outweighing data confidentiality**. The reference framework is **NIST SP 800-61 r2**, adapted by **ISA/IEC 62443-2-4** and **ISO/IEC 27035-2** for Operational Technology (OT) contexts.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Think of a **hospital Emergency Department (ED)**:

| Hospital ED Concept | CPS Incident Handling Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Triage Nurse** at reception | **Tier-1 SOC Analyst** performing initial log review and severity classification |
| **Emergency Physician** taking over critical cases | **Tier-2 Incident Responder / OT Engineer** containing the breach on the plant floor |
| **Calling in a Specialist (Cardiologist)** | **Escalation to Subject Matter Expert (e.g., SCADA specialist, PLC vendor)** |
| **Hospital Administration / Legal** | **CISO, Executive Leadership, Legal, Regulatory (CERT-In, NCIIPC)** |
| **Patient's family being informed** | **Internal & External Stakeholder Communication Plan** |
| **Post-treatment review (Morbidity & Mortality meeting)** | **Post-Incident Activity (Lessons Learned, Root Cause Analysis)** |
| **Defibrillator crash cart** | **Pre-deployed Incident Response Playbooks & Isolated Backups** |

Just as a hospital **cannot afford to "shut down and reboot" a patient mid-heart-attack**, a CPS responder **cannot simply disconnect a refinery column or a power grid substation** without risking physical damage, environmental release, or cascading failure. This is the fundamental shift in incident philosophy.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Glossary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Event:** Any observable occurrence in a system/network. (e.g., a single failed login — *not necessarily an incident*)
> - **Alert:** A notification that a specific event or series of events has triggered a rule. (e.g., SIEM alarm)
> - **Incident:** A confirmed violation or imminent threat of violation of security policies, acceptable use policies, or standard security practices that **adversely affects CPS operations or safety**.
> - **PSIRT (Product Security Incident Response Team):** Vendor-side team handling vulnerabilities in CPS products (PLCs, RTUs, HMI software).
> - **CSIRT (Computer Security Incident Response Team):** Customer/operator-side team handling incidents in deployed environments.
> - **OT-SOC (Operational Technology – Security Operations Center):** Specialized SOC for industrial control systems.
