KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** > *Define "Responsible Disclosure Policy" in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. List **two** CPS-specific reasons why the standard 90-day IT embargo period is often extended.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — Examiner-Key Distribution):** A **Responsible Disclosure Policy (RDP)** is a formally documented, time-bound, and ethically governed process by which a security researcher privately reports a discovered vulnerability to the affected vendor, granting a negotiated remediation window before any public disclosure (1 mark). In CPS, the standard 90-day embargo is often extended because: 1. **CPS devices have long certification cycles** (IEC 62443, FDA pre-market approval) and **15–25 year operational lifespans**, so patches require regulatory and field-validation time (1 mark). 2. **Patch deployment in CPS requires planned physical downtime** (substation outages, clinical-window scheduling, automotive OTA bundles), which cannot always be arranged within 90 days (1 mark). --- ### Question A2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** > *Differentiate between **Coordinated Disclosure**, **Full Disclosure**, and **Silent Patching**. State which one is mandated by ISO/IEC 29147.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Doctrine | Disclosure Behaviour | ISO Alignment | |----------|----------------------|---------------| | **Full Disclosure** | Technical details published immediately upon verification (1 mark) | Not ISO-aligned | | **Silent Patching** | Vendor fixes internally; public is never informed (1 mark) | Not ISO-aligned | | **Coordinated Disclosure** | Private report → joint patch → simultaneous public release (1 mark) | **Mandated by ISO/IEC 29147 + 30111** | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Rule:** Answer **one** of the two alternatives. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. --- ### Question B-A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 4 Choice]** > *(a)* Explain the **six-phase CPS vulnerability lifecycle** under a Responsible Disclosure Policy. For each phase, name one measurable KPI. **(7 Marks)** > *(b)* A water-treatment SCADA system has a vulnerability with CVSS v3.1 vector `AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H`. The CPS site is rated **SL-4** per IEC 62443 and the threat-actor capability is $T = 0.9$. Using $L = 365 \cdot e^{-0.6 R_d}$ with $L_{\max}=365$ and $\lambda=0.6$, compute the recommended embargo length $L$. Show every step. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) The **six-phase lifecycle** of a CPS vulnerability under RDP (1 mark per phase + KPI): 1. **Discovery** — Researcher identifies the flaw. *KPI:* Time-to-Detect (TTD). 2. **Reporting** — Researcher submits structured report to PSIRT via `security.txt`. *KPI:* Report completeness (%). 3. **Acknowledgement** — Vendor confirms receipt and opens a ticket. *KPI:* Time-to-Acknowledge (TTA, target ≤ 3 days). 4. **Triage & Scoring** — Vulnerability is reproduced in a sandbox and scored with **CVSS v3.1** + **SSVC** decision tree. *KPI:* CVSS Base / Environmental score. 5. **Remediation** — Patch developed, digitally signed, validated in a digital-twin replica, and field-tested. *KPI:* Mean Time to Remediate (MTTR). 6. **Coordinated Public Release** — Joint advisory (vendor + researcher + CERT) is published; SBOM is updated; mitigation workarounds are documented. *KPI:* Embargo compliance (computed $L$ vs actual release date). The **why** behind the lifecycle: it ensures that the **confidentiality** of the flaw is preserved (no adversary weaponization), the **integrity** of the patch is verified (digital-twin test), and the **availability** of the CPS plant is maintained (scheduled downtime). This triad aligns RDP with the **CIA-plus-safety** model required by IEC 62443-4-2. #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Identify the CVSS metrics (1 mark):** The vector is the canonical "worst-case" CPS vector. From the standard CVSS v3.1 table: - $AV=0.85,\; AC=0.77,\; PR=0.85,\; UI=0.85$ - $C=I=A=0.56,\; S:U$ (multiplier 1.0) **Step 2 — Compute Exploitability sub-score (1 mark):** $$ E = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \approx 3.887 $$ Normalize: $V = 3.887 / 10 = 0.3887$. **Step 3 — Compute Impact sub-score (1 mark):** $$ I_{\text{raw}} = 1 - (0.44)^3 = 1 - 0.0852 = 0.9148 $$ **Step 4 — Apply SL-4 CPS safety uplift (1 mark):** For SL-4, the safety multiplier is $\omega = 1.50$ (highest tier, life-safety critical). $$ I_{\text{CPS}} = \min(1.0,\; 0.9148 \times 1.50) = \min(1.0,\; 1.372) = 1.0 $$ **Step 5 — Compute $R_d$ (1 mark):** $$ R_d = T \times V \times I = 0.9 \times 0.3887 \times 1.0 = 0.3498 $$ **Step 6 — Compute $L$ (2 marks):** $$ L = 365 \times e^{-0.6 \times 0.3498} = 365 \times e^{-0.2099} $$ $$ e^{-0.2099} \approx 0.8105 \quad\Rightarrow\quad L = 365 \times 0.8105 \approx 295.8 \text{ days} $$ > **Final Answer:** $L \approx \mathbf{296\ \text{days}}$ (~10 months). At an SL-4 site with a capable adversary, the embargo is *longer* than the IT default (90 days) but *shorter* than the 365-day maximum, because the threat-actor capability is high. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** > The most common mistake is **forgetting the SL-4 safety uplift**. A student who writes $I = 0.9148$ (without the $\omega=1.5$ multiplier) will get $L \approx 324$ days — wrong by **~30 days** and loses 1 mark. The second most common mistake is **forgetting to cap $I$ at 1.0** after the uplift; both errors must be avoided. --- ### Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 4 Choice]** > *(a)* With neat block diagrams, explain the **stakeholder map** of a CPS Responsible Disclosure Policy. For each stakeholder, state **one** legal shield or regulatory mandate. **(7 Marks)** > *(b)* Critically compare **Coordinated Disclosure** and **Full Disclosure** for a CPS that controls a **medical insulin pump** delivering to a patient. Recommend the appropriate doctrine and justify with **three** safety / legal reasons. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) The CPS-RDP stakeholder map comprises **eight** interacting parties (1 mark for the diagram, 1 mark per stakeholder × 6 stakeholders with shield/mandate, 1 mark for synthesis). A neat block diagram is essential. **Block diagram (textual representation acceptable in KTU):** $$ \text{Researcher} \rightarrow \text{Vendor PSIRT} \leftrightarrow \text{MITRE CNA} $$ $$ \text{Vendor PSIRT} \rightarrow \text{CERT} \rightarrow \text{Public} $$ $$ \text{Vendor PSIRT} \rightarrow \text{Asset Owner / Operator} \rightarrow \text{CPS Plant} $$ | Stakeholder | Legal Shield / Mandate | |-------------|-------------------------| | **Researcher** | DMCA §1201 exemption (US); CISA Binding Operational Directive 20-01 (1 mark) | | **Vendor PSIRT** | Vendor's published Safe-Harbour Policy; NDA with researcher (1 mark) | | **MITRE CNA** | MITRE CVE Numbering Authority contract; ISO/IEC 29147 conformance (1 mark) | | **National CERT** | Statutory mandate under IT Act §70B (India) / NIS2 Art. 12 (EU) (1 mark) | | **Asset Owner / Operator** | NERC CIP-007-6 R2 (US grid); NIS2 Directive (EU) (1 mark) | | **Regulator (FDA)** | FDA Pre-Market Cybersecurity Guidance 2023 (1 mark) | | **Public / Patients** | Right-to-be-informed under Consumer Protection Act / GDPR (1 mark) | #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Dimension | Full Disclosure | Coordinated Disclosure | |-----------|----------------|------------------------| | Time to Public Awareness | Immediate | After patch + embargo $L$ | | Patient Safety Risk (insulin pump) | **High** — attacker weaponizes before patch | **Low** — patch deployed first | | Legal Compliance (FDA) | **Non-compliant** | **Compliant** | | Patch Readiness | Zero | Vendor-validated | **Recommended doctrine: Coordinated Disclosure (1 mark).** **Three Justifications (1 mark each):** 1. **Safety:** An insulin-pump flaw can cause a **fatal overdose**. Full disclosure gives adversaries (or curious attackers) the recipe to harm patients before any patch is installed. Coordinated disclosure ensures the patch is deployed *first*. 2. **Regulatory:** The **FDA Pre-Market Cybersecurity Guidance (2023)** explicitly requires medical-device OEMs to maintain a coordinated disclosure process and an SBOM. Full disclosure violates 21 CFR Part 820 design-controls. 3. **Legal Safe-Harbour:** Coordinated disclosure operates under a **safe-harbour policy** that protects the researcher from prosecution; full disclosure exposes the researcher to CFAA / IT Act §66F liability with no patient-safety benefit. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** > Students often write *"Full disclosure is better because it's transparent."* This is **wrong for CPS** and will cost 2 marks. The examiner's key explicitly downgrades "generic IT arguments" when the question specifies a medical CPS. Always tie your answer to **physical safety**, **regulatory compliance**, and **patient protection**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4 / CPS Responsible Disclosure** - **RDP definition** — A documented, time-bound, ethical vulnerability-reporting process that grants a vendor a confidential remediation window before public release. - **Three doctrines** — Full / Silent / **Coordinated** (only the third is ISO/IEC 29147-compliant). - **Embargo default** — **90 days** (IT) but **180–365 days** (CPS) due to certification + downtime. - **Key standards** — ISO/IEC **29147** (receiving), ISO/IEC **30111** (handling), **IEC 62443-4-2** (industrial), **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5** (federal). - **CPS-specific files** — FDA Pre-Market 2023, NERC CIP-007-6 R2, NIS2, EO 14028 (SBOM). - **Core equation** — $R_d = T \times V \times I$ and $L = L_{\max} \cdot e^{-\lambda R_d}$. - **CVSS v3.1** — Base score 0.0 to 10.0; the worst-case CPS vector is `AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H` (Base 4.9 Medium, uplifted by IEC 62443 SL). - **SSVC** — CPS-aware triage: *Exploitation → Technical Impact → Automatable → Mission Impact → Decision* (Track / Act / Attend / Defer). - **SBOM** — Mandatory under EO 14028 and FDA 2023; lists affected software components. - **Stakeholders (8)** — Researcher, Vendor PSIRT, MITRE CNA, National CERT, Asset Owner, Regulator, Public, SBOM Publisher. - **Legal shields** — DMCA §1201 (researcher), Safe-Harbour Policy (vendor), IT Act §70B (CERT), NIS2 (operator). - **Lifecycle (6 phases)** — Discovery → Reporting → Acknowledgement → Triage → Remediation → Coordinated Public Release. - **KPIs** — TTD, TTA (≤ 3 days), CVSS, MTTR, Embargo compliance. - **Safety triad** — Confidentiality (of flaw) + Integrity (of patch) + Availability (of CPS plant). - **Mnemonic** — "3 Sides of RDP": Researcher / Vendor / Public. - **Exam mantra** — Always tie CPS-disclosure answers to **physical safety**, **regulatory compliance**, and **embargo calculations**.

topics.title: Integrating Security into the Development Lifecycle of CPS Projects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Integrating Security into the Development Lifecycle of CPS Projects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Security Integration into the CPS Development Lifecycle** is the systematic, end-to-end engineering discipline of embedding confidentiality, integrity, availability, authentication, authorization, non-repudiation, and resilience safeguards into every phase of a Cyber-Physical System's lifecycle — from initial concept, requirements, design, implementation, integration, deployment, operation, and decommissioning. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, this corresponds to the implementation of a **Secure Software Development Lifecycle (Secure SDLC / S-SDLC)** tailored for cyber-physical environments, often instantiated through the **DevSecOps** paradigm and governed by domain standards such as **IEC 62443** (industrial automation), **ISO/SAE 21434** (automotive), and **NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 1** (systems security engineering).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For a CPS, security is not an "add-on" feature. Because physical processes can be harmed (machinery, human life, environment), security must be **co-engineered** with safety and functionality from day-zero (a "shift-left" approach).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of building a house for a family living in a flood-prone area:

- **Traditional Approach (Security as afterthought):** Build the entire house, then ask a security company to "add a flood wall" at the end. The result is brittle — the foundation is weak, the doors still leak, the windows shatter.
- **Secure Development Lifecycle:** Before the architect draws a single line, the *geologist*, *structural engineer*, and *flood-security consultant* all sit together. They pick an elevated plot (architecture), reinforce the foundation against scouring (design), use waterproof concrete (secure coding), pressure-test every pipe (security testing), and install continuous water-level sensors that automatically close floodgates (continuous monitoring).

A CPS is the same — the "house" interacts with a physical world that can be attacked (flooded). Security must be poured into the foundation.

## 1.3 Core Tenets of the Integrated Lifecycle

| Tenet | Description | KTU CO Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **Security by Design** | Threats considered at architectural stage | CO4 |
| **Privacy by Design** | Data minimization, consent baked-in | CO4 |
| **Defense in Depth** | Layered controls (network + host + app + physical) | CO4, CO5 |
| **Fail-Safe / Fail-Secure** | Compromised component fails to a safe state | CO4, CO5 |
| **Continuous Monitoring** | Runtime anomaly detection (IDS, SOC) | CO5 |
| **Provenance & SBOM** | Track every software/hardware component origin | CO4 |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. Therefore, every lifecycle phase must be mapped to a Course Outcome (CO) — typically **CO4: Design secure CPS architectures** and **CO5: Evaluate security mechanisms in operational CPS**.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Benchmarks

Key performance metrics used to evaluate lifecycle security integration include:

- **MTTD (Mean Time To Detect)** — *target: minutes, not days* for OT networks.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Respond / Remediate)** — *target: < 1 hour* for safety-critical patches.
- **Vulnerability Density** — vulnerabilities per **KLOC (thousand lines of code)**.
- **Patch Latency** — time from CVE disclosure to deployed patch.
- **SBOM Coverage** — **% of components with verified Software Bill of Materials**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Industry benchmark: A mature DevSecOps pipeline achieves **< 5%** of production deployments being vulnerable to *known* critical CVEs — compared to **> 60%** in non-integrated pipelines (Gartner, 2023).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phases of the Secure CPS Development Lifecycle

The S-SDLC for CPS consists of **seven (7) iterative, non-linear phases**. Each phase has explicit security "gates" — artifacts that must be approved before proceeding.

### Phase 1 — Concept & Feasibility
- Identify physical assets, safety cases, regulatory regime (e.g., FDA for medical, IEC 61508 for industrial).
- Preliminary **Threat Surface Sketch** (not yet a full TARA).
- *Output:* Security Vision Document, Feasibility Risk Register.

### Phase 2 — Security Requirements Engineering
- Derive **Security Functional Requirements (SFRs)** and **Security Assurance Requirements (SARs)**.
- Apply **STRIDE-per-Element** decomposition.
- Map requirements to standards: **IEC 62443-3-3 SL levels 1–4**, **ISO/SAE 21434 CSL (Cybersecurity Level) 1–4**.
- *Output:* SRS-Security Annex, Traceability Matrix.

### Phase 3 — Architectural & Detailed Design
- Apply **Security Design Patterns**: defense-in-depth, least-privilege, zero-trust zones, secure enclaves (TPM, HSM), network segmentation.
- Conduct **TARA (Threat Analysis & Risk Assessment)** per ISO/SAE 21434.
- *Output:* Security Architecture Diagram, TARA report, Cryptographic Key Management Plan.

### Phase 4 — Secure Implementation (Coding)
- Apply **Secure Coding Standards**: **CERT C/C++**, **MISRA-C**, **CWE Top 25** avoidance.
- Use vetted cryptographic libraries (e.g., **libsodium, OpenSSL FIPS-validated, wolfSSL**).
- Hardware-rooted trust: secure boot, measured boot, TPM sealing.
- *Output:* Source code + Static Analysis Report (SAST).

### Phase 5 — Security Testing & Verification
- **SAST (Static Application Security Testing)** — scans source for known patterns.
- **DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing)** — black-box fuzzing of running system.
- **IAST (Interactive)** — instrumentation-based.
- **SCA (Software Composition Analysis)** — checks third-party components (SBOM).
- **Penetration Testing** of the OT/IT boundary.
- **Fault Injection & Side-Channel Testing** for hardware.
- *Output:* Penetration Test Report, Vulnerability Closure Log.

### Phase 6 — Secure Deployment & Commissioning
- **Hardening** of PLCs, RTUs, edge gateways.
- Network segmentation (Purdue Model Levels 0–5).
- **Certificate provisioning** (PKI, mutual TLS between field devices).
- *Output:* Commissioning Security Checklist, As-Built Architecture.

### Phase 7 — Operations, Maintenance & Decommissioning
- **Continuous Monitoring**: SIEM, ICS-IDS (e.g., Dragos, Claroty, Nozomi).
- **Patch Management** (with safety-impact analysis first).
- **Incident Response** per NIST SP 800-61.
- **Secure Decommissioning**: cryptographic erase, physical destruction of media.
- *Output:* SOC Reports, Patch Log, End-of-Life Disposition Record.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Lifecycle — Justification Logic

- **Cost of late fixes:** A defect found in design costs ~**$100**; the same defect in production costs **>$10,000** (IBM Systems Sciences Institute).
- **Safety-Security Coupling in CPS:** In IT systems, a breach is *financial*. In CPS, a breach can be *physical* (explosion, blackout, death). Hence **security is a non-functional safety prerequisite**.
- **Regulatory compulsion:** EU **NIS2 Directive**, **Cyber Resilience Act (CRA)**, India's **NCIIPC** directives, US **Executive Order 14028** all mandate lifecycle security.

## 2.3 DevSecOps for CPS — The CI/CD Security Pipeline

DevSecOps injects security **automated gates** into Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment:

$$\text{DevSecOps Pipeline} = \text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Code} \rightarrow \text{Build} \rightarrow \text{Test} \rightarrow \text{Release} \rightarrow \text{Deploy} \rightarrow \text{Operate} \rightarrow \text{Monitor}$$

Each arrow is a **Security Gate**:

| Stage | Security Gate | Tool Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Plan | Threat model auto-generated from design | IriusRisk, ThreatModeler |
| Code | Pre-commit hooks (secrets, lint) | git-secrets, pre-commit |
| Build | SAST, SCA, SBOM generation | SonarQube, Snyk, Trivy |
| Test | DAST, Fuzzing, IAST | OWASP ZAP, AFL, Burp |
| Release | Signed artifacts, attestation | Sigstore, in-toto, SLSA |
| Deploy | Policy-as-Code, Gatekeeper | OPA, Kyverno, Conftest |
| Operate | Runtime IDS, baselining | Falco, Dragos, Wazuh |
| Monitor | SIEM, SOAR playbooks | Splunk, Elastic, Cortex XSOAR |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Equation / Boundary | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $n_{vuln}$ | Vulnerability density | $n_{vuln} = \dfrac{N_{v}}{KLOC}$ | vulns / KLOC |
| $CVSS$ | Common Vulnerability Scoring System | $CVSS \in [0, 10]$ | dimensionless |
| $R$ | Risk (per NIST SP 800-30) | $R = f(T, V, L, I)$ where $T$ = threat, $V$ = vulnerability, $L$ = likelihood, $I$ = impact | qualitative/categorical |
| $L$ | Annualized Loss Expectancy | $L = ARO \times SLE$ | currency |
| $ARO$ | Annualized Rate of Occurrence | $ARO \in \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ | events / year |
| $SLE$ | Single Loss Expectancy | $SLE = AV \times EF$ | currency |
| $AV$ | Asset Value | $AV > 0$ | currency |
| $EF$ | Exposure Factor | $EF \in [0, 1]$ | dimensionless |
| $ALE$ | Annual Loss Expectancy | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | currency / year |
| $ROS$ | Return on Security Investment | $ROS = \dfrac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - C_{control}}{C_{control}}$ | dimensionless |
| $T_{patch}$ | Patch latency | $T_{patch} = t_{deploy} - t_{disclose}$ | days / hours |
| $Cov_{SBOM}$ | SBOM coverage | $Cov_{SBOM} = \dfrac{N_{component}^{documented}}{N_{component}^{total}} \times 100$ | percent |
| $MTTD$ | Mean Time To Detect | $MTTD = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{detect}^{i} - t_{occur}^{i})$ | time unit |
| $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Remediate | $MTTR = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{fix}^{i} - t_{detect}^{i})$ | time unit |
| $P_{compromise}$ | Compromise probability (Markov model) | $P_{compromise} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{k}(1 - p_i)$ | dimensionless |
| $SL$ | Security Level (IEC 62443) | $SL \in \lbrace 1,2,3,4 \rbrace$ | categorical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU rule:** When two Security Levels are referenced, the **target SL-T** must be $\ge$ the **achieved SL-A**, which must be $\ge$ the **capability SL-C** of each zone. If $SL-A < SL-T$, the design fails the gate.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (ISO/SAE 21434):** A modern car has ~**100 ECUs** and ~**100 million lines of code**. Lifecycle security ensures the *Software Update Management System (SUMS)* — mandated by UN R156 — cannot be hijacked.
- **Smart Grid (IEC 62443):** A substation's IEDs (Intelligent Electronic Devices) must be patched over 30+ year lifecycles. Lifecycle security ensures the *cryptographic agility* to migrate from RSA-2048 to post-quantum algorithms.
- **Medical (IEC 80001-1, FDA premarket cybersecurity guidance):** Pacemakers and insulin pumps must have a *Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)* in regulatory submissions — a direct lifecycle artifact.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Risk Reduction Justification for Security Investment

A common KTU question: *"Justify security investment using the ALE/ROS framework."*

### Given (Typical KTU Numerical):
- Asset Value $AV = ₹\,50{,}00{,}000$
- Exposure Factor $EF = 0.40$
- Annualized Rate of Occurrence $ARO = 0.50$ (i.e., once every 2 years)
- Cost of proposed control $C_{control} = ₹\,6{,}00{,}000$ per year
- After deploying control, the new $ARO$ becomes $0.05$ (mitigation effectiveness ~**90%**)

### Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1 — Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE):**

$$\begin{aligned}
SLE &= AV \times EF \\
&= ₹\,50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 \\
&= ₹\,20{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* A single incident destroys 40% of the asset's value.

**Step 2 — Compute Annual Loss Expectancy BEFORE control:**

$$\begin{aligned}
ALE_{before} &= SLE \times ARO_{before} \\
&= ₹\,20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 \\
&= ₹\,10{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* On average, half such incident happens per year → expected annual loss.

**Step 3 — Compute Annual Loss Expectancy AFTER control:**

$$\begin{aligned}
ARO_{after} &= 0.05 \\
ALE_{after} &= SLE \times ARO_{after} \\
&= ₹\,20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 \\
&= ₹\,1{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* Control drops the occurrence rate 10×.

**Step 4 — Compute Return on Security Investment (ROS):**

$$\begin{aligned}
ROS &= \frac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - C_{control}}{C_{control}} \\
&= \frac{10{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 0.50 \text{ or } 50\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Decision Rule:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } ROS > 0 &\Rightarrow \text{ Investment is justified (net positive return).} \\
\text{If } ROS \leq 0 &\Rightarrow \text{ Investment is NOT justified.}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $ROS = +0.50$, the control is justified — every ₹1 spent saves ₹1.50 in expected loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Insight (KTU):** Examiners award marks for *interpreting* the ROS, not just computing. Always state: *"A positive ROS indicates the security control yields net positive value over its operational life."*

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — SBOM Coverage as a Lifecycle Maturity Metric

**Scenario:** A CPS has **137** third-party software components (libraries, drivers, RTOS modules). During a S-SDLC audit, only **102** have verified origin, license, version, and hash.

### Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1 — Identify documented components:**

$$N_{documented} = 102$$

**Step 2 — Identify total components:**

$$N_{total} = 137$$

**Step 3 — Compute SBOM coverage:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Cov_{SBOM} &= \frac{N_{documented}}{N_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{102}{137} \times 100 \\
&\approx 74.45\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Maturity classification (per NTIA minimum elements):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Cov_{SBOM} \geq 95\% &\Rightarrow \text{Mature (Grade A)} \\
85\% \leq Cov_{SBOM} < 95\% &\Rightarrow \text{Acceptable (Grade B)} \\
70\% \leq Cov_{SBOM} < 85\% &\Rightarrow \text{Deficient (Grade C)} \\
Cov_{SBOM} < 70\% &\Rightarrow \text{Non-compliant (Grade F)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** With $Cov_{SBOM} \approx 74.45\%$, the project is **Grade C — Deficient** and must remediate the missing **35 components** before regulatory submission.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Markov Chain Compromise Probability for Defense-in-Depth

For a CPS with **3 layered controls** (firewall + IDS + HSM) of independent compromise probabilities:

$$p_1 = 0.10, \quad p_2 = 0.08, \quad p_3 = 0.05$$

### Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1 — Apply the series (AND) model for defense-in-depth:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{compromise} &= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{k}(1 - p_i) \\
&= 1 - \big[(1 - p_1) \times (1 - p_2) \times (1 - p_3)\big]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{compromise} &= 1 - (0.90 \times 0.92 \times 0.95) \\
&= 1 - 0.7866 \\
&= 0.2134
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{compromise} &\approx 21.34\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compare to a single-control baseline ($p_1$ alone):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{single} &= p_1 = 0.10 = 10\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Analysis (the paradox of layered defense):**

Layered defense *increases* the cumulative probability (from 10% to 21.34%) because each layer is an *additional attack surface*. However, this is misleading because:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{survive\,all\,layers} &= \prod_{i=1}^{k} p_i = 0.10 \times 0.08 \times 0.05 = 0.0004 = 0.04\%
\end{aligned}$$

This represents the probability that an attacker **simultaneously** breaches all three layers. The proper engineering metric is therefore the **time-to-compromise**, not single-event probability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often state that "more layers = more security." This is *not* universally true. Each layer adds attack surface. True defense-in-depth is achieved when layers are **complementary** (e.g., preventive + detective + responsive), not merely additive.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Lifecycle Phase × Security Activity × CO Mapping

| Lifecycle Phase | Primary Security Activity | Deliverable Artifact | Tool/Standard | CO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Concept | Asset identification, Safety-Security coupling | Security Vision Doc | NIST SP 800-30 | CO4 |
| Requirements | STRIDE, TARA, SFR derivation | SRS-Sec Annex | IEC 62443-3-3, ISO/SAE 21434 | CO4 |
| Design | Architectural patterns, crypto design | Security Arch Diagram | SABSA, TOGAF-Sec | CO4, CO5 |
| Implementation | Secure coding, code review, signing | Source + SAST report | CERT C, MISRA-C, Sigstore | CO4 |
| Verification | Pen-test, fuzz, SCA | VAPT Report | OWASP, CWE, NVD | CO5 |
| Deployment | Hardening, PKI provisioning | Commissioning Checklist | CIS Benchmarks, IEC 62443-3-2 | CO5 |
| Operations | SOC monitoring, patching | SOC Logs, Patch Log | SIEM, ICS-IDS | CO5 |
| Decommissioning | Crypto-erase, destruction | EOL Disposition | NIST SP 800-88 | CO4, CO5 |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Patch Latency in a CPS

A CVE for a popular RTOS library is disclosed on **Day 0**. A vendor releases a patched firmware on **Day 21**. A CPS operator in a chemical plant completes safety-impact analysis and deploys on **Day 42**.

### Step-by-step:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{vendor} &= 21 - 0 = 21 \text{ days} \quad \text{(vendor patch latency)} \\
T_{deploy} &= 42 - 0 = 42 \text{ days} \quad \text{(total patch latency)} \\
T_{safety-review} &= 42 - 21 = 21 \text{ days} \quad \text{(operator review window)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPS-Specific Reality:** Unlike IT systems that can patch in 24 hours, CPS operators must conduct a *safety hazard analysis* before patching, because firmware updates can change deterministic timing on the physical process. The 21-day safety review is **legitimate engineering**, not negligence.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Secure CPS Development Lifecycle (V-Model Adaptation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Concept and Feasibility] --> A2[Security Requirements Engineering]
    A2 --> A3[Architectural and Detailed Design]
    A3 --> A4[Secure Implementation Coding]
    A4 --> A5[Security Testing and Verification]
    A5 --> A6[Secure Deployment and Commissioning]
    A6 --> A7[Operations Maintenance and Monitoring]
    A7 --> A8[Secure Decommissioning]

    A2 -.->|Traceability| A5
    A3 -.->|TARA validation| A5
    A4 -.->|Static analysis gate| A5
    A5 -.->|Penetration report| A6
    A6 -.->|Hardening checklist| A7
    A7 -.->|End of life crypto erase| A8

    G1[Gate Security Vision Approval]:::gate
    G2[Gate SFR and SAR Approval]:::gate
    G3[Gate Security Architecture Review]:::gate
    G4[Gate Code Review and SAST]:::gate
    G5[Gate VAPT Sign off]:::gate
    G6[Gate Commissioning Sign off]:::gate
    G7[Gate SOC Acceptance]:::gate
    G8[Gate EOL Disposition]:::gate

    A1 --- G1
    A2 --- G2
    A3 --- G3
    A4 --- G4
    A5 --- G5
    A6 --- G6
    A7 --- G7
    A8 --- G8

    classDef gate fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — DevSecOps CI/CD Pipeline with Security Gates

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[1 Plan] --> P2[2 Code Commit]
    P2 --> P3[3 Build and Compile]
    P3 --> P4[4 Test Stage]
    P4 --> P5[5 Release and Sign]
    P5 --> P6[6 Deploy to Staging]
    P6 --> P7[7 Deploy to Production]
    P7 --> P8[8 Operate and Monitor]

    S1[Threat Model Auto Gen]:::sec --> P1
    S2[Pre commit Secrets Scan]:::sec --> P2
    S3[SAST and SCA]:::sec --> P3
    S4[DAST and Fuzzing]:::sec --> P4
    S5[Sigstore and SLSA Attestation]:::sec --> P5
    S6[Policy as Code Gate]:::sec --> P6
    S7[Canary and Blue Green]:::sec --> P7
    S8[Runtime IDS and SIEM]:::sec --> P8

    classDef sec fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Purdue Model with Security Zones (CPS Reference Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L5[Level 5 Enterprise Network]
        E1[ERP and Cloud Services]
    end
    subgraph L4[Level 4 Site Business Planning]
        E2[Historian and MES]
    end
    subgraph L3_5[Level 3.5 DMZ Security Zone]
        E3[Industrial DMZ Firewalls and Jump Hosts]
    end
    subgraph L3[Level 3 Site Operations]
        E4[HMI and Engineering Workstations]
    end
    subgraph L2[Level 2 Area Supervisory Control]
        E5[SCADA and Alarm Servers]
    end
    subgraph L1[Level 1 Basic Control]
        E6[PLCs and RTUs]
    end
    subgraph L0[Level 0 Physical Process]
        E7[Sensors and Actuators]
    end

    L5 --> L3_5
    L4 --> L3_5
    L3_5 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 --> L0

    Z1[Zone Identification per IEC 62443 3 3]:::zone
    L1 --- Z1
    Z2[Conduit Security between Zones]:::zone
    L2 --- Z2
    Z3[SL Target per Zone]:::zone
    L3 --- Z3

    classDef zone fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Security Gate Decision Logic (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Lifecycle Phase Input Artifact] --> CHECK{Automated Policy Check}
    CHECK -->|Pass| VAULT[Store in Evidence Vault with Hash]
    CHECK -->|Fail| BLOCK[Block Pipeline and Notify Security Champion]
    VAULT --> REVIEW[Manual Security Architect Review]
    REVIEW -->|Approve| NEXT[Proceed to Next Phase]
    REVIEW -->|Reject| REMEDIATE[Generate Remediation Ticket]
    REMEDIATE --> INPUT
    BLOCK --> REMEDIATE

    AUDIT[Audit Trail] --> VAULT
    AUDIT --> REVIEW
    AUDIT --> NEXT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in CPS:** Unlike IT DevOps where a pipeline can auto-approve, CPS security gates often require **human-in-the-loop** because of safety implications. The diagram above models a *semi-automated* gate — automation handles the deterministic checks, while humans handle the safety-security risk acceptance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
Define the term **"Security by Design"** in the context of CPS. State TWO key activities performed in the design phase of a Secure SDLC for CPS.

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
*Security by Design* is a methodology where security controls and threat mitigations are architected into a Cyber-Physical System from the earliest design stage, rather than being added after development. It is mandated by standards like IEC 62443 and ISO/SAE 21434 because CPS failures have physical consequences. **Two key design-phase activities:** (1) Conducting a **Threat Analysis & Risk Assessment (TARA)** to identify attack surfaces, threat agents, and impact severity. (2) Defining **Security Zones and Conduits** per the Purdue/ISA-99 model and applying the **Security Level (SL)** concept for each zone. (1.5 marks each — definition: 1 mark, two activities: 2 marks)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
What is a **Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)**? Why is it critical for CPS security lifecycle management?

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
An **SBOM** is a formal, machine-readable inventory of all software components, libraries, versions, licenses, and cryptographic hashes that constitute a system — typically formatted per **CycloneDX** or **SPDX** standards. (1 mark) It is critical for CPS security lifecycle management because: (1) It enables **rapid vulnerability correlation** when a new CVE is disclosed — operators can immediately identify if the affected component is present in their deployed system. (2) It supports **regulatory compliance** under the US Executive Order 14028 and EU Cyber Resilience Act, which require SBOMs for critical infrastructure. (3) It enables **software provenance verification**, preventing supply-chain attacks like the SolarWinds or 3CX incidents. (2 marks — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three justifications, or expanded 2-mark elaboration on any two)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **seven phases of a Secure Software Development Lifecycle (S-SDLC) for Cyber-Physical Systems**. For each phase, identify the primary security deliverable artifact.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CPS controls a regional power substation. The asset value of the substation's control system is estimated at **₹ 2,00,00,000**. The estimated **Exposure Factor (EF)** if a successful cyber-physical attack occurs is **0.60**. The current **Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)** is **0.40** (i.e., 1 attack every 2.5 years). The utility proposes to deploy a multi-layered defense-in-depth solution (network segmentation + ICS-IDS + HSM) costing **₹ 12,00,000 per year**, which is expected to reduce the ARO to **0.05**. Using the ALE/ROS framework, justify whether the investment is economically justified.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — S-SDLC Phases [7 Marks]

The S-SDLC for CPS consists of **seven phases** that are iterative and gated:

1. **Concept & Feasibility** — Identify the CPS mission, physical assets, regulatory regime (IEC 62443, ISO/SAE 21434), and stakeholders. *Artifact:* **Security Vision Document**.
2. **Security Requirements Engineering** — Derive **Security Functional Requirements (SFRs)** using STRIDE-per-element; map to **Security Levels (SL-T 1–4)**. *Artifact:* **SRS-Security Annex + Traceability Matrix**.
3. **Architectural Design** — Define **zones & conduits**, select **cryptographic primitives**, plan **key management (PKI, HSM)**. *Artifact:* **Security Architecture Document + TARA Report**.
4. **Secure Implementation** — Apply **CERT C / MISRA-C** coding standards; use vetted crypto libraries; integrate **secure boot** and **TPM measured boot**. *Artifact:* **Source code + SAST report**.
5. **Security Testing & Verification** — Execute **SAST, DAST, IAST, SCA, penetration testing, fault injection, side-channel analysis**. *Artifact:* **VAPT Report + Closure Log**.
6. **Secure Deployment & Commissioning** — **Harden** PLCs/RTUs, provision **device certificates** (mTLS), segment network per **Purdue Model**. *Artifact:* **Commissioning Security Checklist + As-Built Architecture**.
7. **Operations, Maintenance & Decommissioning** — **Continuous SOC monitoring**, **patch management with safety review**, **incident response**, and **crypto-erase** at end-of-life. *Artifact:* **SOC Reports + EOL Disposition Record**.

*(Award: 0.5 mark per phase description, 0.5 mark per artifact = 7 marks. Diagram of the S-SDLC adapted from Section 4.1 carries 1 bonus mark if clearly drawn.)*

#### Part (b) — ALE/ROS Economic Justification [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE):**

$$\begin{aligned}
SLE &= AV \times EF \\
&= ₹\,2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 \\
&= ₹\,1{,}20{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute ALE BEFORE the control:**

$$\begin{aligned}
ALE_{before} &= SLE \times ARO_{before} \\
&= ₹\,1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 \\
&= ₹\,48{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute ALE AFTER the control:**

$$\begin{aligned}
ALE_{after} &= SLE \times ARO_{after} \\
&= ₹\,1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 \\
&= ₹\,6{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the avoided annual loss:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta ALE &= ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} \\
&= ₹\,48{,}00{,}000 - ₹\,6{,}00{,}000 \\
&= ₹\,42{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Return on Security Investment (ROS):**

$$\begin{aligned}
ROS &= \frac{\Delta ALE - C_{control}}{C_{control}} \\
&= \frac{42{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \\
&\approx 2.50 \text{ or } 250\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Decision & Justification:**

Since $ROS = +2.50 \gg 0$, the investment is **strongly justified**. The control yields a **net annual benefit of ₹ 30,00,000** and a **2.5× return** on the security expenditure. (1.5 marks for SLE, 1 mark for ALE-before, 1 mark for ALE-after, 1 mark for Δ ALE, 1.5 marks for ROS + interpretation)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Students often **forget to subtract $C_{control}$ from $\Delta ALE$** in the numerator, computing $ROS = \Delta ALE / C_{control}$ instead. This yields a wrong value of $3.50$ (350%) instead of the correct $2.50$ (250%). Marks are deducted for the conceptual error. The numerator must represent **NET** benefit, not gross benefit.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE) **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **DevSecOps** as applied to Cyber-Physical Systems. With a suitable block diagram, describe the **eight stages of a DevSecOps CI/CD pipeline** and identify the **automated security gate** at each stage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CPS for a medical infusion pump has **210 third-party software components**. After a S-SDLC audit, the security team finds that **178 components** have verified SBOM records, while the remaining **32 components** lack either version traceability or cryptographic hash verification. Compute the **SBOM coverage** and classify the project into a maturity grade. Also explain, with a derivation, why SBOM coverage of less than 100% can be a **safety hazard** (not merely a security gap) in medical CPS.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — DevSecOps for CPS [7 Marks]

**DevSecOps** is the cultural, procedural, and tooling integration of *Development*, *Security*, and *Operations* into a single, automated, continuously iterating pipeline. In a CPS context, it is adapted to accommodate the **safety-criticality, deterministic timing, and long operational life** of physical systems.

**Eight Stages and Security Gates:**

| Stage | Activity | Security Gate | Tool Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1 Plan** | Requirements, threat model | Auto threat-model generation from design docs | IriusRisk, ThreatModeler |
| **2 Code** | Developer commits | Pre-commit hooks: secrets, lint, license | git-secrets, pre-commit |
| **3 Build** | Compile, image build | **SAST** (static analysis) + **SCA** (dep check) | SonarQube, Snyk, Trivy |
| **4 Test** | Functional, integration | **DAST** + **Fuzzing** + **IAST** | OWASP ZAP, AFL, Burp |
| **5 Release** | Artifact versioning | **Sigstore signing, SLSA attestation** | Sigstore, in-toto |
| **6 Deploy (Staging)** | Staging rollout | **Policy-as-Code** gate (CIS, NIST) | OPA, Kyverno |
| **7 Deploy (Production)** | Production rollout | **Canary / Blue-Green** with safety interlocks | Argo, Spinnaker |
| **8 Operate & Monitor** | Runtime | **Runtime IDS, SIEM, anomaly detection** | Falco, Dragos, Wazuh |

*(Award: 0.5 mark per stage description, 0.5 mark per security gate = 8 marks → cap at 7.)*

**CPS-Specific Deviations from IT DevSecOps:**
- **Mandatory human-in-the-loop** before production deployment, because firmware changes can affect physical process timing (deterministic control loops).
- **Digital twin validation** in stage 6 before physical deployment.
- **Safety interlock checks** in stage 7 to prevent unsafe states during rollout.

#### Part (b) — SBOM Coverage Computation & Safety Hazard Analysis [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify component counts:**

$$N_{documented} = 178, \quad N_{total} = 210, \quad N_{undocumented} = 32$$

**Step 2 — Compute SBOM coverage:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Cov_{SBOM} &= \frac{N_{documented}}{N_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{178}{210} \times 100 \\
&\approx 84.76\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Maturity classification (per NTIA / KTU reference):**

| Range | Grade |
|---|---|
| $\geq 95\%$ | A — Mature |
| $85\% \leq Cov < 95\%$ | B — Acceptable |
| $70\% \leq Cov < 85\%$ | C — Deficient |
| $< 70\%$ | F — Non-compliant |

Since $84.76\% \in [70\%, 85\%)$, the project is **Grade C — Deficient**.

**Step 4 — Safety hazard derivation (NOT just a security concern):**

In a medical infusion pump, the device must deliver a precise drug dose (e.g., $\pm 5\%$ accuracy). If one of the undocumented components (say, a math library used in dose calculation) contains a subtle numerical precision bug discovered *after* deployment, the operator cannot:

- **Trace** the affected firmware batch (no version map).
- **Recall** the device precisely (no component hash to verify integrity).
- **Patch safely** without a full re-validation, because the unknown component's interaction with safety logic is uncertain.

**Quantitative hazard expression (informal):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{undocumented\,CVE\,impact} &= P_{CVE\,in\,undoc.} \times P_{component\,used\,in\,safety\,path} \\
&\text{If } Cov_{SBOM} = 84.76\%, \text{ then } 15.24\% \text{ of code paths are "blind spots."}
\end{aligned}$$

In a **safety-integrity** framework (IEC 61508 / IEC 62304), even a single unknown dependency in a safety-critical path is a **systematic failure** that cannot be quantified — a direct violation of SIL requirements. Thus, SBOM coverage is not just a security metric; it is a **safety assurance prerequisite**.

*(Award: 1.5 marks for coverage calculation, 1.5 marks for grade classification, 2 marks for safety hazard derivation, 2 marks for connecting to safety standards like IEC 62304.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Many students compute the coverage correctly but **fail to connect SBOM to safety** — they treat it purely as a security concern. For CPS questions, the examiner expects the answer to bridge the **security-safety continuum**. A purely security-focused answer will be capped at **4 of 7 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must know for KTU 2024 ESE)**

- ✅ **S-SDLC for CPS** has **7 phases** (Concept → Requirements → Design → Implementation → Testing → Deployment → Operations/Decommissioning) — know all artifacts.
- ✅ **DevSecOps** is the modern instantiation of S-SDLC with **8 stages** and **automated security gates** at every arrow.
- ✅ **STRIDE** is the canonical threat taxonomy: **S**poofing, **T**ampering, **R**epudiation, **I**nformation Disclosure, **D**enial of Service, **E**levation of Privilege.
- ✅ **TARA** (Threat Analysis & Risk Assessment) is mandatory in **ISO/SAE 21434** and produces the basis for cyber goals.
- ✅ **Purdue Model** has levels **0 (process) → 1 (basic control) → 2 (supervisory) → 3 (operations) → 3.5 (DMZ) → 4 (site) → 5 (enterprise)** — always enforce the DMZ at level 3.5.
- ✅ **IEC 62443-3-3** defines **4 Security Levels** (SL 1–4); **Target SL ≥ Achieved SL ≥ Capability SL** of every zone.
- ✅ **SBOM formats:** **SPDX** (Linux Foundation) and **CycloneDX** (OWASP) are the two accepted standards; **minimum 7 elements** per NTIA.
- ✅ **Key formulas to memorize for numerical problems:**
  - $SLE = AV \times EF$
  - $ALE = SLE \times ARO$
  - $ROS = \dfrac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - C_{control}}{C_{control}}$
  - $Cov_{SBOM} = \dfrac{N_{documented}}{N_{total}} \times 100$
  - $MTTD = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum (t_{detect} - t_{occur})$
  - $MTTR = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum (t_{fix} - t_{detect})$
- ✅ **CPS-specific reality:** Patch latency in OT is bounded by *safety re-validation*, not by IT speed. Do not apply IT SLA standards directly to CPS.
- ✅ **Standards mapping for KTU exam answers (always cite the standard):**
  - Industrial → **IEC 62443**
  - Automotive → **ISO/SAE 21434**
  - Medical → **IEC 62304 + FDA premarket cybersecurity guidance**
  - General systems → **NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 1**
- ✅ **"Shift-Left"** means moving security activities to *earlier* phases of the lifecycle — this reduces cost, defect density, and rework.
- ✅ **Defense-in-Depth** is *complementary* layers (preventive + detective + responsive), not merely additive.
- ✅ **Decommissioning** must include **cryptographic erasure** per **NIST SP 800-88 Rev. 1** — not merely file deletion.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Topic: Integrating Security into the Development Lifecycle of CPS Projects.**
> The next topic in Module 4 should be studied only after mastering the seven lifecycle phases, the DevSecOps pipeline, and the ALE/ROS/SBOM numerical framework above.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Improvement and Adaptation of CPS Security Practices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Continuous Improvement and Adaptation of CPS Security Practices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Continuous Improvement and Adaptation of CPS Security Practices** is the systematic, cyclical, and governance-driven process of iteratively refining, updating, and evolving the security posture, controls, policies, architectures, and operational procedures of a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) in response to newly discovered threats, vulnerabilities, environmental changes, lessons learned from incidents, technological advancements, and regulatory updates.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is grounded in three interconnected pillars:
- **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) Deming Cycle** applied to security operations.
- **Adaptive Security Architecture (ASA)** based on the Gartner model of *Predict-Prevent-Detect-Respond*.
- **Security Maturity Model Integration (e.g., CMMI, C2M2, NIST CSF)** for measuring incremental progress.

The mathematical expression of continuous improvement is often expressed as:

$$S_{t+1} = f(S_t, T_{new}, V_{discovered}, L_{learned}, R_{regulatory})$$

where $S_t$ is the security state at time $t$, $T_{new}$ represents the new threat landscape, $V_{discovered}$ denotes newly identified vulnerabilities, $L_{learned}$ captures lessons from incidents, and $R_{regulatory}$ represents updated compliance mandates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Continuous improvement is **NOT** a one-time project. It is a *perpetual feedback-controlled loop* mandated by ISO 27001 (Clause 10), NIST SP 800-61r2 (Post-Incident Activity), and IEC 62443 (Security Lifecycle).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Human Immune System**
>
> Imagine your body as a CPS — your **heart, lungs, and nervous system** are physical processes, and your **brain + nerves** are the cyber (computational) layer. Your immune system NEVER stops improving. When you catch a flu, your body doesn't just fight it and forget it — your **B-cells and T-cells "remember"** the pathogen's signature, and the next time the same threat appears, the response is *10 to 1000 times faster*. This biological "memory" is exactly what continuous security improvement aims to engineer into a CPS.
>
> - The **threat intelligence feed** = the body's pathogen recognition system.
> - The **patch management system** = the body's antibody production line.
> - The **incident response team** = the white blood cells.
> - The **post-incident review (PIR)** = the immune memory that "learns."
> - The **maturity model** = how "fit and trained" your immune system is.

This analogy makes it clear: a CPS that does *not* learn from past attacks and does *not* adapt is doomed to keep falling for the same exploit (or worse, an evolved one).

### 1.3 Why Continuous Improvement is Non-Negotiable in CPS

| Reason | Engineering Reality |
|---|---|
| **Zero-Day Proliferation** | A typical CPS faces **15–50 new CVEs per month** per major vendor (ICS-CERT advisories). |
| **Long Operational Lifespan** | Industrial CPS run for **20–30 years** vs. 3–5 years for IT systems. |
| **Air-Gap Erosion** | Legacy isolation assumptions are invalidated by **IIoT, 5G, and remote vendor access**. |
| **Adversary Innovation** | Threat actors evolve tactics using **AI-driven polymorphic malware**. |
| **Regulatory Tightening** | Frameworks like **NERC CIP, IEC 62443, NIS2** keep raising the bar. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always cite the **PDCA cycle** and at least **one maturity framework** (NIST CSF, C2M2, or CMMI) when defining continuous improvement. Examiners reward framework-level vocabulary.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Continuous CPS Security Improvement

Continuous improvement rests on four interlocking pillars. Each pillar must be operationalized within a CPS environment.

---

#### Pillar 1: **Plan (Establish Baseline & Objectives)**

The "Plan" phase is where the organization defines its desired security posture, risk appetite, and the scope of improvement.

- **Asset Inventory Refresh:** Catalog all physical assets (PLCs, RTUs, SCADA servers, sensors, actuators) and cyber assets (HMI, historians, engineering workstations, network gear).
- **Risk Assessment Update:** Re-evaluate likelihood and impact using qualitative (L×I matrix) and quantitative (FAIR, ALE) methods.
- **Control Gap Analysis:** Compare current controls to a reference framework (NIST SP 800-53, IEC 62443-3-3).
- **Metric Definition:** Define SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) security KPIs.

The **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** for each identified risk is computed as:

$$ALE = SLE \times ARO$$

where $SLE$ is the Single Loss Expectancy and $ARO$ is the Annualized Rate of Occurrence.

---

#### Pillar 2: **Do (Implement Improvements)**

The execution phase where the planned changes are rolled out.

- **Patch & Firmware Deployment:** Roll out vendor-validated security patches using OT-aware change windows.
- **Configuration Hardening:** Apply CIS Benchmarks or vendor-specific hardening guides.
- **Network Segmentation Refinement:** Adjust zones and conduits per IEC 62443-3-3.
- **Identity & Access Hardening:** Implement RBAC, MFA, and Just-in-Time privilege elevation.
- **Security Awareness:** Train OT operators and engineers on the *new* threat landscape (e.g., spear-phishing targeting plant engineers).

---

#### Pillar 3: **Check (Monitor, Measure, and Audit)**

The verification phase — *did the improvement work?*

- **Continuous Monitoring:** SIEM + SOAR + OT-native IDS (e.g., Nozomi, Claroty, Dragos) feeding dashboards.
- **Vulnerability Reassessment:** Rescan to confirm remediation effectiveness.
- **Penetration Testing (Red Team):** Validate that attacker kill chains are now broken at earlier stages.
- **Compliance Audit:** Internal/external audit against regulatory frameworks.
- **KPI Measurement:** Compare actual vs. target metrics (MTTD, MTTR, patch latency, false-positive rate).

The **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** and **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** are core operational metrics:

$$MTTD = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{detect,i} - t_{introduce,i})$$

$$MTTR = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{resolve,i} - t_{detect,i})$$

where $N$ is the number of incidents in the measurement window, $t_{introduce}$ is the time the threat entered the environment, $t_{detect}$ is the detection time, and $t_{resolve}$ is the resolution time.

---

#### Pillar 4: **Act (Standardize or Iterate)**

The decision phase — based on the "Check" results, the organization either:
1. **Standardizes** the improvement (codifies it into policy, baseline, automation), OR
2. **Iterates** by returning to "Plan" with revised hypotheses.

This is the **Deming Wheel** or **PDCA Cycle** originally developed by Walter Shewhart (1939) and popularized by W. Edwards Deming.

---

### 2.2 The Adaptive Security Architecture (Gartner ASA)

The ASA is a strategic framework that overlays PDCA with a *defense-in-depth* philosophy. It consists of four continuous functions:

| Function | Continuous Improvement Question |
|---|---|
| **Predict** | *What attacks are likely given our exposure, threat intel, and adversary behavior?* |
| **Prevent** | *Are our preventive controls (patching, hardening, segmentation) keeping up?* |
| **Detect** | *How quickly are we noticing anomalies, IOCs, and behavioral drifts?* |
| **Respond** | *How fast and effectively do we contain, eradicate, and recover?* |

The mathematical *defense-effectiveness ratio* of an adaptive system is:

$$E_{adaptive} = \frac{D_{detection} \times R_{response} \times P_{prevention}}{(1 - P_{prevention}) \times T_{latency}}$$

where $D_{detection}$ is detection probability, $R_{response}$ is response success rate, $P_{prevention}$ is prevention success rate, and $T_{latency}$ is the mean attacker dwell time (normalized).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** As $P_{prevention} \to 1$, the system becomes asymptotically secure. However, real-world CPS prevention rates hover at $P_{prevention} \approx 0.7 - 0.9$, making the detection and response functions critical.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet — Master These for KTU University Exam**

| Concept / Formula | Symbol / Form | Engineering Meaning | Typical KTU Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PDCA Cycle** | Plan-Do-Check-Act | Iterative improvement loop | 2–3 |
| **ALE (Annualized Loss Expectancy)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Expected yearly financial loss from a risk | 3–5 |
| **MTTD** | $\frac{1}{N}\sum (t_{detect} - t_{introduce})$ | Speed of threat detection | 3–5 |
| **MTTR** | $\frac{1}{N}\sum (t_{resolve} - t_{detect})$ | Speed of incident resolution | 3–5 |
| **Maturity Score** | $M = \frac{\sum w_i \cdot L_i}{L_{max} \cdot \sum w_i}$ | Weighted capability maturity | 2–3 |
| **Patch Coverage Ratio** | $PCR = \frac{N_{patched}}{N_{total\_critical}} \times 100\%$ | Proportion of critical assets patched | 2–3 |
| **Adaptive Effectiveness** | $\frac{D \times R \times P}{(1-P) \times T}$ | Composite defensive capability | 2–4 |
| **Security State Update** | $S_{t+1} = f(S_t, T, V, L, R)$ | Iterative security evolution | 3–5 |
| **Risk Reduction Ratio** | $RRR = 1 - \frac{Risk_{post}}{Risk_{pre}}$ | Effectiveness of a control | 2–3 |
| **Defense-in-Depth Layers** | $L \geq 3$ (minimum) | Number of independent controls | 1–2 |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the absolute value notation is rendered as `\vert` for safety. For example, $\vert x \vert$ would be used instead of the pipe symbol `\| x \|` to avoid breaking table syntax.

---

### 2.4 The Role of Threat Intelligence in Continuous Adaptation

A CPS security program that does not consume threat intelligence will stagnate. The adaptation loop integrates three intelligence streams:

1. **Strategic Intelligence** — high-level adversary motivations, sector trends (e.g., "Energy sector targeted by APT-X").
2. **Operational Intelligence** — TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures) mapped to MITRE ATT&CK for ICS.
3. **Technical Intelligence** — IOCs (Indicators of Compromise): IPs, hashes, YARA rules, Sigma rules.

The intelligence freshness metric is:

$$F_{intel} = 1 - \frac{\Delta t_{since\_update}}{T_{stale\_threshold}}$$

A value $F_{intel} \to 0$ means the intel is stale and should be re-ingested.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The PDCA Effectiveness Function for a CPS

Let us mathematically derive the cumulative security posture of a CPS after $n$ PDCA cycles. Assume each cycle improves the *security effectiveness* $E$ by a learning rate $\alpha$ subject to decay $\beta$ from new threats.

**Step 1: Define the single-cycle improvement.**
Let $E_n$ be the security effectiveness after the $n$-th cycle. Without decay, the improvement gained in cycle $n$ is:

$$\Delta E_n^{ideal} = \alpha \cdot (E_{max} - E_{n-1})$$

where $E_{max}$ is the maximum achievable effectiveness and $\alpha \in (0, 1]$ is the *learning rate* capturing how much of the remaining gap is closed per cycle.

**Step 2: Introduce threat-driven decay.**
Between cycles, new threats erode effectiveness at rate $\beta$:

$$\Delta E_n^{decay} = -\beta \cdot E_{n-1}$$

**Step 3: Net per-cycle update equation.**

$$E_n = E_{n-1} + \alpha (E_{max} - E_{n-1}) - \beta E_{n-1}$$

**Step 4: Rearrange into closed form.**

$$E_n = E_{n-1} (1 - \alpha - \beta) + \alpha E_{max}$$

Let $r = 1 - \alpha - \beta$ (the per-cycle retention coefficient). Then:

$$E_n = r \cdot E_{n-1} + \alpha E_{max}$$

**Step 5: Solve the linear recurrence.**

$$E_n = r^n E_0 + \alpha E_{max} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} r^k$$

**Step 6: Apply the geometric series identity** $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} r^k = \dfrac{1 - r^n}{1 - r}$ (for $r \neq 1$):

$$E_n = r^n E_0 + \alpha E_{max} \cdot \frac{1 - r^n}{1 - r}$$

**Step 7: Steady-state analysis as $n \to \infty$.**

If $\vert r \vert < 1$, then $r^n \to 0$ and:

$$E_{\infty} = \frac{\alpha E_{max}}{1 - r} = \frac{\alpha E_{max}}{1 - (1 - \alpha - \beta)} = \frac{\alpha E_{max}}{\alpha + \beta}$$

**Step 8: Interpretation — the asymptotic ceiling.**

$$\boxed{E_{\infty} = \frac{\alpha E_{max}}{\alpha + \beta}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The *steady-state* security effectiveness is bounded by the ratio of the *learning rate* to the *sum of learning and decay rates*. To raise $E_{\infty}$ toward $E_{max}$, an organization must either (a) increase $\alpha$ (faster, better-integrated improvement cycles) or (b) decrease $\beta$ (slower threat decay via proactive controls). This is the mathematical reason that **mature security programs invest in fast, automated PDCA cycles.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Composite Maturity Score

Let a CPS security program be evaluated against $k$ domains (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover) with weights $w_i$ summing to 1. Each domain is scored on a 0–5 scale (CMMI-style).

$$M = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot L_i}{L_{max}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 1: Plug in the canonical NIST CSF weights** $w_{ID}=0.20$, $w_{PR}=0.25$, $w_{DE}=0.20$, $w_{RS}=0.20$, $w_{RC}=0.15$, with $L_{max}=5$:

$$M = \frac{0.20 L_{ID} + 0.25 L_{PR} + 0.20 L_{DE} + 0.20 L_{RS} + 0.15 L_{RC}}{5} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2: Numerical Example.**

A CPS site reports: $L_{ID}=4$, $L_{PR}=3$, $L_{DE}=2$, $L_{RS}=3$, $L_{RC}=2$. Compute the numerator:

$$Numerator = 0.20(4) + 0.25(3) + 0.20(2) + 0.20(3) + 0.15(2)$$

$$= 0.80 + 0.75 + 0.40 + 0.60 + 0.30 = 2.85$$

**Step 3: Compute maturity percentage.**

$$M = \frac{2.85}{5} \times 100\% = 57\%$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.** A score of 57% places the program in the **"Repeatable" to "Defined"** band (CMMI Level 2–3), indicating structured processes exist but detection and recovery are lagging — exactly where a CPS in industrial settings often sits.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Drivers of Continuous Improvement

| Driver | Mechanism | Direct Effect on $E_n$ | KTU-Relevant Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Threat Intelligence Ingestion** | Updates IOCs/TTPs | Raises $\alpha$, lowers $\beta$ | STIX/TAXII, MITRE ATT&CK for ICS |
| **Post-Incident Review (PIR)** | Feeds lessons into Plan phase | Raises $\alpha$ | NIST SP 800-61r2 |
| **Patch Automation** | Reduces human latency | Raises $\alpha$, lowers $\beta$ | IEC 62443-2-4 |
| **Tabletop Exercises** | Stress-tests response | Raises $R_{response}$ | NIST SP 800-84 |
| **Red/Purple Teaming** | Validates detection paths | Raises $D_{detection}$ | TIBER-EU, NIST SP 800-115 |
| **Regulatory Updates** | Mandates new controls | Lowers $E_{max}$ baseline risk | NIS2, NERC CIP, ISA 99 |
| **AI/ML-based UEBA** | Detects behavioral drift | Raises $D_{detection}$, lowers $T_{latency}$ | MITRE ATLAS, ISO/IEC 42001 |
| **SBOM / VEX** | Tracks component exposure | Reduces $V_{discovered}$ impact | NTIA SBOM, CISA VEX |
| **Zero-Trust Microsegmentation** | Limits blast radius | Raises effective $P_{prevention}$ | NIST SP 800-207 |
| **DevSecOps for CPS** | Embeds security in lifecycle | Raises $\alpha$ | IEC 62443-4-1 |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: PDCA Cycle for a Water Treatment CPS

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A municipal water utility operates a SCADA-controlled treatment plant. A spear-phishing campaign targeted its engineers, leading to credential theft (TRITON/INCONTROLLER-style precursor).

**Plan Phase:**
- Re-evaluate phishing risk using $ALE = SLE \times ARO$.
- Assume $SLE = \$500{,}000$ (downtime + reputation) and $ARO = 0.3$ (once every ~3 years).
- $ALE = 500{,}000 \times 0.3 = \$150{,}000$ per year.
- Gap analysis against IEC 62443-3-3 reveals SR 1.7 (Strength of Passwords) and SR 2.1 (Authorization Enforcement) are at Maturity Level 2; target Level 3.

**Do Phase:**
- Deploy phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2) on all engineering workstations.
- Enforce least-privilege access on HMI accounts.
- Update firewall rules to block outbound C2 to known ICS-themed APT infrastructure.

**Check Phase:**
- MTTD before improvement: 72 hours. After: 8 hours.
- MTTR before: 96 hours. After: 18 hours.
- Re-pen-test: phishing success dropped from 28% to 4%.
- Patch coverage of critical PLCs rose from 60% to 92%.

**Act Phase:**
- Codify the new MFA policy as Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) WTR-SEC-014.
- Schedule quarterly phishing simulations to sustain the gain.
- Identify the *next* improvement: ICS-aware EDR on engineering workstations.

> [!TIP]
> This end-to-end example is the **gold-standard structure** for a 14-mark KTU Part B answer. Examiners expect: (1) phase headings, (2) one metric per phase, (3) a maturity framework reference, (4) a closing reflection on what the *next* cycle should address.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PDCA (Deming) Cycle — CPS Security Adaptation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PLAN\nDefine objectives\nRisk assessment\nControl gap analysis\nMaturity baseline"] --> B["DO\nPatch deployment\nConfiguration hardening\nNetwork segmentation\nSecurity awareness"]
    B --> C["CHECK\nContinuous monitoring\nVulnerability re-scan\nRed team exercises\nKPI measurement"]
    C --> D{"Did the\nimprovement\nwork?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["ACT\nStandardize\nCodify into policy\nAutomate\nUpdate baseline"]
    D -- No --> A
    E --> F["Next Cycle\nFeedback from incidents\nNew threat intel\nRegulatory change"]
    F --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `D` node is a decision diamond (rhombus) asking whether the verified improvement met the success criteria. On "Yes," the change is institutionalized; on "No," the loop restarts at Plan with a refined hypothesis.

---

### 4.2 Adaptive Security Architecture (Gartner ASA) for CPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["PREDICT\nThreat modeling\nAdversary profiling\nRisk forecasting"] --> V["PREVENT\nPatching\nHardening\nSegmentation\nAccess control"]
    V --> DT["DETECT\nSIEM + OT IDS\nUEBA\nAnomaly detection"]
    DT --> R["RESPOND\nSOAR playbooks\nIR playbooks\nContainment"]
    R --> L["LESSONS LEARNED\nPost-incident review\nUpdate threat models"]
    L --> P
```

The four functions are tightly coupled. Detection failures feed back as prevention gaps; response failures feed back as detection blind spots. The cycle never terminates — it is a **continuous adaptation engine**.

---

### 4.3 Maturity Model Progression (CMMI-Style Levels)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["LEVEL 1: INITIAL\nAd-hoc, heroic efforts\nNo standardization"] --> L2["LEVEL 2: REPEATABLE\nBasic policies exist\nSome measurement"]
    L2 --> L3["LEVEL 3: DEFINED\nDocumented processes\nOrganization-wide standards"]
    L3 --> L4["LEVEL 4: MANAGED\nQuantitative measurement\nPredictive analytics"]
    L4 --> L5["LEVEL 5: OPTIMIZING\nContinuous improvement\nInnovation culture"]
    L5 -. Continuous PDCA cycles .-> L5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dashed self-loop on Level 5 reflects the asymptotic $E_{\infty} = \frac{\alpha E_{max}}{\alpha + \beta}$ derived in Section 3.1. A Level 5 organization does not "graduate" — it perpetually refines.

---

### 4.4 Continuous Improvement Feedback Loop for a CPS Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["REQUIREMENTS\n& DESIGN"] --> D["DEVELOPMENT\n& INTEGRATION"]
    D --> O["OPERATION\n& MONITORING"]
    O --> M["MAINTENANCE\n& PATCHING"]
    M --> RT["RETIREMENT\n& REPLACEMENT"]
    O -- "Threat intel &\nincident data" --> I["IMPROVEMENT\nENGINE"]
    M -- "Vulnerability &\npatch feedback" --> I
    RT -- "Lessons learned\n& SBOM archive" --> I
    I -- "Updated security\nrequirements" --> R
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *closed-loop lifecycle model* mandated by **IEC 62443-4-1** (Product Development Lifecycle) and **NIST SP 800-161r1** (C-SCRM). The "Improvement Engine" ingests operational, maintenance, and retirement data, and pushes refined security requirements back into the design phase.

---

### 4.5 Threat Intelligence → Detection → Response → Improvement Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TI["Threat Intelligence\nSources (STIX/TAXII,\nISACs, vendor feeds)"] --> SIEM["SIEM / SOAR\n(Correlation &\nOrchestration)"]
    OT["OT-native IDS\n(Dragos, Nozomi,\nClaroty)"] --> SIEM
    SIEM --> IR["Incident Response\nTeam"]
    IR --> PIR["Post-Incident Review\n(PIR) & RCA"]
    PIR --> KBase["Knowledge Base\nUpdate (MISP)"]
    KBase --> TI
    PIR --> PM["Patch &\nConfig Management"]
    PM --> OT
```

> [!TIP]
> The bi-directional arrows from the Knowledge Base back to Threat Intelligence and from Patch Management back to OT sensors capture the **adaptive** nature of the system: lessons learned tighten both upstream intelligence and downstream controls.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECPT413]**
**Define the PDCA cycle and explain its relevance to continuous improvement of CPS security practices. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** is a four-phase iterative management method originally developed by Walter Shewhart (1939) and popularized by W. Edwards Deming for continuous quality improvement. In CPS security, the cycle operates as follows:

- **Plan:** Define security objectives, perform risk assessment, and identify control gaps using frameworks like NIST CSF or IEC 62443.
- **Do:** Implement improvements such as patching, hardening, segmentation, and training.
- **Check:** Monitor, measure KPIs (MTTD, MTTR, patch coverage), and audit against the baseline.
- **Act:** Standardize successful changes into policy and SOPs, or restart the cycle if objectives are not met.

Its relevance lies in its ability to **institutionalize learning**: every incident, audit, or threat-intel update becomes input to the next Plan phase, ensuring the CPS adapts to a constantly evolving threat landscape rather than remaining static.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECPT413]**
**List and briefly explain any three security maturity models used for continuous improvement in CPS. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF):** A five-function model (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover) with four maturity tiers (Partial, Risk-Informed, Repeatable, Adaptive) used to benchmark and progressively improve CPS security programs.
2. **C2M2 (Cybersecurity Capability Maturity Model):** Developed by the U.S. DOE for the energy sector; ten domains scored on 0–3 maturity levels, enabling sector-specific benchmarking.
3. **CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration):** A five-level (1–5) generic maturity model applicable to security processes, where Level 5 represents the "Optimizing" stage of continuous improvement.
4. *(Alternative)* **OWASP SAMM:** Software Assurance Maturity Model, used when CPS software/firmware development is in scope.

These models provide a **common vocabulary, measurable tiers, and a roadmap** for sustained improvement.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Do **NOT** describe the four PDCA phases in one bullet point each and call it a 7-mark answer. Examiners expect **at least one quantitative metric, one framework reference, and one diagram or example per phase**.
> - Avoid the trap of writing only about **patching**. Continuous improvement spans *people, process, and technology* — touching governance, training, threat intel, detection, and response.
> - Citing "**ISO 27001**" or "**NIST CSF**" without specifying the *clause/function* is a common reason students lose 1–2 marks. Always pin the reference (e.g., "NIST CSF Detect Function, DE.AE-2").
> - For maturity-model questions, students often forget to **map their CPS example to a specific tier**, losing the application marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) Discuss in detail how the PDCA cycle is applied to continuously improve the security of a Cyber-Physical System. Illustrate with a real-world CPS example (e.g., smart grid or water treatment SCADA). (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (1 Mark)**
The PDCA cycle is the de-facto continuous-improvement engine mandated by ISO 27001 (Clause 10) and IEC 62443-2-4. In a CPS context, it is uniquely challenging because changes must balance *security improvement* against *operational continuity* — a 30-second downtime in a power grid can cascade into millions in losses.

**2. Plan Phase (1.5 Marks)**
- Asset inventory refresh: list PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, engineering workstations, historian servers.
- Risk assessment using $ALE = SLE \times ARO$.
- Gap analysis against IEC 62443-3-3 SL-2 requirements.
- Define SMART KPIs (MTTD < 8 hrs, PCR > 90%, phishing success < 5%).

**3. Do Phase (1.5 Marks)**
- Deploy vendor-validated firmware patches during planned maintenance windows.
- Enforce MFA on all OT-credentialed accounts.
- Implement VLAN-based segmentation per Purdue Model (Levels 0–3.5).
- Conduct OT-specific phishing awareness training.

**4. Check Phase (1.5 Marks)**
- Continuous monitoring via SIEM + OT-native IDS (e.g., Dragos).
- Re-pen-test: phishing rate dropped 28% → 4%; MTTD dropped 72 hrs → 8 hrs.
- PCR rose 60% → 92%.

**5. Act Phase (1 Mark)**
- Codify the new MFA policy as **SOP-SEC-021**.
- Schedule quarterly phishing simulations.
- Roll findings into the **next** Plan phase as inputs.

**6. Conclusion (0.5 Mark)**
The cycle institutionalizes learning; each iteration closes a measurable gap, raising $E_n$ asymptotically toward $E_{\infty} = \frac{\alpha E_{max}}{\alpha + \beta}$.

---

**(b) Explain the Adaptive Security Architecture (ASA) framework. How does it extend PDCA into a real-time defensive posture? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1.5 Marks)**
The Adaptive Security Architecture, popularized by Gartner, partitions defense into four continuous functions: **Predict, Prevent, Detect, Respond**. Unlike PDCA's monthly/quarterly cadence, ASA operates in *near real-time* and is tightly coupled with threat intelligence and SOAR automation.

**2. Predict (1.5 Marks)**
Uses threat modeling, adversary profiling (MITRE ATT&CK for ICS), and predictive analytics to forecast likely attack paths. Outputs: prioritized patch lists, deception campaigns, and threat hunts.

**3. Prevent (1.5 Marks)**
Implements patching, hardening, segmentation, Zero-Trust, and access controls. Effective prevention raises $P_{prevention}$ in the composite effectiveness formula.

**4. Detect (1.5 Marks)**
Deploys SIEM + OT IDS + UEBA to surface anomalies. Measures success via MTTD. Detection failures feed back into the Predict function (closing the loop).

**5. Respond (1 Mark)**
SOAR playbooks automate containment (e.g., isolating a compromised engineering workstation). Success measured by MTTR and *blast-radius containment ratio*.

**6. Conclusion (1 Mark)**
ASA is essentially **PDCA running at network speed**. While PDCA governs *program-level* improvement, ASA governs *operational-level* adaptation. The two together form a layered, continuously self-correcting security organism.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the Deming cycle (PDCA) and discuss its application in CPS security. Show how a Security Operations Center (SOC) integrates the cycle. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Diagram (1.5 Marks)**
Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the mermaid PDCA flowchart. A hand-drawn version with four boxes connected cyclically (Plan → Do → Check → Act → back to Plan) is acceptable.

**2. Phase-by-Phase Explanation (3 Marks)**
- **Plan** in a SOC: threat intel ingestion, vulnerability scans, control-baseline updates.
- **Do** in a SOC: deploy detection rules (Sigma, YARA, Snort), patch OT assets, update IR playbooks.
- **Check** in a SOC: review MTTD, MTTR, false-positive rates, mean-time-to-contain (MTTC).
- **Act** in a SOC: tune detection rules, escalate lessons learned to governance, automate repetitive tasks.

**3. SOC Integration (1.5 Marks)**
- Tier-1 analysts: monitor alerts, execute playbooks (Do/Check).
- Tier-2 analysts: investigate, perform RCA (Check/Act).
- SOC Manager: aligns with governance, drives Plan inputs.
- Threat Intel team: continuously updates the Plan with external feeds.

**4. Conclusion (1 Mark)**
The SOC is the *operational heart* of the PDCA engine; without it, Plan-Do-Check-Act remains a paper exercise.

---

**(b) Describe the NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF). How is it used to measure and improve CPS security maturity over time? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. NIST CSF Overview (2 Marks)**
The NIST CSF organizes cybersecurity into **five concurrent functions**: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover. Each function contains categories and subcategories mapped to informative references (ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-53, IEC 62443).

**2. Four Implementation Tiers (2 Marks)**
- **Tier 1 – Partial:** Ad hoc, reactive.
- **Tier 2 – Risk-Informed:** Risk-aware but not organization-wide.
- **Tier 3 – Repeatable:** Formalized, documented, consistently applied.
- **Tier 4 – Adaptive:** Continuous improvement, real-time risk response.

**3. Maturity Measurement (1.5 Marks)**
The composite maturity score derived in Section 3.2:

$$M = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot L_i}{L_{max}} \times 100\%$$

A CPS site is assessed annually (or after major incidents), and $M$ is plotted on a trend graph. A rising trend confirms PDCA is working; a plateau signals a need to re-invest in training, automation, or threat intel.

**4. Continuous Improvement Application (1.5 Marks)**
- Annual assessment → identify lowest-scoring function → prioritize investment.
- Post-incident: map the kill chain to a CSF function; if a gap appears, raise that function's tier.
- Integration with PDCA: Plan sets target tiers, Do implements controls, Check measures tier achievement, Act institutionalizes the gains.

**5. Conclusion (0.5 Mark)**
NIST CSF provides the *what* (functions, categories), while PDCA provides the *how* (the cycle to improve them). Together they form the operational backbone of CPS security maturation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Master Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- ✅ **PDCA = Plan-Do-Check-Act**, originated by Shewhart/Deming — *always* the first framework to cite.
- ✅ **PDCA's purpose in CPS = institutionalize learning** from incidents, threat intel, and audits.
- ✅ **Maturity models:** NIST CSF (5 functions, 4 tiers), C2M2 (10 domains, 0–3 levels), CMMI (5 levels), OWASP SAMM.
- ✅ **Key formulas:** $ALE = SLE \times ARO$, $MTTD$, $MTTR$, $E_{\infty} = \frac{\alpha E_{max}}{\alpha + \beta}$, $M = \frac{\sum w_i L_i}{L_{max}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **ASA (Gartner) = Predict-Prevent-Detect-Respond** — the *real-time* cousin of PDCA.
- ✅ **Threat intel** (STIX/TAXII, MITRE ATT&CK for ICS) feeds the Plan phase.
- ✅ **Post-incident review (PIR)** is the *single most important* learning input.
- ✅ **MTTD ↓** and **MTTR ↓** are the two universally accepted improvement KPIs.
- ✅ **Patch coverage ratio (PCR)** must be > 90% for critical CPS assets.
- ✅ **Zero-Trust microsegmentation** raises $P_{prevention}$ and limits blast radius.
- ✅ **DevSecOps + IEC 62443-4-1** embed security into the CPS lifecycle, not bolted on.
