- ✅ **Closed-loop lifecycle:** Requirements → Dev → Operate → Maintain → Retire → Improvement Engine → back to Requirements.
- ✅ Always tie your answer back to a **specific CPS domain** (power grid, water SCADA, autonomous vehicle, medical IoT) — generic IT examples lose marks.
- ✅ Cite **both** the framework *and* the clause/subcategory (e.g., "NIST CSF DE.AE-2", "IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.7").
- ✅ Use **diagrams** wherever the question allows 7+ marks; PDCA and ASA loops are visually rewarding.
- ✅ For 14-mark questions, the magic formula is: **Definition + 4 Phases/Functions + 1 Example + 1 Metric + 1 Framework Reference + Conclusion**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Cybersecurity Standards Applicable to CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overview of Cybersecurity Standards Applicable to CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **Cybersecurity Standard for Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** is a formally documented, consensus-based, and often legally enforceable specification that prescribes a systematic set of security controls, design principles, risk management processes, testing methodologies, and operational governance practices specifically engineered to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of the cyber layer, the physical process layer, and the critical human–machine interface bridging them in a CPS.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (CO4 / Module 4):**
> Standards form the *backbone of regulatory and engineering compliance* in CPS. They are not merely checklists — they encode decades of incident lessons (Stuxnet, BlackEnergy, Triton, Mirai–Botnet, Colonial Pipeline) into reusable, auditable engineering practices.

### 1.2 Key Characteristics of CPS-Oriented Standards
- **Sector-Specific Tailoring:** Distinct guidance exists for energy, medical, automotive, manufacturing, water, and transportation CPS.
- **Lifecycle Coverage:** A standard must address the full CPS lifecycle — *concept → design → build → deploy → operate → maintain → decommission*.
- **Conformance vs. Certification:** Conformance is self-declared; certification requires an accredited third-party audit.
- **Defense-in-Depth Encoding:** Standards codify layered security (network, host, application, physical, policy).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Code" Metaphor
Think of a CPS (e.g., a smart power grid) as a **high-rise hospital building**. The cyber components (PLC, SCADA servers, IoT sensors) are the *electrical wiring, elevators, and oxygen pipelines*; the physical process is the *patients and the medical staff*. Cybersecurity standards are the **National Building Code + Fire Safety Code + Hospital Accreditation Manual** rolled into one.

Just as a building must withstand fire (resilience), resist intruders (access control), and recover quickly (incident response) — a CPS must do the same, and **standards codify exactly *how* to design, prove, and audit that capability** before occupants are allowed inside.

### 1.4 Why Standards Are Non-Negotiable in CPS

| Engineering Reality | Standard-Driven Solution |
|---|---|
| CPS failures can cause **physical harm or death** | Functional safety + cybersecurity standards (e.g., IEC 62443-3-3 SL3) |
| CPS attacks are **asymmetric** (low cost, high damage) | Prescriptive baseline controls (e.g., NERC CIP) |
| CPS supply chains are **global & opaque** | SBOM requirements, vendor risk management |
| CPS lifecycles are **20–40 years** | Patch and configuration governance standards |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory in KTU Answers:** Whenever a 14-mark question asks about CPS security, you **must cite at least one specific standard by name and clause number** (e.g., "IEC 62443-3-3 System Security Requirement 1.5 — *Accountability*) to score full marks under KTU valuation patterns.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Big Five" Standards Families Relevant to CPS
The following framework families dominate the global CPS cybersecurity landscape and are routinely tested in KTU examinations.

#### A. NIST Family (USA — Federal Baseline)
- **NIST CSF 2.0 (Cybersecurity Framework):** Govern → Identify → Protect → Detect → Respond → Recover. The *universal language* of cyber risk.
- **NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3:** *Guide to Operational Technology (OT) Security* — the de-facto CPS-specific extension of generic IT security.
- **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5:** Catalog of 1000+ controls mapped to CSF and used by US Federal CPS deployments.
- **NIST SP 800-161 Rev. 2:** *Cybersecurity Supply Chain Risk Management (C-SCRM)* — critical for CPS vendors.

#### B. IEC 62443 Family (International — Industrial Automation)
- **62443-1-1:** Concepts and models
- **62443-3-3:** System Security Requirements & Security Levels (SL 1 → SL 4)
- **62443-4-1:** Secure Product Development Lifecycle (SDLC)
- **62443-4-2:** Component Security Requirements
- Defines **Security Level (SL)** — a measure of attacker capability a system must withstand:
  - **SL 1:** Casual / coincidental
  - **SL 2:** Intentional with simple means / low resources
  - **SL 3:** Intentional with sophisticated means / moderate resources
  - **SL 4:** Intentional with sophisticated means / extensive resources (state-level)

#### C. ISO/IEC 27000 Family (Information Security Management)
- **ISO/IEC 27001:** ISMS — *Information Security Management System* (the certifiable standard).
- **ISO/IEC 27002:** Code of practice — 93 controls in 4 themes.
- **ISO/IEC 27019:** Energy-sector specific extension of 27002 for process control systems.

#### D. Sector-Specific / Regulatory Standards
- **NERC CIP (North America — Bulk Electric System):** CIP-002 through CIP-014 — *legally enforceable* in the USA/Canada.
- **IEC 62351:** Security for power system communication protocols (IEC 60870, IEC 61850, DNP3).
- **ISO/SAE 21434:** *Road vehicles — Cybersecurity engineering* (mandatory for UNECE WP.29 compliance).
- **FDA Cybersecurity in Medical Devices (2023 Premarket Guidance):** Required for US market entry of connected medical devices.
- **TSA Pipeline Security Directives:** Mandatory for US oil & gas pipeline operators post–Colonial Pipeline.
- **ENISA NIS2 Directive (EU):** *Network and Information Security Directive 2* — covers essential entities including energy, transport, health, water.

#### E. Industry / Niche Standards
- **IEEE 1686-2013:** Intelligent Electronic Device (IED) security in substations.
- **API 1164 (Pipeline SCADA Security):** Oil & gas pipeline industry.
- **CISA ICS-CERT Advisories & MITRE ATT&CK for ICS:** *Threat-informed defense* frameworks, not certifiable standards but widely adopted.

### 2.2 Core Theoretical Construct — The "Zone-Conduit" Model (IEC 62443)
A foundational concept heavily tested:

- **Zone:** A grouping of physical and logical assets sharing common security requirements.
- **Conduit:** A logical grouping of communication channels between zones.
- **Security Level (SL-T / SL-A / SL-C / SL-I):** Target / Achieved / Capability / Interest.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Framework Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Symbol / Value | Meaning / Unit | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Security Level | $SL \in \{1,2,3,4\}$ | Attacker capability threshold | Answer SL-related 7-mark sub-questions |
| Conformance Level | $C_L \in \{1,2,3,4\}$ | Zone capability rating | Map to NIST CSF tiers |
| Risk Magnitude (qualitative) | $R = L \times I$ | Likelihood × Impact | Justify SL choice |
| CSF Function Coverage | $n_{cov} \in [0,100]\%$ | % controls implemented | Scoring in compliance questions |
| MTTR (CPS context) | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum t_{recover}}{N_{incidents}}$ | Hours / incident | Service-level compliance |
| Patch Latency | $T_{patch} = t_{deploy} - t_{release}$ | Days | KPI for IEC 62443-2-4 |

> [!NOTE]
> For non-formulaic standards questions, the "formula" is the **control mapping** itself. Always memorize the mapping: *Standard X → Controls Y → CPS Layer Z*.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility
These standards are used by:
- **Smart grid operators** (NERC CIP, IEC 62351)
- **Automotive OEMs** (ISO/SAE 21434 → UNECE WP.29)
- **Medical device manufacturers** (FDA, IEC 80001-2-2)
- **Smart manufacturing / Industry 4.0 plants** (IEC 62443)
- **Water utility operators** (NIST SP 800-82, NIS2)
- **Critical infrastructure regulators** (CISA, NIS2, NERC)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mapping a CPS Architecture to Applicable Standards
A systematic KTU-style derivation of which standard applies to *which* CPS subsystem.

**Step 1 — Decompose the CPS into canonical layers.**

$$\text{CPS} = \left\{ L_{SENS}, L_{CTRL}, L_{COMM}, L_{CLOUD}, L_{PROC} \right\}$$

where $L_{SENS}$ = sensor/field layer, $L_{CTRL}$ = controller/PLC layer, $L_{COMM}$ = communication/network, $L_{CLOUD}$ = IT/enterprise, $L_{PROC}$ = physical process.

> *Textual interpretation:* Any CPS — a power substation, a self-driving car, an insulin pump — can be decomposed into these five functional layers.

**Step 2 — Identify applicable standards per layer.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{SENS} &\rightarrow \text{IEC 62443-4-2 (component), IEEE 1686} \\
L_{CTRL} &\rightarrow \text{IEC 62443-3-3 (system SL), NIST SP 800-82} \\
L_{COMM} &\rightarrow \text{IEC 62351, IEEE 802.1X, NIST SP 800-53 SC family} \\
L_{CLOUD} &\rightarrow \text{ISO/IEC 27001, NIST CSF, SOC 2} \\
L_{PROC} &\rightarrow \text{IEC 61508 (safety interlock), NIST SP 800-82} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* Each layer has a *primary* standard (the one that defines its specific controls) and one or more *complementary* standards.

**Step 3 — Compute the cross-layer coverage ratio.**

Let $C_x \in \{0,1\}$ be 1 if a standard's controls are applied at layer $x$, 0 otherwise. Then the **Standard Coverage Index (SCI)** for standard $s$ is:

$$
SCI_s = \dfrac{1}{5} \sum_{x=1}^{5} C_{x,s}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked example (Smart Grid Substation).**
Layer $\rightarrow$ IEC 62443 coverage:
- $L_{SENS} = 1$ (component-level certification)
- $L_{CTRL} = 1$ (SL-2 system)
- $L_{COMM} = 1$ (zone-conduit defined)
- $L_{CLOUD} = 0$ (not in scope of IEC 62443)
- $L_{PROC} = 0$ (covered by IEC 61508 safety, not cybersecurity)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SCI_{IEC\,62443} &= \dfrac{1 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 0}{5} = \dfrac{3}{5} = 0.60
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* IEC 62443 covers 60% of the substation's CPS layers. The remaining 40% requires **NIST CSF (cloud/enterprise)** and **IEC 61508 (safety)** — illustrating that no single standard is sufficient.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for 14-Mark Answers)

| Standard | Scope | CPS Layers Covered | Certifiable? | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NIST CSF 2.0** | Risk management framework | All (high-level) | No (self-assess) | Universal, flexible | Not prescriptive |
| **NIST SP 800-82** | OT security guidance | $L_{SENS}, L_{CTRL}, L_{COMM}$ | No | OT-specific | US-centric |
| **IEC 62443** | Industrial automation | $L_{SENS}, L_{CTRL}, L_{COMM}$ | Yes (multiple levels) | SL concept, SDLC | Implementation cost |
| **ISO/IEC 27001** | ISMS | $L_{CLOUD}$ primarily | Yes (ISO cert) | Global recognition | IT-centric, not OT-aware |
| **NERC CIP** | North American grid | All (utility-specific) | Yes (legally enforced) | Enforceable, audited | Regional |
| **IEC 62351** | Power protocols | $L_{COMM}$ only | Yes | Protocol-level | Narrow scope |
| **ISO/SAE 21434** | Automotive | All in-vehicle layers | Yes (TÜV etc.) | Vehicle lifecycle | Sector-specific |
| **FDA Premarket** | Medical devices | All medical CPS | Yes (US market) | Patient-safety focus | National |
| **ENISA NIS2** | EU critical infra | All (essential entities) | Yes (compliance) | Pan-EU, legal force | Regional |
| **CISA / MITRE ATT&CK ICS** | Threat intelligence | All (advisory) | No | Real-world TTPs | Non-prescriptive |

### 3.3 Derivation: Security Level (SL) Justification
KTU often tests the logic of *how to choose an SL* for a CPS.

**Step 1 — Identify the worst-case impact.**

$$
I = \max\left\{ I_{safety}, I_{financial}, I_{environmental}, I_{operational} \right\}
$$

**Step 2 — Assess adversary capability.**

$$
A \in \{SL_1, SL_2, SL_3, SL_4\}
$$

**Step 3 — Set Target SL.**

$$
SL_T = A \quad \text{(when impact is catastrophic: life loss, mass outage)}
$$

$$
SL_T = A - 1 \quad \text{(when impact is serious but bounded)}
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* For an insulin pump (life-critical, low attacker capability needed), $SL_T = 3$ or higher is justified. For a vending machine, $SL_T = 1$ suffices.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper 1)]**
*Define the term "Security Level (SL)" as used in IEC 62443. List all four SL values and the adversary capability each represents.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Security Level (SL)** in IEC 62443 is a measure of the robustness of a CPS zone or conduit against an intentional cyber attack, expressed as the attacker's capability the system is designed to withstand.
- **SL 1 — Casual / coincidental:** No attack skill required; protection against accidental misuse.
- **SL 2 — Intentional with simple means, low resources, and low motivation:** e.g., script kiddies.
- **SL 3 — Intentional with sophisticated means, moderate resources, and moderate motivation:** e.g., hacktivists, insider threats.
- **SL 4 — Intentional with sophisticated means, extensive resources, and high motivation:** e.g., state-sponsored APTs.

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks for listing SL1–SL4 correctly = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Retrieved Pattern)]**
*Mention any three differences between NIST CSF and IEC 62443 with respect to CPS security.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | NIST CSF | IEC 62443 |
|---|---|---|
| **Certifiability** | Voluntary self-assessment framework | Certifiable standard (multiple certification levels) |
| **Sector Focus** | Sector-agnostic, generic | Industrial automation / OT specific |
| **Structure** | 6 Functions (Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover) | 4 layers (General, Policies, System, Component) with Security Levels |
| **CPS Lifecycles** | Operational & risk focus | Full SDLC including product development (62443-4-1) |

*(1 mark per correct difference; full 3 marks for any 3 valid points)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern (PECPT413 2024 Scheme):** Module 4 carries one 14-mark compulsory OR choice question. Either Question A *or* Question B must be answered.

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Expected]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain in detail the architecture of IEC 62443, listing the key sub-standards and the role of each. What is the Zone–Conduit model and why is it central to CPS security?*
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** *A smart water-treatment plant deploys IoT sensors, a SCADA system, a cloud analytics dashboard, and a chemical dosing process. Identify the relevant cybersecurity standards applicable at each layer and justify your answer using the Standard Coverage Index (SCI) concept.*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — 7 Marks**
IEC 62443 is the global reference standard for **Industrial Automation and Control Systems (IACS)** cybersecurity, structured in four parts:
- **General (62443-1-x):** Concepts, terminology, and metrics. Establishes the foundational vocabulary such as "zone", "conduit", and "Security Level".
- **Policies and Procedures (62443-2-x):** Defines what an operator (i.e., asset owner) must do. Example: 62443-2-4 prescribes service-provider security patch obligations.
- **System (62443-3-x):** Defines requirements for a system as a whole. **62443-3-3** specifies 7 Foundational Requirements (FR 1–FR 7): Identification & Authentication Control, Use Control, System Integrity, Data Confidentiality, Restricted Data Flow, Timely Response to Events, Resource Availability.
- **Component (62443-4-x):** **62443-4-1** = Secure Development Lifecycle for vendors; **62443-4-2** = Component Security Requirements (akin to "Common Criteria" for IACS).

The **Zone–Conduit model** is the cornerstone of IEC 62443's risk-based architecture:
- A **Zone** groups CPS assets (PLCs, RTUs, sensors) sharing common security requirements.
- A **Conduit** is a communication path *between* zones, treated as a controlled boundary.
- Each zone is assigned a **Security Level Target (SL-T)** based on worst-case consequence; the **Conduit** must enforce at least the SL of the highest zone it connects.

*(4 marks architecture + 3 marks zone–conduit = 7 marks)*

**(b) — 7 Marks**
Decomposition of the water-treatment plant into CPS layers:

| Layer | Examples | Applicable Standard | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L_{SENS}$ (Sensor/Field) | pH sensors, flow meters | **IEC 62443-4-2** | Component-level security requirements |
| $L_{CTRL}$ (Control) | SCADA master, PLCs | **IEC 62443-3-3 (SL 2)** | System-level SL with FR 1–FR 7 |
| $L_{COMM}$ (Network) | Industrial Ethernet, 4G gateway | **NIST SP 800-82 §6.2** | OT network segmentation |
| $L_{CLOUD}$ (Enterprise) | Cloud analytics, mobile dashboards | **ISO/IEC 27001 + NIST CSF** | Information security + risk framework |
| $L_{PROC}$ (Physical) | Chemical dosing pumps | **IEC 61511 (functional safety) + IEC 62443-3-3** | Safety-security co-engineering |

**SCI Calculation:**
- $L_{SENS} \rightarrow 1$ (62443-4-2 applied)
- $L_{CTRL} \rightarrow 1$ (62443-3-3 applied)
- $L_{COMM} \rightarrow 1$ (NIST 800-82 applied)
- $L_{CLOUD} \rightarrow 1$ (ISO 27001 applied)
- $L_{PROC} \rightarrow 1$ (62443 + IEC 61511 applied)

$$
SCI = \dfrac{1+1+1+1+1}{5} = 1.00
$$

A coverage index of 1.00 indicates **complete cross-layer coverage** — the gold standard for a critical-infrastructure CPS such as water treatment.

*(4 marks mapping table + 2 marks SCI calculation + 1 mark conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Expected]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** *Discuss the NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) 2.0 and NIST SP 800-82 in the context of CPS. How do they complement IEC 62443?*
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** *Compare the regulatory enforcement models of NERC CIP, ENISA NIS2, and FDA Premarket Cybersecurity Guidance. Why is a single global CPS standard impractical?*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — 7 Marks**
- **NIST CSF 2.0** organizes cybersecurity into six functions: **Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover**. It is *voluntary*, *sector-agnostic*, and provides a common language between IT and OT teams.
- **NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3** specializes the generic NIST guidance for **Operational Technology (OT) / ICS**, providing overlay profiles for sectors like water, energy, and manufacturing. It adapts IT controls to OT realities (e.g., legacy Windows, deterministic protocols, no-patch windows).
- **Complementarity with IEC 62443:**
  - CSF provides the *management/governance layer*; IEC 62443 provides the *technical/engineering layer*.
  - CSF's "Identify" maps to IEC 62443-3-2 (zone & conduit risk assessment).
  - CSF's "Protect" maps to IEC 62443-3-3 (FR 1–FR 7).
  - A mature CPS organization typically uses **CSF as the executive dashboard** and **IEC 62443 as the engineering specification**.

*(3 marks CSF + 2 marks 800-82 + 2 marks complementarity = 7 marks)*

**(b) — 7 Marks**

| Dimension | NERC CIP | ENISA NIS2 | FDA Premarket |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Region** | North America (USA/Canada) | European Union | USA (medical device market) |
| **Sector** | Bulk Electric System | All critical/essential entities | Medical devices |
| **Legal Force** | Federal statute (mandatory) | EU directive (national law) | Pre-market approval gate |
| **Audit Body** | NERC + FERC | National CSIRTs | FDA reviewers |
| **Penalty** | $1M+ fines, license revocation | €10M or 2% global turnover | Refusal to market |
| **Focus** | Reliability + cybersecurity | Operational resilience | Patient safety + cyber |

**Why a single global standard is impractical:**
1. **Sectoral asymmetry** — power, medical, and automotive CPS have fundamentally different failure modes.
2. **Sovereignty and liability** — nations reserve the right to enforce their own critical infrastructure rules.
3. **Lifecycle mismatch** — automotive (15 yr) vs medical (10 yr) vs energy (40 yr) products.
4. **Cultural/legal differences** — US self-declaration vs EU conformity assessment vs China's MLPS 2.0.
5. **Innovation pace** — a single slow standard would lag attacker TTPs.

*(4 marks comparison + 3 marks "impractical" justification = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Naming standards without context:** Writing "ISO 27001" without explaining *which clause* (e.g., A.5.15 Access Control) earns *partial* credit only. **Always cite the clause number.**
> 2. **Conflating IT and OT:** Applying ISO 27001 controls verbatim to a PLC is wrong — show awareness of OT-specific overlays (NIST 800-82).
> 3. **Ignoring safety-security interaction:** CPS standards MUST be discussed alongside functional safety (IEC 61508/61511). Forgetting this loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **No diagram in 14-mark answers:** KTU examiners expect *at least one* labeled diagram or table in 7-mark sub-questions. Always include a mapping table.
> 5. **Missing sector context:** "CPS security" is too generic — specify *power grid*, *medical device*, or *connected vehicle* to score high.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **CPS security is governed by a *family* of standards, not a single document** — know the "Big Five": NIST, IEC 62443, ISO 27001, NERC CIP, sector-specific.
- ✅ **IEC 62443 is the engineering backbone** for industrial CPS; it has 4 parts (1-x general, 2-x policies, 3-x system, 4-x component).
- ✅ **Security Levels (SL 1 → SL 4)** quantify adversary capability; choose SL-T based on worst-case impact.
- ✅ **Zone–Conduit model** is the central architectural construct of IEC 62443.
- ✅ **NIST CSF 2.0** adds the *Govern* function — a top-level management layer.
- ✅ **NIST SP 800-82** is the OT-specific extension — essential for ICS/SCADA/PLC deployments.
- ✅ **Sector-specific standards** to memorize: NERC CIP (energy), ISO/SAE 21434 (auto), FDA (medical), IEC 62351 (power protocols), ENISA NIS2 (EU critical infra), API 1164 (oil & gas).
- ✅ **No single global standard** exists — sovereignty, sector asymmetry, and lifecycle mismatch prevent it.
- ✅ **Always pair security with safety** (IEC 61508/61511) when discussing CPS.
- ✅ **For KTU answers:** cite *Standard name + clause number + CPS layer + justification* in every 7-mark sub-question.
- ✅ **Key acronyms to define in any exam:** SL, IACS, OT, SCADA, PLC, ISMS, C-SCRM, FR (Foundational Requirement), TTP, APT.
- ✅ **One formula to remember:** $SCI_s = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{x=1}^{5} C_{x,s}$ — a high-yield derivation for 14-mark answers.
- ✅ **Three real-world incidents** worth referencing for full marks: Stuxnet (Iran, 2010 — IEC 62443 relevance), BlackEnergy (Ukraine, 2015 — NERC CIP), Triton (Saudi Arabia, 2017 — safety-system bypass).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compliance Considerations in CPS Deployments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT413 - SECURITY OF CYBER-PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Compliance Considerations in CPS Deployments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Compliance** in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) refers to the systematic adherence of a CPS deployment to a defined set of regulatory, industry, organizational, and technical standards, laws, and policies that govern its design, development, operation, maintenance, and decommissioning. It is the process of ensuring that a CPS meets all **mandatory** and **recommended** security, safety, privacy, and operational requirements imposed by external regulators, standard-setting bodies, and internal governance.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with the PECPT413 syllabus, compliance is treated as a **multi-dimensional control discipline** that intersects:

- **Legal/Regulatory compliance** — statutes, government mandates (e.g., GDPR, IT Act 2000, DPDP Act 2023, NIS2 Directive).
- **Industry/Technical compliance** — domain-specific standards (e.g., IEC 62443, NIST CSF 2.0, NERC CIP, ISO/IEC 27001:2022, ISO 21434, IEC 61508).
- **Contractual compliance** — service-level agreements, vendor obligations, customer-mandated controls.
- **Organizational/Internal compliance** — corporate security policies, board-level governance directives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Compliance is **not** equivalent to security. A CPS can be secure but non-compliant, and conversely, a compliant CPS may still have residual security gaps. Compliance = **demonstrable evidence of conformance** to a specified control baseline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Distinction:**
> - **Security** = *Are we protected?* (technical capability)
> - **Compliance** = *Can we prove we are doing what is required?* (documentary and procedural evidence)
> - **Governance** = *Who is accountable and how are decisions made?* (oversight structure)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Driving License Analogy

Imagine you have built a powerful car (your **CPS**). Before you are allowed to drive it on public roads, you must:

1. **Register** the vehicle (analogous to **asset registration** under compliance).
2. **Pass safety tests** — brakes, lights, emissions (analogous to **security & safety control testing**).
3. **Get a fitness certificate** every year (analogous to **periodic audits**).
4. **Carry proof documents** — insurance, PUC, license (analogous to **compliance evidence/audit trails**).
5. **Follow traffic rules** — speed limits, lane discipline (analogous to **continuous policy enforcement**).

If you skip any of these, the car is still drivable on a private road, but **illegal on public roads**. Similarly, a CPS can function internally but become **non-compliant** the moment it interfaces with regulated environments — patients (healthcare), power grids (energy), financial transactions (banking), or international data flows.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Intuition Check:** If a SCADA system controlling a water treatment plant is hacked but has all audit logs and policy documents in place, it is **compliant but breached**. If it has no audit trail but no attack occurred, it is **insecure but possibly compliant on paper**. Compliance = paper trail; Security = actual protection.

## 1.3 Why Compliance is a First-Class Concern in CPS

CPS are unique because they bridge two regulatory worlds:

| Domain | Regulatory Concern | Example Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical/Safety** | Human life, environmental damage | IEC 61508, ISO 26262, FDA 21 CFR Part 820 |
| **Cyber/Information** | Data, privacy, intellectual property | GDPR, DPDP Act 2023, HIPAA |
| **Operational** | Critical infrastructure continuity | NERC CIP, NIS2, ISA/IEC 62443 |
| **Sectoral** | Industry-specific mandates | PCI-DSS (payments), FAA Part 25 (avionics) |

A non-compliant CPS deployment can attract **financial penalties**, **license revocations**, **criminal liability of officers**, **contractual damages**, and **reputational ruin** — even without a single cyber-incident.

## 1.4 Compliance Metrics and Standard Boundaries

Common quantitative compliance metrics used in industry:

- **Compliance Rate (CR)** — Percentage of controls fully implemented:

$$CR = \frac{\text{Number of Compliant Controls}}{\text{Total Number of Applicable Controls}} \times 100\%$$

- **Audit Finding Closure Time (AFCT)** — Average days to remediate a non-conformity.
- **Mean Time to Compliance (MTTC)** — Time from policy issuance to full organizational conformance.
- **Control Coverage Ratio (CCR)** — Fraction of critical assets covered by the applicable control set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard Benchmark:** Most regulated industries target a **Compliance Rate ≥ 95%** for mandatory controls, with **100% coverage** required for safety-critical and life-critical controls (per IEC 62443-3-3 SL-3 and above).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Compliance Lifecycle in CPS Deployments

Compliance is not a one-time event — it follows a **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle aligned with ISO governance best practices. For CPS specifically, this lifecycle is **continuous and adaptive** because:

- Threats evolve (e.g., new ICS malware like PIPEDREAM, TRITON).
- Standards get revised (e.g., NIST CSF 1.1 → 2.0 in 2024).
- System configurations change (patching, new sensors, network additions).
- Regulatory environments shift (e.g., EU NIS2 superseding NIS Directive in 2024).

### Lifecycle Phases (KTU Board-Examiner Favourite)

**Phase 1 — Identify (Plan)**
- Inventory CPS assets (OT/IT/IoT components, PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, sensors, actuators).
- Map applicable laws, regulations, and standards to each asset class.
- Define the **compliance scope** — the boundary of the audit universe.
- Identify the **compliance owner** for each control family.

**Phase 2 — Design (Plan)**
- Map controls to CPS architecture layers (Perception → Network → Control → Application).
- Document policies, procedures, baselines.
- Perform **gap analysis** vs. the target compliance posture.
- Build a **Compliance Risk Register**.

**Phase 3 — Implement (Do)**
- Deploy technical controls (firewalls, ACLs, RBAC, MFA, encryption).
- Deploy procedural controls (training, change management, incident response).
- Collect **evidence artifacts** — configurations, logs, signed approvals.
- Integrate compliance into **DevSecOps pipelines** for CPS software components.

**Phase 4 — Assess (Check)**
- Internal audits, vulnerability scans, penetration tests.
- Third-party certification audits (ISO 27001, SOC 2, IEC 62443-2-4).
- Continuous compliance monitoring via GRC platforms.
- Regulatory examinations.

**Phase 5 — Remediate & Improve (Act)**
- Close audit findings within **agreed SLAs**.
- Update policies and controls based on lessons learned.
- Report to **board / CISO / regulator** as mandated.
- Feed improvements back into Phase 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "explain the compliance lifecycle", always reference **PDCA** explicitly. Examiners reward students who use the formal cycle names.

## 2.2 Key Compliance Frameworks for CPS (Exam-Relevant Matrix)

| Framework | Domain | Scope | Mandatory? | Issuing Body |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NIST CSF 2.0** | Cross-sector cyber | Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover + **Govern** (new) | Voluntary baseline | NIST, USA |
| **NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3** | OT/ICS | CPS-specific security guide | Voluntary, de-facto | NIST |
| **IEC 62443** | Industrial Automation & Control Systems (IACS) | Zones, conduits, SL-T, SR | Industry-mandatory | ISA / IEC |
| **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5** | Federal information systems | 1000+ control catalog | US Federal mandatory | NIST |
| **NERC CIP-002 through CIP-014** | Bulk Electric System (North America) | Power grid | **Legally mandatory** | FERC / NERC |
| **ISO/IEC 27001:2022** | Information Security Management | ISMS framework | Certifiable | ISO / IEC |
| **ISO/SAE 21434** | Automotive cybersecurity | Vehicle lifecycle | Industry mandatory (OEMs) | ISO / SAE |
| **IEC 61508 / 61511** | Functional Safety (SIS) | Safety instrumented systems | Mandatory in process industries | IEC |
| **ISO 26262** | Automotive functional safety | E/E systems in road vehicles | Mandatory for ASIL-rated systems | ISO |
| **FDA 21 CFR Part 820** | Medical device quality | QSR for medical CPS | US Federal mandatory | US FDA |
| **HIPAA** | Healthcare PHI | Medical data handling | US Federal mandatory | HHS |
| **GDPR / DPDP Act 2023** | Personal data | Data subject rights, cross-border | Mandatory in EU / India | EU / Govt. of India |
| **PCI-DSS 4.0** | Payment card data | Cardholder data environment | Contractually mandatory | PCI SSC |
| **NIS2 Directive (2022/2555)** | Essential & Important Entities | EU-wide baseline cybersecurity | **Legally mandatory** in EU since Oct 2024 | European Union |
| **IT Act 2000 + Cert-In Directions 2022** | Indian cyber law | Logging, incident reporting | **Legally mandatory** in India | MeitY / Cert-In |

## 2.3 Compliance Assessment Methodology — The Five-Step Decomposition

A **compliance assessment** is a structured evaluation that determines the degree to which a CPS deployment conforms to a specified baseline. The KTU-aligned methodology is:

**Step 1 — Define the Assessment Scope**
- Identify the **system under assessment (SUA)**, its boundaries, and the **target compliance baseline**.

**Step 2 — Select the Control Set**
- Pick the relevant control catalog (e.g., IEC 62443-3-3 System Security Requirements).
- Filter to **applicable controls** based on the SUA's risk profile and regulatory environment.

**Step 3 — Evidence Collection**
- Gather objective evidence through:
  - Document review (policies, configs, network diagrams).
  - Technical testing (scans, configuration audits).
  - Personnel interviews (operators, engineers, CISO).
  - Observation (physical walkthrough of OT environment).

**Step 4 — Control Evaluation**
- For each control, classify the implementation status:
  - **Compliant (C)** — Implemented, effective, evidenced.
  - **Partially Compliant (PC)** — Implemented but with gaps.
  - **Non-Compliant (NC)** — Not implemented or ineffective.
  - **Not Applicable (NA)** — Justified exclusion.

**Step 5 — Reporting and Remediation Planning**
- Produce a **Statement of Applicability (SoA)**.
- Issue a **Corrective Action Plan (CAP)** with deadlines and owners.
- Trigger continuous monitoring cadence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula:** The **Compliance Rate (CR)** is the master metric used in nearly all audit reports:

$$CR = \frac{C + (0.5 \times PC)}{C + PC + NC} \times 100\%$$

This formula gives **partial credit** for partially compliant controls — a real-world practice recognized by ISO/IEC 27001 lead auditors.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Compliance Rate (CR) | $CR = \dfrac{\#\text{Compliant Controls}}{\#\text{Applicable Controls}} \times 100\%$ | Audit scoring |
| Weighted Compliance Rate | $WCR = \dfrac{\sum (w_i \times s_i)}{\sum w_i}$ where $w_i$ is control criticality and $s_i \in \{0, 0.5, 1\}$ | Risk-prioritized audits |
| Residual Compliance Risk | $RCR = P_{\text{audit failure}} \times I_{\text{non-compliance}}$ | Compliance risk register |
| MTTC (Mean Time to Compliance) | $MTTC = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (t_{\text{close},i} - t_{\text{issue},i})$ | Program effectiveness |
| Audit Coverage Ratio | $ACR = \dfrac{\#\text{audited assets}}{\#\text{in-scope assets}} \times 100\%$ | Audit scope completeness |
| Penalty Exposure (financial) | $PE = \sum_{j} (V_j \times P_j)$ where $V_j$ = violation value, $P_j$ = probability | Board-level risk reporting |
| GDPR Fine Ceiling | $\max\{\, \text{€20M},\ 4\% \text{ global turnover} \,\}$ | Privacy compliance |
| HIPAA Penalty Tier (max) | **$1.5M per violation / year** (per category) | US healthcare |
| NERC CIP Penalty Cap | **$1M per violation per day** | North American grid |
| IEC 62443 Security Level | $\text{SL-T} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ — Target / Achieved / Capability | IACS conformance |
| Functional Safety Integrity Level | $\text{SIL} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ (IEC 61508) | Process industry |
| Automotive SIL Equivalent | $\text{ASIL} \in \{\text{QM},\ \text{A},\ \text{B},\ \text{C},\ \text{D}\}$ (ISO 26262) | Vehicle systems |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **SL-T (Security Level Target)** from IEC 62443 with **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** from IEC 61508. They are **different dimensions**: SL-T measures **security strength**; SIL measures **safety risk reduction**. A CPS can have SL-T = 3 and SIL = 2 simultaneously.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grids** use NERC CIP compliance evidence to keep operating licenses.
- **Automotive OEMs** use ISO/SAE 21434 work products to obtain type approval in the EU (UNECE WP.29 R155).
- **Medical device manufacturers** must demonstrate FDA 21 CFR Part 820 + IEC 62304 + ISO 14971 to obtain 510(k) clearance.
- **Critical Infrastructure Operators** under EU NIS2 must appoint a CISO at management level and report incidents within **24 hours (early warning)** and **72 hours (notification)**.
- **Indian CPS operators** must comply with Cert-In Directions 2022: log retention for **180 days**, incident reporting within **6 hours**, and enable ID synchronization with Cert-In servers.

## 2.6 Compliance-as-Code (Modern CPS Practice)

Emerging practice in Industry 4.0 environments is **Compliance-as-Code (CaC)** — expressing compliance controls as machine-readable policies (e.g., Open Policy Agent, OPA; HashiCorp Sentinel; AWS Config rules). For CPS, CaC enables:

- **Automated evidence collection** from SCADA historians, PLC configurations, firewall rules.
- **Continuous compliance** rather than point-in-time audits.
- **Drift detection** — alert when a deployed configuration deviates from the compliance baseline.

This aligns with the KTU 2024 emphasis on **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** — measurable, demonstrable outcomes rather than procedural conformance.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Compliance Rate and the Weighted Compliance Rate

Let us derive the two principal compliance metrics from first principles, because KTU examiners often ask for the **justification** of formulas, not just their statement.

### 3.1.1 The Basic Compliance Rate

**Step 1 — Define the control universe.**
Let $A = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots, a_n\}$ be the finite, non-empty set of **applicable controls** identified in scope for the CPS deployment. Here $n = \vert A \vert$ is the cardinality of the applicable-control set.

**Step 2 — Define the compliance state function.**
For each control $a_i \in A$, we define a state function $s(a_i)$ whose value is determined by the auditor's evaluation:

$$s(a_i) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } a_i \text{ is Compliant (C)} \\ 0.5 & \text{if } a_i \text{ is Partially Compliant (PC)} \\ 0 & \text{if } a_i \text{ is Non-Compliant (NC)} \end{cases}$$

This state function is **objective-evidence based** and is independent of the auditor's subjective preference.

**Step 3 — Compute the aggregate score.**
The total compliance score of the deployment is the sum of all per-control states:

$$S_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} s(a_i)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Compliance Rate.**
The Compliance Rate is the total score normalized by the maximum possible score (i.e., the case where every control is fully compliant, giving $s(a_i) = 1$ for all $i$):

$$CR = \frac{S_{\text{total}}}{n} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} s(a_i)$$

Substituting the three possible states explicitly:

$$CR = \frac{1 \cdot N_C + 0.5 \cdot N_{PC} + 0 \cdot N_{NC}}{N_C + N_{PC} + N_{NC}}$$

where $N_C$, $N_{PC}$, and $N_{NC}$ are the number of controls in each respective state, and $N_C + N_{PC} + N_{NC} = n$.

**Step 5 — Convert to percentage (industry convention).**
To express the rate as a percentage:

$$CR_{\%} = CR \times 100\% = \frac{N_C + 0.5 \, N_{PC}}{n} \times 100\%$$

This is the **industry-standard Compliance Rate** used in ISO 27001 audit reports, NERC CIP self-reports, and SOC 2 attestation reports.

---

### 3.1.2 The Weighted Compliance Rate (WCR)

**Step 1 — Assign criticality weights.**
Not all controls carry equal consequence. A control protecting a **safety-critical** function (e.g., emergency shutdown of a chemical reactor) should be weighted far more heavily than a control protecting a non-critical administrative log retention. We assign a criticality weight $w_i \in [1, 10]$ to each applicable control $a_i$ (the scale is an industry convention; some frameworks use 1–5).

**Step 2 — Define the weighted score.**
The total weighted score is:

$$S_w = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s(a_i)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum possible weighted score.**
The maximum occurs when every control is fully compliant ($s(a_i) = 1$ for all $i$):

$$S_{w,\max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i$$

**Step 4 — Compute WCR.**

$$WCR = \frac{S_w}{S_{w,\max}} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s(a_i)}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$

In expanded form:

$$WCR = \frac{\sum_{i \in C} w_i + 0.5 \sum_{i \in PC} w_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$

**Step 5 — Real-world interpretation.**
A CPS may report a high *unweighted* CR (e.g., 90%) but a low *weighted* CR (e.g., 60%) if its non-compliant controls happen to be the most safety-critical ones. WCR is the metric regulators use for **risk-based supervision**.

---

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard)

**Problem.** A CPS deployment is assessed against **10 applicable controls** drawn from IEC 62443-3-3. The audit results are:

| Control ID | Description | Criticality $w_i$ | State | $s(a_i)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | Identification & authentication | 8 | Compliant | 1.0 |
| C2 | Account management | 7 | Compliant | 1.0 |
| C3 | Software/Information integrity | 10 | Partially Compliant | 0.5 |
| C4 | Data confidentiality | 9 | Compliant | 1.0 |
| C5 | Restricted data flow | 6 | Non-Compliant | 0.0 |
| C6 | Timely response to events | 8 | Partially Compliant | 0.5 |
| C7 | Resource availability | 7 | Compliant | 1.0 |
| C8 | Backup & recovery | 5 | Compliant | 1.0 |
| C9 | Network segmentation | 9 | Non-Compliant | 0.0 |
| C10 | Audit log generation | 4 | Compliant | 1.0 |

**Step A — Compute unweighted CR.**

$$CR = \frac{(1 + 1 + 0.5 + 1 + 0 + 0.5 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 1)}{10} = \frac{7}{10} = 0.70 = 70\%$$

**Step B — Compute weighted CR.**

First, sum of all weights:
$$\sum w_i = 8 + 7 + 10 + 9 + 6 + 8 + 7 + 5 + 9 + 4 = 73$$

Next, weighted sum of compliance states:
$$\sum w_i \cdot s(a_i) = (8)(1) + (7)(1) + (10)(0.5) + (9)(1) + (6)(0) + (8)(0.5) + (7)(1) + (5)(1) + (9)(0) + (4)(1)$$

Computing each term:

$$= 8 + 7 + 5 + 9 + 0 + 4 + 7 + 5 + 0 + 4 = 49$$

Therefore:

$$WCR = \frac{49}{73} \approx 0.6712 = 67.12\%$$

**Step C — Interpret.**

Even though the unweighted CR is **70%**, the weighted CR is **67.12%** because the non-compliant and partially compliant controls (C3, C5, C6, C9) have **above-average criticality weights**. The deployment is **more non-compliant** than a naive percentage suggests.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** If an exam question gives you a table of controls with weights and states, always compute **both** CR and WCR, and explicitly state that they differ — then explain **why** they differ in terms of the criticality of the failed controls.

---

### 3.1.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping CPS Layers to Compliance Domains

| CPS Layer (NIST CPS Framework) | Typical CPS Components | Compliance Frameworks Most Relevant | Typical Control Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Perception (Sensing)** | Sensors, RFID, smart meters, cameras | ISO 27001 (A.8 assets), GDPR (if personal data) | Device authentication, sensor data integrity |
| **Communication (Network)** | Industrial buses, MQTT, 5G, TSN, OPC-UA | IEC 62443-3-3, NIST 800-82, NERC CIP-005 | Segmentation, encryption-in-transit, DPI |
| **Control (Processing)** | PLCs, RTUs, SCADA, DCS, edge controllers | IEC 62443-3-3 SL-2/3, IEC 61508/61511, NERC CIP-007 | Access control, logging, patch management |
| **Application (Business Logic)** | HMIs, historians, MES, analytics platforms | ISO 27001 A.14, GDPR, sectoral mandates (HIPAA, PCI-DSS) | SDLC security, vulnerability management, DPIA |
| **Actuation (Physical Effect)** | Motors, valves, breakers, robotic arms | IEC 61508 SIL, ISO 26262 ASIL, FDA 21 CFR Part 820 | Safety interlocks, fail-safe modes, validation testing |
| **Cross-cutting Governance** | Policies, risk management, audit | NIST CSF 2.0 (Govern), ISO 27001, NIS2 | CISO appointment, supply chain risk, incident reporting |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question Pattern:** *"Map the layers of a CPS architecture to the applicable compliance frameworks."* Use the matrix above as your answer skeleton.

---

### 3.1.5 Penalty Exposure Calculation — Derivation

The **Penalty Exposure (PE)** of a non-compliant CPS deployment is the expected financial loss due to regulatory or contractual penalties.

**Step 1 — Identify the applicable violation categories.**
Let $J = \{j_1, j_2, \ldots, j_m\}$ be the set of distinct violation categories (e.g., GDPR Art. 32, NERC CIP-007, HIPAA Security Rule).

**Step 2 — For each category, identify the penalty value and the probability of enforcement.**
For each $j_k \in J$:
- $V_k$ = maximum statutory or contractual penalty value for category $j_k$.
- $P_k$ = probability that the regulator/auditor enforces this penalty (estimated via historical enforcement data, risk register, or actuarial analysis).

**Step 3 — Compute per-category expected loss.**

$$E_k = V_k \cdot P_k$$

**Step 4 — Sum across all categories** (assuming independence for first-order estimation):

$$PE = \sum_{k=1}^{m} E_k = \sum_{k=1}^{m} V_k \cdot P_k$$

**Step 5 — Risk-adjusted view.**
If we want to incorporate the **likelihood of being audited** in a given year, $P_{\text{audit}}$:

$$PE_{\text{annual}} = P_{\text{audit}} \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{m} V_k \cdot P_k$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in CPS:** Board members and CFOs understand money. Expressing compliance gaps in **rupees of penalty exposure** converts a technical problem into a **financial risk** that the board is legally obliged to consider under fiduciary duty.

---

## 3.2 The Compliance Risk Register (Analytical Tool)

A **Compliance Risk Register** is the central artifact of a CPS compliance program. It contains, for every identified compliance risk:

1. **Risk ID** — unique identifier (e.g., CR-2024-001).
2. **Description** — what the gap is.
3. **Affected Control(s)** — mapping to the framework.
4. **Affected CPS Component(s)** — PLC, HMI, network segment.
5. **Inherent Risk Rating** — Likelihood × Impact (typically 1–5 scale each).
6. **Residual Risk Rating** — after existing controls.
7. **Mitigation Plan** — actions, owners, deadlines.
8. **Target Closure Date**.
9. **Status** — Open, In Progress, Closed, Accepted.

**Risk Score Formula:**

$$RS = L \times I$$

where $L \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ (Likelihood: Rare → Almost Certain) and $I \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ (Impact: Negligible → Catastrophic).

Risk categories based on $RS$:

| Risk Score | Category | Treatment |
|---|---|---|
| 1 – 4 | **Low** | Monitor, periodic review |
| 5 – 9 | **Medium** | Plan mitigation within 6 months |
| 10 – 15 | **High** | Active mitigation, monthly reporting |
| 16 – 25 | **Critical** | Immediate escalation, executive sponsor |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Common Question:** *"How do you prioritize compliance remediation in a CPS deployment with limited resources?"* — Answer with the **Weighted Compliance Rate** + **Risk Register** combined approach: focus on (a) high-weight non-compliant controls and (b) critical safety/privacy risks.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CPS Compliance Management Lifecycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([CPS Deployment Initiated]) --> identifyA[Identify Applicable Regulations]
    identifyA --> scopeA[Define Compliance Scope and Baseline]
    scopeA --> policyA[Author Policies and Procedures]
    policyA --> controlsA[Implement Technical and Procedural Controls]
    controlsA --> evidenceA[Collect Compliance Evidence Artifacts]
    evidenceA --> auditA[Conduct Internal and External Audits]
    auditA --> assessA[Assess Control Effectiveness]
    assessA --> decisionA{Compliant?}
    decisionA -- Yes --> certA[Obtain Certification or Attestation]
    decisionA -- No --> gapA[Document Gaps in Compliance Risk Register]
    gapA --> remediateA[Execute Corrective Action Plan]
    remediateA --> reassessA[Re-audit After Closure]
    reassessA --> auditA
    certA --> monitorA[Continuous Compliance Monitoring]
    monitorA --> reviewA[Periodic Management Review]
    reviewA --> identifyA
    monitorA --> reportA[Report to Board and Regulators]
    reportA --> monitorA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flowchart is cyclic. The feedback loop from `reassessA` back to `auditA` is the **PDCA "Check"** stage. The outer loop from `monitorA` back to `identifyA` represents the **annual policy refresh** cycle. This is the standard representation in KTU model-answer sheets.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
**Q: Define "Compliance" in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. How is it different from "Security"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Compliance in CPS refers to the **demonstrable adherence** of the system to a defined set of **regulatory, industry, organizational, and technical standards** that govern its design, operation, and maintenance. (1 mark)

It is different from security in that:
- **Security** is the *technical and procedural capability* to protect the CPS from threats, vulnerabilities, and attacks. (1 mark)
- **Compliance** is the *documented evidence* that the CPS conforms to a specified control baseline mandated by external regulators or internal policy. (1 mark)

A CPS may be **secure but non-compliant** (e.g., strong crypto not yet certified by FIPS 140-3) and **compliant but insecure** (e.g., all required audit logs present but an unpatched zero-day exists).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: List any three mandatory compliance requirements applicable to CPS deployments in India.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Information Technology Act, 2000** — Governs cyber offences, data protection (legacy), and digital signatures applicable to CPS handling electronic records. (1 mark)

2. **Cert-In Directions 2022** — Mandatory for all entities, including CPS operators, to (a) retain logs for **180 days**, (b) report cyber incidents within **6 hours**, and (c) enable synchronization with Cert-In's network time protocol. (1 mark)

3. **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act)** — Requires CPS that process personal data (e.g., smart-city surveillance, connected health devices) to implement consent, purpose limitation, data minimization, breach notification, and cross-border transfer controls. (1 mark)

> [!NOTE]
> Any **three valid statutory or sectoral mandates** that apply to CPS in India earn full marks. Acceptable alternatives: sector-specific RBI mandates for CPS in BFSI, BIS standards for smart meters, CEA regulations for grid cybersecurity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** In Part B questions, students must answer **either** Choice A **or** Choice B in full. Each choice has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Solutions below are exhaustive model answers.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional Model Paper]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) compliance lifecycle** as applied to a CPS deployment. Identify the **key deliverables** at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **IEC 62443 framework** in detail — its structure, the concept of **Security Levels (SL-T, SL-A, SL-C)**, and the role of **zones and conduits** in CPS security and compliance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — The PDCA Compliance Lifecycle for CPS

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The PDCA cycle, also known as the Deming cycle, is the universal governance loop recommended by ISO/IEC 27001:2022 and adopted in CPS compliance programs for continuous improvement.

**PLAN Stage (2 marks):**
- **Identify** the regulatory landscape applicable to the CPS — e.g., IEC 62443, NIST CSF 2.0, GDPR, sectoral standards.
- **Define scope** — physical and logical boundaries of the CPS in scope.
- **Risk Assessment** — identify threats, vulnerabilities, and impacts to determine the target security level (SL-T).
- **Select Controls** — pick the relevant control baseline (e.g., IEC 62443-3-3 SL-2).
- **Documentation** — policies, Statement of Applicability (SoA), Risk Treatment Plan.

**DO Stage (2 marks):**
- **Implement** the selected technical controls (firewalls, RBAC, MFA, patch management, network segmentation).
- **Implement** procedural controls (training, change management, backup, incident response).
- **Collect Evidence** — configurations, screenshots, signed approvals, audit logs.
- **Operate** the CPS under the documented controls.

**CHECK Stage (1 mark):**
- **Monitor** control effectiveness continuously.
- **Internal audits** and **vulnerability assessments**.
- **Compliance Rate (CR)** measurement and reporting.
- **Compliance Risk Register** updates.

**ACT Stage (1 mark):**
- **Corrective actions** for non-conformities.
- **Update policies and controls** based on audit findings and incidents.
- **Management review** and **continuous improvement**.
- **Re-audit** to verify closure of gaps.

**Conclusion (extra credit):** The PDCA cycle ensures that CPS compliance is **not a one-time exercise** but a continuously improving discipline aligned with evolving threats and regulations.

#### Model Solution to (b) — IEC 62443 Deep Dive

**Introduction (1 mark):**
IEC 62443 is the **de-facto global standard** for Industrial Automation and Control Systems (IACS) cybersecurity. It is published by the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) in partnership with the International Society of Automation (ISA).

**Structure of the Standard (2 marks):**
The standard is organized into four series:
- **General (62443-1-x)** — Concepts, models, terminology.
- **Policies and Procedures (62443-2-x)** — Security program requirements for IACS service providers and asset owners.
- **System Requirements (62443-3-x)** — Security technology requirements at the system level (famous 62443-3-3 with 7 Foundational Requirements).
- **Component Requirements (62443-4-x)** — Security requirements for embedded devices, hosts, and network components.

**The 7 Foundational Requirements (FR) of IEC 62443-3-3 (1 mark):**
1. **FR 1 — Identification and Authentication Control**
2. **FR 2 — Use Control**
3. **FR 3 — System Integrity**
4. **FR 4 — Data Confidentiality**
5. **FR 5 — Restricted Data Flow**
6. **FR 6 — Timely Response to Events**
7. **FR 7 — Resource Availability**

**Security Levels (2 marks):**
IEC 62443 defines **three Security Level concepts**:
- **SL-T (Target)** — The desired security strength for a zone, set by the asset owner based on risk assessment.
- **SL-A (Achieved)** — The security strength actually delivered by the implemented controls, measured during audit.
- **SL-C (Capability)** — The security strength a control (e.g., a firewall) can provide on its own.

The standard defines **four numeric levels**:
- **SL-1** — Casual / coincidental attack.
- **SL-2** — Intentional attack with simple means, low resources.
- **SL-3** — Intentional attack with sophisticated means, moderate resources.
- **SL-4** — Intentional attack with sophisticated means, extensive resources, nation-state level.

**Zones and Conduits (1 mark):**
- A **Zone** is a grouping of physical and logical assets that share common security requirements. Example: all PLCs controlling Boiler-1 form Zone B1.
- A **Conduit** is a communication path between two zones, treated as a security boundary. Example: the firewall-controlled link between Zone B1 and the SCADA Zone S1.
- All traffic crossing a conduit must pass through a **security control** whose SL-C ≥ SL-T of the destination zone.

**Conclusion:** IEC 62443 enables CPS operators to demonstrate **risk-based, defense-in-depth compliance** that scales across the entire industrial environment.

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q: (a)** Discuss the **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) version 2.0**. Highlight the new **"Govern" function** introduced in 2024 and explain how each function applies to CPS deployments. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CPS deployment in a power utility is subject to **NERC CIP standards**. The compliance team has assessed 12 applicable controls. The audit results are:

| Control | Criticality $w_i$ | State |
|---|---|---|
| CIP-002 BES Cyber System Categorization | 9 | Compliant |
| CIP-003 Security Management Controls | 8 | Compliant |
| CIP-004 Personnel and Training | 7 | Partially Compliant |
| CIP-005 Electronic Security Perimeter | 10 | Non-Compliant |
| CIP-006 Physical Security | 8 | Compliant |
| CIP-007 System Security Management | 9 | Partially Compliant |
| CIP-008 Incident Reporting and Response | 6 | Compliant |
| CIP-009 Recovery Plans | 8 | Compliant |
| CIP-010 Configuration Change Management | 9 | Non-Compliant |
| CIP-011 Information Protection | 7 | Compliant |
| CIP-013 Supply Chain Risk Management | 6 | Partially Compliant |
| CIP-014 Physical Security (Substations) | 5 | Compliant |

**Compute the (i) unweighted Compliance Rate and (ii) Weighted Compliance Rate. Identify the top-3 highest-priority remediation targets.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — NIST CSF 2.0 and the "Govern" Function

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF), originally released in 2014 and updated to **version 2.0** in **February 2024**, is a voluntary risk-based framework widely adopted across industries, including CPS, for organizing cybersecurity activities.

**The Six Functions of CSF 2.0 (2.5 marks):**
1. **GOVERN (GV)** — *New in 2.0.* The cross-cutting function that establishes and monitors the organization's cybersecurity risk management strategy, policies, roles, and supply chain risk. Promoted to a top-tier function to emphasize that **governance is foundational** to all other security activities.
2. **IDENTIFY (ID)** — Develop an organizational understanding of cybersecurity risk to systems, people, assets, data, and capabilities. For CPS: asset inventory of PLCs, RTUs, sensors, network segments; risk assessment; supply chain mapping.
3. **PROTECT (PR)** — Implement appropriate safeguards. For CPS: access control, training, data security, protective technology, configuration management, secure configuration baselines.
4. **DETECT (DE)** — Implement activities to identify the occurrence of a cybersecurity event. For CPS: continuous monitoring, anomaly detection in OT traffic, security event logging.
5. **RESPOND (RS)** — Take action regarding a detected cybersecurity incident. For CPS: incident response plans, communications, analysis, mitigation, and improvement.
6. **RECOVER (RC)** — Maintain plans for resilience and restoration. For CPS: recovery planning, improvements, and communication.

**The New "Govern" Function in Detail (1.5 marks):**
The Govern function was elevated from a sub-category under Identify to a standalone function. It addresses:
- **Organizational Context (GV.OC)** — Mission, stakeholder expectations, legal/regulatory requirements.
- **Risk Management Strategy (GV.RM)** — Risk tolerance, risk appetite, integration with enterprise risk.
- **Roles and Responsibilities (GV.RR)** — Clear assignment of cybersecurity accountability at the executive level (e.g., CISO, board cyber committee).
- **Policies and Procedures (GV.PO)** — Documented, communicated, reviewed.
- **Oversight (GV.OV)** — Performance evaluation, independent review.
- **Cybersecurity Supply Chain Risk Management (GV.SC)** — Vendor assessments, third-party risk.

**Application to CPS (2 marks):**
- For a **smart grid**, Govern function ensures the utility's CISO has board-level authority and that OT/IT cybersecurity is integrated with enterprise risk.
- For a **connected medical device manufacturer**, Govern function mandates that security risk is part of the Quality Management System per FDA QSR.
- For an **automotive CPS**, Govern function supports UNECE WP.29 R155 requirements for a certified Cybersecurity Management System (CSMS).

**Conclusion:** CSF 2.0's Govern function **closes the long-standing gap** between cybersecurity and enterprise governance — a critical need for CPS, where cyber risk can directly cause physical harm.

#### Model Solution to (b) — NERC CIP Compliance Calculation

**Given:** 12 NERC CIP controls, each with criticality $w_i$ and compliance state.

**Step 1 — Tally the states (1 mark):**
- Compliant (C): CIP-002, CIP-003, CIP-006, CIP-008, CIP-009, CIP-011, CIP-014 → $N_C = 7$
- Partially Compliant (PC): CIP-004, CIP-007, CIP-013 → $N_{PC} = 3$
- Non-Compliant (NC): CIP-005, CIP-010 → $N_{NC} = 2$
- Total: $7 + 3 + 2 = 12$ ✓

**Step 2 — Compute unweighted CR (1 mark):**

$$CR = \frac{N_C + 0.5 \cdot N_{PC}}{N_C + N_{PC} + N_{NC}} = \frac{7 + 0.5 \cdot 3}{12} = \frac{7 + 1.5}{12} = \frac{8.5}{12}$$

$$CR \approx 0.7083 = 70.83\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute sum of all weights (1 mark):**

$$\sum w_i = 9 + 8 + 7 + 10 + 8 + 9 + 6 + 8 + 9 + 7 + 6 + 5 = 92$$

**Step 4 — Compute weighted sum (1 mark):**
Weighted sum = $w_i \times s(a_i)$ for each control:

$$\sum w_i s_i = (9)(1) + (8)(1) + (7)(0.5) + (10)(0) + (8)(1) + (9)(0.5) + (6)(1) + (8)(1) + (9)(0) + (7)(1) + (6)(0.5) + (5)(1)$$

Computing each term:

$$= 9 + 8 + 3.5 + 0 + 8 + 4.5 + 6 + 8 + 0 + 7 + 3 + 5 = 62$$

**Step 5 — Compute Weighted CR (1 mark):**

$$WCR = \frac{62}{92} \approx 0.6739 = 67.39\%$$

**Step 6 — Identify Top-3 Highest-Priority Remediation Targets (2 marks):**

Priority is determined by the **criticality of a non-compliant or partially compliant control** — the higher the weight, the higher the priority.

| Rank | Control | Weight | State | Priority Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **CIP-005** (Electronic Security Perimeter) | **10** | Non-Compliant | **Critical**: Defines the entire OT/IT network boundary; failure exposes all BES cyber systems. |
| **2** | **CIP-010** (Configuration Change Management) | **9** | Non-Compliant | **Critical**: Without change control, the perimeter and system integrity cannot be trusted. |
| **3** | **CIP-007** (System Security Management) | **9** | Partially Compliant | **High**: Patch management, logging, malware detection — foundational cyber hygiene. |

**Alternative rank 3** (accept either): **CIP-004** (Personnel & Training) with weight 7 partially compliant, if the examiner emphasizes the **safety-of-life** linkage of training gaps in BES operations.

**Conclusion (extra):** The utility's compliance posture is approximately **70.83% unweighted / 67.39% weighted**. NERC CIP enforcement can attract penalties of up to **$1M per violation per day**, so the two NC controls (CIP-005, CIP-010) demand **immediate executive escalation and remediation within 30 days**.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in Compliance Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SL-T with SIL** — SL-T is **Security Level Target** (IEC 62443); SIL is **Safety Integrity Level** (IEC 61508). They measure different things. Students who use them interchangeably lose 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Conflating PDCA with ISO 27001** — PDCA is the **underlying cycle**; ISO 27001 is one of many standards that use it. If the question asks about PDCA, describe the **four stages** with deliverables, not the 10 clauses of ISO 27001.
>
> 3. **Skipping the derivation in numerical questions** — Examiners expect the **table of states, summation, and final ratio** explicitly. A bare answer "CR = 70.83%" with no work shown loses 1.5 of the 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Treating "Govern" as a sub-function** — In NIST CSF **2.0 (2024)**, Govern is a **top-level function** alongside Identify-Protect-Detect-Respond-Recover. If you write "Govern is a sub-category of Identify", you have cited the **wrong version** of the framework and lose marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the evidence dimension** — Saying a control is "compliant" without saying "evidenced by X, Y, Z" is incomplete. **Compliance = implementation + evidence.**
>
> 6. **Mixing up HIPAA and HITECH, GDPR and DPDP, NERC and FERC** — These are **distinct** regulatory bodies and acts. Examiners deduct for terminological imprecision.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Compliance Considerations in CPS Deployments"**

- **Definition:** Compliance = demonstrable conformance to laws, standards, and policies governing CPS design, operation, and maintenance.
- **Difference from Security:** Security = actual protection; Compliance = documented evidence of protection as required.
- **PDCA Lifecycle:** Plan (identify, scope, baseline) → Do (implement controls, collect evidence) → Check (audit, measure CR) → Act (remediate, improve) — applied continuously.
- **NIST CSF 2.0 (2024)** has **6 functions**: **Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover.** Govern is **new and top-level**.
- **IEC 62443-3-3** has **7 Foundational Requirements (FRs)** and **4 Security Levels (SL-1 to SL-4)** with three SL concepts: **Target, Achieved, Capability (SL-T, SL-A, SL-C).**
- **Zones and Conduits:** Zone = grouping of assets with shared security requirements; Conduit = security-controlled communication path between zones.
- **NERC CIP-002 to CIP-014** are mandatory for North American Bulk Electric System; max penalty **$1M/violation/day**.
- **GDPR** max penalty: **€20M or 4% of global turnover**; **HIPAA** tier-4 max: **$1.5M per violation per year**.
- **India-specific:** IT Act 2000, Cert-In Directions 2022 (logs 180 days, incident reporting 6 hours), DPDP Act 2023.
- **Key Formula:** $CR = \frac{N_C + 0.5 N_{PC}}{n} \times 100\%$; $WCR = \frac{\sum w_i s_i}{\sum w_i} \times 100\%$.
- **Compliance Risk Register** ranks gaps by $RS = L \times I$ (1–5 scale), with categories Low, Medium, High, Critical.
- **Penalty Exposure:** $PE = \sum V_k P_k$; risk-adjusted: $PE_{\text{annual}} = P_{\text{audit}} \cdot \sum V_k P_k$.
- **Compliance-as-Code (CaC)** is the modern practice of expressing controls as machine-readable policies for continuous, automated CPS compliance.
- **Common pitfall:** A system can be **secure but non-compliant**, or **compliant but insecure** — these are independent dimensions.
- **Audit-evidence examples:** configuration backups, signed approvals, log samples, interview records, walkthrough observations, vulnerability scan outputs.
- **Sectoral overlap:** A typical CPS faces 5–10 simultaneous frameworks — use the **Unified Compliance Framework (UCF)** approach to reduce audit fatigue.
- **CPS Layer-Framework Mapping:** Perception (ISO 27001, GDPR) → Network (IEC 62443, NERC CIP-005) → Control (IEC 62443-3-3, NERC CIP-007) → Application (ISO 27001 A.14) → Actuation (IEC 61508, ISO 26262) → Governance (NIST CSF 2.0 Govern, ISO 27001).
- **Continuous compliance > periodic audits:** Industry 4.0 CPS deployments shift from annual to **continuous, automated** compliance monitoring via GRC platforms and Compliance-as-Code.
